## Agent 3 / C — O2 cofactor-vector nonrecurrence

The Agent 3 target is the pointwise implication

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Longrightarrow
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A},
\]

for the full canonical object

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

not merely for \(P_2\), \(B_2\), or a zero-side proxy. The coordinator explicitly warns that exterior coefficient mass and averaged large-sieve information are insufficient unless paired with a bad-zero exclusion mechanism. 

## Verdict

O2 is **not closed** by the current machinery.

What can be reduced cleanly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2 follows from canonical exterior coefficient mass}
+
\text{a polynomial bad-zero exclusion / Bezout-resultant input.}
}
\]

The missing input is not local algebra. It is an actual arithmetic nonrecurrence theorem saying that a zero of \(Z_\alpha\) cannot also lie polynomially close to the cofactor-flat variety of the full canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-jet block.

---

## 1. Canonical algebraic reduction

Let

\[
f(m,\alpha):=q''_{\can}(m,\alpha).
\]

Ignoring only the harmless basis-conditioning constants from the operators \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\), O2 is equivalent to a lower exterior volume bound for the \((R+1)\times(R+2)\) Hankel-type jet matrix

\[
\widetilde Q_{j\ell}(m)
=
\mathcal D^{j+\ell} f(m),
\qquad
0\le j\le R,\quad 0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

The cofactor vector satisfies

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\star(Q_0\wedge\cdots\wedge Q_R),
\]

and therefore

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}\|^2
=
\det\bigl(\langle Q_i,Q_j\rangle\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le R}
=
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}|M_c|^2,
\]

where

\[
M_c=(-1)^c\det Q^{\widehat c}
\]

is the maximal minor obtained by deleting column \(c\).

Thus O2 is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the canonical }q''_{\can}\text{-jet rows have exterior volume }\ge Q^{-A}
\text{ at every admissible }Z_\alpha\text{-zero.}
}
\]

This is stronger than saying that one row is nonzero, or that the coefficient expansion of one minor is nontrivial.

---

## 2. Exterior coefficient mass: available as a structural reduction, not enough

For the oscillatory packet part, freeze the slowly varying AFE weights to first order and write schematically

\[
f_{\osc}(m)
=
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal N}
a_\nu(m,\alpha)e^{-im\omega_\nu},
\qquad
y_\nu:=-i\lambda\omega_\nu.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal D^k f_{\osc}(m)
\approx
\sum_{\nu}
a_\nu(m,\alpha)y_\nu^k e^{-im\omega_\nu}.
\]

For the monomial-column version of the cofactor coordinate \(M_c\), Cauchy--Binet gives

\[
M_c^{\fr}(m)
=
\sum_{\substack{S\subset\mathcal N\\ |S|=R+1}}
A_{c,S}(m,\alpha)
e^{-im\Omega_S},
\qquad
\Omega_S:=\sum_{\nu\in S}\omega_\nu,
\]

with

\[
A_{c,S}
=
\pm
\left(\prod_{\nu\in S}a_\nu\right)
V(y_S)^2
e_{R+1-c}(y_S),
\]

where

\[
V(y_S)=\prod_{\mu<\nu,\ \mu,\nu\in S}(y_\nu-y_\mu),
\]

and \(e_k\) is the elementary symmetric polynomial of degree \(k\).

So the cofactor vector is a subset-product exponential polynomial. The full canonical object adds:

\[
B_2,\quad D_2,\quad E_2,
\]

plus derivatives of the AFE weights. These are manageable only if canonical transfer is available through

\[
0\le k\le 2R+1.
\]

A reasonable structural lemma is therefore:

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Exterior coefficient mass for the canonical cofactor block]
Assume the ordinary packet-frequency regime
\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,\qquad
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]
and assume the basis operators \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\) are polynomially
conditioned.  Suppose the full canonical expansion
\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]
admits a packet-frequency representation through normalized derivative depth
\(2R+1\), with \(E_2\) bounded below the exterior-volume error budget.  Suppose
also that the oscillatory packet contains a collision-free separated subfamily
whose Vandermonde volume and coefficient mass satisfy
\[
\sum_{\substack{S:\ |S|=R+1}}
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}
|A_{c,S}|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]
Then the canonical cofactor vector is not symbolically zero:
\[
\sum_{\Omega}
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}
|A_{c,\Omega}^{\can}|^2
\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]
\end{lemma}
```

This is useful, but it is **not O2**.

It only proves that the exponential polynomial \(\mathbf M_{\can}(m,\alpha)\) has nontrivial coefficient mass. It does not prevent cancellation at a prescribed zero \(m_0\).

---

## 3. Why averaged estimates do not prove O2

Even a strong averaged estimate such as

\[
\int_I \|\mathbf M_{\can}(m,\alpha)\|^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-A}|I|
\]

or

\[
\sum_{\substack{m_j\in I\\ Z_\alpha(m_j)=0}}
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_j,\alpha)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}N_{Z_\alpha}(I)
\]

does not imply

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

for a specified zero \(m_0\).

The obstruction is elementary. An exponential polynomial can have large coefficient mass and large average while vanishing at a prescribed point:

\[
M(t)=e^{it}-1,
\qquad
M(0)=0,
\qquad
\int_0^{2\pi}|M(t)|^2dt>0.
\]

Thus the logical gap is not an analytic-norm gap. It is a **bad-zero exclusion** gap.

In the RH draft’s language, exterior coefficient mass proves

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}\not\equiv0,
\]

but O2 requires

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)\not\approx0.
\]

That is a joint nonrecurrence assertion for the actual prime-log orbit.

---

## 4. Exact additional arithmetic input needed

The cleanest usable input is a polynomial-height Bezout certificate.

### Proposed O2-reducing input

For every admissible ordinary interval \(I\), every admissible \(\alpha\), and the full canonical cofactor coordinates

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}=(M_0,\ldots,M_{R+1}),
\]

there exist controlled analytic/exponential-polynomial multipliers

\[
U_0,U_1,\ldots,U_{R+2}
\]

such that on \(I\),

\[
U_0(m)Z_\alpha(m)
+
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}U_{c+1}(m)M_c(m,\alpha)
=
1+\mathcal E_{\Bez}(m),
\]

with

\[
\|U_c\|_{L^\infty(I)}\le Q^A,
\qquad
\|\mathcal E_{\Bez}\|_{L^\infty(I)}\le Q^{-B},
\]

where \(B\) is chosen much larger than \(A\).

Then at a zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\),

\[
\frac12
\le
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}|U_{c+1}(m_0)|\,|M_c(m_0,\alpha)|
\le
Q^A\sqrt{R+2}\,\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

the factor \(\sqrt{R+2}\) is harmless, giving

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|
\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Polynomial Bezout certificate}
\Longrightarrow
\text{O2}.
}
\]

But this Bezout certificate is precisely the missing arithmetic theorem. It is equivalent to a polynomial lower bound for the analytic resultant/common-small-value obstruction between \(Z_\alpha\) and the full canonical cofactor vector.

---

## 5. Reduction statement to add to the paper

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O2-BZ: Z--cofactor bad-zero exclusion]
\label{hyp:O2-BZ-z-cofactor-bad-zero-exclusion}
Work in the admissible ordinary packet-frequency regime
\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,\qquad
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]
Let
\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]
be the full canonical source, controlled through normalized derivative depth
\(2R+1\).  Let
\[
Q_j(m,\alpha)
=
\left(
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}(m,\alpha)
\right)_{\ell=0}^{R+1},
\qquad
0\le j\le R,
\]
and
\[
\mathbf M_{\can}(m,\alpha)
=
\star(Q_0\wedge\cdots\wedge Q_R)
=
(M_0,\ldots,M_{R+1}).
\]
There exist constants \(A,B>0\), with \(B\) sufficiently large relative to
\(A\), such that on every admissible ordinary interval \(I\) there are
analytic/exponential-polynomial multipliers
\[
U_0,U_1,\ldots,U_{R+2}
\]
satisfying
\[
\|U_c\|_{L^\infty(I)}\le Q^A
\]
and
\[
U_0 Z_\alpha+\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}U_{c+1}M_c
=
1+\mathcal E_{\Bez},
\qquad
\|\mathcal E_{\Bez}\|_{L^\infty(I)}\le Q^{-B}.
\]
\end{hypothesis}

\begin{lemma}[O2 from bad-zero exclusion]
\label{lem:O2-from-BZ}
Assume Hypothesis~\ref{hyp:O2-BZ-z-cofactor-bad-zero-exclusion}.  Then for
every admissible ordinary-scale zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\),
\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]
Hence O2 holds.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Evaluate the Bezout identity at \(m=m_0\).  Since \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\),
\[
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}U_{c+1}(m_0)M_c(m_0,\alpha)
=
1+\mathcal E_{\Bez}(m_0).
\]
For \(B\) large, the right-hand side has modulus at least \(1/2\).  Therefore
\[
\frac12
\le
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}|U_{c+1}(m_0)|\,|M_c(m_0,\alpha)|
\le
Q^A\sqrt{R+2}\,\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|.
\]
Because \(R\asymp\log Q/\log\log Q\), the factor \(\sqrt{R+2}\) is absorbed
into \(Q^{o(1)}\).  Thus
\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A'}
\]
for some fixed \(A'\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[What remains unproved in O2]
Exterior coefficient mass for \(\mathbf M_{\can}\) shows only that the cofactor
vector is not identically zero as an exponential polynomial.  It does not
exclude cancellation at a prescribed zero of \(Z_\alpha\).  Therefore O2
requires a genuine bad-zero exclusion theorem, equivalently a polynomial
resultant/Bezout lower bound for the system
\[
Z_\alpha=0,\qquad
M_0=\cdots=M_{R+1}=0,
\]
with all objects formed from the full canonical source
\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]
A theorem for \(P_2\), \(B_2\), a zero-side proxy, or an averaged large-sieve
estimate is diagnostic only unless it supplies this bad-zero exclusion.
\end{remark}
```

## Final status for O2

| Component | Status | Reason |
|---|---:|---|
| Cofactor algebra / Hodge-dual formulation | Green | \(\|\mathbf M\|^2\) is the Gram determinant / sum of maximal minors. |
| Exterior coefficient expansion | Green–Yellow | Cauchy--Binet gives subset-product phases; canonical derivative transfer still required. |
| Exterior coefficient mass | Yellow | Plausible from packet separation and Vandermonde mass, but must be stated for full \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\). |
| Averaged large-sieve route | Red as standalone | Cannot exclude a specified bad zero. |
| Pointwise O2 | Red/open | Needs polynomial bad-zero exclusion / Bezout-resultant theorem for \((Z_\alpha,\mathbf M_{\can})\). |

Net: **O2 should remain a named open arithmetic input unless the project proves the Bezout/resultant bad-zero exclusion above.**

## Agent 3 / C — O2-BZ attack result

The Agent 3 instruction is to attack the O2-BZ route: prove or disprove a polynomial Bezout certificate

\[
U_0Z_\alpha+\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}U_{c+1}M_c
=
1+\mathcal E_{\Bez},
\qquad
\|U_c\|_\infty\le Q^A,
\qquad
\|\mathcal E_{\Bez}\|_\infty\le Q^{-B},
\]

for the full canonical cofactor vector built from

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The instruction also explicitly forbids relying on exterior coefficient mass or averaged large sieve alone; the point is pointwise cofactor nonflatness at specified \(Z_\alpha\)-zeros. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2-BZ is not closed by any currently available Bezout/resultant route.}
}
\]

The canonical cofactor structure gives a useful **differential-jet reformulation**, but it does **not** avoid the super-polynomial losses of subset-frequency inversion. The correct output is therefore a sharper no-go/reduction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2 requires a new prime-log cofactor-corona theorem.}
}
\]

---

# 1. Exact Plücker reformulation of O2

Let

\[
f=q''_{\can},
\]

and

\[
Q_j(m,\alpha)
=
\left(
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j f(m,\alpha)
\right)_{\ell=0}^{R+1},
\qquad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Let \(Q\) be the \((R+1)\times(R+2)\) matrix with rows \(Q_j\). Then

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\star(Q_0\wedge\cdots\wedge Q_R)
\]

is the vector of maximal minors. Thus

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)=0
\]

if and only if the rows \(Q_0,\ldots,Q_R\) have rank at most \(R\), equivalently there exists a nonzero vector

\[
a=(a_0,\ldots,a_{R+1})
\]

such that

\[
Q_j(m_0,\alpha)\cdot a=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Define the variable coefficient operator

\[
\mathcal L_a
:=
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}a_\ell C_\ell(\mathcal D).
\]

Then cofactor collapse is equivalent to

\[
\mathcal D^j\mathcal L_a f(m_0,\alpha)=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le R.
\]

So O2 is equivalent, up to the conditioning of the \(C_\ell\)-basis, to the statement:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Longrightarrow
\inf_{\|a\|=1}
\sum_{j=0}^{R}
\left|
\mathcal D^j\mathcal L_a q''_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the cleanest form of the missing theorem.

It is stronger and more structural than “exterior coefficient mass,” but it is still a pointwise prime-log nonrecurrence assertion.

---

# 2. Why generic Bezout/resultant loses super-polynomially

Write the oscillatory canonical source schematically as

\[
q''_{\can}(m,\alpha)
=
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal N}
a_\nu(m,\alpha)e^{-im\omega_\nu}
+
\text{background}
+
\text{dual}
+
\text{tail},
\]

where the main packet frequencies satisfy

\[
\omega_\nu\sim \log n,
\qquad
c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q.
\]

After freezing slowly varying weights only for the purpose of frequency bookkeeping, Cauchy--Binet gives for each cofactor coordinate

\[
M_c(m,\alpha)
=
\sum_{\substack{S\subset\mathcal N\\ |S|=R+1}}
A_{c,S}(m,\alpha)e^{-im\Omega_S}
+
\text{canonical transfer terms},
\]

with

\[
\Omega_S=\sum_{\nu\in S}\omega_\nu.
\]

For integer frequencies coming from \(\log n\),

\[
\Omega_S=\log N_S,
\qquad
N_S=\prod_{\nu\in S}n_\nu.
\]

Since each \(n_\nu\le e^{C Q}\) and \(|S|=R+1\),

\[
N_S\le e^{CQR}.
\]

For distinct subset products,

\[
|\Omega_S-\Omega_T|
=
\left|\log\frac{N_S}{N_T}\right|
\gtrsim e^{-CQR}.
\]

With

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this gives

\[
e^{-CQR}
=
\exp\!\left(
-CQ\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}
\right),
\]

which is much smaller than any \(Q^{-A}\).

Any construction that separates these subset frequencies by Vandermonde inversion, filtered minors, Prony-type recovery, or classical exponential-polynomial resultants pays at least

\[
\delta^{-1}
\quad\text{with}\quad
\delta\asymp e^{-CQR},
\]

and typically

\[
\delta^{-O(R)}
=
\exp(CQR^2).
\]

Both are super-polynomial in \(Q\). Therefore such a route cannot produce

\[
\|U_c\|_\infty\le Q^A.
\]

This is the main obstruction.

---

# 3. Does canonical cofactor structure remove the loss?

I do not see a mechanism that removes it.

The cofactor structure is special in the **row/rank** sense:

\[
M_c=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists \mathcal L_a:
\mathcal D^j\mathcal L_a q''_{\can}(m_0)=0,
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

But once expanded as an exponential polynomial, the Plücker coordinates still contain subset-product phases

\[
\Omega_S=\log\prod_{\nu\in S}n_\nu.
\]

The shared AFE origin of \(Z_\alpha\) and \(q''_{\can}\) does not linearize these phases. \(Z_\alpha\) contains single AFE frequencies \(\log n\), while \(M_c\) contains \((R+1)\)-fold subset-product frequencies. Multipliers \(U_c\) would still need to separate or cancel those subset-product phases unless a new arithmetic identity is supplied.

The background/gamma term \(B_2\), dual contribution \(D_2\), and tail \(E_2\) also do not improve the situation. They are essential for canonical correctness, but they do not collapse the subset-product frequency set into a polynomially separated family. If anything, they add more transfer terms that must be controlled.

So the canonical structure gives a better **statement** of O2, but not a proof of O2-BZ.

---

# 4. Local analytic Nullstellensatz / Cartan route

A local analytic corona statement would be enough if one could prove

\[
\inf_{z\in \mathcal N_\rho(I)}
\left(
|Z_\alpha(z)|^2+
\sum_{c=0}^{R+1}|M_c(z,\alpha)|^2
\right)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then standard one-variable corona/Bezout machinery would give controlled multipliers, with constants depending polynomially on the lower bound and the derivative norms.

But this lower bound is essentially the missing theorem. In particular, at the real zeros of \(Z_\alpha\), it reduces exactly to

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Longrightarrow
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Cartan-type estimates can show that an analytic function is not too small outside a small exceptional set, but the zeros of \(Z_\alpha\) may lie inside that exceptional set. Thus Cartan gives “most points,” not specified \(Z_\alpha\)-zeros.

So the localized Nullstellensatz route is circular unless supplemented by a zero-restricted lower bound.

---

# 5. Differentiated common-zero route

Assume O2 fails at a zero \(m_0\):

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\qquad
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\le Q^{-A}.
\]

Assume the rows \(Q_j\) have polynomial upper bounds

\[
\|Q_j\|\le Q^C.
\]

Then small cofactor volume implies an approximate right-kernel vector \(a\), \(\|a\|=1\), such that

\[
\sum_{j=0}^{R}
\left|
\mathcal D^j\mathcal L_a q''_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)
\right|^2
\le Q^{C_R}Q^{-2A},
\]

where \(C_R=O_R(C)\), polynomially harmless for fixed bookkeeping if \(A\) is chosen large enough.

Thus O2 failure gives:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathcal L_a q''_{\can}
\text{ has an }R\text{-jet almost-zero at }m_0.
\]

This is an overdetermined-looking condition, but it does **not** contradict current inputs. There is no established local identity forcing a high-order zero of \(\mathcal L_a q''_{\can}\) at a \(Z_\alpha\)-zero to create extra zeros of \(Z_\alpha\), violate zero spacing, or contradict finite-jet energy.

So the differentiated route reduces O2-BZ to the following missing theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{\substack{Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\\ \|a\|=1}}
\left(
\sum_{j=0}^{R}
\left|
\mathcal D^j
\left(
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}a_\ell C_\ell(\mathcal D)
\right)
q''_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)
\right|^2
\right)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is probably the sharpest formulation of O2.

---

# 6. Relation between O2-BZ and O3

O2-BZ does **not** reduce to O3.

O3 is an angle condition:

\[
|\langle \mathbf z',\mathbf M\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A}\|\mathbf z'\|\,\|\mathbf M\|.
\]

It presupposes that both \(\mathbf z'\) and \(\mathbf M\) have meaningful size. O2 supplies the size of \(\mathbf M\). Therefore O3 cannot replace O2.

Conversely, O2-BZ does not imply O3, because \(\mathbf M\) can be large while nearly orthogonal to \(\mathbf z'\).

A stronger unified theorem would imply both:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Longrightarrow
|\langle \mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha),\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\rangle|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Together with upper/lower bounds for \(\mathbf z'\), this would imply O2 and O3. But this is strictly stronger than O2-BZ and is essentially the unified prime-log incidence theorem identified by the coordinator.

---

# 7. Final missing theorem

The clean missing theorem is:

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O2-BZ prime-log cofactor corona]
\label{hyp:O2-BZ-prime-log-cofactor-corona}
Work in the admissible ordinary packet-frequency regime
\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,
\qquad
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]
Let
\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]
be the full canonical source, and define
\[
Q_j(m,\alpha)
=
\left(
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}(m,\alpha)
\right)_{\ell=0}^{R+1},
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]
Let
\[
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\star(Q_0\wedge\cdots\wedge Q_R).
\]
Then there exists \(A>0\) such that for every admissible zero
\(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\),
\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\inf_{\substack{Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\\ \|a\|=1}}
\left(
\sum_{j=0}^{R}
\left|
\mathcal D^j
\left(
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}a_\ell C_\ell(\mathcal D)
\right)
q''_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)
\right|^2
\right)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]
\end{hypothesis}
```

And the precise no-go remark:

```latex
\begin{remark}[Why generic Bezout does not prove O2-BZ]
The cofactor coordinates \(M_c\) have Cauchy--Binet expansions over
subset-product frequencies
\[
\Omega_S=\log\prod_{\nu\in S}n_\nu,
\qquad |S|=R+1.
\]
Since \(n_\nu\le e^{CQ}\), these products are bounded by \(e^{CQR}\), and
distinct subset-product logarithms may be separated only by
\[
\exp(-CQR).
\]
With \(R\asymp\log Q/\log\log Q\), this is super-polynomially smaller than
\(Q^{-A}\).  Therefore any Bezout, resultant, Vandermonde, filtered-minor, or
frequency-inversion argument that separates the subset-product phases incurs
super-polynomial constants and cannot yield multipliers satisfying
\[
\|U_c\|_\infty\le Q^A.
\]
The canonical Pluecker structure reformulates O2 as the exclusion of an
\(R\)-jet zero of
\[
\left(
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}a_\ell C_\ell(\mathcal D)
\right)q''_{\can}
\]
at a zero of \(Z_\alpha\), but it does not by itself supply the required
zero-restricted lower bound.  O2-BZ is therefore a genuine prime-log
cofactor-corona input for the full canonical object
\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]
\end{remark}
```

## Status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Deliverable B: current Bezout/resultant routes incur super-polynomial losses.}
}
\]

O2-BZ should remain a named upstream arithmetic input unless the project proves the prime-log cofactor-corona theorem above.

## Agent 3 continuation — O2-BZ, corrected attack

The prompt’s target remains O2-BZ for the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

with cofactor vector

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\star(Q_0\wedge\cdots\wedge Q_R),
\qquad
Q_j=
\left(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}\right)_{\ell=0}^{R+1}.
\]

The task is pointwise nonrecurrence at specified zeros \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), not exterior mass or an averaged large-sieve substitute. 

I found a useful correction and a sharper reduction.

---

# 1. Correct linear algebra: O2 failure is a **left recurrence**, not a right-kernel condition

Let \(F=q''_{\can}\), and form the matrix

\[
Q(m_0)=
\bigl[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jF(m_0)
\bigr]_{
0\le j\le R,\ 0\le \ell\le R+1}.
\]

This is an \((R+1)\times(R+2)\) matrix.

The cofactor vector satisfies

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}\|^2
=
\det(QQ^*).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}\|
=
\prod_{a=0}^{R}\sigma_a(Q),
\]

the product of the \(R+1\) nonzero singular values, counted with multiplicity.

Important correction: because \(Q\) has more columns than rows, it **always** has a nonzero right-null vector. So the earlier right-kernel formulation

\[
Q a=0
\]

is not itself meaningful. That condition is automatic for full-row-rank \(Q\).

The real content of O2 is that the rows of \(Q\) have non-small exterior volume. Therefore O2 failure means a **small left singular value**.

Assume the crude row-size bound

\[
\|Q\|_{\op}\le Q^C.
\]

If

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\|\le Q^{-A},
\]

then

\[
\sigma_{\min}(Q)
\le
Q^{CR-A}.
\]

Thus there exists a unit vector

\[
\beta=(\beta_0,\ldots,\beta_R),
\qquad
\|\beta\|_2=1,
\]

such that

\[
\left\|
\sum_{j=0}^R \overline{\beta_j}Q_j
\right\|_2
\le
Q^{CR-A}.
\]

Equivalently, for every \(0\le \ell\le R+1\),

\[
\sum_{j=0}^R
\overline{\beta_j}
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jF(m_0)
=
O(Q^{CR-A}).
\]

Since \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\) and \(\mathcal D\) commute, define

\[
P_\beta(\mathcal D)
:=
\sum_{j=0}^{R}\overline{\beta_j}\mathcal D^j.
\]

Then O2 failure gives

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)P_\beta(\mathcal D)F(m_0)
=
O(Q^{CR-A}),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

If the \(C_\ell\)-basis is polynomially conditioned against the ordinary derivative basis, this is equivalent to

\[
\mathcal D^k
\left[
P_\beta(\mathcal D)F
\right](m_0)
=
O(Q^{C'R-A}),
\qquad
0\le k\le R+1.
\]

So the sharpened meaning of O2-BZ is:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\text{cannot coexist with an }(R+1)\text{-jet near-zero of }
P_\beta(\mathcal D)q''_{\can}
\text{ for any unit }P_\beta.
}
\]

This is the correct recurrence form.

---

# 2. Moment/Hankel interpretation

In the ordinary packet window, write locally

\[
F(m)
\approx
\sum_n a_n(m_0,\alpha)e^{-im\omega_n},
\qquad
\omega_n\asymp Q.
\]

Let

\[
s_k(m_0)
:=
\mathcal D^kF(m_0)
\approx
\sum_n a_n(-i\omega_n)^k e^{-im_0\omega_n}.
\]

Modulo the \(C_\ell\)-basis change, \(Q\) is a rectangular Hankel matrix:

\[
Q_{j\ell}\sim s_{j+\ell},
\qquad
0\le j\le R,\quad 0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

O2 failure is therefore the existence of a degree-\(R\) polynomial

\[
P_\beta(x)=\sum_{j=0}^R\overline{\beta_j}x^j
\]

such that the weighted moment sequence

\[
\sum_n a_n e^{-im_0\omega_n}
P_\beta(-i\omega_n)(-i\omega_n)^k
\]

is small for all

\[
0\le k\le R+1.
\]

That is a finite recurrence / Prony degeneracy condition:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The canonical }(2R+1)\text{-jet of }q''_{\can}
\text{ at }m_0
\text{ is nearly generated by }\le R\text{ exponentials.}
}
\]

So O2 is exactly a quantitative **no-low-complexity Hankel recurrence** theorem at zeros of \(Z_\alpha\).

---

# 3. A positive-measure surrogate would prove O2, but the actual object is complex

There is a useful comparison theorem.

Suppose instead that the packet moments came from a positive measure

\[
d\mu(\tau)\ge0
\]

on a fixed interval

\[
[c_-,c_+],
\]

with polynomial mass in \(R+1\) separated subintervals. Then no nonzero degree-\(R\) polynomial \(P\) can make

\[
\int P(\tau)\tau^k\,d\mu(\tau)
\]

small for all \(0\le k\le R+1\). Indeed,

\[
\int |P(\tau)|^2\,d\mu(\tau)
\]

is a linear combination of those moments with coefficients controlled by \(P\). Standard Remez/Markov inequalities on a fixed window give

\[
\int |P|^2\,d\mu
\ge
\exp(-O(R\log R))\|P\|_{\ell^2}^2.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

the loss

\[
\exp(-O(R\log R))
\]

is only polynomial in \(Q\). Thus a positive or sectorial packet measure would imply O2 with polynomial constants.

But the actual canonical packet measure is not positive. Its weights are complex:

\[
a_n(m_0,\alpha)e^{-im_0\log n},
\]

with rapidly rotating phases across the packet. The zero condition

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

imposes only one global cancellation relation. It does not put the packet weights into a sector, nor does it give positivity of the Hankel form.

Thus the positive-measure route proves that the obstruction is not the finite moment geometry. The obstruction is the **complex prime-log phase orbit**.

---

# 4. Why \(m\)- or \(\alpha\)-differentiation does not yet close O2

Assume O2 fails. Then for some unit \(P_\beta\),

\[
\mathcal D^k[P_\beta(\mathcal D)F](m_0)
\approx0,
\qquad
0\le k\le R+1.
\]

One might hope this forces a contradiction under \(m\)-differentiation, but it does not by itself. It only says that a new function

\[
G_\beta(m):=P_\beta(\mathcal D)F(m)
\]

has an approximate zero of order \(R+2\) at \(m_0\).

Without a theorem connecting high-order smallness of \(G_\beta\) to \(Z_\alpha\), this does not force:

\[
Z_\alpha'(m_0)\approx0,
\]

nor an extra zero of \(Z_\alpha\), nor a local zero-density contradiction.

Similarly, differentiating in \(\alpha\) only changes the coefficients and weights of the same complex moment problem. It gives another recurrence-like constraint, but not a sign or positivity mechanism.

So the differentiated route sharpens O2-BZ to a recurrence-exclusion theorem, but it does not prove it.

---

# 5. Dimension count: why the recurrence obstruction is plausible but not proved

O2 failure gives unknowns:

- \(m_0\), one real variable;
- possibly \(\alpha\), one real variable;
- \(\beta\in\mathbb C^{R+1}\), projectively normalized, about \(2R\) real variables.

The equations are:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

plus

\[
\mathcal D^k[P_\beta(\mathcal D)F](m_0)\approx0,
\qquad
0\le k\le R+1.
\]

That is roughly \(1+2(R+2)\) real conditions if the jet equations are complex, against about \(2R+1\) or \(2R+2\) real parameters.

So O2 failure is overdetermined by a fixed number of real equations. This is encouraging: generically it should not happen.

But “generically” is not proof. A proof would need a transversality or algebraic-independence theorem for the actual prime-log orbit. The current machinery does not supply that.

---

# 6. Relation to generic Bezout losses

The corrected left-recurrence view also explains why generic Bezout still fails.

The cofactor coordinates \(M_c\) are Plücker coordinates of a Hankel/moment matrix. Expanding them gives subset-product phases

\[
\Omega_S=\sum_{\nu\in S}\omega_\nu
=
\log\prod_{\nu\in S}n_\nu,
\qquad
|S|=R+1.
\]

Distinct subset products may be separated only by

\[
\exp(-CQR),
\]

and since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is super-polynomially small. Any proof that separates those phases by Vandermonde/resultant inversion pays super-polynomial constants. That cannot produce the desired polynomial multipliers

\[
\|U_c\|_\infty\le Q^A.
\]

So the only plausible route is not generic Bezout. It must exploit the recurrence structure directly.

---

# 7. Sharpened missing theorem

The precise missing theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-restricted complex Hankel anti-recurrence.}
}
\]

At every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), for every unit polynomial

\[
P(\mathcal D)=\sum_{j=0}^{R}\beta_j\mathcal D^j,
\qquad
\sum|\beta_j|^2=1,
\]

one has

\[
\sum_{k=0}^{R+1}
\left|
\mathcal D^k
\left[
P(\mathcal D)q''_{\can}
\right](m_0)
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\sigma_{\min}
\left[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}(m_0)
\right]_
{0\le j\le R,\ 0\le\ell\le R+1}
\ge Q^{-A}
\]

up to polynomial row-size normalization.

This is stronger and cleaner than the previous Bezout formulation. It avoids the misleading right-null-vector picture and expresses O2 as a genuine finite Hankel nonrecurrence condition.

---

# 8. Current status

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2-BZ remains open, but the exact missing input is now sharper.}
}
\]

The route through positive/sectorial moments would prove it with polynomial losses, but the actual canonical packet is complex and phase-oscillatory. Generic Bezout/resultant methods still lose super-polynomially. The only viable path I see is a new theorem excluding complex Hankel recurrences of the full canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-jet at specified \(Z_\alpha\)-zeros.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 18:42:16)

## Agent 3 continuation — O2 as a cell-spread theorem

I pushed the corrected O2-BZ formulation one level further. The main new point is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2 is not merely anti-recurrence; it requires }R+1
\text{ separated active canonical cells.}
}
\]

A degree-\(R\) recurrence can annihilate \(R\) separated cells. Therefore O2 cannot follow from O1a-style total cell mass unless that mass is spread across more than \(R\) independent cells.

---

# 1. Model reduction to cell moments

Let

\[
F=q''_{\can}.
\]

In the ordinary packet window, write the normalized frequency variable as

\[
\tau_n:=\frac{\log n}{Q}\in[c_-,c_+].
\]

Partition \([c_-,c_+]\) into separated cells \(I_r\) of width

\[
\Delta \asymp R^{-B_0},
\]

with representative point \(\tau_r\). Locally freeze the slowly varying amplitudes to isolate the moment structure:

\[
F(m_0)
\approx
\sum_r A_r(m_0,\alpha),
\]

where

\[
A_r(m_0,\alpha)
:=
\sum_{\tau_n\in I_r}
a_n(m_0,\alpha)e^{-im_0\log n}.
\]

Then normalized derivatives satisfy

\[
\mathcal D^kF(m_0)
\approx
Q^k\sum_r A_r\tau_r^k
+
\text{cell-width error}
+
\text{canonical transfer error}.
\]

After removing the harmless \(Q^k\)-normalization and basis-conditioning factors from \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\), the O2 matrix is modeled by the Hankel/moment matrix

\[
H_{j\ell}
=
\sum_r A_r\tau_r^{j+\ell},
\qquad
0\le j\le R,\quad 0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
H
=
V_R(\tau)\operatorname{diag}(A_r)V_{R+1}(\tau)^T,
\]

where

\[
V_R(\tau)_{jr}=\tau_r^j,
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

Thus O2 is a lower bound for the row exterior volume of this complex moment matrix.

---

# 2. Positive result: \(R+1\) separated active cells imply O2

Here is the useful theorem.

Assume there exist \(R+1\) cells

\[
r_0,\ldots,r_R
\]

such that

\[
|\tau_{r_a}-\tau_{r_b}|\ge cR^{-B_0}
\quad(a\ne b),
\]

and

\[
|A_{r_a}(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-C_0},
\qquad
0\le a\le R.
\]

Let \(H^{(S)}\) be the contribution from these \(R+1\) cells:

\[
H^{(S)}
=
V_S\operatorname{diag}(A_{r_0},\ldots,A_{r_R})W_S^T,
\]

with

\[
(V_S)_{ja}=\tau_{r_a}^j,
\qquad
0\le j\le R,
\]

and

\[
(W_S)_{\ell a}=\tau_{r_a}^{\ell},
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

Choose any \(R+1\) columns of \(W_S^T\), say \(\ell=0,\ldots,R\). The resulting square minor is

\[
\det(V_S)\,
\left(\prod_{a=0}^{R}A_{r_a}\right)
\det(V_S^T)
=
\left(\prod_{a=0}^{R}A_{r_a}\right)
\det(V_S)^2.
\]

The Vandermonde determinant satisfies

\[
|\det(V_S)|
=
\prod_{a<b}|\tau_{r_b}-\tau_{r_a}|
\ge
(cR^{-B_0})^{R(R+1)/2}.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

we have

\[
R^{-O(R^2)}
=
\exp(-O(R^2\log R))
=
Q^{-o(\log Q/\log\log Q)}.
\]

This is worse than \(Q^{-O(1)}\) if one keeps the full Vandermonde determinant at depth \(R\). So the naive full determinant estimate is too expensive.

But this is only the **monomial basis** loss. The paper’s \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\)-basis is precisely supposed to avoid catastrophic monomial conditioning. If the \(C_\ell\)-basis is a polynomially conditioned orthogonal/Chebyshev-type basis on \([c_-,c_+]\), the corresponding evaluation matrix obeys

\[
\sigma_{\min}(V_S^{C})
\ge Q^{-C_1}
\]

for the chosen separated cells, provided the separation scale is not smaller than \(R^{-O(1)}\) and the basis is normalized in the correct weighted \(L^2\) geometry.

Then

\[
\sigma_{\min}(H^{(S)})
\ge
Q^{-C_1}
\cdot
Q^{-C_0}
\cdot
Q^{-C_1}
=
Q^{-C_2}.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
R+1\text{ separated active canonical cells}
+
\text{polynomially conditioned }C_\ell\text{-basis}
\Longrightarrow
\text{O2}.
}
\]

This is a real positive route, but it requires a stronger input than O1a.

---

# 3. Why O1a is insufficient

O1a gives only

\[
\sum_r |B_r|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

That permits all mass to concentrate in \(s\le R\) cells.

If the moment matrix is generated by only \(s\le R\) cells, then

\[
H
=
V_s\operatorname{diag}(A_r)W_s^T
\]

has rank at most \(s\le R\). Since O2 needs row rank \(R+1\), the cofactor volume can vanish:

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}=0.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a total mass cannot imply O2.}
}
\]

Even worse, a degree-\(R\) polynomial

\[
P(\tau)
\]

can vanish at any prescribed \(R\) cell centers. Therefore the recurrence polynomial from O2 failure can annihilate all the mass allowed by O1a.

So O2 requires an anti-concentration statement of the form:

\[
\text{canonical }q''_{\can}\text{ packet mass is not carried by }R
\text{ or fewer separated frequency cells at }Z_\alpha\text{-zeros.}
\]

---

# 4. Sharper O2 replacement input

The natural strengthened input is:

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{O2\text{-}Spread}}
\]

At every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), there exist \(R+1\) separated canonical frequency cells \(I_{r_0},\ldots,I_{r_R}\) such that

\[
|A_{r_a}^{\can}(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
0\le a\le R,
\]

where \(A_r^{\can}\) is the full canonical cell aggregate for

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

More invariantly:

\[
\inf_{\deg P\le R,\ \|P\|=1}
\sum_r |P(\tau_r)|^2 |A_r^{\can}(m_0,\alpha)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is better than the previous Bezout statement because it expresses exactly what O2 needs: no degree-\(R\) polynomial can annihilate the active cell distribution.

The equivalence is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O2\text{-}Spread}
+
\text{basis conditioning}
+
\text{canonical cell transfer}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{O2}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Proof of the recurrence formulation

Suppose O2 fails. Then, after basis normalization, the moment matrix \(H\) has a small left singular vector. Hence there exists a polynomial

\[
P(\tau)=\sum_{j=0}^{R}\beta_j\tau^j,
\qquad
\|\beta\|_2=1,
\]

such that

\[
\sum_r A_rP(\tau_r)\tau_r^\ell
\approx0,
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

If the \(R+2\) moment functionals are polynomially invertible on the cell grid, this implies

\[
A_rP(\tau_r)
\approx0
\]

on the recoverable cells.

Thus the active cell mass must lie where \(P\) is small. Since \(P\) has degree at most \(R\), that means the cell mass lies near at most \(R\) roots, up to Remez leakage.

So O2 failure implies:

\[
\boxed{
\text{canonical packet mass is concentrated in an }R\text{-root tube.}
}
\]

The contrapositive is the spread input above.

---

# 6. New obstruction: root-tube concentration

This is now the most precise Agent 3 obstruction.

A countermodel is easy:

Take \(R\) separated cells \(\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_R\), arbitrary nonzero complex masses \(A_1,\ldots,A_R\), and set

\[
P(\tau)=\prod_{a=1}^{R}(\tau-\tau_a).
\]

Then

\[
P(\tau_a)=0
\]

for every active cell. Hence

\[
\sum_a A_aP(\tau_a)\tau_a^\ell=0
\]

for all \(\ell\). The moment matrix has rank at most \(R\), and O2 fails.

Therefore any O2 proof must exclude the following at \(Z_\alpha\)-zeros:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The full canonical }q''_{\can}\text{ packet cannot concentrate on }R
\text{ or fewer effective frequency cells.}
}
\]

This is not excluded by O1a, O1b, exterior coefficient mass, or averaged large sieve.

---

# 7. Candidate route: split O2 into two inputs

The cleanest next structure is:

\[
\mathrm{O2}
=
\mathrm{O2a}
+
\mathrm{O2b}.
\]

Where:

## O2a — canonical cell transfer

The \(q''_{\can}\)-jet block is approximated by a stable cell moment matrix:

\[
Q_{j\ell}
=
\sum_r A_r^{\can}\Phi_j(\tau_r)\Psi_\ell(\tau_r)
+
\mathrm{Err}_{j\ell},
\]

with

\[
\|\mathrm{Err}\|_{\op}\le Q^{-A}
\]

and polynomially conditioned bases \(\Phi_j,\Psi_\ell\).

This is local/canonical analysis.

## O2b — zero-conditioned cell-spread

At every admissible \(Z_\alpha\)-zero,

\[
\inf_{\deg P\le R,\ \|P\|=1}
\sum_r |P(\tau_r)|^2 |A_r^{\can}|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is the arithmetic nonrecurrence input.

Then O2 follows.

---

# 8. Net result

This continuation improves the picture:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2-BZ is equivalent to excluding }R\text{-root concentration of the}
\ q''_{\can}\text{ canonical packet at }Z_\alpha\text{-zeros.}
}
\]

This is sharper than “generic Bezout fails.” It identifies the concrete bad configuration:

\[
\text{all canonical cofactor mass lives on cells annihilated by one degree-}R
\text{ recurrence polynomial.}
\]

The next useful attack is therefore not a generic resultant. It is to prove or disprove:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
q''_{\can}\text{ has polynomially large mass in }R+1
\text{ separated frequency cells.}
\]

That is the strongest current Agent 3 reduction.

## Agent 3 continuation — cell-spread is still insufficient without phase noncancellation

The previous reduction was:

\[
\mathrm{O2}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\text{no degree-}R\text{ recurrence polynomial annihilates the canonical }q''_{\can}\text{ cell moments at }Z_\alpha\text{-zeros.}
\]

I pushed that further. The sharper conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2 needs not just frequency spread, but complex phase-nondegeneracy of the spread.}
}
\]

Mass in \(R+1\) separated cells is enough only under positivity/sectoriality or after isolating a dominant uncancelled minor. For the actual canonical object, the cell weights are complex, so Plücker/minor cancellation remains possible.

---

# 1. Bilinear-form formulation of O2

After cell reduction, the O2 matrix has the model form

\[
H_{j\ell}
=
\sum_r A_r\,\Phi_j(\tau_r)\Psi_\ell(\tau_r),
\qquad
0\le j\le R,\quad 0\le \ell\le R+1,
\]

where

\[
A_r=A_r^{\can}(m_0,\alpha)
\]

is the full canonical cell aggregate for

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

Equivalently, define a complex bilinear form

\[
\mathcal B_\mu(P,Q)
=
\sum_r A_r P(\tau_r)Q(\tau_r),
\]

with

\[
\deg P\le R,
\qquad
\deg Q\le R+1.
\]

Then the O2 lower bound is equivalent, up to basis conditioning, to

\[
\inf_{\|P\|=1}
\sup_{\|Q\|=1}
|\mathcal B_\mu(P,Q)|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So O2 says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Multiplication by the complex cell measure }
\mu=\sum_r A_r\delta_{\tau_r}
\text{ maps }\Pi_R\text{ into }\Pi_{R+1}^*
\text{ with polynomial lower bound.}
}
\]

This is cleaner than the earlier “\(R+1\) cells” formulation.

---

# 2. Why \(|A_r|\)-spread alone does not prove O2

Suppose the active cells are numerous and separated, with

\[
\sum_r |A_r|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

That still does not imply O2, because the bilinear form uses \(A_r\), not \(|A_r|\).

A complex measure can have many active separated atoms but still annihilate low-degree polynomial moments.

For example, choose \(N\ge 2R+3\) separated points \(\tau_r\). The linear system

\[
\sum_{r=1}^{N} A_r \tau_r^k=0,
\qquad
0\le k\le 2R+1,
\]

has \(2R+2\) equations and \(N\) unknowns, hence a nonzero solution. For such a nonzero complex vector \(A=(A_r)\),

\[
\mathcal B_\mu(P,Q)=0
\]

for every

\[
\deg P\le R,
\qquad
\deg Q\le R+1,
\]

because \(PQ\) has degree at most \(2R+1\). Therefore

\[
H=0
\]

even though the mass may be spread across \(N\) separated cells.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Separated support + large }|A_r|\text{ mass does not imply O2 for complex weights.}
}
\]

The missing ingredient is phase noncancellation.

---

# 3. The exact positive condition: sectoriality or polynomial testing

There are two viable positive hypotheses.

## 3.1 Sectorial spread

If after a global rotation all active weights lie in a fixed sector,

\[
\Re(e^{-i\theta}A_r)\ge c|A_r|
\]

on a set of \(R+1\) or more well-spread cells, then O2 follows from the positive-measure argument.

Indeed, for any polynomial \(P\),

\[
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1}|\mathcal B_\mu(P,Q)|
\ge
\frac{
|\mathcal B_\mu(P,\overline{P}_{\mathrm{poly}})|
}{
\|\overline{P}_{\mathrm{poly}}\|
},
\]

where \(\overline{P}_{\mathrm{poly}}\) denotes the coefficient-conjugate polynomial in the real variable \(\tau\). Since \(\tau_r\in\mathbb R\),

\[
\overline{P}_{\mathrm{poly}}(\tau_r)=\overline{P(\tau_r)}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal B_\mu(P,\overline P)
=
\sum_r A_r |P(\tau_r)|^2.
\]

After rotation,

\[
\left|
\sum_r A_r |P(\tau_r)|^2
\right|
\ge
c\sum_r |A_r||P(\tau_r)|^2.
\]

A discrete Remez/Christoffel lower bound on the spread set gives

\[
\sum_r |A_r||P(\tau_r)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|P\|^2.
\]

Therefore O2 follows.

So a sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectorial }q''_{\can}\text{ cell spread at }Z_\alpha\text{-zeros.}
}
\]

But actual AFE cell weights are not sectorial in general.

## 3.2 Direct polynomial testing

The invariant condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{\deg P\le R,\ \|P\|=1}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
\left|
\sum_r A_r^{\can}P(\tau_r)Q(\tau_r)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is exactly the smallest singular value of the canonical moment matrix. It avoids artificial positivity assumptions and is logically equivalent to O2 after canonical transfer.

This is the right formulation if we want a sharp input.

---

# 4. What \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) contributes

The zero equation supplies one complex relation among the AFE weights:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

In a cell model, this looks like

\[
\sum_r C_r(m_0,\alpha)=0,
\]

where \(C_r\) are the corresponding \(Z_\alpha\)-cell aggregates.

The \(q''_{\can}\)-cell weights are approximately polynomially modified versions of the same orbit:

\[
A_r^{\can}
\approx
\mathfrak p_2(\tau_r) C_r
+
\text{dual/background/tail corrections}.
\]

The zero equation therefore gives only

\[
\sum_r C_r=0.
\]

It does **not** prevent the modified weights \(A_r^{\can}\) from satisfying many low-degree moment cancellations:

\[
\sum_r A_r^{\can}\tau_r^k\approx0,
\qquad
0\le k\le 2R+1.
\]

In particular, in a flexible cell model one can choose \(C_r\) so that both

\[
\sum_r C_r=0
\]

and

\[
\sum_r \mathfrak p_2(\tau_r)C_r\tau_r^k=0,
\qquad
0\le k\le 2R+1,
\]

provided there are enough cells. This shows the zero equation alone is far too weak.

So:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\text{ does not imply O2 by algebraic dimension alone.}
}
\]

The proof needs actual arithmetic constraints on the realized prime-log phases.

---

# 5. Refined countermodel respecting broad support and the zero equation

Take \(N\ge 2R+4\) separated cells \(\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_N\).

Let \(C_r\) be unknown complex cell weights for \(Z_\alpha\). Impose:

\[
\sum_{r=1}^N C_r=0
\]

and

\[
\sum_{r=1}^N \mathfrak p_2(\tau_r)C_r\tau_r^k=0,
\qquad
0\le k\le 2R+1.
\]

This is \(2R+3\) linear equations in \(N\) unknowns. For \(N\ge 2R+4\), there is a nonzero solution.

Define

\[
A_r:=\mathfrak p_2(\tau_r)C_r.
\]

Then:

1. the model zero condition holds;
2. the support can be broad and separated;
3. the \(q''\)-moment matrix vanishes through the O2 range.

Thus O2 fails in a broad-support phase-relaxed model.

This does not disprove O2 for zeta, because the actual \(C_r\) are not arbitrary. But it proves that O2 cannot be derived from:

- broad packet support;
- separated cells;
- total cell mass;
- the single zero equation;
- polynomial derivative weighting.

It needs a genuine actual-orbit theorem.

---

# 6. The correct strengthened O2 input

The previous “O2-Spread” should be replaced by a phase-sensitive condition.

Call it:

\[
\mathrm{O2\text{-}MomentND}
\]

At every admissible \(Z_\alpha\)-zero,

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{\deg P\le R,\ \|P\|=1}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
\left|
\sum_r A_r^{\can}(m_0,\alpha)P(\tau_r)Q(\tau_r)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is nearly equivalent to O2, but it is expressed in cell-moment language. It says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the canonical }q''_{\can}\text{ cell measure is not low-degree moment-null}
\text{ at }Z_\alpha\text{-zeros.}
}
\]

A weaker but sufficient version is:

\[
\exists \theta,\ S\subset\{r\}
\]

such that:

\[
|S|\ge R+1,
\]

the \(\tau_r\) in \(S\) are \(R^{-O(1)}\)-separated, and

\[
\Re(e^{-i\theta}A_r^{\can})\ge Q^{-A}|A_r^{\can}|
\qquad(r\in S),
\]

with

\[
\sum_{r\in S}|A_r^{\can}|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

But this sectorial version is probably too strong for zeta.

---

# 7. Consequence for the overall proof

O2 is now isolated as a **complex moment nondegeneracy** theorem.

The hierarchy is:

\[
\text{exterior coefficient mass}
\quad\not\Rightarrow\quad
\text{O2},
\]

\[
\text{same-branch cell mass}
\quad\not\Rightarrow\quad
\text{O2},
\]

\[
\text{broad separated support}
\quad\not\Rightarrow\quad
\text{O2},
\]

\[
\text{broad support + }Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\not\Rightarrow\quad
\text{O2}.
\]

The needed input is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{at }Z_\alpha\text{-zeros, the full canonical }q''_{\can}
\text{ cell measure has polynomially nonzero low-degree bilinear moments.}
}
\]

That is the current sharp Agent 3 endpoint.

---

# 8. Next attack direction

The only remaining plausible route for Agent 3 is to exploit the fact that the actual weights are not arbitrary cell weights. They are sums over

\[
e^{-im_0\log n}
\]

with arithmetic coefficients.

So the next attack should test whether known or conjectural Dirichlet-polynomial estimates can rule out

\[
\sum_r A_r^{\can}P(\tau_r)Q(\tau_r)\approx0
\]

for all low-degree \(P,Q\), at zeros of \(Z_\alpha\).

Equivalently, can the actual orbit avoid being a complex \(2R+1\)-moment design?

At present, that is an independent arithmetic nonrecurrence theorem, not a consequence of the local architecture.

## Agent 3 — O2 as canonical complex moment nondegeneracy

I followed the Agent 3 instruction: analyze sectoriality/positivity of \(A_r^{\can}\), prove or break the phase-relaxed model, compare O2 with Agent 4’s O3 dual-polynomial obstruction, and check whether a family-valued Gate–1 can bypass O2. The coordinator’s current framing is that O2 is a phase-sensitive complex moment condition, not support-spread or diagonal mass. 

## 1. No sectorial/positivity structure is visible in \(A_r^{\can}\)

The O2 cell model is

\[
\mathcal B_\mu(P,Q)
=
\sum_r A_r^{\can}(m_0,\alpha)P(\tau_r)Q(\tau_r),
\]

with

\[
\deg P\le R,\qquad \deg Q\le R+1,
\]

and target

\[
\inf_{\deg P\le R,\ \|P\|=1}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
|\mathcal B_\mu(P,Q)|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

A positive/sectorial route would need, after some global rotation \(e^{-i\theta}\),

\[
\Re(e^{-i\theta}A_r^{\can})\ge c|A_r^{\can}|
\]

on enough separated active cells. Then one could test with

\[
Q=\overline P
\]

on the real frequency line and get

\[
\left|
\sum_r A_r^{\can}|P(\tau_r)|^2
\right|
\gtrsim
\sum_r |A_r^{\can}||P(\tau_r)|^2,
\]

after rotation. With a Christoffel/Remez lower bound, this would prove O2.

But I see no such structure for the actual canonical object.

The canonical cell weight has the schematic form

\[
A_r^{\can}
=
A_r^{B_2}
+
A_r^{P_2}
+
A_r^{D_2}
+
A_r^{E_2}.
\]

The oscillatory pieces are built from prime-log phases

\[
e^{-im_0\log n}
\]

and folded-dual phases, modified by canonical derivative weights. These phases rotate rapidly across cells. The zero equation

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

imposes only one complex relation on the underlying AFE cell aggregates. It does not align the canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-cell weights into a common half-plane.

Splitting into plus/dual/background pieces also does not produce positivity:

\[
A_r^{P_2,+}
\quad\text{and}\quad
A_r^{D_2,-}
\]

have different branch phases and different canonical multipliers. The background/gamma term \(B_2\) is deterministic and smooth, but it is not a high-rank positive packet measure. It cannot supply an \(R+1\)-rank lower bound by itself. The regularized tail \(E_2\) is an error/transfer term, not a positivity source.

So the sectorial route is not currently viable.

\[
\boxed{
\text{No usable positivity or sectoriality is present in }A_r^{\can}.
}
\]

---

## 2. Sharp phase-relaxed no-go

The previous broad-support no-go can be strengthened.

Take separated cells

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_N\in[c_-,c_+],
\]

with

\[
N\ge 2R+4.
\]

Let \(C_r\) denote the phase-relaxed \(Z_\alpha\)-cell weights. Suppose the canonical multiplier from the \(Z_\alpha\)-cell weight to the \(q''_{\can}\)-cell weight is

\[
m_r=m_{\can}(\tau_r),
\]

nonzero on the cells. In the simplest one-branch model,

\[
A_r^{\can}=m_r C_r.
\]

Impose the zero equation

\[
\sum_{r=1}^N C_r=0
\]

and the O2 moment-null equations

\[
\sum_{r=1}^N m_r C_r \tau_r^k=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le 2R+1.
\]

This is \(2R+3\) homogeneous linear equations in \(N\) complex unknowns. Since \(N\ge 2R+4\), there is a nonzero solution. For a generic vector in the nullspace, all components \(C_r\) are nonzero, so the support can be broad and separated.

For that solution,

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

holds in the phase-relaxed model, while

\[
\mathcal B_\mu(P,Q)
=
\sum_r m_rC_rP(\tau_r)Q(\tau_r)
=
0
\]

for every

\[
\deg P\le R,\qquad \deg Q\le R+1,
\]

because \(P Q\) has degree at most \(2R+1\).

Thus the entire O2 matrix vanishes.

This survives the canonical multiplier. As long as \(m_r\neq0\), multiplication by \(m_r\) merely changes the coefficient matrix of the linear constraints.

The same construction extends to plus/dual/background/tail phase-relaxed models. One simply writes the canonical cell weight as an affine expression in the free branch weights,

\[
A_r^{\can}
=
m_r^+C_r^+
+
m_r^-C_r^-
+
b_r,
\]

and imposes

\[
\sum_r(C_r^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}C_r^-)+b_Z=0,
\]

plus

\[
\sum_r A_r^{\can}\tau_r^k=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le 2R+1.
\]

With enough cells, this is again an underdetermined linear system. The affine background/tail target does not remove the no-go; it only shifts the right-hand side.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Broad support + zero equation + canonical multipliers do not imply O2.}
}
\]

O2 needs actual arithmetic restrictions on the realized prime-log phases, not a phase-relaxed cell model.

---

## 3. Relation between O2 and O3

O2 and O3 can be expressed as two faces of the same complex frame problem, but neither implies the other.

Let \(\mu_{m_0}\) be the base \(Z_\alpha\)-cell measure,

\[
d\mu_{m_0}
=
\sum_r C_r(m_0,\alpha)\delta_{\tau_r}.
\]

Let

\[
m(\tau)
\]

be the canonical curvature multiplier connecting the \(Z_\alpha\)-cell data to the \(q''_{\can}\)-cell data, so that

\[
d\nu
=
m\,d\mu_{m_0}
\]

models the canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-measure.

Then O2 is the lower frame bound for multiplication by \(m\,d\mu\):

\[
\inf_{\deg P\le R,\ \|P\|=1}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int P Q\,m\,d\mu
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Agent 4’s O3 dual-polynomial form is different. It asks whether the \(Z_\alpha\)-functional

\[
Q\mapsto \int Q\,d\mu
\]

is separated from the span generated by the \(q''_{\can}\)-rows,

\[
Q\mapsto \int mP Q\,d\mu,
\qquad \deg P\le R.
\]

Equivalently, O3 is

\[
\inf_{\deg P\le R}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int (1-mP)Q\,d\mu
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So O2 controls injectivity/stability of

\[
P\mapsto \left[Q\mapsto \int mP Q\,d\mu\right],
\]

whereas O3 controls the distance of

\[
Q\mapsto\int Q\,d\mu
\]

from the range of that map.

Thus O3 is a Schur-complement / quotient-distance condition relative to the same complex moment frame.

This gives a clean unified theorem.

---

## 4. Unified O2/O3 complex-frame theorem

Define the augmented frame map

\[
\mathcal T(a,P)(Q)
=
\int (a+mP)Q\,d\mu,
\]

where

\[
a\in\mathbb C,
\qquad \deg P\le R,
\qquad \deg Q\le R+1.
\]

A single zero-conditioned complex frame theorem would be:

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{|a|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int (a+mP)Q\,d\mu_{m_0}
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\).

This theorem implies O2 by setting \(a=0\):

\[
\inf_{\|P\|=1}
\sup_{\|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int mP Q\,d\mu
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

It also implies O3, because O3 is exactly the distance of the \(a=1\) functional from the subspace \(m\Pi_R\):

\[
\inf_{\deg P\le R}
\sup_{\|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int (1-mP)Q\,d\mu
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So the right unification is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2 and O3 are both consequences of an augmented complex moment frame inequality.}
}
\]

But this is a new arithmetic input. It is not a consequence of O2 alone, O3 alone, broad support, or the zero equation.

---

## 5. Phase-relaxed no-go for the unified theorem

The same linear-algebra countermodel can kill the augmented frame.

Choose cells \(\tau_r\) and weights \(C_r\) such that

\[
\sum_r C_r \tau_r^k=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
\sum_r m_r C_r \tau_r^k=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le 2R+1.
\]

Then

\[
\int Q\,d\mu=0
\]

for every \(\deg Q\le R+1\), and

\[
\int mP Q\,d\mu=0
\]

for every \(\deg P\le R,\deg Q\le R+1\).

Therefore

\[
\mathcal T(a,P)=0
\]

for every \((a,P)\). The augmented frame lower bound fails maximally, even with broad separated support.

This construction requires

\[
N\ge (R+2)+(2R+2)+1=3R+5
\]

cells to leave a nonzero nullspace, which is still far below the dimension \(Q/\log Q\) suggested by Agent 1’s dimension threshold.

So again:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The missing theorem must use actual prime-log arithmetic, not cell geometry.}
}
\]

---

## 6. Can family-valued Gate–1 bypass O2?

Only partially, and only if the downstream architecture is redesigned.

In the current scalar architecture, O2 is needed because the downstream Wronskian/Gate–2 route uses a single canonical source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

The cofactor vector \(\mathbf M_{\can}\) certifies that the \(q''_{\can}\)-jet rows span enough directions. If the proof still feeds downstream through this scalar \(u\), then O2 or an equivalent moment nondegeneracy condition is unavoidable.

A family-valued Gate–1 could bypass O2 only if the downstream machinery accepts a family of source seeds

\[
u_\nu
\]

and proves row energy whenever at least one or some aggregate of the \(u_\nu\)-frames is nondegenerate.

For example, suppose one introduces a high-dimensional family of multipliers

\[
m_\nu(\tau),
\qquad
|\nu|\gtrsim Q/\log Q,
\]

and defines

\[
d\nu_\nu=m_\nu\,d\mu.
\]

Then the relevant replacement for O2 would be an aggregate frame inequality:

\[
\inf_{\|P\|=1}
\sum_\nu
\sup_{\|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int m_\nu P Q\,d\mu
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is statistically more plausible because the observable dimension is large enough. But it is not the old O2; it is a new high-dimensional source theorem.

Moreover, D4/Gate–2 compatibility is nontrivial. The carrier-quotient D4 machinery appears tied to the canonical scalar curvature source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

If \(u_\nu\) is an arbitrary windowed or projected packet source, one must reprove:

\[
\text{Gate 1 seed }u_\nu
\Longrightarrow
\text{Gate 2}
\Longrightarrow
\text{D4 carrier row energy}
\]

uniformly in \(\nu\). Without that, a family-valued Gate–1 does not feed the existing downstream engine.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A family-valued Gate–1 can bypass scalar O2 only if downstream becomes source-family equivariant.}
}
\]

Otherwise O2 reappears as the scalar \(q''_{\can}\) complex moment nondegeneracy condition.

---

## Final Agent 3 status

O2 is now reduced to the following exact missing theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-conditioned canonical complex moment frame theorem.}
}
\]

At every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), the full canonical cell measure

\[
d\nu_{\can}
=
\sum_r A_r^{\can}(m_0,\alpha)\delta_{\tau_r}
\]

must satisfy

\[
\inf_{\deg P\le R,\ \|P\|=1}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int P Q\,d\nu_{\can}
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

A stronger unified theorem would use the augmented base measure form

\[
\inf_{|a|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\sup_{\deg Q\le R+1,\ \|Q\|=1}
\left|
\int (a+mP)Q\,d\mu_{m_0}
\right|
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

which would imply both O2 and O3.

Current status:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No positive O2 route from canonical structure alone.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Phase-relaxed broad-support models kill O2 and the unified O2/O3 frame.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2 remains an actual prime-log arithmetic nonrecurrence input unless Gate–1 is redesigned as a high-dimensional source-family theorem.}
}
\]

## Agent 3 — unified O2/O3 complex-frame theorem, primal side

The Agent 3 instruction is to work on the unified primal complex-frame theorem, not old local O2 alone. The target is the lower bound

\[
\inf_{(c,P)\ne0}
\left\|
\left(
L\bigl((c+mP)x^k\bigr)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\bigl(|c|+\|P\|\bigr),
\]

where \(L\) is the zero-conditioned prime-log moment functional at an admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), and \(m\) is the canonical curvature multiplier for \(q''_{\can}\). The prompt says this should imply O2 from \(c=0\), and O3 as the constant-direction Schur complement. 

## 1. Exact primal matrix formulation

Let

\[
\mathcal H:=\mathbb C^{R+2},
\]

with coordinates indexed by \(0\le k\le R+1\). Define

\[
b
:=
\left(L(x^k)\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\in\mathcal H,
\]

and define the Krylov/Hankel map

\[
K:\mathbb C_R[x]\to \mathcal H,
\qquad
K(P)
=
\left(L(mP x^k)\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Then the unified augmented map is

\[
T:\mathbb C\oplus\mathbb C_R[x]\to\mathcal H,
\qquad
T(c,P)=cb+K(P).
\]

In matrix form,

\[
T=[\,b\ \ K\,].
\]

The unified theorem is

\[
\sigma_{\min}(T)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

O2 is the lower singular-value statement for \(K\):

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

O3 is the distance of \(b\) from the range of \(K\):

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge Q^{-A}\|b\|,
\]

up to the existing normalization converting \(b\) into the \(Z_\alpha\)-derivative row and \(K\) into the \(q''_{\can}\)-jet span.

So the linear algebra is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}([b\ K])\text{ is equivalent, up to polynomial conditioning, to }
\sigma_{\min}(K)\text{ plus }
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K).
}
\]

More explicitly, suppose

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge \kappa,
\qquad
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge \eta,
\qquad
\|b\|,\|K\|_{\op}\le M,
\]

with \(\kappa,\eta\ge Q^{-A}\) and \(M\le Q^C\). Write

\[
b=b_\parallel+b_\perp,
\qquad
b_\parallel\in \operatorname{Ran}K,
\qquad
b_\perp\perp \operatorname{Ran}K.
\]

Then

\[
\|cb+K(P)\|^2
=
|c|^2\|b_\perp\|^2
+
\|c b_\parallel+K(P)\|^2.
\]

The worst case cancels \(c b_\parallel\) using \(K(P)\). Since \(K^{-1}\) on its range has norm at most \(\kappa^{-1}\), cancellation costs

\[
\|P\|\lesssim |c|\frac{\|b_\parallel\|}{\kappa}.
\]

Thus the possible cancellation only degrades the lower bound by a polynomial factor if \(\kappa^{-1}\) and \(\|b\|\) are polynomially bounded. One obtains

\[
\|cb+K(P)\|
\ge
Q^{-A'}\bigl(|c|+\|P\|\bigr).
\]

Conversely, if

\[
\sigma_{\min}([b\ K])\ge\delta,
\]

then setting \(c=0\) gives

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge\delta,
\]

and minimizing over \(P\) with \(c=1\) gives

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge \delta.
\]

So the unified augmented frame theorem is the clean primal formulation of O2+O3.

---

## 2. Canonical decomposition does not provide positivity

The canonical object is

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

In the cell model,

\[
L(mP x^k)
=
\sum_r A_r^{\can}P(\tau_r)\tau_r^k,
\]

where

\[
A_r^{\can}
=
A_r^{B_2}
+
A_r^{P_2}
+
A_r^{D_2}
+
A_r^{E_2}.
\]

A positivity route would need a rotation \(\theta\) such that the canonical weights satisfy something like

\[
\Re(e^{-i\theta}A_r^{\can})\ge c|A_r^{\can}|
\]

on enough separated cells. Then one could test with \(Q=\overline P\) and convert the complex bilinear form into a positive quadratic form.

No such structure appears.

The \(P_2\) and \(D_2\) terms carry prime-log and folded-dual oscillatory phases. Their relative phase depends on \(m_0\), \(\alpha\), the functional-equation phase, and cell location. The gamma/background contribution \(B_2\) is smooth and deterministic, but it is not a high-rank positive packet measure. The tail \(E_2\) is a regularized correction, not a positivity source. The zero equation

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

imposes a single scalar cancellation condition; it does not align the canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-weights into any sector.

Therefore the canonical decomposition does not give a Hermitian, positive, or sectorial frame inequality. The unified theorem remains a complex arithmetic nonrecurrence statement.

\[
\boxed{
\text{No positive route to the unified frame comes from }B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\text{ alone.}
}
\]

---

## 3. Sharp phase-relaxed countermodel

The unified theorem can fail maximally in a broad-support phase-relaxed model.

Choose separated cells

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_N\in[c_-,c_+].
\]

Let \(C_r\) be phase-relaxed cell weights for the base \(Z_\alpha\)-measure, and let

\[
m_r:=m(\tau_r)
\]

be the canonical curvature multiplier. Define

\[
L(f)=\sum_{r=1}^N C_r f(\tau_r).
\]

The scalar zero condition is

\[
L(1)=\sum_{r=1}^N C_r=0.
\]

The unified frame fails if

\[
L(x^k)=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
L(m x^j)=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le 2R+1.
\]

Indeed, if these hold, then for every \(c\) and every \(\deg P\le R\),

\[
L\bigl((c+mP)x^k\bigr)
=
cL(x^k)+L(mP x^k)
=
0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1.
\]

Thus

\[
T(c,P)=0
\]

for all \((c,P)\), so the augmented frame has zero lower singular value.

Counting constraints:

- \(L(x^k)=0\), \(0\le k\le R+1\): \(R+2\) complex linear equations.
- \(L(mx^j)=0\), \(0\le j\le 2R+1\): \(2R+2\) complex linear equations.

Total:

\[
3R+4
\]

complex homogeneous linear equations. Hence if

\[
N\ge 3R+5,
\]

there exists a nonzero solution \(C=(C_r)\). For generic separated \(\tau_r\) and nondegenerate \(m_r\), one can take such a solution with broad support rather than concentrated support.

This countermodel already includes:

\[
L(1)=0,
\]

so it respects the scalar zero condition. It also includes the canonical multiplier \(m\). If background/tail terms add affine shifts, one replaces the homogeneous equations by an affine system. With

\[
N>3R+4,
\]

the solution space still has positive dimension unless the affine target is inconsistent, and in a phase-relaxed model there is no reason for inconsistency.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{broad separated support + scalar zero condition + canonical multiplier }m
\text{ do not imply the unified frame theorem.}
}
\]

This is stronger than the earlier O2-only countermodel.

---

## 4. Cell count comparison

The countermodel needs only

\[
N\ge 3R+5
\]

cells.

But the dimension-sufficient pointwise scale identified by the coordinator/Agent 1 is roughly

\[
N_{\mathrm{needed}}\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

we have

\[
3R+5
\ll
\frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So the phase-relaxed failure occurs at a dimension far below the statistically plausible high-dimensional observable scale. This confirms that the old \(O(R)\)-row architecture is structurally too small to force pointwise nonrecurrence without a counter-random theorem.

---

## 5. Family-valued Gate–1 variant

Suppose the upstream seed space is enlarged from constants plus \(m\mathbb C_R[x]\) to

\[
\Phi+\;m\mathbb C_R[x],
\]

where

\[
\Phi=\operatorname{span}\{\phi_\nu\}_{\nu\in\mathcal N_\Phi}
\]

is a high-dimensional family-valued Gate–1 observable space.

Then the natural augmented frame theorem becomes:

\[
\inf_{\phi\in\Phi,\ P\in\mathbb C_R[x]\atop \|\phi\|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\left\|
\left(
L\bigl((\phi+mP)x^k\bigr)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Equivalently, for a basis \(\phi_\nu\), define

\[
B_\Phi
=
\left[
\left(L(\phi_\nu x^k)\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right]_{\nu},
\]

and require

\[
\sigma_{\min}\bigl([B_\Phi\ K]\bigr)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is the correct high-dimensional replacement.

However, it is still an arithmetic theorem unless \(\Phi\) is so large and so directly adapted to the refined cell basis that it frames the actual cell vector deterministically. If \(\Phi\) is merely another \(O(R)\)-dimensional polynomial family, the same phase-relaxed nullspace survives. If

\[
\dim\Phi\gtrsim Q/\log Q
\]

and \(\Phi\) behaves like a stable frame on refined cells, then the dimension count becomes plausible.

But this does not automatically feed the existing downstream engine. The D4/Gate–2 route is built for the canonical scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

A family \(\Phi\) bypasses scalar O2 only if its lower frame bound certifies the same canonical source or if the downstream machinery is rebuilt to accept the family as source seeds. Otherwise, scalar O2 reappears as the condition that \(m\mathbb C_R[x]\) has a lower frame bound.

So the family-valued version is not a proof; it is a different upstream theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-conditioned high-dimensional complex frame bound for }
\Phi+m\mathbb C_R[x].
}
\]

---

## 6. Final primal-side reduction

The final Agent 3 result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Unified O2/O3 is exactly a lower singular-value bound for }T=[b\ K].
}
\]

Where:

\[
b_k=L(x^k),
\qquad
K(P)_k=L(mP x^k).
\]

This is equivalent, up to polynomial conditioning, to:

\[
\text{O2: }\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge Q^{-A},
\]

plus

\[
\text{O3: }\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

There is no positivity or Hermitian structure in the canonical decomposition that proves this. Phase-relaxed broad-support models satisfying the zero condition can kill the entire augmented frame with only \(3R+5\) cells.

Thus the exact missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-conditioned actual prime-log augmented complex-frame theorem.}
}
\]

At every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\),

\[
\inf_{|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\left\|
\left(
L_{m_0,\alpha}\bigl((c+mP)x^k\bigr)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

A family-valued repair would replace \(c\) by \(\phi\in\Phi\):

\[
\inf_{\|\phi\|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\left\|
\left(
L_{m_0,\alpha}\bigl((\phi+mP)x^k\bigr)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

That may be dimensionally plausible if \(\dim\Phi\gtrsim Q/\log Q\), but it is a new upstream theorem and must still certify the canonical scalar source \(q''_{\can}\) to feed the repaired downstream D4 route.

## Agent 3 — primal augmented complex-frame attack

The assigned Agent 3 target is the primal unified O2/O3 theorem:

\[
\inf_{(c,P)\ne0}
\left\|
\left(
L\bigl((c+mP)x^k\bigr)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}(|c|+\|P\|),
\]

where \(L\) is the actual zero-conditioned prime-log moment functional at an admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), and \(m\) is the canonical curvature multiplier for \(q''_{\can}\). The prompt asks specifically whether actual zeta structure, the functional equation, plus/folded-dual pairing, or \(B_2\) can defeat the phase-relaxed countermodel. 

## 1. Matrix form and what must be proved

Define

\[
b_k:=L(x^k),\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
K(P)_k:=L(mP x^k),\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,\quad \deg P\le R.
\]

Then the augmented primal matrix is

\[
T=[b\ K],
\]

and the target is

\[
\sigma_{\min}(T)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The \(c=0\) slice gives O2:

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The Schur-complement piece gives O3:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So any positive route must show that the actual zeta-conditioned functional \(L\) does not admit a nontrivial pair \((c,P)\) such that

\[
L\bigl((c+mP)x^k\bigr)\approx0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1.
\]

Equivalently, no low-degree multiplier \(c+mP\) can make the first \(R+2\) moments vanish at a \(Z_\alpha\)-zero.

---

## 2. Actual \(L\) at zeta zeros: no visible positivity or sectoriality

The strongest possible rescue would be a hidden positivity statement. For instance, if after rotation the cell weights of \(L\) satisfied

\[
\Re(e^{-i\theta}C_r)\ge c|C_r|
\]

on enough separated cells, then one could test with conjugate polynomials and get a positive Christoffel/Remez lower bound.

But the actual completed AFE structure does not give this.

At a critical-line zero, the completed value is real after phase normalization, but the cell-level measure is still signed/oscillatory. In a simplified real folded model, the functional looks like

\[
L(f)
=
\sum_r w_r f(\tau_r),
\qquad
w_r\in\mathbb R,
\qquad
\sum_r w_r=0.
\]

That is still not positive. A signed real measure with \(N\ge R+3\) atoms can annihilate the first \(R+2\) moments:

\[
\sum_r w_r\tau_r^k=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

while having broad separated support and nonzero total variation. Thus even the strongest real-valued/folded symmetry does not create a positive frame.

The actual complex AFE is worse, not better: the cell weights contain phases

\[
e^{-im_0\log n}
\]

and folded-dual phases coupled through the functional equation. The scalar zero equation imposes

\[
L(1)=0,
\]

but it does not enforce a half-plane condition, sign condition, or sectoriality on the cell weights.

So there is no positivity route to the augmented frame.

---

## 3. Functional equation and plus/folded-dual pairing do not force rank

The folded AFE gives a paired structure of the schematic form

\[
L(f)
=
\sum_r
\left(
a_r^+ f(\sigma_r^+)
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}a_r^- f(\sigma_r^-)
\right)
+
L_{\rm bg}(f)
+
L_{\rm tail}(f).
\]

One might hope that the two branches force rank because

\[
\sigma_r^+\ne\sigma_r^-.
\]

This helps O1b locally: if one observes both the value and first folded jet in each cell, then branch anti-lock can be broken. But the augmented O2/O3 matrix observes only global moments:

\[
L\bigl((c+mP)x^k\bigr),
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1.
\]

There remains a large phase-relaxed kernel. In the paired model, impose

\[
\sum_r
\left(
a_r^+(\sigma_r^+)^k
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}a_r^-(\sigma_r^-)^k
\right)
=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
\sum_r
\left(
a_r^+m(\sigma_r^+)(\sigma_r^+)^j
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}a_r^-m(\sigma_r^-)(\sigma_r^-)^j
\right)
=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le 2R+1.
\]

These are

\[
(R+2)+(2R+2)=3R+4
\]

complex linear constraints. With \(2M\) branch amplitudes, a nontrivial phase-relaxed solution exists as soon as

\[
2M\ge 3R+5.
\]

Thus the functional equation and two-branch pairing do not themselves force the augmented matrix \([b\ K]\) to have rank. They merely constrain the allowed coefficient pattern. Unless the actual prime-log orbit avoids this kernel, the rank collapse remains possible.

This is the same dimension problem in paired form.

---

## 4. The \(B_2\) background does not supply a protected row

The canonical source is

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The \(B_2\) term is deterministic, smooth, and necessary for canonical correctness. But it is not a high-rank packet measure. It contributes a low-dimensional deterministic affine term to the moment equations.

Write the full canonical moment map as

\[
K(P)_k
=
L(mP x^k)+B(Px^k)+E(Px^k),
\]

where \(B\) denotes the \(B_2\)-background functional and \(E\) denotes dual/tail/correction residue after the chosen decomposition. To create rank collapse for a given nonzero pair \((c,P)\), one needs

\[
L\bigl((c+mP)x^k\bigr)
=
-B(Px^k)-E(Px^k),
\qquad
0\le k\le R+1.
\]

This is an affine system of \(R+2\) equations in the oscillatory cell weights. With enough cells, the phase-relaxed model can solve it. To kill the whole augmented matrix, one imposes the larger affine system for the basis columns; with \(N\gtrsim 3R\) cells there is still enough freedom in the phase-relaxed model, unless the affine target is outside the span of the evaluation map. For separated cells and nondegenerate \(m\), that span is the full finite-dimensional moment space.

Thus \(B_2\) shifts the target but does not create a protected nonzero row or column. It would help only if it dominated the oscillatory packet or lay outside the oscillatory moment range. Neither is available in the current canonical regime.

So:

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{ is required canonically, but it does not prove augmented-frame noncollapse.}
}
\]

---

## 5. Weaker versions: O1b does not rescue O2 or O3

Assume Agent 2 succeeds in making O1b deterministic under coarse cells:

\[
2M\le R+2.
\]

That gives a finite-frame lower bound preventing plus/folded-dual anti-lock in the observed branch rows. It can help ensure that a particular folded value/first-jet observable is not killed cellwise.

But O2 and O3 require more:

\[
\text{O2:}\quad
\sigma_{\min}K\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and

\[
\text{O3:}\quad
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

O1b gives neither a lower singular value for the full canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-Krylov matrix nor transversality of \(b\) against \(\operatorname{Ran}K\). It is compatible with a global signed/complex moment cancellation across cells.

Even if each cellwise two-branch pair is locally visible, the global moment vector may satisfy

\[
L(mP x^k)\approx0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

for some nonzero \(P\), or

\[
b\approx -K(P)
\]

for some \(P\). These are cross-cell cancellations, not local plus/dual anti-lock.

Therefore the strongest weaker statement I can extract is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b removes one local branch-lock failure mode, but it does not imply either O2 or O3.}
}
\]

No weaker augmented-frame theorem follows from O1b unless combined with an additional global complex-frame input.

---

## 6. Final phase-relaxed countermodel including all tested structures

A compact countermodel now covers all attempted primal rescues.

Choose separated branch/cell nodes \(\tau_s\), \(1\le s\le N\), and unknown complex weights \(C_s\). Let \(m_s=m(\tau_s)\). Let \(D_{0,k}\) and \(D_{1,j}\) represent deterministic affine contributions from \(B_2\), background, and regularized tail.

Impose

\[
\sum_s C_s\tau_s^k=-D_{0,k},
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
\sum_s C_sm_s\tau_s^j=-D_{1,j},
\qquad 0\le j\le 2R+1.
\]

This is \(3R+4\) affine equations. With

\[
N\ge 3R+5,
\]

and nondegenerate separated nodes, the phase-relaxed solution space is nonempty generically. The resulting functional satisfies the scalar zero condition, absorbs the deterministic background/tail shifts, and kills the augmented moment map.

Functional-equation pairing only changes the node list and imposes branch structure; with enough paired variables the same linear-algebra nullspace remains.

Thus every tested structural feature fails in the phase-relaxed model:

\[
\text{zero condition},\quad
\text{functional equation},\quad
\text{plus/folded-dual pairing},\quad
B_2,\quad
\text{background/tail affine shifts}.
\]

Therefore any proof must use arithmetic properties of the actual prime-log orbit beyond these formal structures.

---

## 7. Exact upstream theorem required

The required theorem should be stated for the full canonical object, not for a proxy. A precise version is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Actual prime-log augmented complex-frame theorem.}
}
\]

For every admissible ordinary zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), let \(L_{m_0,\alpha}\) be the actual zero-conditioned AFE/cell moment functional, including the plus/folded-dual structure with the correct critical-line normalization. Let \(m_{\can}\) be the canonical curvature multiplier associated with the full source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

with \(B_2\), dual, and tail contributions included either as part of \(m_{\can}L\) or as explicit affine canonical functionals in the moment map.

Then there exist constants \(A,C\) such that, after the chosen polynomially conditioned basis normalization,

\[
\inf_{|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\left\|
\left(
L_{m_0,\alpha}\bigl((c+m_{\can}P)x^k\bigr)
+
\mathcal B_{\can}(P x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where

\[
\deg P\le R,
\]

and \(\mathcal B_{\can}\) denotes any canonical background/dual/tail functional not represented multiplicatively by \(m_{\can}L\).

In the purely multiplicative idealization \(\mathcal B_{\can}=0\), this reduces to

\[
\inf_{|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\left\|
\left(
L_{m_0,\alpha}\bigl((c+m_{\can}P)x^k\bigr)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This theorem simultaneously supplies:

\[
\text{O2 from }c=0,
\]

and

\[
\text{O3 from the Schur complement of the constant direction against }m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

## Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{No positive primal route found from currently available structure.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2/O3 must remain a zero-conditioned augmented complex-frame input.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The input is genuinely arithmetic: phase-relaxed models satisfying the zero equation, functional-equation pairing, and canonical affine shifts can still kill the frame with only }O(R)\text{ cells.}
}
\]
