## Agent 3 — primal augmented complex-frame theorem

The assigned Agent 3 task is to attack the unified O2/O3 theorem from the primal side: prove the linear-algebra equivalence, test actual zeta/canonical structure, build the sharp phase-relaxed countermodel if structure fails, and decide whether weaker inputs suffice. 

## 1. Exact linear-algebra equivalence

Let

\[
b=\bigl(L(x^k)\bigr)_{0\le k\le R+1}\in\mathbb C^{R+2},
\]

and

\[
K:\mathbb C_R[x]\to\mathbb C^{R+2},
\qquad
K(P)=\bigl(L(mP x^k)\bigr)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Define

\[
T:\mathbb C\oplus\mathbb C_R[x]\to\mathbb C^{R+2},
\qquad
T(c,P)=cb+KP.
\]

So

\[
T=[\,b\ K\,].
\]

The unified target is

\[
\|T(c,P)\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\bigl(|c|+\|P\|\bigr).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\sigma_{\min}(T)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

### O2 slice

Taking \(c=0\),

\[
\|KP\|_2\ge Q^{-A}\|P\|.
\]

Thus

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is exactly the primal O2 nondegeneracy: the canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-Krylov/moment block has polynomial lower singular value.

### O3 Schur-complement slice

O3 is the statement that the constant-direction column \(b\) is not nearly contained in the range of \(K\):

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)
=
\inf_{P}\|b+KP\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}\|b\|_2
\]

after the usual normalization connecting \(b\) to the \(Z_\alpha\)-derivative row.

This is obtained from \(T\) by setting \(c=1\):

\[
\|b+KP\|_2
=
\|T(1,P)\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}(1+\|P\|).
\]

In particular,

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

### Converse

Assume

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge \kappa,
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge \rho.
\]

Write

\[
b=b_\parallel+b_\perp,
\qquad
b_\parallel\in\operatorname{Ran}K,
\qquad
b_\perp\perp\operatorname{Ran}K,
\]

so

\[
\|b_\perp\|=\rho.
\]

For any \(c,P\),

\[
\|cb+KP\|^2
=
|c|^2\rho^2
+
\|c b_\parallel+KP\|^2.
\]

The second term may cancel, but cancellation of \(c b_\parallel\) requires a polynomial \(P\) of size at most controlled by \(\kappa^{-1}\|b_\parallel\||c|\). More concretely, if

\[
\|P\|>2\kappa^{-1}\|b_\parallel\||c|,
\]

then

\[
\|c b_\parallel+KP\|
\ge
\kappa\|P\|-\|b_\parallel\||c|
\ge
\frac{\kappa}{2}\|P\|.
\]

If instead

\[
\|P\|\le 2\kappa^{-1}\|b_\parallel\||c|,
\]

then

\[
\|cb+KP\|
\ge
|c|\rho.
\]

Assuming the standard polynomial upper bounds

\[
\|b\|,\|K\|_{\op}\le Q^C,
\qquad
\kappa,\rho\ge Q^{-A},
\]

this gives

\[
\|cb+KP\|
\ge
Q^{-A'}(|c|+\|P\|).
\]

Therefore, up to polynomial conditioning,

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}([b\ K])\ge Q^{-A}
\iff
\text{O2 lower singular value}+\text{O3 Schur-complement separation}.
}
\]

This equivalence is now clean.

---

## 2. Search for actual zeta structure

I tested the possible structural sources.

### Functional equation / plus–folded-dual pairing

The functional equation pairs branch contributions schematically as

\[
L(f)
=
\sum_r
\left[
a_r^+ f(\sigma_r^+)
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}a_r^-f(\sigma_r^-)
\right]
+
L_{\rm bg}(f)
+
L_{\rm tail}(f).
\]

This helps against local folded anti-lock when each cellwise two-branch pair is observed with enough local jets. But the augmented frame observes only global moments:

\[
L((c+mP)x^k),\qquad 0\le k\le R+1.
\]

The paired branch structure does not prevent global moment cancellation. In a phase-relaxed model, choose branch amplitudes \(a_r^\pm\) satisfying

\[
\sum_r
\left[
a_r^+(\sigma_r^+)^k
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}a_r^-(\sigma_r^-)^k
\right]
=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
\sum_r
\left[
a_r^+m(\sigma_r^+)(\sigma_r^+)^j
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}a_r^-m(\sigma_r^-)(\sigma_r^-)^j
\right]
=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le 2R+1.
\]

These are \(3R+4\) complex constraints. With at least \(3R+5\) free branch variables, a nonzero phase-relaxed solution exists generically. So plus/folded-dual pairing does not force rank.

### Real critical-line normalization

Critical-line normalization can make the completed value real-valued at the scalar level, but it does not make the cell measure positive.

A signed real measure can annihilate low moments:

\[
\sum_r w_r\tau_r^k=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

with broad separated support. Thus even replacing complex weights by signed real weights is insufficient.

What would be needed is positivity or sectoriality:

\[
\Re(e^{-i\theta}C_r)\ge c|C_r|
\]

on enough cells. Critical-line reality gives no such half-plane condition.

### \(B_2\) background

The \(B_2\) term is canonical and important, but it does not give a protected row.

If \(B_2\), \(D_2\), and \(E_2\) are represented as affine corrections to the moment equations, the collapse system becomes

\[
\sum_s C_s\tau_s^k=-D_{0,k},
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
\sum_s C_sm_s\tau_s^j=-D_{1,j},
\qquad 0\le j\le 2R+1.
\]

This is an affine system. With \(N\ge 3R+5\) separated cells, the phase-relaxed model still has enough degrees of freedom to solve it generically. The background shifts the target; it does not remove the nullspace.

So:

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{ completes the canonical source but does not supply frame positivity.}
}
\]

### \(D_2/E_2\) completion

The dual and regularized tail terms likewise add necessary canonical corrections. They do not create a Hermitian or positive structure. In the finite-dimensional moment model, they either modify the multiplier \(m\) or contribute affine shifts. Neither prevents rank collapse in a broad-support phase-relaxed system.

### Non-scalar canonical multiplier

A non-scalar multiplier \(m\) is necessary for O3. If \(m\) were constant on the packet, then \(m\mathbb C_R[x]\) would be too aligned with the constant direction.

But non-scalar variation is not sufficient. A complex measure \(L\) can annihilate both

\[
\{x^k:0\le k\le R+1\}
\]

and

\[
\{m x^j:0\le j\le 2R+1\}
\]

as long as there are enough free cells. Thus multiplier variation is an analytic prerequisite, not the arithmetic lower bound.

---

## 3. Sharp phase-relaxed countermodel

Here is the cleanest collapse model.

Choose separated nodes

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_N\in[c_-,c_+],
\]

with

\[
N\ge 3R+5.
\]

Let \(m_s=m(\tau_s)\), and define

\[
L(f)=\sum_{s=1}^N C_s f(\tau_s).
\]

Impose the homogeneous moment constraints

\[
\sum_s C_s\tau_s^k=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

and

\[
\sum_s C_s m_s\tau_s^j=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le 2R+1.
\]

There are

\[
(R+2)+(2R+2)=3R+4
\]

linear equations in \(N\) unknowns. Since \(N\ge 3R+5\), there is a nonzero solution. For generic separated nodes and nondegenerate \(m_s\), this solution can have broad support.

Then for every \(c\) and every \(\deg P\le R\),

\[
L((c+mP)x^k)
=
cL(x^k)+L(mP x^k)=0,
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1.
\]

So

\[
T(c,P)=0
\]

for all \((c,P)\). The augmented frame collapses completely.

This countermodel satisfies the scalar zero condition because \(L(1)=0\) is included among the first moment constraints.

Affine background/tail corrections are handled by replacing the zero right-hand side with a prescribed vector. The system still has solutions once \(N\) exceeds the constraint rank, provided the evaluation map has full rank, which is generic for separated nodes.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{broad support + scalar zero equation + non-scalar canonical multiplier + affine canonical shifts}
\not\Rightarrow
\text{augmented frame}.
}
\]

This is the sharp phase-relaxed no-go.

---

## 4. Are weaker theorems enough?

### O2 only

O2 alone gives

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

It says the \(q''_{\can}\)-Krylov block is nondegenerate. But it does not imply O3, because \(b\) may still lie close to \(\operatorname{Ran}K\).

So O2 alone is insufficient for ordinary Gate–1 Wronskian transversality.

### O3 only

O3 alone gives

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{Ran}K)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

But if \(K\) is nearly singular, the cofactor vector is small and the Wronskian lower bound can still fail after normalization. So O3 alone is insufficient.

### O1b plus one slice

Even if Agent 2 makes O1b deterministic, that only removes local folded branch anti-lock. It does not control global cross-cell complex moment cancellation in \(K\) or \(T=[b\ K]\).

So O1b cannot replace the augmented frame theorem.

### Smaller subspace

One could weaken the theorem by restricting \(P\) to a smaller subspace

\[
\mathcal P_0\subset \mathbb C_R[x].
\]

Then the frame dimension shrinks, and the phase-relaxed no-go needs fewer constraints. This may prove a weaker lower bound, but it does not recover the full cofactor-vector O2 unless the downstream proof only uses \(\mathcal P_0\). Current O2 uses the full \(R+1\)-dimensional canonical Krylov block.

Thus the only meaningful weaker theorem is not a subspace theorem, but a redesign of the downstream engine to use fewer directions. That would be a different architecture.

---

## 5. Final missing input

The exact missing upstream theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Actual prime-log augmented complex-frame theorem.}
}
\]

For every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), let \(L_{m_0,\alpha}\) be the actual zero-conditioned AFE/cell moment functional, including the plus/folded-dual critical-line normalization. Let \(m_{\can}\) be the canonical curvature multiplier associated with the full source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

Then one needs constants \(A,C\) such that

\[
\inf_{|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\left\|
\left(
L_{m_0,\alpha}((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
+
\mathcal B_{\can}(P x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where

\[
\deg P\le R,
\]

and \(\mathcal B_{\can}\) denotes the canonical background/dual/tail part not represented multiplicatively by \(m_{\can}L\). In the ideal multiplicative model this is simply

\[
\inf_{|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\left\|
\left(
L_{m_0,\alpha}((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This theorem supplies O2 from \(c=0\) and O3 from the Schur complement.

## Final Agent 3 status

\[
\boxed{
\text{No positive primal route found from current canonical structure.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The functional equation, branch pairing, critical-line reality, }B_2,D_2,E_2,
\text{ and non-scalar }m
\text{ do not defeat the phase-relaxed collapse.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2/O3 must remain a zero-conditioned actual prime-log augmented complex-frame input unless the upstream architecture is redesigned.}
}
\]

## Agent 3 — Path B2: source-family downstream redesign

The assigned Agent 3 task is to test the radical Path B: replace scalar canonical Gate–1 with a family-valued upstream observable

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Longrightarrow
\sum_{\nu\in I}|u_\nu(m_0)|^2\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad |I|\asymp Q/\log Q,
\]

where the \(u_\nu\) are projected/frequency-filtered canonical components, and then ask whether the downstream chain can be generalized. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Source-family downstream does not currently survive.}
}
\]

It fails at the scalar geometric steps: **Gate 2 real rigidity, wall escape, and D4**. The repaired downstream engine is not a generic source-energy converter; it is tied to the scalar canonical curvature source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Thus radical Path B either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{reduces back to proving that the family certifies scalar }u=q''_{\can},
}
\]

or requires a genuinely new downstream theory with new phase, Gram, wall, and D4 identities for the family.

---

# 1. Source thickening

Assume a family decomposition

\[
u=\sum_{\nu\in I}u_\nu
\]

or, more generally, observables \(u_\nu=T_\nu X\) extracted from the refined cell vector.

From

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu(m_0)|^2\ge Q^{-A},
\]

one can extract at least one index \(\nu_0\) with

\[
|u_{\nu_0}(m_0)|\ge |I|^{-1/2}Q^{-A/2}.
\]

Since

\[
|I|\asymp Q/\log Q,
\]

this only costs a polynomial factor:

\[
|u_{\nu_0}(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So the first extraction step is not the problem.

The problem is that the extracted object is \(u_{\nu_0}\), not the scalar canonical source \(u=q''_{\can}\). A large component does not imply

\[
|u(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Countermodel:

\[
u_1(m_0)=A,\qquad u_2(m_0)=-A,\qquad u_\nu=0\ \text{otherwise}.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu(m_0)|^2=2|A|^2,
\]

but

\[
u(m_0)=\sum_\nu u_\nu(m_0)=0.
\]

Thus family energy does not imply scalar Gate–1 unless one assumes an additional phase-alignment or lower-compression theorem:

\[
\left|\sum_\nu u_\nu(m_0)\right|
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(\sum_\nu |u_\nu(m_0)|^2\right)^{1/2}.
\]

That is exactly another low-dimensional/counter-random projection theorem in disguise.

---

# 2. Gate 2 real rigidity

Current Gate 2 is scalar. It uses identities involving

\[
u=q''_{\can},
\]

and the canonical Gram-wall combinations

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q+2q^3,
\]

plus higher differentiated/corrected variants such as \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

These are not linear in \(u\). In particular,

\[
\Gamma_G(q)
=
q''-6q+2q^3.
\]

If

\[
q=\sum_\nu q_\nu,
\qquad
q_\nu''=u_\nu,
\]

then

\[
q^3
=
\left(\sum_\nu q_\nu\right)^3
\]

contains all cross terms

\[
q_\nu q_\mu q_\lambda.
\]

There is no decomposition

\[
\Gamma_G(q)
=
\sum_\nu \Gamma_G(q_\nu)
\]

compatible with the scalar wall geometry.

Therefore a lower bound for one component \(u_\nu\), or even for

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu|^2,
\]

does not imply any lower bound for the scalar real-rigidity quantity used by Gate 2.

A source-family Gate 2 would need new identities of the form

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu|^2
\Longrightarrow
\text{some family wall-defect lower bound}
\]

or

\[
u_\nu=q_\nu''
\Longrightarrow
\Gamma_{G,\nu}
=
u_\nu-6q_\nu+2q_\nu^3
\]

with a valid geometric interpretation. But \(q_\nu\) is just a projected primitive; it is not the phase of the canonical kernel. So \(\Gamma_{G,\nu}\) has no current Gram/wall meaning.

Thus Gate 2 is the first serious failure point.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Family energy does not feed scalar Gate 2 unless it certifies scalar }u.
}
\]

---

# 3. Wall escape

Wall escape is even more rigidly scalar. It concerns the canonical local variables

\[
q_0,\quad u=q''_{\can},\quad \Gamma_G,\quad \Gamma_{G,2},
\]

and proves escape from the Gram walls in this scalar coordinate system.

For a family component \(u_\nu\), one would need a corresponding primitive \(q_\nu\) and wall quantities

\[
\Gamma_{G,\nu},\qquad \Gamma_{G,2,\nu},
\]

such that the same wall identities hold.

But the walls are not properties of an arbitrary source. They arise from the scalar local phase/kernel geometry. A projected/frequency-filtered component \(u_\nu\) is not itself the curvature of a valid completed zeta phase. It does not define the same Gram matrix, whitening, or wall variables.

So the implication

\[
|u_\nu|\text{ large}
\Longrightarrow
\text{wall escape}
\]

is not meaningful in the current architecture.

Aggregate family energy also does not help:

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu|^2\text{ large}
\]

is compatible with

\[
u=\sum_\nu u_\nu=0,
\qquad
\Gamma_G=0,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}=0,
\]

by cancellation among components.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Wall escape fails for arbitrary source families.}
}
\]

It survives only for the scalar canonical source or for a new theory in which each \(u_\nu\) comes from its own legitimate phase/Gram geometry.

---

# 4. D4 carrier quotient

D4 is intrinsically scalar/canonical in the current proof.

The clean carrier quotient uses the scalar relations

\[
H\sim -\frac{1}{288}q_0u^3,
\]

and the carrier coordinates

\[
H\Gamma_G,\qquad H\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

The repaired D4 step is Green only for the canonical scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

For a family component \(u_\nu\), there is no existing identity

\[
H_\nu\sim -\frac{1}{288}q_{\nu,0}u_\nu^3
\]

with the same Gram meaning. Nor is there a valid family analogue of the clean triangular carrier matrix

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
B_7\\
B_9
\end{pmatrix}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

One might try summing componentwise cubic outputs:

\[
\sum_\nu u_\nu^3,
\]

but this does not recover

\[
u^3=\left(\sum_\nu u_\nu\right)^3.
\]

The cross terms are essential:

\[
u^3
=
\sum_\nu u_\nu^3
+
3\sum_{\nu\ne\mu}u_\nu^2u_\mu
+
6\sum_{\nu<\mu<\lambda}u_\nu u_\mu u_\lambda.
\]

Thus source-family D4 would require an entirely new multilinear carrier calculus tracking all cross terms. That is not a minor extension; it is a different downstream proof.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is not source-family equivariant.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Row mobility

Row mobility can plausibly sum or move row energy **after** row energy has been produced. It does not itself create row energy.

If a valid source-family D4 produced row energy for each \(\nu\), then one could ask whether row mobility aggregates those outputs. But since Gate 2, wall escape, and D4 do not currently produce family row energy, row mobility has nothing to propagate.

Thus row mobility is not the primary failure; it is downstream of the failure.

---

# 6. Can aggregate family energy force scalar energy?

The only way to preserve the existing downstream chain is to turn family energy back into scalar canonical energy:

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|u|= \left|\sum_\nu u_\nu\right|\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

But this is false for arbitrary decompositions by cancellation.

A weaker possible requirement is not pointwise scalar value but scalar finite-jet energy:

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|J_R(u)\|_2\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

This is also false in phase-relaxed models. High-frequency or filtered components can have large energy while the first \(R\) scalar moments cancel.

So any theorem of this type is again a counter-random projection theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{family energy avoids the kernel of the scalar canonical compression.}
}
\]

This is essentially Path A restated.

---

# 7. What a genuine source-family downstream would require

A real Path B would need to replace all scalar objects by family-compatible ones.

At minimum:

### Source-family Gate 2

A theorem of the form

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\Longrightarrow
\mathcal R_{\rm family}\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

where \(\mathcal R_{\rm family}\) is a new real-rigidity observable with a valid analytic/geometric meaning.

### Family wall escape

A family wall theorem identifying the correct wall variables

\[
\Gamma_{\nu},\quad \Gamma_{\nu,2},
\]

or an aggregate wall variable, and proving escape from its bad set.

### Family D4

A new carrier quotient identity, probably multilinear, replacing

\[
H\Gamma_G,\quad H\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

It would need to account for cross terms among family components, not just diagonal \(\nu\)-terms.

### Family row mobility

A summed row-mobility theorem after family row energy is produced.

None of these identities currently exist in the scalar downstream engine.

Therefore Path B is not a local repair. It is a new proof architecture.

---

# Hard verdict

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{B/C: source-family downstream fails unless it certifies scalar }u=q''_{\can}.
}
\]

More explicitly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path B2 fails at Gate 2 / wall escape / D4.}
}
\]

A high-dimensional family-valued Gate–1 theorem does not feed the repaired downstream engine unless it implies the old scalar canonical Gate–1 lower bound

\[
\|J_R(q''_{\can})(m_0)\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

But proving that implication is precisely the low-dimensional canonical projection theorem from Path A.

So the fork is sharper:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Either prove/assume Path A’s counter-random scalar projection theorem,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{rebuild Gate 2, wall escape, D4, and row mobility for a genuinely new source-family geometry.}
}
\]

The current source-family route does not preserve the repaired downstream engine.

## Agent 3 — downstream on a good subset

The assigned task is to test Path C after good-subset extraction: assume a subset \(G\) of routed centers has pointwise canonical Gate–1, then decide whether the repaired downstream chain can be applied only on \(G\), summed, and converted into aggregate row energy. The current coordinator direction is Path C: aggregate canonical Gate–1 plus good-subset downstream extraction, with D4 green only for the canonical scalar source \(u=q''_{\can}\). 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Good-subset downstream works conditionally.}
}
\]

It does **not** require every routed packet. It requires a polynomial-weight good subset plus two additional aggregate controls:

\[
\boxed{
\text{bounded bad-wall / bad-Gram loss}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{bounded-overlap row mobility.}
}
\]

If those two controls hold, the existing pointwise downstream engine can be restricted to \(G\) and summed.

---

# 1. Setup

Let \(G\subset \mathcal C_{\rm routed}\) be a good subset with weight

\[
W(G):=\sum_{m_0\in G}\rho(m_0)>0.
\]

Assume pointwise canonical Gate–1 holds on \(G\):

\[
E_1(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_1},
\qquad m_0\in G.
\]

Here \(E_1\) is the canonical scalar Gate–1 energy for

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

This is crucial. The downstream engine is not source-family equivariant.

Also assume each \(m_0\in G\) remains in the valid local route: ordinary packet, real/canonical gauge, completed \(q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\), and whatever pointwise O2/O3 or augmented-frame input is needed to make Gate–1 genuine.

---

# 2. Gate 2 is local and survives restriction to \(G\)

The Gate–1 \(\Rightarrow\) Gate–2 step is local. It uses only the packet at \(m_0\), the canonical scalar source, source thickening, and \(L^2\)-input real rigidity.

Thus, with uniform constants, one gets

\[
E_2(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\qquad m_0\in G.
\]

There is no need for Gate–1 outside \(G\).

So the restricted implication is valid:

\[
m_0\in G,\quad E_1(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_1}
\Longrightarrow
E_2(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

The only caveat is that all local hypotheses used by Gate 2 must also hold on \(G\). If \(G\) contains ordinary, transition, and singular packets, this must be branch-routed before applying a single theorem.

---

# 3. Wall escape requires a bad-wall loss bound

The next possible failure is wall/gauge/Gram degeneration.

Define the bad subset

\[
G_{\rm bad}
=
\{m_0\in G:\text{Gram-bad, wall-bad, gauge-bad, or D4-ineligible}\}.
\]

To continue, we need a weighted loss bound:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m_0\in G_{\rm bad}}\rho(m_0)
\le
(1-\delta)W(G)
}
\]

with

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-C_{\rm wall}}.
\]

The cleanest usable version is stronger:

\[
\sum_{m_0\in G_{\rm bad}}\rho(m_0)
\le
\frac12 W(G).
\]

Then

\[
G_*:=G\setminus G_{\rm bad}
\]

has

\[
W(G_*)\ge \frac12 W(G).
\]

On \(G_*\), wall escape gives the relevant nonwall quantity, schematically

\[
\mathcal W_{\rm esc}(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_3}.
\]

Without this loss bound, good-subset downstream can fail: the aggregate Gate–1 subset might lie entirely inside bad-wall or Gram-bad regions, where the D4 carrier conversion is not allowed.

Thus the first real downstream obligation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{bad-wall/bad-Gram weighted loss is polynomially bounded.}
}
\]

---

# 4. D4 sums cleanly on \(G_*\)

On \(G_*\), D4 is available because the source is still the canonical scalar

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

The repaired D4 carrier quotient gives a pointwise row-energy lower bound of the form

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm row}^{\rm loc}(m_0)
\ge
Q^{-A_4},
\qquad m_0\in G_*.
\]

More generally, if the D4 output scales with Gate–2 energy,

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm row}^{\rm loc}(m_0)
\ge
Q^{-C}E_2(m_0),
\]

then Gate 2 gives

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm row}^{\rm loc}(m_0)
\ge
Q^{-A_4}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_{m_0\in G_*}\rho(m_0)
\mathcal E_{\rm row}^{\rm loc}(m_0)
\ge
Q^{-A_4}W(G_*).
\]

Using the wall-loss bound,

\[
\sum_{m_0\in G_*}\rho(m_0)
\mathcal E_{\rm row}^{\rm loc}(m_0)
\ge
Q^{-A_5}W(G).
\]

So D4 itself is not the obstruction for Path C, provided the good subset remains canonical-scalar and D4-eligible.

---

# 5. Row mobility needs bounded overlap

The next issue is converting the sum of local row-energy certificates into a global row-energy lower bound without overcounting the same target rows too many times.

Let each \(m_0\in G_*\) produce row energy on a target region or row packet

\[
\mathcal R(m_0).
\]

We need a bounded-overlap condition:

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{\mathcal R}
\sum_{\substack{m_0\in G_*\\ \mathcal R(m_0)\cap \mathcal R\ne\emptyset}}
\rho(m_0)
\le
Q^{C_{\rm ov}}.
}
\]

Equivalently, if \(N_{\rm ov}\) is the maximum multiplicity with which local certificates can charge the same global row energy, require

\[
N_{\rm ov}\le Q^{C_{\rm ov}}.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{\text{global rows}}\mathcal E_{\rm row}
\ge
Q^{-C_{\rm ov}}
\sum_{m_0\in G_*}
\rho(m_0)\mathcal E_{\rm row}^{\rm loc}(m_0).
\]

Combining with D4:

\[
\sum_{\text{global rows}}\mathcal E_{\rm row}
\ge
Q^{-A_6}W(G).
\]

Thus row mobility can be summed over a good subset if it is a positive-energy mobility theorem with polynomial overlap.

If row mobility is purely pointwise but not overlap-controlled, Path C fails here.

---

# 6. Good-subset downstream theorem

The resulting theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Good-subset downstream propagation theorem.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. \(G\) has polynomial total weight:

\[
W(G)>0
\]

and is not exponentially small relative to the routed population.

2. Pointwise canonical Gate–1 holds on \(G\):

\[
E_1(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_1}.
\]

3. Gate–1 \(\Rightarrow\) Gate–2 is local and uniform:

\[
E_2(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

4. Bad-wall/Gram/gauge loss is bounded:

\[
W(G_{\rm bad})\le (1-\delta)W(G),
\qquad
\delta\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

5. D4 carrier quotient is valid on \(G_*=G\setminus G_{\rm bad}\):

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm row}^{\rm loc}(m_0)\ge Q^{-A_4}.
\]

6. Row mobility has polynomial overlap:

\[
N_{\rm ov}\le Q^{C_{\rm ov}}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\text{global row targets}}\mathcal E_{\rm row}
\ge
Q^{-A}W(G).
}
\]

This is exactly the aggregate row-energy lower bound needed from the good subset.

---

# 7. First possible failure points

The chain does **not** fail merely because \(G\) is a subset. The local downstream steps are compatible with restriction.

The actual possible failure points are:

## Failure 1: bad-wall concentration

If

\[
G\subseteq G_{\rm bad},
\]

then Gate–1 may hold on \(G\), but D4 cannot be applied. This requires a wall-loss theorem.

## Failure 2: row-mobility overcounting

If many \(m_0\in G\) charge the same global row target with unbounded multiplicity, summing local certificates overcounts. This requires bounded overlap or an orthogonality/additivity replacement.

## Failure 3: branch incompatibility

If \(G\) mixes ordinary, transition, and singular packets, the local Gate–2/D4 route must be branch-aware. A single ordinary downstream theorem cannot automatically apply to transition/singular packets.

## Failure 4: O2/O3 not included in Gate–1

If “Gate–1” on \(G\) only means raw projection visibility but not the full Wronskian/augmented-frame input, then Gate 2 may not follow. The good subset must satisfy the completed local Gate–1 package.

---

# 8. Final answer

\[
\boxed{
\text{Good-subset downstream extraction is mathematically viable.}
}
\]

It preserves the repaired scalar downstream engine and does not require the pointwise projection theorem at every routed center.

But Path C needs two new downstream-compatible aggregate controls:

\[
\boxed{
W(G_{\rm bad})\le (1-\delta)W(G),\quad \delta\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
N_{\rm ov}\le Q^C.
}
\]

So the next downstream attacks should target exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{bad-wall / bad-Gram weighted loss}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{bounded-overlap row mobility for extracted good subsets}.
}
\]

If both are proved, then aggregate canonical Gate–1 plus good-subset extraction can feed the existing pointwise Gate–2/D4/row-mobility engine.

_Thought for 33s_

## Agent 3 — AggFrame\(_{23}\): aggregate augmented O2/O3

The assigned task is to formulate and attack the aggregate O2/O3 input for Stack C2. At each routed center \(m\), the local augmented map is

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\qquad
\deg P\le R,
\]

and pointwise O2/O3 is

\[
E_{23}(m):=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Path C asks for an aggregate replacement that allows good-subset extraction. 

## Verdict

The best aggregate O2/O3 input for Stack C2 is **not** a fixed-vector averaged frame and not a raw determinant average. The right formulation is a **normalized determinant / least-singular-value aggregate**:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

or the slightly more algebraic surrogate

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
\frac{|\det \mathcal A_m|^2}
     {(1+\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^2)^{n-1}}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad n=R+2.
}
\]

The normalized determinant version is the better proof target if one wants an expandable arithmetic moment. The least-singular-value version is the cleaner extraction target.

---

# 1. Dimensions and normalization

The domain of \(\mathcal A_m\) has dimension

\[
1+\dim\mathbb C_R[x]=R+2.
\]

The codomain also has dimension

\[
R+2.
\]

Set

\[
n:=R+2.
\]

After choosing a polynomially conditioned basis for \(\mathbb C_R[x]\), write

\[
\mathcal A_m
\in \mathbb C^{n\times n}.
\]

Then

\[
E_{23}(m)
=
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

Pointwise O2/O3 is exactly

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

An aggregate theorem should be stated in a scale-normalized basis. Otherwise determinant and singular-value statements are meaningless due to basis rescaling.

Assume throughout:

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le \mathcal M_Q
\]

with

\[
\mathcal M_Q\le Q^C
\]

or, preferably after normalization,

\[
\mathcal M_Q\le \exp(O(R)).
\]

The latter is better because

\[
\exp(O(R))=Q^{o(1)}.
\]

---

# 2. Candidate A: average least singular value

The most direct aggregate input is

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}:\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is exactly what good-subset extraction wants.

If additionally

\[
0\le \sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\le Q^C
\]

or simply \(\le\mathcal M_Q^2\), then the standard extraction lemma gives a subset

\[
\mathcal G_{23}
=
\{m:\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\ge Q^{-A'}\}
\]

with polynomially large \(\mu\)-weight, after choosing \(A'\) sufficiently larger than \(A\).

So \(\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}\) is sufficient.

The problem is proof: \(\sigma_{\min}\) is non-polynomial and hard to expand arithmetically.

---

# 3. Candidate B: determinant energy

The algebraic candidate is

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is expandable. The determinant is the augmented O2/O3 Wronskian determinant. In a cell model, it is a Plücker-type sum over subset-product frequencies.

The determinant controls the least singular value only through

\[
|\det \mathcal A_m|
=
\prod_{j=1}^n \sigma_j(\mathcal A_m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)\,
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^{n-1}.
\]

Hence

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}
     {\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}}.
\]

Therefore raw determinant energy implies \(\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}\) only if the operator norm loss is controlled.

If

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le Q^C,
\]

then

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}
\le Q^{O(R)}.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is

\[
Q^{O(\log Q/\log\log Q)},
\]

which is **not** a fixed polynomial loss. That is too expensive for the existing \(Q^{-A}\) bookkeeping if \(A\) is meant fixed.

If instead the basis and normalization give

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le \exp(O(1))
\]

or even

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le \exp(O(R^0)),
\]

the loss is harmless. More realistically, if

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le \exp(O(1))
\quad\text{after orthonormal normalization,}
\]

then determinant extraction is clean. If only

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le \exp(O(R)),
\]

then the determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) loss is

\[
\exp(O(R^2))
=
\exp\!\left(O\!\left(\frac{(\log Q)^2}{(\log\log Q)^2}\right)\right),
\]

still super-polynomial. So even \(\exp(O(R))\) is not good enough for fixed-polynomial bookkeeping.

Thus raw determinant energy is dangerous.

---

# 4. Best algebraic surrogate: normalized determinant energy

The scale-stable determinant quantity is

\[
D_{23}(m)
:=
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}
     {(1+\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^2)^{n-1}}.
\]

Then pointwise,

\[
D_{23}(m)\le \sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

Indeed,

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
(1+\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^2)^{n-1}.
\]

Therefore an aggregate lower bound

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggDetNorm}_{23}:\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
D_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

implies

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

So \(\mathrm{AggDetNorm}_{23}\) is sufficient for extraction and avoids the determinant-to-singular-value loss.

The downside is that the denominator makes the expression less directly expandable. But if a uniform operator-norm bound can be proven in the chosen normalized basis, then one may replace \(D_{23}\) by a scaled raw determinant.

---

# 5. Candidate C: fixed-vector lower frame

Another natural aggregate theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{\|v\|=1}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
\|\mathcal A_m v\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}d\mu(m)\,
\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is an attractive lower-frame theorem and may be the most accessible by second-moment methods.

But it is **not sufficient for Stack C2 good-subset extraction**.

Counterexample: let \(n=2\), and let \(\mathcal A_m\) range over rank-one projections whose directions are evenly distributed. Then

\[
\sum_m \mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m
\]

can be well-conditioned, while every individual \(\mathcal A_m\) has

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)=0.
\]

So fixed-vector aggregate lower frame does not produce centers where local O2/O3 holds pointwise.

It would be useful only if downstream were redesigned to use averaged O2/O3 directly. Stack C2 wants extracted centers with pointwise O2/O3, so fixed-vector lower frame is insufficient.

---

# 6. Expansion of determinant energy

In the ideal multiplicative model,

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Choose a basis

\[
e_0=(1,0),\qquad e_j=(0,P_j),\quad 1\le j\le R+1,
\]

for \(\mathbb C\oplus\mathbb C_R[x]\). Then

\[
(\mathcal A_m)_{kj}
=
L_m(\phi_j x^k),
\]

where

\[
\phi_0=1,\qquad \phi_j=m_{\can}P_j.
\]

Writing the cell functional as

\[
L_m(f)=\sum_r C_r(m)f(\tau_r),
\]

we have

\[
(\mathcal A_m)_{kj}
=
\sum_r C_r(m)\phi_j(\tau_r)\tau_r^k.
\]

By Cauchy--Binet,

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{S:\ |S|=n}
\left(\prod_{r\in S}C_r(m)\right)
\det\bigl(\tau_r^k\bigr)_{k,r\in S}
\det\bigl(\phi_j(\tau_r)\bigr)_{r,j\in S}.
\]

Thus

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\]

is a double sum over \(S,T\) of size \(n\), with phases

\[
\exp\left(-im\left(\sum_{r\in S}\omega_r-\sum_{t\in T}\omega_t\right)\right).
\]

This is the same subset-product frequency structure encountered before, but now averaged over \(m\in\mathcal C\). Therefore the aggregate determinant theorem reduces to a zero-restricted lower sampling problem for subset-product frequencies:

\[
\Omega_S-\Omega_T
=
\log\frac{\prod_{r\in S}n_r}{\prod_{t\in T}n_t}.
\]

The issue is near-collisions. Distinct subset-product frequencies may be separated only by

\[
\exp(-CQR),
\]

which is much smaller than any fixed polynomial scale. Hence a naive diagonal dominance proof of determinant energy is not available.

However, unlike the pointwise theorem, this aggregate determinant problem is at least a true second-moment/lower-sampling problem. It is not dimensionally impossible; it is an arithmetic lower-frame theorem.

---

# 7. Random matrix heuristic

For a normalized random \(n\times n\) complex matrix with entries of size \(n^{-1/2}\), one expects

\[
\sigma_{\min}^2\sim n^{-2}
\]

at typical points, up to distributional tails. Since

\[
n=R+2\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is

\[
n^{-2}=Q^{-o(1)}.
\]

Thus the aggregate lower bound

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

is heuristically plausible. It is vastly more plausible than requiring pointwise lower bounds at every routed center.

For determinants, a normalized random matrix also has non-negligible log determinant on average, but determinant tails are heavy. This makes determinant energy plausible but more delicate. The normalized determinant surrogate is better because it directly lower-bounds \(\sigma_{\min}^2\) only when it is large.

---

# 8. Full canonical terms

The augmented map should be defined using the full canonical object.

There are two equivalent formulations.

## Multiplicative formulation

If the canonical source can be represented at the cell level as a multiplier:

\[
q''_{\can}\text{-cell weight}
=
m_{\can}(\tau)\cdot Z\text{-cell weight},
\]

then

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Here plus/folded-dual structure is included in \(L_m\), and \(m_{\can}\) includes the canonical \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) transfer.

## Affine canonical formulation

More accurately, some pieces such as \(B_2\), \(D_2\), or \(E_2\) may not be purely multiplicative against the same \(L_m\). Then define

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
+
\mathcal B_{\can,m}(P x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Here \(\mathcal B_{\can,m}\) contains the nonmultiplicative canonical background/dual/tail corrections.

For the aggregate theorem, these terms must be included inside \(\mathcal A_m\), not treated as removable diagnostics. If they are perturbative, the perturbation bound must be at the singular-value scale:

\[
\|\mathcal B_{\can,m}\|_{\op}\le Q^{-A_{\rm big}}
\]

on average or pointwise. Otherwise they are part of the main frame.

---

# 9. Recommended AggFrame\(_{23}\) theorem

The cleanest Stack C2 theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}:
\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

For proof work, use the stronger algebraic sufficient condition:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggDetNorm}_{23}:
\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}
     {(1+\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^2)^{n-1}}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then

\[
\mathrm{AggDetNorm}_{23}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}
\Longrightarrow
\text{good-subset extraction for pointwise O2/O3}.
\]

A raw determinant theorem is acceptable only if one also proves a sufficiently tight operator-norm normalization so that determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) losses remain fixed-polynomial.

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggFrame}_{23}\text{ is plausible as an aggregate lower-frame theorem.}
}
\]

But the proof cannot rely on naive subset-frequency diagonal dominance, because determinant expansion contains near-colliding subset-product frequencies.

The exact missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-restricted aggregate augmented complex-frame lower bound.}
}
\]

The strongest usable form is \(\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}\). The most algebraic proof target is \(\mathrm{AggDetNorm}_{23}\). A fixed-vector aggregate frame is not sufficient for Stack C2 because it does not extract centers with pointwise O2/O3.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:28:18)

I’ll continue by replacing the crude determinant normalization with a sharper scale-invariant quantity: \(\det(A^*A)/e_{n-1}(A^*A)\), which is equivalent to \(\sigma_{\min}^2\) up to a factor \(n\).

_Thought for 22s_

## Agent 3 continuation — sharper aggregate O2/O3 energy

I refined the aggregate O2/O3 target. The previous candidates were:

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\]

or determinant-based surrogates. The determinant surrogate had a serious problem: converting \(|\det \mathcal A_m|^2\) to \(\sigma_{\min}^2\) through \(\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}\) can lose too much when \(n=R+2\).

The better object is the **harmonic exterior ratio**

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
:=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
     {e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
}
\]

where

\[
n=R+2,
\]

and \(e_{n-1}\) is the \((n-1)\)-st elementary symmetric polynomial of the eigenvalues of \(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m\).

This is scale-invariant and is equivalent to \(\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\) up to a factor \(n\), which is harmless because

\[
n=R+2\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}=Q^{o(1)}.
\]

---

# 1. Exact singular-value identity

Let

\[
\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n
\]

be the eigenvalues of

\[
\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m,
\]

so

\[
\lambda_j=\sigma_j(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

Then

\[
\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
=
\prod_{j=1}^n\lambda_j,
\]

and

\[
e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n\prod_{j\ne i}\lambda_j.
\]

Therefore, when \(\det\ne0\),

\[
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
     {e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
=
\frac{1}
     {\sum_{i=1}^n\lambda_i^{-1}}.
\]

This is the reciprocal trace of the inverse Gram matrix:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{1}{\operatorname{tr}((\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)^{-1})}.
\]

If the matrix is singular, define

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)=0.
\]

Since

\[
\lambda_{\min}^{-1}
\le
\sum_i\lambda_i^{-1}
\le
n\lambda_{\min}^{-1},
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
\frac{1}{n}\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\asymp_{Q^{o(1)}} E_{23}(m).
}
\]

This is much sharper than determinant divided by an operator-norm power.

---

# 2. Recommended aggregate O2/O3 theorem

Replace the determinant-normalized candidate by:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}:
\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}d\mu(m)\,
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Because

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\le E_{23}(m),
\]

this immediately implies

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}d\mu(m)\,
E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

And because

\[
E_{23}(m)\le \|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^2,
\]

with a polynomial or \(Q^{o(1)}\) upper bound, good-subset extraction gives a polynomial-weight subset where

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

So the chain is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}
\Longrightarrow
\text{pointwise O2/O3 on a polynomial-weight subset.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Why this is better than raw determinant energy

Raw determinant energy is

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
=
\prod_i\lambda_i.
\]

It can be large merely because the top \(n-1\) singular values are large, even if \(\lambda_{\min}\) is small. To recover \(\lambda_{\min}\), one divides by an upper bound for the other singular values:

\[
\lambda_{\min}
\ge
\frac{\prod_i\lambda_i}
     {\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}}.
\]

This is where the dangerous \(n\)-dependent loss appears.

The harmonic exterior ratio divides by the exact \((n-1)\)-volume:

\[
e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
=
\|\wedge^{n-1}\mathcal A_m\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2.
\]

So it removes the large-singular-value inflation at the same center \(m\), without requiring a global operator-norm loss.

This is the correct scale-invariant object.

---

# 4. Algebraic form

Let

\[
G_m:=\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m.
\]

Then

\[
\det G_m=|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
\]

Also,

\[
e_{n-1}(G_m)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{adj}G_m)
=
\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|^2,
\]

equivalently the Hilbert--Schmidt norm of all \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) minors of \(\mathcal A_m\).

So

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}
     {\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|^2}.
\]

This is explicitly Plücker-algebraic. The numerator and denominator are both expandable in prime-log subset-product phases.

The price is that it is a ratio, not a polynomial. But it is the right ratio.

---

# 5. Dyadic denominator reduction

To make \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) more attackable, decompose by dyadic ranges of the denominator

\[
D_{n-1}(m):=e_{n-1}(G_m).
\]

Suppose

\[
D_{n-1}(m)\in [\Lambda,2\Lambda].
\]

On this slice,

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\asymp
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}{\Lambda}.
\]

Thus it is enough to prove that for some dyadic slice,

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\Lambda}
d\mu(m)\,
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

or, more invariantly,

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\mathbf 1_{D_{n-1}(m)\sim\Lambda}
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\mu(\mathcal C_\Lambda).
\]

This converts the ratio problem into a family of determinant-energy lower bounds on controlled \((n-1)\)-volume strata.

That is more realistic than raw determinant lower bounds over all centers.

---

# 6. Relation to exterior powers

Another formulation uses exterior powers.

Let

\[
\wedge^n\mathcal A_m
\]

be the top exterior map. Since the domain and codomain have dimension \(n\),

\[
\|\wedge^n\mathcal A_m\|^2=|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
\]

And

\[
\|\wedge^{n-1}\mathcal A_m\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
=
e_{n-1}(G_m).
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\|\wedge^n\mathcal A_m\|^2}
     {\|\wedge^{n-1}\mathcal A_m\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2}.
\]

So the aggregate theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)
\frac{\|\wedge^n\mathcal A_m\|^2}
     {\|\wedge^{n-1}\mathcal A_m\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This says: the top exterior volume is not systematically small relative to the next exterior volume.

This is exactly the aggregate anti-rank-collapse statement needed for O2/O3.

---

# 7. Proof obstruction remains arithmetic

The harmonic ratio fixes the determinant-to-singular-value loss, but it does not prove the lower bound.

Expanding \(\det\mathcal A_m\), as before, gives subset-product frequencies:

\[
\Omega_S=\sum_{r\in S}\omega_r.
\]

The determinant square contains phases

\[
e^{-im(\Omega_S-\Omega_T)}.
\]

Since

\[
|\Omega_S-\Omega_T|
\]

can be as small as

\[
\exp(-CQR),
\]

diagonal dominance remains false at the raw frequency-separation level.

Thus a proof of \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) still needs a **zero-restricted lower sampling theorem for exterior-power prime-log packets**, not a naive large-sieve diagonal argument.

But this is now the correct theorem; it no longer suffers an artificial operator-norm loss.

---

# 8. Extraction consequence

Assume:

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\mathcal H_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

and

\[
E_{23}(m)=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\le Q^C.
\]

Since

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\le E_{23}(m),
\]

we get

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

For any \(B>A\), let

\[
\mathcal G_{23}:=\{m:E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-B}\}.
\]

Then

\[
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
+
Q^C\mu(\mathcal G_{23}).
\]

Choosing \(B>A+1\),

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_{23})
\ge
Q^{-A-C-o(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

So one obtains a polynomial-weight subset with pointwise O2/O3.

---

# 9. Updated recommendation for Stack C2

Use the following hierarchy:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{GoodSubset}_{23}.
}
\]

Where

\[
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}:
\quad
\sum_m d\mu(m)
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}
     {\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|^2}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This should replace raw determinant energy as the preferred algebraic aggregate O2/O3 theorem.

## Current status

\[
\boxed{
\text{This improves the formulation but does not close the theorem.}
}
\]

The remaining missing input is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-restricted aggregate exterior-ratio lower sampling}
}
\]

for the augmented prime-log complex-frame matrices \(\mathcal A_m\).

That is the precise Agent 3 target now.

## Agent 3 continuation — proof routes for \(\mathrm{AggFrame}_{23}\)

The current Stack C2 asks for an aggregate O2/O3 theorem strong enough to extract centers where the local augmented frame

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\]

has

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The last pass identified the harmonic exterior ratio

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
     {e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)},
\qquad n=R+2,
\]

as the right scale-invariant local quantity because

\[
\frac1n\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

So

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\mathcal H_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

is a valid aggregate theorem for extracting pointwise O2/O3-good centers. This matches the current Stack C2 requirement to replace pointwise O2/O3 by an aggregate lower-frame input. 

The question is whether \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) is actually provable by known-looking lower sampling methods.

## 1. Main obstruction: exterior amplification makes the determinant too long

Expanding

\[
\det \mathcal A_m
\]

by Cauchy--Binet gives subset-product phases of size \(n=R+2\). If the original packet frequencies are

\[
\omega_r=\log n_r,
\qquad
n_r\le e^{CQ},
\]

then determinant frequencies are

\[
\Omega_S=\sum_{r\in S}\omega_r
=
\log\prod_{r\in S}n_r,
\qquad |S|=n.
\]

Thus the determinant behaves like a Dirichlet polynomial of effective length

\[
\prod_{r\in S}n_r
\le e^{CQn}.
\]

Since

\[
n=R+2\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this length is

\[
e^{CQ R}.
\]

If the zero range is \(T\sim e^Q\), then the determinant length is roughly

\[
T^{\,R}.
\]

This is far longer than the sampling horizon. Distinct determinant frequencies may be separated only by

\[
e^{-CQR},
\]

while the zero sample over length \(T\) resolves frequencies only down to about

\[
T^{-1}=e^{-Q}.
\]

Because

\[
e^{-CQR}\ll e^{-Q},
\]

ordinary diagonal-dominance or Ingham-type lower sampling cannot see individual determinant frequencies.

So the determinant route fixes the singular-value normalization problem, but it creates a new arithmetic difficulty:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the exterior determinant is a very long prime-log packet sampled on a much shorter zero range.}
}
\]

This is not automatically fatal, but it means \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) is not a routine lower-large-sieve theorem.

---

## 2. Fixed-vector aggregate frames are insufficient

A tempting weaker theorem is

\[
\inf_{\|v\|=1}
\sum_m d\mu(m)\|\mathcal A_m v\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is much more accessible because for fixed \(v\), \(\mathcal A_m v\) is only a scalar/vector prime-log packet of original length, not exterior length.

But it does not imply good centers with pointwise O2/O3.

Countermodel: let \(n=R+2\), and let

\[
\mathcal A_m=I-v_m v_m^*
\]

where \(v_m\) ranges over many directions in \(\mathbb C^n\). Then every \(\mathcal A_m\) is rank \(n-1\), so

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)=0
\]

for every \(m\). However, if the directions \(v_m\) are well distributed, then for every fixed unit vector \(v\),

\[
\sum_m \|\mathcal A_m v\|^2
=
\sum_m (1-|\langle v,v_m\rangle|^2)
\gg
\sum_m 1.
\]

Thus the fixed-vector aggregate frame can be perfect while every local augmented frame is singular.

Therefore Stack C2 needs a theorem controlling **moving bad directions**, not only fixed directions.

---

## 3. Reformulation as moving-kernel exclusion

The aggregate singular-value theorem is exactly

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)
\min_{\|v\|=1}\|\mathcal A_m v\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Equivalently, it says that one cannot choose a unit vector field

\[
v(m)\in\mathbb C^n,
\qquad
\|v(m)\|=1,
\]

such that

\[
\mathcal A_m v(m)
\]

is small for most \(m\).

So the correct non-determinantal formulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{zero-restricted aggregate moving-kernel exclusion.}
}
\]

For every measurable or algebraically controlled unit vector field \(v(m)\),

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\|\mathcal A_m v(m)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

With no restriction on \(v(m)\), this is tautologically equivalent to \(\mathrm{AggSV}_{23}\). The point is that the bad vector field in the actual problem is not arbitrary; it is a singular vector of the analytic matrix \(\mathcal A_m\). Thus it has algebraic dependence on the entries of \(\mathcal A_m\), except near singular-value crossings.

A possible proof route is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{fixed-vector lower sampling}
+
\text{complexity control on singular-vector motion}
\Longrightarrow
\text{moving-kernel exclusion}.
}
\]

The missing analytic component would be a bound on the metric entropy or variation of the singular-vector graph

\[
m\mapsto v_{\min}(m).
\]

---

## 4. Entropy calculation: naive nets are too expensive

The domain dimension is

\[
n=R+2\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

To prove a pointwise-in-\(v\) frame estimate at scale \(Q^{-A}\) by a net over the unit sphere in \(\mathbb C^n\), one needs mesh size about

\[
\varepsilon\sim Q^{-A}.
\]

The net size is roughly

\[
(1/\varepsilon)^{2n}
=
Q^{O(An)}
=
\exp\!\left(
O_A\!\left(\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}\right)
\right).
\]

This is super-polynomial in \(Q\). Therefore a naive union bound over a sphere net destroys fixed-polynomial bookkeeping.

So to prove moving-kernel exclusion from fixed-vector estimates, one needs more than a net. One needs actual structural control on the moving singular vector.

---

## 5. Analytic singular-vector control: possible but conditional

Let

\[
G_m:=\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m.
\]

The bad vector \(v_{\min}(m)\) satisfies

\[
G_m v_{\min}(m)
=
\lambda_{\min}(m)v_{\min}(m).
\]

When \(\lambda_{\min}\) is simple and separated from the next eigenvalue, perturbation theory gives

\[
\|v_{\min}'(m)\|
\lesssim
\frac{\|G_m'(m)\|}{\operatorname{gap}(m)}.
\]

The problem is that near rank-collapse,

\[
\operatorname{gap}(m)
\]

may also be small. Then the bad vector can rotate quickly. Fast rotation is precisely how the fixed-vector frame can hold while local singular values vanish.

Thus a viable theorem would need an aggregate spectral-gap or stratification estimate:

\[
\text{either }\lambda_{\min}\text{ is not often small,}
\]

which is the desired result, or

\[
\lambda_{\min}\text{ small but singular-vector motion is controlled enough to contradict fixed-vector sampling.}
\]

This is circular unless the small-gap strata can be bounded independently.

So analytic motion control is not currently a proof, but it suggests a refined target:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{zero-restricted aggregate stratified singular-vector exclusion.}
}
\]

---

## 6. Exterior-ratio route versus moving-kernel route

There are now two equivalent serious formulations.

### Route A: exterior-ratio lower sampling

Prove

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)
\frac{
\|\wedge^n\mathcal A_m\|^2
}{
\|\wedge^{n-1}\mathcal A_m\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is exact, scale-stable, and directly extractable.

Weakness: expansion uses long subset-product frequencies of length \(T^R\).

### Route B: moving-kernel exclusion

Prove

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)
\|\mathcal A_m v(m)\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

for the singular-vector fields \(v(m)\) generated by the actual augmented matrix.

Weakness: requires structural control of moving singular vectors, not just fixed-vector lower sampling.

These are mathematically equivalent in strength but attack different difficulties.

---

## 7. Phase-relaxed no-go for moving-kernel control

The rank-one projection countermodel also shows why some arithmetic input is unavoidable.

Let \(v_m\) be chosen freely, and set

\[
\mathcal A_m=I-v_mv_m^*.
\]

Then

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)=0
\]

for every \(m\), but fixed-vector aggregate lower frames hold if \(v_m\) is equidistributed.

This can be mimicked by phase-relaxed moment matrices: for each center \(m\), choose cell weights so that \(\mathcal A_m\) has a prescribed null vector \(v_m\), while preserving the scalar zero condition. Because the cell dimension is much larger than \(n\), this is possible in the relaxed model.

Therefore moving-kernel exclusion cannot follow from dimension or fixed-vector sampling alone. It must use the actual coupling of weights across \(m\), i.e. prime-log arithmetic.

---

## 8. Updated exact missing theorem

The sharp Agent 3 theorem is now one of the following equivalent forms.

### Form 1: aggregate harmonic exterior theorem

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}d\mu(m)
\frac{
\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
}{
e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

### Form 2: aggregate least-singular theorem

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}d\mu(m)
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

### Form 3: moving-kernel exclusion

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}d\mu(m)
\|\mathcal A_m v_{\min}(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

where \(v_{\min}(m)\) is any unit least-singular vector.

All are essentially equivalent for Stack C2.

---

## 9. Current status

\[
\boxed{
\text{AggFrame}_{23}\text{ is better posed than pointwise O2/O3, but it is still a new arithmetic theorem.}
}
\]

The promising part is that it is aggregate and dimensionally natural.

The hard part is that neither:

\[
\text{fixed-vector lower sampling}
\]

nor

\[
\text{raw determinant energy}
\]

is sufficient by itself.

The next useful subattack should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can the actual prime-log dependence across }m
\text{ control the moving singular vector }v_{\min}(m)?
}
\]

If yes, AggFrame\(_{23}\) may be accessible through fixed-vector lower sampling plus singular-vector motion control.

If no, the aggregate O2/O3 input must remain a named zero-restricted aggregate exterior-ratio theorem.

## Agent 3 — AggFrame\(_{23}\) via harmonic exterior ratio

The assigned task is to attack aggregate O2/O3 using

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\qquad \deg P\le R,
\]

with \(n=R+2\), and the harmonic exterior ratio

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
     {e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}.
\]

This is part of the corrected Stack C2 route, where aggregate O2/O3 must feed good-subset extraction rather than assume pointwise O2/O3 everywhere. 

---

# 1. Local singular-value comparison

Let

\[
G_m:=\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m,
\]

and let

\[
\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n\ge0
\]

be the eigenvalues of \(G_m\). These are the squared singular values of \(\mathcal A_m\):

\[
\lambda_j=\sigma_j(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

Assume first that \(G_m\) is invertible. Then

\[
\det G_m=\prod_{j=1}^n\lambda_j,
\]

and

\[
e_{n-1}(G_m)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n\prod_{j\ne i}\lambda_j.
\]

Therefore

\[
\frac{\det G_m}{e_{n-1}(G_m)}
=
\frac{\prod_j\lambda_j}
     {\sum_i\prod_{j\ne i}\lambda_j}
=
\frac{1}{\sum_i\lambda_i^{-1}}
=
\frac{1}{\operatorname{tr}(G_m^{-1})}.
\]

If \(G_m\) is singular, define

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)=0.
\]

Let

\[
\lambda_{\min}=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

Since

\[
\lambda_{\min}^{-1}
\le
\sum_i\lambda_i^{-1}
\le
n\lambda_{\min}^{-1},
\]

we get the sharp comparison

\[
\boxed{
\frac1n\,\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
}
\]

Because

\[
n=R+2\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}=Q^{o(1)},
\]

\(\mathcal H_{23}\) is equivalent to \(E_{23}:=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\) up to a harmless \(Q^{o(1)}\) factor.

This is the correct scale-invariant replacement for raw determinant energy.

---

# 2. Aggregate harmonic theorem and extraction

The aggregate theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}:\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C} d\mu(m)\,\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

By the local comparison,

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\le E_{23}(m),
\]

so

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C} d\mu(m)\,E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Assume a polynomial local upper bound

\[
E_{23}(m)\le Q^C.
\]

For a threshold \(Q^{-B}\), define

\[
\mathcal G_{23}
=
\{m\in\mathcal C:E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-B}\}.
\]

Then

\[
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
+
Q^C\mu(\mathcal G_{23}).
\]

Taking \(B>A+1\), this gives

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_{23})
\ge
Q^{-A-C-o(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Thus \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) gives a polynomial-weight subset where pointwise O2/O3 holds.

---

# 3. Exterior-power and cofactor form

Let

\[
A=\mathcal A_m.
\]

Then

\[
\det(A^*A)=|\det A|^2
=
\|\wedge^n A\|^2.
\]

Also,

\[
e_{n-1}(A^*A)
=
\|\wedge^{n-1}A\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2.
\]

For an \(n\times n\) matrix, this can also be written as the Hilbert–Schmidt norm of the cofactor matrix:

\[
e_{n-1}(A^*A)
=
\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(A)|^2.
\]

Hence

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}
     {\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|^2}
=
\frac{\|\wedge^n\mathcal A_m\|^2}
     {\|\wedge^{n-1}\mathcal A_m\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2}.
}
\]

This says: aggregate O2/O3 is an aggregate anti-rank-collapse theorem. The top exterior volume cannot be too small relative to the next exterior volume on average over routed centers.

---

# 4. Dyadic denominator strategy

Set

\[
D_{n-1}(m):=e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
=
\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|^2,
\]

and

\[
N_n(m):=|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)=\frac{N_n(m)}{D_{n-1}(m)}.
\]

Decompose \(\mathcal C\) into dyadic slices

\[
\mathcal C_\Lambda
=
\{m:\Lambda\le D_{n-1}(m)<2\Lambda\}.
\]

On \(\mathcal C_\Lambda\),

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\asymp
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}{\Lambda}.
\]

Thus a sufficient dyadic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\exists \Lambda\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\Lambda}
d\mu(m)\,|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

A stronger but cleaner slice-relative version is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\Lambda}
d\mu(m)\,|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\mu(\mathcal C_\Lambda)
}
\]

on enough dyadic mass to cover a polynomial fraction of \(\mu(\mathcal C)\).

This reduces \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) to determinant-energy lower bounds on strata where the \((n-1)\)-volume is controlled.

---

# 5. Arithmetic difficulty

Expanding \(\det\mathcal A_m\) gives exterior prime-log phases. In the ideal multiplicative cell model,

\[
(\mathcal A_m)_{kj}
=
\sum_r C_r(m)\phi_j(\tau_r)\tau_r^k,
\]

where

\[
\phi_0=1,\qquad
\phi_j=m_{\can}P_j.
\]

By Cauchy–Binet,

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{S:\ |S|=n}
\left(\prod_{r\in S}C_r(m)\right)
\det(\tau_r^k)_{k,r\in S}
\det(\phi_j(\tau_r))_{r,j\in S}.
\]

If

\[
C_r(m)\sim c_r e^{-im\omega_r},
\]

then determinant frequencies are

\[
\Omega_S=\sum_{r\in S}\omega_r.
\]

For \(\omega_r=\log n_r\),

\[
\Omega_S=\log\prod_{r\in S}n_r.
\]

Since \(|S|=n=R+2\) and \(n_r\le e^{CQ}\),

\[
\prod_{r\in S}n_r\le e^{CQR}.
\]

Thus determinant frequencies live at effective length

\[
e^{CQR}=T^{O(R)}
\]

if \(T\sim e^Q\).

Distinct subset-product frequencies can be separated only by

\[
e^{-CQR},
\]

much smaller than the sampling resolution

\[
T^{-1}=e^{-Q}.
\]

Therefore naive diagonal dominance or ordinary Ingham separation does not prove the determinant lower bound.

The exact missing arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{zero-restricted aggregate exterior-ratio lower sampling}
}
\]

for the actual augmented prime-log determinant packets, not arbitrary packets and not separated-frequency models.

---

# 6. Comparison with determinant in an \(R^C\)-normalized basis

Raw determinant energy can imply singular-value energy if the operator norm is sufficiently controlled.

Because

\[
|\det A|^2
\le
\sigma_{\min}(A)^2\|A\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)},
\]

we get

\[
\sigma_{\min}(A)^2
\ge
\frac{|\det A|^2}{\|A\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}}.
\]

If

\[
\|A\|_{\op}\le R^C,
\]

then

\[
\|A\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}
\le
R^{O(R)}
=
\exp(O(R\log R)).
\]

Since

\[
R\log R
\asymp
\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}\log\left(\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}\right)
=
O(\log Q),
\]

this is only

\[
Q^{O(1)}.
\]

So in an \(R^C\)-normalized basis, raw determinant energy is usable.

But if only

\[
\|A\|_{\op}\le Q^C,
\]

then

\[
\|A\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}
=
Q^{O(R)},
\]

which is super-polynomial in the fixed-\(Q^{-A}\) bookkeeping.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{determinant energy is acceptable only after }R^C\text{-level basis normalization.}
}
\]

Without such normalization, \(\mathcal H_{23}\) is strictly better because it divides out the actual \((n-1)\)-volume at the same center.

---

# 7. Best proof route

The most plausible proof route is not universal lower sampling and not raw determinant diagonal dominance. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-coefficient dyadic exterior-ratio lower sampling.}
}
\]

More explicitly:

1. Normalize the augmented basis so that \(\mathcal A_m\) is \(R^C\)-conditioned when local canonical data is ordinary-sized.

2. Stratify by

\[
D_{n-1}(m)=e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m).
\]

3. On a dyadic slice \(D_{n-1}\sim\Lambda\), prove an actual-coefficient determinant-energy lower bound:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\Lambda}
d\mu(m)\,|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\mu(\mathcal C_\Lambda),
\]

or prove that slices failing this have negligible total measure.

4. Use the dyadic decomposition to conclude

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is still a hard arithmetic theorem, but it is the cleanest non-pointwise formulation.

---

# 8. Final Agent 3 deliverable

The recommended aggregate O2/O3 theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}:\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
     {e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

It implies aggregate least-singular-value energy and hence good-subset extraction for pointwise O2/O3.

A determinant version is acceptable only under an \(R^C\)-normalized augmented basis:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\quad
\text{and}
\quad
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le R^C.
}
\]

The remaining obstruction is not local algebra. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual-coefficient zero-restricted aggregate exterior-ratio lower sampling.}
}
\]

That is the precise AggFrame\(_{23}\) bottleneck.

The current Agent 3 assignment is to continue the aggregate O2/O3 attack using

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\qquad \deg P\le R,
\]

and the harmonic exterior ratio

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
     {e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)},
\qquad n=R+2.
\]

The coordinator’s latest instruction is to treat this as part of corrected Stack C2, where aggregate O2/O3 must be wall-compatible and extract pointwise-good centers. 

---

# 1. Local singular-value comparison

Let

\[
G_m:=\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m.
\]

Let

\[
\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n\ge0
\]

be the eigenvalues of \(G_m\), so

\[
\lambda_j=\sigma_j(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

If \(G_m\) is invertible, then

\[
\det G_m=\prod_{j=1}^n\lambda_j,
\]

and

\[
e_{n-1}(G_m)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n\prod_{j\ne i}\lambda_j.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\prod_j\lambda_j}
     {\sum_i\prod_{j\ne i}\lambda_j}
=
\frac{1}{\sum_i\lambda_i^{-1}}
=
\frac{1}{\operatorname{tr}(G_m^{-1})}.
\]

If \(G_m\) is singular, define

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)=0.
\]

Let

\[
\lambda_{\min}=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

Then

\[
\lambda_{\min}^{-1}
\le
\sum_{i=1}^n\lambda_i^{-1}
\le
n\lambda_{\min}^{-1}.
\]

\boxed{
\frac1n\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
}
\]

Since

\[
n=R+2\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}=Q^{o(1)},
\]

\(\mathcal H_{23}\) is equivalent to \(E_{23}=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\) for fixed-polynomial extraction.

---

# 2. Aggregate theorem and extraction

The correct aggregate O2/O3 theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}:
\qquad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Because

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\le E_{23}(m),
\]

this implies

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)\,E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Assume a polynomial local upper bound

\[
E_{23}(m)\le Q^C.
\]

For \(B>A+1\), define

\[
\mathcal G_{23}
=
\{m\in\mathcal C:E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-B}\}.
\]

Then

\[
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
+
Q^C\mu(\mathcal G_{23}),
\]

so

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_{23})
\ge
Q^{-A-C-o(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Thus \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) gives a polynomial-weight subset where the local augmented O2/O3 frame holds pointwise.

---

# 3. Exterior-power and cofactor form

For \(A=\mathcal A_m\),

\[
\det(A^*A)=|\det A|^2=\|\wedge^n A\|^2.
\]

Also,

\[
e_{n-1}(A^*A)
=
\|\wedge^{n-1}A\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
e_{n-1}(A^*A)
=
\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(A)|^2.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}
     {\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|^2}
=
\frac{\|\wedge^n\mathcal A_m\|^2}
     {\|\wedge^{n-1}\mathcal A_m\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2}.
}
\]

Interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal H_{23}
\text{ measures top exterior volume relative to next exterior volume.}
}
\]

It is the right local anti-rank-collapse quantity. Raw determinant energy is not scale-stable; \(\mathcal H_{23}\) is.

---

# 4. Dyadic denominator strategy

Define

\[
D_{n-1}(m):=e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
=
\sum_{i,j}|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|^2,
\]

and

\[
N_n(m):=|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)=\frac{N_n(m)}{D_{n-1}(m)}.
\]

Decompose into dyadic denominator slices

\[
\mathcal C_\Lambda
=
\{m\in\mathcal C:\Lambda\le D_{n-1}(m)<2\Lambda\}.
\]

On \(\mathcal C_\Lambda\),

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\asymp
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}{\Lambda}.
\]

A sufficient dyadic theorem is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\Lambda}
d\mu(m)\,|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\mu(\mathcal C_\Lambda)
}
\]

for enough dyadic slices to cover a polynomial fraction of \(\mu(\mathcal C)\).

Equivalently, one may state the weaker global slice condition:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\Lambda}
\Lambda^{-1}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\Lambda}
d\mu(m)\,|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is exactly \(\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\) after dyadic decomposition.

The dyadic strategy removes the unstable determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) operator-norm loss. It asks for determinant energy only after controlling the \((n-1)\)-exterior volume scale.

---

# 5. Raw determinant energy under \(R^C\)-normalization

Raw determinant energy can be used if \(\mathcal A_m\) is represented in a sufficiently normalized basis.

Since

\[
|\det A|^2
=
\prod_{j=1}^n\sigma_j(A)^2
\le
\sigma_{\min}(A)^2\|A\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)},
\]

we get

\[
\sigma_{\min}(A)^2
\ge
\frac{|\det A|^2}{\|A\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}}.
\]

If

\[
\|A\|_{\op}\le R^C,
\]

then

\[
\|A\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}
\le
R^{O(R)}
=
\exp(O(R\log R)).
\]

With

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

we have

\[
R\log R=O(\log Q),
\]

so

\[
R^{O(R)}=Q^{O(1)}.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\|A_m\|_{\op}\le R^C
\Longrightarrow
\text{determinant energy converts to }E_{23}
\text{ with only polynomial loss.}
}
\]

But if only

\[
\|A_m\|_{\op}\le Q^C,
\]

then

\[
\|A_m\|_{\op}^{2(n-1)}=Q^{O(R)},
\]

which is super-polynomial. That is not acceptable for fixed-polynomial bookkeeping.

So determinant energy is safe only after \(R^C\)-level Riesz/orthogonal normalization of both domain and codomain.

---

# 6. Normalized bases: what is needed

A viable normalization should use bases whose evaluation and moment maps have condition numbers bounded by \(R^C\), not \(Q^C\).

For the domain

\[
\mathbb C\oplus \mathbb C_R[x],
\]

use an orthonormal polynomial basis on the ordinary window

\[
x\in[c_-,c_+],
\]

or a Chebyshev/Legendre basis rescaled to the packet support. The multiplier \(m_{\can}\) then acts by multiplication before moment sampling.

For the codomain

\[
\mathbb C^{R+2}
\]

indexed by \(0\le k\le R+1\), do not use raw monomials \(x^k\) if they create large conditioning. Use the corresponding dual orthonormal polynomial test functions

\[
\psi_k(x),
\qquad 0\le k\le R+1.
\]

Then define

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)\psi_k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

In this basis the desired operator norm bound is

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le R^C
}
\]

under ordinary-size local canonical data.

If this normalization cannot be proved, one should avoid raw determinant energy and use \(\mathcal H_{23}\) directly.

---

# 7. Arithmetic obstruction

Even after the local algebra is repaired, the aggregate lower bound remains arithmetic.

Expanding the determinant by Cauchy–Binet gives

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{S:\ |S|=n}
C_S(m)\,
V_S^{\rm row}\,
V_S^{\rm col},
\]

where \(C_S(m)\) contains the product of \(n\) cell weights.

If each cell weight has phase

\[
e^{-im\omega_r},
\]

then determinant frequencies are

\[
\Omega_S=\sum_{r\in S}\omega_r.
\]

For prime-log frequencies

\[
\omega_r=\log n_r,
\]

we have

\[
\Omega_S
=
\log\prod_{r\in S}n_r.
\]

Since \(|S|=n=R+2\) and \(n_r\le e^{CQ}\),

\[
\prod_{r\in S}n_r\le e^{CQR}.
\]

Thus the determinant packet has effective length

\[
e^{CQR}=T^{O(R)}
\]

if \(T\sim e^Q\).

Distinct subset-product frequencies can be separated only by

\[
e^{-CQR},
\]

well below sampling resolution \(T^{-1}=e^{-Q}\). Therefore naive diagonal dominance is unavailable.

The required theorem is not a generic separated-frequency lower large sieve. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual-coefficient zero-restricted aggregate exterior-ratio lower sampling.}
}
\]

It must exploit the actual canonical prime-log coefficients and routed-center distribution, not arbitrary determinant packets.

---

# 8. Full canonical terms

The augmented map must be built from the full canonical object.

In the ideal multiplicative model,

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)\psi_k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Here:

- plus/folded-dual structure is part of \(L_m\);
- \(m_{\can}\) is the canonical curvature multiplier;
- the packet represents the full \(q''_{\can}\) source.

If some terms are not multiplicative relative to the same \(L_m\), define instead

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)\psi_k)
+
\mathcal B_{\can,m}(P,\psi_k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

The correction functional \(\mathcal B_{\can,m}\) must include the nonmultiplicative parts of

\[
B_2,\quad D_2,\quad E_2.
\]

These terms cannot be discarded as diagnostics. They are either part of the main augmented map or must be shown perturbative at the \(Q^{-A}\) singular-value scale.

---

# 9. Final AggFrame\(_{23}\) theorem

The recommended theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}^{\can}:
\quad
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
d\mu(m)
\frac{
\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
}{
e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)
}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

where:

\[
n=R+2,
\]

\(\mathcal A_m\) is defined in \(R^C\)-conditioned polynomial bases, and it includes the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

A proof may proceed through dyadic denominator slices:

\[
D_{n-1}(m)=e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)\sim\Lambda,
\]

and determinant-energy lower bounds on those slices:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_\Lambda}
d\mu(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\mu(\mathcal C_\Lambda).
\]

If one proves

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|_{\op}\le R^C
\]

uniformly in the chosen basis, then an alternate determinant-energy theorem is acceptable:

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

with the understood \(R^{O(R)}=Q^{O(1)}\) determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) loss.

---

# Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Local algebra for AggFrame}_{23}\text{ is now clean.}
}
\]

The harmonic exterior ratio exactly matches least-singular-value energy up to \(R+2\). It avoids the unstable raw determinant normalization.

The remaining obstruction is purely arithmetic:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{zero-restricted aggregate exterior-ratio lower sampling}
}
\]

for the actual canonical augmented prime-log matrices \(\mathcal A_m\), including

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

This is the precise Agent 3 bottleneck for corrected Stack C2.
