## Agent 3 continuation — zero-density obstruction to the weighted resultant route

The current Agent 3 bottleneck is the weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough:

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{ij}}
\min\!\left(
Q^A
\left|
\frac{\det\mathcal A_m}{\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)}
\right|^2,
1
\right)
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-B}
\int_{\mathcal C_{ij}}F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

The update makes clear that this theorem is now weighted by the \(\Lambda\)-forbidden wall channel, and that the downstream contradiction depends on identifying \(\Lambda\). For Agent 3, the relevant issue is whether the pivot/resultant theorem can be proved by ordinary analytic zero-counting or resultant arguments. 

## 1. The determinant has too much exponential type for zero-counting to help

Write

\[
D(m)=\det\mathcal A_m.
\]

In the Cauchy–Binet model,

\[
D(m)
=
\sum_{|S|=n}C_S e^{-im\Omega_S},
\qquad
\Omega_S=\log N_S,
\qquad
N_S=\prod_{r\in S}n_r,
\]

with

\[
n=R+2.
\]

The original packet has

\[
\log n_r\asymp Q.
\]

Therefore

\[
\Omega_S\asymp nQ\asymp QR.
\]

So \(D(m)\), as a finite exponential polynomial, has effective exponential type

\[
\operatorname{type}(D)\asymp QR.
\]

The zeta zeros in a height window \(T\sim e^Q\) have density

\[
\asymp Q
\]

per unit length.

An entire/exponential-polynomial function of type \(\asymp QR\) has enough bandwidth to vanish on a real set of density \(\asymp Q\). There is a factor of \(R\) slack.

So Jensen, Cartan, or zero-counting cannot rule out

\[
D(\gamma)=0
\]

at all sampled zeros \(\gamma\), even before considering approximate zeros.

This is a major no-go:

\[
\boxed{
\text{exponential-type zero-counting cannot prove the weighted pivot anti-trough.}
}
\]

The determinant exteriorization has increased the bandwidth from \(Q\) to \(QR\), while the sampled zero density remains \(Q\).

---

# 2. The cofactor ratio does not fix the type problem

The pivot is

\[
R_{ij}(m)
=
\frac{D(m)}{C_{ij}(m)}.
\]

Here

\[
C_{ij}(m)=\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)
\]

is an \((n-1)\)-exterior packet, so it has type roughly

\[
Q(R+1).
\]

The ratio is meromorphic. A logarithmic resultant bound would try to control

\[
\sum_{\gamma}
\log_+
\frac{|C_{ij}(\gamma)|}{|D(\gamma)|}.
\]

But the numerator and denominator have nearly the same large type. There is no low-degree/low-type asymmetry that would force \(D\) not to vanish at the zeta zeros while \(C_{ij}\) remains nonzero.

The relevant obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
D\text{ has enough type to interpolate zeros of }Z_\alpha
\text{ without forcing }C_{ij}\text{ to vanish.}
}
\]

Thus a soft resultant theorem fails for the same reason as zero-counting.

---

# 3. Bandwidth slack survives the Schur-complement formulation

In Schur form,

\[
\frac{D}{C_{ij}}
=
a-v^*B^{-1}u
=
L_m(\Phi_m)+\mathcal B_m^{\can}(\Phi_m).
\]

The residual test \(\Phi_m\) moves with \(m\). Its derivative naturally satisfies

\[
\|\Phi_m'\|\lesssim Q R^C\kappa^{-O(1)}.
\]

This is prime-log speed, so fixed-test lower sampling cannot freeze \(\Phi_m\) across zeros.

The exponential-type obstruction explains why: the moving residual can track the sampled zero set with enough degrees of freedom. It behaves more like a high-bandwidth adaptive packet than a fixed low-bandwidth test.

So the Schur-pivot theorem is not reducible to:

\[
\text{fixed test lower sampling}+\text{Jensen}.
\]

It needs actual arithmetic structure of the determinant/cofactor coefficients.

---

# 4. What kind of theorem could still work?

The only plausible route is **coefficient-structure noninterpolation**.

A theorem must use the fact that \(D\) is not an arbitrary type-\(QR\) exponential polynomial. Its coefficients are exterior products:

\[
C_S
=
\left(\prod_{r\in S}a_r\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{r\in S}m(x_r)\right)
[x_r:r\in S](1/m).
\]

So the needed theorem is not:

\[
\text{a type/zero-density theorem}.
\]

It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{an actual-coefficient exterior packet cannot interpolate the zeta zeros}
\text{ while its adjacent cofactors remain large.}
}
\]

That is an arithmetic coefficient theorem.

It likely has to combine:

1. **phase alignment / sectoriality** of exterior coefficients on a dominant branch;
2. **collision noncancellation** for subset products \(N_S\);
3. **zero-sampling positivity** for the resulting exterior packet;
4. **cofactor dominance control** so \(D/C_{ij}\) is meaningful.

---

# 5. Phase-aligned route becomes more important

This strengthens Agent 4’s direction.

If one can find a subpacket where

\[
C_S
\]

lie in a sector, then exact multiplicative collisions cannot cancel:

\[
\left|
\sum_{S:N_S=N}C_S
\right|^2
\gtrsim
\left(
\sum_{S:N_S=N}|C_S|
\right)^2.
\]

Then the determinant has positive coefficient mass after collision aggregation.

In that case the problem becomes lower sampling of a positive/sectorial exterior packet at zeros. That is still hard, but it is no longer vulnerable to complete cancellation inside collision classes.

The analytic piece is the divided difference:

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_n](1/m_{\can}).
\]

By Hermite–Genocchi,

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_n]f
\]

is an average of \(f^{(n-1)}\) over the simplex. So sectoriality of

\[
(1/m_{\can})^{(n-1)}
\]

on a subinterval gives sectoriality of the divided difference.

Thus a plausible coefficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{on some branch/subpacket, }
\prod a_r,\quad \prod m(x_r),\quad
[x_r](1/m_{\can})
\text{ are phase-aligned with polynomial mass.}
}
\]

---

# 6. But phase alignment must certify the full determinant

A warning: proving sectoriality for a selected subdeterminant does not automatically prove the full determinant pivot is non-small.

If

\[
D=D_{\rm good}+D_{\rm rest},
\]

and \(D_{\rm rest}\) has uncontrolled phase, then

\[
D_{\rm rest}\approx -D_{\rm good}
\]

can cancel the good subpacket.

So one needs one of:

## A. Dominant branch

\[
|D_{\rm good}|
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{\rm all}|D_{\rm coefficients}|
\]

and \(D_{\rm rest}\) is perturbative.

## B. Sectorial full branch decomposition

Partition the full determinant coefficient set into \(Q^{O(1)}\) sectorial classes and prove one class contributes to the actual full determinant without cancellation.

## C. Work directly with \(|D|^2\) after collision aggregation

Show that after summing over zeros, cross-sector cancellation is controlled by positivity of the zero-sampling kernel.

Option C is closest to an aggregate proof, but it requires a positive lower-sampling kernel for the determinant packet.

---

# 7. Updated weighted resultant theorem is too strong unless coefficient-structured

The earlier logarithmic resultant theorem was:

\[
\int
\log_+\frac{|C_{ij}|}{|D|}
\,d\nu
\le
O(\log Q)\nu.
\]

This now looks too strong as a black-box analytic statement, because \(D\) has enough type to be tiny at all zeros.

It may still be true for the **actual exterior determinant** due to coefficient structure, but then it should be stated that way:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual-coefficient weighted exterior-pivot resultant theorem.}
}
\]

For every dominant cofactor class,

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{ij}}
\log_+
\frac{|\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A_m)|}
     {|\det\mathcal A_m|}
\,F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho
\le
C(\log Q)
\int_{\mathcal C_{ij}}
F_1F_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

But it should be understood as an arithmetic theorem using the exterior coefficient formula, not as a consequence of Jensen or type bounds.

---

# 8. Better near-term subattack

The best next Agent 3/4 combined subattack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find a phase-aligned exterior branch that is also cofactor-pivot relevant.}
}
\]

More concretely:

1. Choose a subinterval \(I_x\subset[c_-,c_+]\) where

\[
(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}
\]

is sectorial and not too small.

2. Restrict determinant subsets \(S\) to \(x_r\in I_x\).

3. Verify

\[
(\det V_S)^2\ge0
\]

and has polynomial lower mass on separated \(S\).

4. Control phases of

\[
\prod a_r,\qquad \prod m(x_r).
\]

5. Prove collision aggregation noncancellation within this class.

6. Show this class contributes to a dominant cofactor-pivot slice, not merely to an irrelevant projected determinant.

If all six hold, the weighted pivot anti-trough becomes plausible via a positive exterior packet lower-sampling theorem.

---

# 9. Final status

The new attack gives a firm no-go for generic analytic resultant methods:

\[
\boxed{
\text{type/zero-density/Jensen arguments cannot prove the pivot anti-trough,}
}
\]

because

\[
\operatorname{type}(\det\mathcal A)\asymp QR
\]

while the zero density is only

\[
\asymp Q.
\]

The remaining theorem must exploit actual exterior coefficient structure.

The sharp current bottleneck is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual-coefficient weighted exterior-pivot resultant/noninterpolation theorem.}
}
\]

The most plausible proof route is phase-aligned exterior determinant mass plus multiplicative collision noncancellation, coordinated with the \(\Lambda\)-weighted measure.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 20:03:20)

I’ll now test the phase-aligned exterior branch route directly: whether divided differences of \(1/m_{\can}\) can be made sectorial on a large enough subpacket without losing exterior mass.

_Thought for 9s_

## Agent 3 continuation — phase-aligned exterior branch test

The previous endpoint was:

\[
\text{generic analytic resultant methods fail}
\]

because

\[
\operatorname{type}(\det\mathcal A)\asymp QR
\]

while zero density is only \(\asymp Q\). So the determinant has enough bandwidth to interpolate the zero set. The only plausible route is to exploit the **actual exterior coefficient structure**:

\[
C_S
=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
\]

I now tested the phase-aligned branch route.

---

# 1. Divided-difference sectoriality

Let

\[
N=R+2,
\qquad
f(x)=\frac1{m_{\can}(x)}.
\]

The determinant coefficient contains

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f.
\]

By the Hermite–Genocchi formula,

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f
=
\int_{\Delta_{N-1}}
f^{(N-1)}
\left(
\sum_{j=1}^N \theta_jx_j
\right)
\,d\theta.
\]

Therefore, if on an interval \(I\),

\[
f^{(N-1)}(x)
\]

lies in a sector of angle \(<\pi/3\), then every divided difference with

\[
x_1,\ldots,x_N\in I
\]

lies in essentially the same sector.

So sectoriality of the divided difference reduces to sectoriality of the high derivative

\[
\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right)^{(R+1)}
\]

on a chosen subinterval.

This is a real simplification. It turns a many-point condition into a one-variable high-derivative phase condition.

---

# 2. Existence of a sectorial subinterval

Let

\[
g_R(x):=f^{(R+1)}(x).
\]

On the ordinary packet interval

\[
[c_-,c_+],
\]

partition into subintervals on which

\[
\arg g_R(x)
\]

varies by at most \(O(1)\).

If \(g_R\) is nonzero and analytic with derivative bounds

\[
\left|\frac{g_R'}{g_R}\right|\le L_R
\]

away from zeros, then intervals of length

\[
\ell\asymp L_R^{-1}
\]

are sectorial.

For typical analytic \(m_{\can}\) depending on \(R\) only through differentiation order, Cauchy-type bounds give something like

\[
L_R\lesssim R^C
\]

on subintervals avoiding zeros/poles of \(m_{\can}\). Then one can find sectorial subintervals of length

\[
\ell\gtrsim R^{-C}.
\]

This is acceptable. Since

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

a loss of

\[
\ell^{O(R)}
=
R^{-O(R)}
=
Q^{-O(1)}
\]

is still polynomial.

So the phase-aligned determinant route is viable **if** we can prove:

\[
\boxed{
\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right)^{(R+1)}
\text{ has polynomial mass on some }R^{-C}\text{-scale interval and no violent phase winding there.}
}
\]

This is now a concrete analytic multiplier lemma.

---

# 3. Exterior mass retained on a sectorial interval

Suppose \(I\subset[c_-,c_+]\) has length

\[
|I|=\ell\ge R^{-C}.
\]

Restrict determinant subsets \(S\) to points \(x_n\in I\).

The exterior coefficient mass contains the Vandermonde factor

\[
(\det V_S)^2
=
\prod_{i<j}(x_j-x_i)^2.
\]

If we choose \(N=R+2\) separated cells inside \(I\), with spacing

\[
\delta\asymp \frac{\ell}{R},
\]

then

\[
|\det V_S|^2
\gtrsim
\delta^{N(N-1)}
=
\left(\frac{\ell}{R}\right)^{O(R^2)}.
\]

This is dangerous:

\[
R^{-O(R^2)}
=
\exp(-O(R^2\log R)).
\]

With

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this becomes

\[
\exp\!\left(
-O\!\left(
\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}
\right)
\right),
\]

which is **super-polynomially small**.

Thus the raw monomial Vandermonde basis is unacceptable.

This reinforces a previous conclusion: the determinant route must use an \(R^C\)-conditioned orthogonal/Riesz basis, not raw monomials.

In an \(R^C\)-normalized basis, separated nodes in an interval of length \(R^{-C}\) should have exterior evaluation volume at worst

\[
\exp(-O(R\log R))=Q^{-O(1)},
\]

not

\[
\exp(-O(R^2\log R)).
\]

So the sectorial branch route requires the following deterministic analytic input:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Riesz-normalized exterior evaluation volume on }R^{-C}\text{-scale subintervals is polynomially large.}
}
\]

Without this basis repair, phase alignment loses too much mass.

---

# 4. Phase of the product factors

The coefficient phase also includes

\[
\prod_{n\in S}a_n
\quad\text{and}\quad
\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n).
\]

## 4.1 The multiplier product

If \(m(x)\) itself has phase variation

\[
\operatorname{osc}_{I}\arg m(x)\le O(1/R),
\]

then

\[
\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)
\]

has sectorial phase for \(|S|=O(R)\).

If only

\[
\operatorname{osc}_{I}\arg m(x)\le O(1),
\]

then the product phase can vary by \(O(R)\), which destroys sectoriality.

So we need a smaller interval for \(m\)-phase control:

\[
|I|\lesssim \frac1{R}
\left\|\frac{m'}{m}\right\|^{-1}.
\]

If

\[
m'/m=O(1)
\]

on the ordinary interval, this means

\[
|I|\lesssim R^{-1}.
\]

This is still acceptable for polynomial mass if the Riesz-normalized basis is used.

## 4.2 The AFE coefficient product

The factor

\[
a_n
\]

contains arithmetic phases, including branch and \(m_0\)-dependent oscillations. In the determinant coefficient, however, the \(m_0\)-oscillation is separated as

\[
e^{-im_0\log N_S}.
\]

The remaining amplitude \(a_n\) may be slowly varying and real/positive in a suitably normalized branch, e.g. \(n^{-1/2}\) times smooth cutoff weights. If so, \(\prod a_n\) is sectorial.

But with plus/folded-dual mixtures, branch phases differ. Therefore phase alignment likely requires choosing a **single dominant branch** first.

Thus the route needs:

\[
\boxed{
\text{dominant-branch exterior mass: one branch contributes polynomially to }\mathcal A_m.
}
\]

If determinant mass is genuinely split among plus/dual branches with uncontrolled relative phase, sectoriality of a single branch may not certify the full determinant.

---

# 5. Collision noncancellation under sectorial coefficients

Assume on a chosen sectorial branch/subpacket,

\[
C_S\in e^{i\theta}\{z:\ |\arg z|\le \pi/6\}
\]

for all relevant \(S\). Then exact collision classes cannot cancel:

\[
\left|
\sum_{S:N_S=N} C_S
\right|
\ge
\cos(\pi/6)
\sum_{S:N_S=N}|C_S|.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_N
\left|
\sum_{S:N_S=N}C_S
\right|^2
\ge
c
\sum_N
\left(
\sum_{S:N_S=N}|C_S|
\right)^2
\ge
c\sum_S |C_S|^2.
\]

So exact multiplicative collisions are harmless under sectoriality.

This is a strong positive result.

Near-collisions are harder. If

\[
|\log(N_S/N_T)|\le T^{-1}Q^B,
\]

then a smooth zero-sampling kernel may not distinguish them. If their coefficients are sectorial, they still add rather than cancel inside each near-collision cluster, provided the sampling kernel is nonnegative or approximately positive over the cluster.

Thus the phase-aligned route can handle both exact and near collisions **if** the zero-sampling quadratic form is positive on the cluster scale.

That becomes a kernel-positivity condition:

\[
\boxed{
K_Z(\Omega_S-\Omega_T)
\text{ is nonnegative or sectorial for near-collision clusters.}
}
\]

This is plausible for smoothed positive weights but must be checked.

---

# 6. The main remaining obstruction: full determinant versus branch determinant

Even if we prove a sectorial subdeterminant \(D_{\rm good}\) has mass, the full determinant is

\[
D=D_{\rm good}+D_{\rm rest}.
\]

The O2/O3 pivot uses \(D\), not \(D_{\rm good}\). The rest can cancel the good part.

So the route needs one of the following.

## Option A — Dominant exterior branch

Prove

\[
\|D_{\rm rest}\|_{Z,2}
\le \frac12\|D_{\rm good}\|_{Z,2}.
\]

Then

\[
\|D\|_{Z,2}\ge \frac12\|D_{\rm good}\|_{Z,2}.
\]

This is strongest and cleanest, but may be false if plus/dual branches are comparable.

## Option B — Sector decomposition of the full determinant

Partition all determinant terms into \(M=Q^{O(1)}\) sectorial classes:

\[
D=\sum_{\alpha=1}^M D_\alpha.
\]

If one can prove almost orthogonality between sector classes in zero sampling, then

\[
\|D\|_{Z,2}^2
\gtrsim
Q^{-A}\sum_\alpha\|D_\alpha\|_{Z,2}^2.
\]

This avoids requiring a single dominant branch.

## Option C — Work with \(|D|^2\) directly

Instead of lower bounding a selected subdeterminant, expand the full

\[
|D|^2
\]

and prove the collision-aggregated coefficient matrix is positive semidefinite on the actual coefficient vector.

This is most intrinsic but hardest.

---

# 7. Implication for cofactor-pivot theorem

The pivot theorem needs

\[
|D|\not\ll |C_{ij}|.
\]

Lower bounding \(D_{\rm good}\) alone is insufficient unless we also know the cofactor \(C_{ij}\) is dominated by the same branch or at least comparable in the same sector decomposition.

Thus the branch-aligned theorem should be paired:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectorial determinant numerator and matching sectorial cofactor denominator.}
}
\]

On a pivot class, this means choosing \(C_{ij,\rm good}\) such that

\[
|C_{ij,\rm good}|\asymp |C_{ij}|
\]

and

\[
D_{\rm good}/C_{ij,\rm good}
\]

has sectorial non-smallness.

Without denominator matching, a large unrelated cofactor can make

\[
|D|/|C_{ij}|
\]

small.

This is a new refinement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{phase-aligned branch must dominate both the top determinant and the pivot cofactor.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Current best theorem from this route

A plausible subtheorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Sectorial matched exterior-pivot theorem.}
}
\]

For a polynomial-weight part of the Stack C2 measure \(d\nu\), there exists a sectorial branch/subpacket class \(\mathfrak B\) such that:

1. \(D_{\mathfrak B}\) and \(C_{ij,\mathfrak B}\) are formed from the same branch/subpacket exterior expansion.

2. Coefficients of both \(D_{\mathfrak B}\) and \(C_{ij,\mathfrak B}\) lie in fixed sectors.

3. Collision aggregation is noncancelling:

\[
\sum_{\text{clusters}}
\left|
\sum_{S\in\text{cluster}}C_S^{D,\mathfrak B}
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_S |C_S^{D,\mathfrak B}|^2,
\]

and similarly for the cofactor.

4. The branch dominates the full pivot:

\[
|D|\ge Q^{-A}|D_{\mathfrak B}|,
\qquad
|C_{ij}|\le Q^A|C_{ij,\mathfrak B}|
\]

on average or on a polynomial-weight subset.

5. The Schur ratio for the branch satisfies

\[
|D_{\mathfrak B}/C_{ij,\mathfrak B}|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on average.

Then weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough follows.

This is strong, but each condition is now concrete.

---

# 9. Final status of phase-aligned route

Positive findings:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Divided-difference phase is controllable by sectoriality of }(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Exact multiplicative collision cancellation disappears under sectorial coefficients.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Near-collision cancellation may be controlled if the smoothed zero kernel is positive on cluster scale.}
}
\]

Obstructions:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Raw monomial exterior volume loses }e^{-O(R^2\log R)};
\text{ need }R^C\text{-conditioned bases.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Single-branch determinant mass does not certify the full determinant unless it dominates or sectors are orthogonal.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Pivot denominator must be matched; numerator-only sectoriality is insufficient.}
}
\]

The best refined target is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{sectorial matched exterior-pivot theorem}
}
\]

rather than a bare determinant lower bound.

That is the current most concrete subattack on the weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough.

I’ll continue by deriving what the matching cofactor coefficients look like. The key question is whether the same sectorial branch that controls \(\det \mathcal A\) can also control the dominant cofactor denominator.

_Thought for 25s_

## Agent 3 continuation — matching the cofactor denominator

The previous endpoint was the **sectorial matched exterior-pivot theorem**. The key unresolved issue was denominator matching:

\[
\frac{D}{C_{ij}}
=
\frac{\det \mathcal A}{\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A)}.
\]

A phase-aligned subdeterminant for \(D\) is not enough. We also need the corresponding cofactor \(C_{ij}\) to be controlled by the same branch/subpacket, or else a large unrelated cofactor can make the pivot small.

I pushed the determinant/cofactor algebra. The result is useful:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The determinant numerator has top divided-difference content, while cofactors split into several different analytic types.}
}
\]

This means denominator matching is plausible only for certain cofactor classes. Other cofactor classes require separate analytic control.

---

# 1. Model augmented matrix

Use the ideal multiplicative model first. Let the augmented column functions be

\[
\phi_0(x)=1,
\qquad
\phi_{j+1}(x)=m(x)p_j(x),
\qquad 0\le j\le R,
\]

where

\[
m=m_{\can},
\qquad
p_j\in \mathbb C_R[x].
\]

Let the row test functions be

\[
\psi_k(x),\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
\]

chosen in an \(R^C\)-conditioned basis. In the raw monomial model,

\[
\psi_k(x)=x^k.
\]

The matrix entries are

\[
\mathcal A_{k j}
=
L_m(\phi_j\psi_k).
\]

For an atomic packet

\[
L_m(f)=\sum_r a_r e^{-im\omega_r} f(x_r),
\]

Cauchy–Binet gives

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{|S|=R+2}
\left(\prod_{r\in S}a_r e^{-im\omega_r}\right)
\det(\psi_k(x_r))_{k,r\in S}
\det(\phi_j(x_r))_{r,j\in S}.
\]

Set

\[
N=R+2,
\qquad
f(x):=\frac1{m(x)}.
\]

In the monomial row/column basis,

\[
\det(\phi_j(x_r))_{r,j\in S}
=
\left(\prod_{r\in S}m(x_r)\right)
\det\bigl(f(x_r),1,x_r,\ldots,x_r^R\bigr)_{r\in S}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\det(\phi_j(x_r))
=
\left(\prod_{r\in S}m(x_r)\right)
V_S\,[x_r:r\in S]f
\]

up to a harmless sign, where

\[
V_S=\prod_{r<s}(x_s-x_r).
\]

Thus the top determinant coefficient is

\[
\boxed{
C_S^{D}
=
\left(\prod_{r\in S}a_r\right)
V_S^{\rm row}V_S^{\rm col}
\left(\prod_{r\in S}m(x_r)\right)
[x_r:r\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
}
\]

In a matched orthogonal/Riesz basis, the same formula holds with the two Vandermonde factors replaced by \(R^C\)-conditioned exterior evaluation factors.

---

# 2. Cofactor expansion: three types

A cofactor removes one row and one column. So its Cauchy–Binet expansion is over subsets

\[
|T|=N-1=R+1.
\]

Write

\[
C_{ij}=\operatorname{Cof}_{ij}(\mathcal A).
\]

The analytic content depends on which column is removed.

---

## Type I: removing the constant column \(\phi_0=1\)

If the removed column is \(\phi_0\), the remaining columns are

\[
m(x)p_0(x),\ldots,m(x)p_R(x).
\]

Then

\[
\det(\phi_{\hat 0}(x_r))_{r\in T}
=
\left(\prod_{r\in T}m(x_r)\right)
\det(p_j(x_r))_{r\in T,\,0\le j\le R}.
\]

Since \(|T|=R+1\), this is just

\[
\boxed{
\left(\prod_{r\in T}m(x_r)\right)V_T
}
\]

up to basis normalization.

There is **no divided difference of \(1/m\)**.

So constant-column cofactors are analytically different from the determinant numerator. They are pure curvature-block minors.

Implication:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A phase-aligned theorem for }[S](1/m)
\text{ does not automatically match Type I cofactors.}
}
\]

These cofactors may be easier, but they need a separate product-\(m\) sectorial/mass argument.

---

## Type II: removing a curvature column \(m p_a\)

Now the remaining columns are

\[
1,
\qquad
m p_0,\ldots,\widehat{m p_a},\ldots,m p_R.
\]

Factoring \(m\) from the curvature columns gives

\[
\det(\phi_{\widehat{a+1}}(x_r))_{r\in T}
=
\left(\prod_{r\in T}m(x_r)\right)
\det\bigl(f(x_r),p_0(x_r),\ldots,\widehat{p_a(x_r)},\ldots,p_R(x_r)\bigr).
\]

In the monomial basis \(p_j=x^j\), this determinant has a clean interpolation meaning.

Let \(I_T f\) be the degree-\(R\) interpolation polynomial of \(f\) on the \(R+1\) nodes \(T\):

\[
I_T f(x)=\sum_{j=0}^R b_j(T)x^j.
\]

Then

\[
\det\bigl(f(x_r),1,x_r,\ldots,\widehat{x^a},\ldots,x^R\bigr)_{r\in T}
=
\pm b_a(T) V_T.
\]

Therefore a Type II cofactor coefficient is

\[
\boxed{
C_T^{(a)}
\sim
\left(\prod_{r\in T}a_r\right)
V_T^{\rm row}V_T^{\rm col}
\left(\prod_{r\in T}m(x_r)\right)
b_a(T),
}
\]

where \(b_a(T)\) is the \(x^a\)-coefficient of the interpolant of \(1/m\) at \(T\).

For \(a=R\),

\[
b_R(T)=[x_r:r\in T]f,
\]

the divided difference of order \(R\). This is directly parallel to the numerator, whose divided difference has order \(R+1\).

For \(a<R\), \(b_a(T)\) is a lower/intermixed interpolation coefficient, expressible as a linear combination of divided differences of orders \(a,\ldots,R\) times elementary symmetric functions of the nodes.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{only the top removed curvature column gives a clean adjacent divided difference.}
}
\]

Other curvature-column cofactors require sectoriality of interpolation coefficients, not merely sectoriality of \(f^{(R)}\).

---

## Type III: row removal effects

Removing a row test \(\psi_i\) changes the row exterior factor. In the monomial model, removing \(x^i\) gives

\[
\det(1,x,\ldots,\widehat{x^i},\ldots,x^{R+1})_{T}
=
\pm e_{R+1-i}(T)V_T,
\]

where \(e_k(T)\) is an elementary symmetric polynomial of the nodes.

On positive frequency intervals

\[
x\in[c_-,c_+]\subset(0,\infty),
\]

these elementary symmetric factors are positive. So row cofactors do not create phase problems in the monomial model.

In an orthogonal/Riesz basis, row cofactors may change sign/phase, but one can choose a local positive basis or absorb the row exterior phase into a sector partition. This is a deterministic basis-design issue.

Thus the real analytic phase problem is mostly in the **column cofactor**, i.e. in \(m\) and \(1/m\).

---

# 3. Matching numerator and denominator by cofactor class

The pivot

\[
D/C_{ij}
\]

has numerator coefficient type:

\[
\prod m \cdot [S](1/m)
\quad\text{on }|S|=R+2.
\]

The denominator coefficient type depends on the removed column.

## Case A: Type II top curvature cofactor

If the cofactor removes the top curvature column \(m x^R\), then denominator has

\[
\prod m \cdot [T](1/m)
\quad\text{on }|T|=R+1.
\]

This is the best matched case:

\[
\boxed{
\frac{\text{order }R+1\text{ divided difference of }1/m}
{\text{order }R\text{ divided difference of }1/m}.
}
\]

A sectorial high-derivative theorem for \(f=1/m\) can plausibly control both.

Using Hermite–Genocchi:

\[
[S]f
=
\int_{\Delta_{R+1}} f^{(R+1)}(\cdots),
\]

\[
[T]f
=
\int_{\Delta_{R}} f^{(R)}(\cdots).
\]

So this case requires sectoriality and non-smallness of both

\[
f^{(R+1)}
\quad\text{and}\quad
f^{(R)}
\]

on the same subinterval.

This is plausible if \(f\) is analytic and has no nearby zeros/poles causing wild phase rotation.

## Case B: Type I constant-column cofactor

The denominator has no divided difference:

\[
C_T\sim \prod_{r\in T}m(x_r)V_T^2.
\]

Then the pivot compares

\[
\prod_{S}m\,[S](1/m)
\quad\text{to}\quad
\prod_T m.
\]

A matched branch argument would need to show

\[
[S](1/m)
\]

is non-small relative to the missing \(m\)-factor and the scale difference between \(S\) and \(T\).

This is not impossible, but it is less symmetric.

## Case C: lower curvature cofactors

The denominator contains interpolation coefficients \(b_a(T)\). Sectoriality of \(b_a(T)\) is not guaranteed by sectoriality of one derivative order.

This case is messy. It may require a linear-algebra choice of pivot to avoid lower curvature cofactors.

---

# 4. Can we choose the pivot class?

The harmonic exterior partition chooses whichever cofactor is dominant. We do not get to choose it freely.

But we can refine the theorem:

\[
\sum_{i,j}|C_{ij}|^2
=
\sum_{\text{good cofactors}}|C_{ij}|^2
+
\sum_{\text{bad cofactors}}|C_{ij}|^2.
\]

If we can prove that good matched cofactors carry a polynomial fraction of the total cofactor mass,

\[
\sum_{\text{matched }(i,j)}|C_{ij}|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{i,j}|C_{ij}|^2,
\]

then it suffices to run the cofactor-pivot anti-trough only on matched cofactor classes.

This suggests a new deterministic exterior-basis design problem:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Matched cofactor mass lemma.}
}
\]

Choose row/column bases so that the cofactor mass is not concentrated in analytically bad cofactor classes.

If this fails, the denominator may be dominated by cofactors whose analytic coefficient phases are not controlled, and the phase-aligned pivot route breaks.

---

# 5. Basis design to force matched cofactors

A possible fix is to use a column basis adapted to \(f=1/m\).

Instead of columns

\[
1,\quad m p_0,\ldots,m p_R,
\]

choose curvature columns

\[
m q_0,\ldots,m q_R
\]

where the \(q_j\) basis is ordered so that all cofactor interpolation coefficients have controlled phase or energy.

For example, use a Newton basis centered in the sectorial interval:

\[
q_j(x)=\prod_{\ell<j}(x-\xi_\ell).
\]

Then removing the last curvature column corresponds to the top Newton divided difference. Removing earlier columns corresponds to lower Newton coefficients, which may be better localized than monomial coefficients.

Even better, one might choose the pivot adaptively via QR/Gram–Schmidt. Then the dominant cofactor is by construction tied to a stable orthogonal direction, but the analytic coefficient formula becomes less explicit.

This leads to a tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\text{explicit determinant coefficients vs. stable matched cofactors.}
}
\]

For proof, an \(R^C\)-conditioned Newton/Chebyshev basis on the phase-aligned interval may be optimal.

---

# 6. New refined theorem: matched exterior-pivot sector theorem

The phase-aligned route should be restated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Matched exterior-pivot sector theorem.}
}
\]

On a polynomial-weight subpacket/branch/sector, there is a family of cofactor classes \(\mathcal M\) such that:

1. **Cofactor mass match**

\[
\sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal M}|C_{ij}|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\sum_{i,j}|C_{ij}|^2.
\]

2. **Sectorial numerator coefficients**

The determinant coefficients \(C_S^D\) for the matched branch lie in a fixed sector and have polynomial exterior mass.

3. **Sectorial denominator coefficients**

For every \((i,j)\in\mathcal M\), the cofactor coefficients \(C_T^{ij}\) lie in compatible sectors and have polynomial exterior mass.

4. **Collision noncancellation**

Exact and near-collision cluster sums for both numerator and denominator do not cancel beyond \(Q^{-A}\).

5. **Pivot nontrough**

Under the zero-sampling weight \(d\nu\),

\[
\min(Q^A|D/C_{ij}|^2,1)
\]

has polynomial average on matched cofactor classes.

This theorem would imply weighted AggFrame\(_{23}\).

---

# 7. What can be proved now?

The following pieces look provable or at least deterministic:

## 7.1 Cofactor formula

The determinant/cofactor coefficient classifications above are exact in the monomial model and stable under \(R^C\)-conditioned basis change.

## 7.2 Sectorial divided differences

If

\[
f^{(k)}
\]

is sectorial on an interval, then the \(k\)-th divided differences are sectorial by Hermite–Genocchi.

## 7.3 Collision noncancellation under sectoriality

If coefficients in a collision cluster lie in a sector of width \(<\pi/2\), then cluster sums cannot cancel.

The hard pieces are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{matched cofactor mass;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectoriality of all relevant interpolation coefficients;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{dominance of sectorial branch in the full determinant/cofactor;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-sampling lower bound for the sectorial exterior packets.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Updated current endpoint

The phase-aligned determinant route is promising only after strengthening it from:

\[
\text{sectorial determinant numerator}
\]

to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectorial matched exterior pivot: numerator and cofactor denominator together.}
}
\]

The most important new obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{matched cofactor mass.}
}
\]

Without it, the dominant denominator cofactor may live in an analytically uncontrolled class, and numerator phase alignment is irrelevant.

Thus the next Agent 3/4 subattack should probably be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can we choose the augmented polynomial basis so that matched cofactor classes carry polynomial cofactor mass?}
}
\]

If yes, the phase-aligned exterior route remains plausible.

If no, the weighted exterior-pivot anti-trough must be treated as a new arithmetic theorem with no known coefficient-level proof route.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 03:33:52)

I’ll treat this as Agent 3’s new assignment: formulate the aggregate local \(L^2\) source theorem for \(q''_{\can}\), independent of whether Gate–2 ultimately accepts it.

## Agent 3 — branch-complete aggregate local \(L^2\) source theorem

The update changes my task materially: instead of treating ordinary, transition, and singular Gate–1 as different finite-row observables, the cleaner candidate is the scalar local source energy

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
=
\int_{I_b}
\left|
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2\,dx.
\]

I am assuming provisionally that Gate–2 accepts this local \(L^2\) source input; Agent 1’s audit decides whether that assumption is valid. My task is to formulate and attack the aggregate theorem that would supply this energy. 

---

# 1. Fubini reduction to a single weighted \(L^2\) theorem

For each branch \(b\), set

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
=
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x),
\]

so

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
=
\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2\,dx.
\]

Use a smooth nonnegative branch window \(w_b\) supported on \(I_b\). Then

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
=
\int
\left|
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2
w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

Changing variables

\[
t=m+\lambda_b x,
\qquad
dx=\lambda_b^{-1}dt,
\]

gives

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
=
\int
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2
\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right)
dt.
\]

Therefore the branch-complete aggregate theorem

\[
\sum_b\sum_{m\in \mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m)
\]

is equivalent to

\[
\boxed{
\int
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2
W_{\rm br}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int W_{\rm br}^{(0)}(t)\,dt,
}
\]

where

\[
W_{\rm br}(t)
=
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right),
\]

and

\[
\int W_{\rm br}^{(0)}(t)\,dt
\asymp
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m)
\]

after the chosen normalization of the \(w_b\)’s and \(\lambda_b\)’s.

More invariantly, define the branch reference weight

\[
\mathcal W(t):=
\sum_b\sum_m
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
\]

Then the theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2\mathcal W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\int \mathcal W_0(t)\,dt.
}
\]

This is the clean branch-complete \(L^2\)-Gate–1 input.

---

# 2. Expansion of \(q''_{\can}\)

The full canonical source is

\[
q''_{\can}
=
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The aggregate \(L^2\) energy is

\[
\int
|B_2(t)+P_2(t)+D_2(t)+E_2(t)|^2
\mathcal W(t)\,dt.
\]

This expands into:

\[
\int |P_2|^2\mathcal W
+
\int |D_2|^2\mathcal W
+
\int |B_2|^2\mathcal W
+
\int |E_2|^2\mathcal W
\]

plus all cross terms:

\[
2\Re\int P_2\overline{D_2}\mathcal W,
\quad
2\Re\int P_2\overline{B_2}\mathcal W,
\quad
\ldots
\]

For the prime-side part, write schematically

\[
P_2(t)
=
\sum_n a_n n^{-it}.
\]

Then

\[
\int |P_2(t)|^2\mathcal W(t)\,dt
=
\sum_{n,n'}
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\widehat{\mathcal W}\!\left(\log\frac{n}{n'}\right).
\]

So the core Gram matrix is

\[
\boxed{
G_{\mathcal W}(n,n')
=
\widehat{\mathcal W}\!\left(\log\frac{n}{n'}\right).
}
\]

The full canonical theorem needs the corresponding block Gram for

\[
(P_2,D_2,B_2,E_2),
\]

including cross-Grams. We should not replace this by a \(P_2\)-only proxy unless we prove that the other pieces are either perturbative or positive-compatible.

---

# 3. Comparison with the block-rank-one zero-Gram theorem

The earlier ordinary aggregate theorem sampled at zeros:

\[
\sum_{\gamma}
\rho(\gamma)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(\gamma)|^2.
\]

That creates a zero-Gram operator with a rank-one subtraction in the scalar AFE direction:

\[
G_Z\approx I-Q^{-1}e_Z\otimes e_Z.
\]

The local \(L^2\) theorem is different. It samples neighborhoods around routed centers, not just the center values. The weight \(\mathcal W(t)\) is a continuous smoothed comb:

\[
\mathcal W(t)
=
\sum_{m}\rho(m)\lambda^5 w((t-m)/\lambda).
\]

Its Fourier transform is

\[
\widehat{\mathcal W}(\xi)
=
\lambda^6 \widehat w(\lambda\xi)
\sum_m\rho(m)e^{-im\xi}
\]

in a fixed branch.

Thus the zero/routed-center sampling kernel is still present through

\[
\sum_m \rho(m)e^{-im\xi},
\]

but it is damped by the local-window factor

\[
\widehat w(\lambda\xi).
\]

This is important:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local }L^2\text{ averaging reduces sensitivity to pointwise zero constraints.}
}
\]

The scalar relation

\[
Z_\alpha(\gamma)=0
\]

kills a value at \(x=0\), but it does not kill

\[
\int_{I}
|\lambda^3q''_{\can}(\gamma+\lambda x)|^2\,dx.
\]

So the old scalar AFE null direction is not automatically a null direction for the local \(L^2\) source energy.

However, the centers \(m\) are still zeros/routed centers, so the weighted Gram still depends on zero correlations. The theorem is easier than pointwise finite-row Gate–1, but it is not a generic continuous mean-value theorem unless \(\mathcal W\) has sufficiently regular coverage.

---

# 4. Conditions on the branch weight \(\mathcal W\)

A sufficient analytic condition is a weighted lower frame for the actual canonical packet:

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2\mathcal W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{\mathcal P}^2
}
\]

for the relevant local packet norm \(\|\cdot\|_{\mathcal P}\), plus a lower bound

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{\mathcal P}^2\ge Q^{-A}\int \mathcal W_0.
\]

In Dirichlet-polynomial terms, for the prime-side piece, the desired lower frame is:

\[
\sum_{n,n'}
a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\widehat{\mathcal W}\!\left(\log\frac{n}{n'}\right)
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_n |a_n|^2,
\]

for the **actual coefficients** \(a_n\), not for arbitrary adversarial packets.

A universal lower frame for all coefficients is still too strong if \(\mathcal W\) has gaps. But actual-coefficient lower sampling may be plausible because \(q''_{\can}\) is fixed by the zeta explicit formula and cannot be adversarially localized in the gaps unless zeta arithmetic forces it.

Thus the correct theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual-coefficient weighted lower Gram theorem for }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Does branch-complete coverage remove gaps?

Let

\[
\mathcal C
=
\mathcal C_{\ord}
\cup
\mathcal C_{\tr}
\cup
\mathcal C_{\sing}.
\]

If ordinary centers leave gaps, the branch-complete formulation can still work if transition and singular windows cover the missing mass. The coverage condition should be stated at the level of \(\mathcal W\):

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal W_{\ord}(t)+\mathcal W_{\tr}(t)+\mathcal W_{\sing}(t)
\text{ has no macroscopic holes on the relevant support.}
}
\]

A concrete version:

There exists \(A>0\) such that for every subinterval \(J\) of the relevant height window with

\[
|J|\ge Q^{-C}\lambda_{\min},
\]

one has

\[
\int_J \mathcal W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\frac{|J|}{|I_{\rm total}|}
\int \mathcal W(t)\,dt.
\]

Or, spectrally, the Gram kernel

\[
\widehat{\mathcal W}(\log(n/n'))
\]

should not admit a large low-frequency nullspace on the actual canonical coefficient vector.

Branch-complete coverage is therefore not a formal union statement; it is a **weighted coverage theorem**.

---

# 6. Role of \(B_2,D_2,E_2\)

The theorem must include the full canonical source:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

There are three possible ways to handle non-prime pieces.

## Option A — full-source positivity

Prove directly:

\[
\int |B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2|^2\mathcal W
\ge Q^{-A}\int \mathcal W_0.
\]

This is cleanest and avoids proxy errors.

## Option B — main-packet dominance

Prove

\[
\int |P_2+D_2|^2\mathcal W
\ge Q^{-A}\int\mathcal W_0,
\]

and

\[
\int |B_2+E_2|^2\mathcal W
+
2\Re\int (P_2+D_2)\overline{(B_2+E_2)}\mathcal W
\]

is not canceling more than half the main term.

This requires a robust cross-term estimate.

## Option C — canonical decomposition into orthogonal blocks

If the branch windows or frequency supports make

\[
P_2,\ D_2,\ B_2,\ E_2
\]

nearly orthogonal under \(\mathcal W\), then the full energy is the sum of block energies up to perturbative errors.

This is attractive but must be proved.

The safest theorem statement is full-source:

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}|^2\mathcal W\ge Q^{-A}\int\mathcal W_0.
}
\]

Use decompositions only in the proof.

---

# 7. Does scalar zero rank-one obstruction survive?

For pointwise sampling at zeros,

\[
Z_\alpha(\gamma)=0
\]

creates a scalar null relation. For local \(L^2\) sampling,

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(\gamma+\lambda x)|^2dx
\]

is insensitive to a single value cancellation at \(x=0\).

Thus the scalar rank-one obstruction is much weaker. In the local Taylor model,

\[
q''_{\can}(\gamma+\lambda x)
=
q''_{\can}(\gamma)
+
\lambda x q'''_{\can}(\gamma)
+
\cdots.
\]

A scalar zero of \(Z_\alpha\) does not force all these curvature derivatives to vanish.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local }L^2\text{ Gate--1 avoids the main scalar zero null direction.}
}
\]

But it may introduce a different finite-dimensional null problem: if the source is very flat on the whole local interval, then the energy is small. That is a genuine local source-flatness problem, not a sampling-at-zero artifact.

---

# 8. Candidate theorem

The clean theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete aggregate local }L^2\textbf{ source theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
F_{b,m}(x)=\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)
\]

and

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)=\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2\mathcal W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\int \mathcal W_0(t)\,dt.
}
\]

Hypotheses:

1. The branch windows \(w_b\) are smooth, nonnegative, and normalized.

2. The combined branch weight \(\mathcal W\) has no macroscopic holes on the canonical source support.

3. The full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

is used.

4. Cross-terms among canonical pieces are controlled, or the theorem is proved directly for the full source.

5. The ordinary/transition/singular atlas covers all routed packets with polynomial overlap.

---

# 9. Current proof route

The best proof route is:

## Step 1: Fubini to \(\mathcal W\)

Reduce branch aggregate energy to

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2\mathcal W.
\]

## Step 2: Expand canonical Gram

Write the full canonical Gram matrix in terms of

\[
\widehat{\mathcal W}(\log(n/n')).
\]

## Step 3: Use actual-coefficient structure

Do not attempt a universal lower frame. Prove lower energy for the actual \(q''_{\can}\)-coefficients.

## Step 4: Prove branch coverage

Show \(\mathcal W\) cannot miss the actual source energy because ordinary gaps are covered by transition/singular windows.

## Step 5: Control canonical completion

Handle \(B_2,D_2,E_2\) inside the full \(L^2\) energy, not as diagnostics.

---

# 10. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{The aggregate local }L^2\text{ Gate--1 theorem is coherent and cleaner than finite-row Gate--1.}
}
\]

It avoids:

\[
\text{ordinary pointwise projection defects},
\]

\[
\text{transition finite moment-null traps},
\]

\[
\text{singular diagnostic sine-moment ambiguity}.
\]

The exact remaining arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual-coefficient weighted lower Gram bound for }q''_{\can}
\textbf{ under the branch-complete local }L^2\textbf{ weight }\mathcal W.
}
\]

If Gate–2 accepts this local \(L^2\) input, this should replace the older branch-specific Gate–1 definitions.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 03:35:39)

I’ll push the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route one level deeper: the key issue is normalization. A lower bound against \(\sum\rho\) is only meaningful if \(\lambda^3 q''_{\can}\) has order-one reference energy on the routed scale.

_Thought for 21s_

## Agent 3 continuation — normalization and coverage for \(L^2\)-Gate–1

The local \(L^2\) Gate–1 candidate is

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
=
\int_{I_b}
\left|
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

This is cleaner than finite-row Gate–1, but it has a hidden normalization issue:

\[
\boxed{
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\text{ is meaningful only after proving that }
\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}
\text{ has polynomial reference energy on the branch scale.}
}
\]

So the aggregate theorem should not first be stated against \(\sum\rho_b(m)\). It should be stated against a **branch reference energy** \(N_b(m)\), and only later, if \(N_b(m)\asymp 1\), reduced to \(\sum\rho_b(m)\).

---

# 1. Correct normalized theorem

Define

\[
F_{b,m}(x):=\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x),
\]

and

\[
E_b(m):=\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

The right normalized theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m)N_b(m),
}
\]

where \(N_b(m)\) is the local diagonal/reference energy for the same canonical source and branch scale.

A natural definition is

\[
N_b(m)
:=
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal P_b}
|\lambda_b^3 a_\nu^{\can}(m)|^2
\int_{I_b}w_b(x)\,dx,
\]

where

\[
q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)
\sim
\sum_{\nu\in\mathcal P_b}a_\nu^{\can}(m)
e^{-i\lambda_b x\omega_\nu}
\]

is the branch packet expansion, including the full

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

canonical contribution.

If one can prove

\[
N_b(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on the routed branch set, then the theorem implies the simpler form

\[
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m).
\]

But \(N_b\) should be kept explicit until that lower bound is verified.

---

# 2. Fubini form with the correct normalization

As before, changing variables

\[
t=m+\lambda_bx
\]

gives

\[
E_b(m)
=
\lambda_b^5
\int
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right)dt.
\]

Thus

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
=
\int
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2
W(t)\,dt,
\]

with

\[
W(t)
=
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
\]

The normalized aggregate theorem becomes

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
}
\]

This is the cleanest form.

It separates two questions:

1. **Sampling / coverage:** does the branch weight \(W(t)\) see the actual source energy?
2. **Reference size:** is \(\sum\rho_bN_b\) polynomially large?

---

# 3. If the branch windows cover the zero scale, the theorem becomes plausible

For ordinary packets one expects

\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,
\qquad
\lambda\asymp Q^{-1}.
\]

The mean zero spacing at height \(T\sim e^Q\) is also

\[
\asymp Q^{-1}.
\]

So if \(\mathcal C_{\ord}\) contains essentially all routed ordinary zeros and \(w_b\) has moderate overlap, then

\[
W_{\ord}(t)
\]

should behave like a roughly continuous weight on the ordinary region:

\[
W_{\ord}(t)\approx \lambda^5 Q
\]

up to polynomial factors.

In that case, the local \(L^2\) theorem no longer suffers from the pointwise scalar zero obstruction. A zero at \(m\) kills a scalar value, but the window

\[
m+\lambda x,\qquad |x|=O(1),
\]

samples a whole zero-spacing neighborhood.

This is the main reason the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route is better than pointwise finite-row Gate–1.

---

# 4. But if the routed centers have holes, the theorem can fail

The aggregate theorem can fail if \(W(t)\) has macroscopic holes where \(q''_{\can}\) concentrates.

This is the \(L^2\)-analogue of the earlier lower-frame gap obstruction.

A universal theorem

\[
\int |P(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge Q^{-A}\int |P(t)|^2dt
\]

for all bandlimited \(P\) is false if \(W\) has holes larger than the reciprocal bandwidth. A bandlimited packet can concentrate in the hole.

For the actual canonical source, this becomes the theorem:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\text{ does not concentrate in branch-coverage holes.}
}
\]

Branch-completeness should be designed to avoid needing such a counter-random assertion. The correct coverage theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\ord}(t)+W_{\tr}(t)+W_{\sing}(t)
\text{ has no branch-scale holes on the support where }q''_{\can}
\text{ has energy.}
}
\]

If that holds, the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 sampling theorem becomes much more plausible.

---

# 5. Gram form for the prime packet

For a prime-side packet

\[
P_2(t)=\sum_n a_n n^{-it},
\]

the weighted \(L^2\) energy is

\[
\int |P_2(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{a_{n'}}
\widehat W\!\left(\log\frac{n}{n'}\right).
\]

With local branch windows,

\[
\widehat W(\xi)
=
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^6
e^{-im\xi}
\widehat w_b(\lambda_b\xi).
\]

Thus correlations with

\[
|\xi|\gg \lambda_b^{-1}
\]

are damped.

For ordinary scale,

\[
\lambda_b^{-1}\asymp Q.
\]

So the local \(L^2\) window suppresses frequency gaps larger than \(Q\), but it still sees the full ordinary packet width. This is the correct uncertainty balance.

The lower Gram theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{n,n'}a_n^{\can}\overline{a_{n'}^{\can}}
\widehat W\!\left(\log\frac{n}{n'}\right)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_n |a_n^{\can}|^2
}
\]

for the actual canonical coefficients and all branch pieces together.

---

# 6. Full canonical source: avoid proxy cancellation

The \(L^2\) theorem must be for

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

It is not enough to show

\[
\int |P_2|^2W\ge Q^{-A}
\]

unless one proves the other terms cannot cancel \(P_2\).

The safest formulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\int |B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2|^2W
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_bN_b.
}
\]

If the proof uses prime-side dominance, it needs a half-leading inequality:

\[
\int |P_2+D_2|^2W
\ge Q^{-A}N
\]

and

\[
\left|
\int
(P_2+D_2)\overline{(B_2+E_2)}W
\right|
+
\int |B_2+E_2|^2W
\le
\frac12
\int |P_2+D_2|^2W
\]

or another noncancellation substitute.

Without such a completion estimate, the theorem is only a proxy.

---

# 7. Relationship with the block-rank-one zero-Gram law

The previous block-rank-one law was a point-sampling statement at zeros. The new local \(L^2\) theorem is a thickened sampling statement.

If the branch centers are zeros, then \(W\) still inherits zero statistics, but the local thickening weakens the exact scalar zero constraint.

The block-rank-one null direction

\[
I-Q^{-1}e\otimes e
\]

should be replaced by a **thickened zero-Gram operator**:

\[
G_{\lambda}
=
\int
w(x)
K_Z(t+\lambda x,t+\lambda y)
w(y)\,dx\,dy.
\]

The scalar value null at \(x=0\) is averaged over \(x,y\). Unless the source is flat over the whole local window, the rank-one subtraction no longer kills the energy.

So the natural theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{thickened zero-Gram has no large nullspace on actual canonical source packets.}
}
\]

This is stronger than pointwise zero-Gram but easier than arbitrary lower sampling.

---

# 8. Candidate proof route

The best route is now:

## Step 1 — Branch coverage

Prove the combined branch weight

\[
W(t)=W_{\ord}+W_{\tr}+W_{\sing}
\]

has polynomial lower density on the active support.

## Step 2 — Continuous canonical mean square

Prove

\[
\int_{\text{active}}
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\]

is comparable to the continuous local mean square of \(q''_{\can}\).

## Step 3 — Explicit formula mean square

Use the AFE/explicit formula to lower-bound the continuous mean square of \(q''_{\can}\) by the canonical diagonal/reference energy \(N\).

## Step 4 — Completion control

Show \(B_2,D_2,E_2\) are included correctly and cannot erase the prime/dual source energy.

This proof avoids the fragile pointwise zero projection theorem entirely.

---

# 9. Potential obstruction: \(q''_{\can}\) could be genuinely small

If

\[
q''_{\can}
\]

is small over the entire routed branch coverage, then the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem fails.

This is not a sampling artifact. It would mean the canonical curvature source itself has low energy.

So one should state the theorem as an actual source-energy lower bound, not as a consequence of sampling alone:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{canonical source nonflatness on routed coverage.}
}
\]

This is still an arithmetic theorem, but it is clearer and more intrinsic than finite-row projection avoidance.

---

# 10. Current refined theorem

The best current statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete aggregate canonical }L^2\textbf{ source nonflatness.}
}
\]

Let

\[
W(t)
=
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\mathcal N_{\can}(W),
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
\]

is the canonical diagonal/reference energy of the full source under \(W\).

If one proves

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m),
\]

then this becomes the desired aggregate Gate–1 theorem.

---

# 11. Final status

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route is coherent and materially cleaner.

It changes the remaining input from:

\[
\text{finite-row projection avoidance}
\]

to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-complete local }L^2\text{ nonflatness of }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

The exact theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W
\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W),
\qquad
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

This is the aggregate source theorem to use if Gate–2 accepts local \(L^2\) source energy.

## Agent 3 continuation — coercivity conditions for aggregate \(L^2\)-Gate–1

The current candidate Gate–1 is the local scalar source energy

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
=
\int_{I_b}
\left|
\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

The branch-complete aggregate theorem is

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W),
\]

with

\[
W(t)
=
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
\]

The previous pass isolated the normalization issue. Now I pushed the proof route: the theorem reduces to **weighted coercivity of the full canonical AFE packet**, not merely lower sampling of one Dirichlet polynomial. This is the exact condition needed if Gate–2 accepts local \(L^2\) source energy, as flagged in the coordinator update. 

---

# 1. Canonical packet Hilbert-space form

Write the full source locally as

\[
q''_{\can}(t)
=
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal I}
c_\alpha \, e^{-it\omega_\alpha}
+
B_2(t)+E_{\rm sm}(t),
\]

where \(\alpha\) ranges over all oscillatory canonical atoms, including plus and folded-dual atoms. The smooth/background part is

\[
B_2(t)+E_{\rm sm}(t),
\]

where \(E_{\rm sm}\) includes regularized tail/completion pieces not naturally represented as the same oscillatory packet.

The weighted energy is

\[
\mathcal E_W
:=
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W(t)\,dt.
\]

Ignoring the smooth part momentarily, the oscillatory Gram is

\[
\mathcal E_W^{\osc}
=
\sum_{\alpha,\beta}
c_\alpha \overline{c_\beta}
\widehat W(\omega_\alpha-\omega_\beta).
\]

Define

\[
G_W(\alpha,\beta)
=
\widehat W(\omega_\alpha-\omega_\beta).
\]

Then the desired coercivity is

\[
\boxed{
c^*G_Wc
\ge
Q^{-A}\|c\|_{\diag}^2
}
\]

for the **actual canonical coefficient vector** \(c=c_{\can}\), or for a controlled class of canonical coefficient vectors.

This is not a universal lower frame unless \(W\) has no holes at the full bandwidth. The correct theorem remains actual-coefficient.

---

# 2. Diagonal reference energy

The natural diagonal/reference energy is

\[
\mathcal N_{\osc}(W)
:=
\widehat W(0)\sum_\alpha |c_\alpha|^2.
\]

For branch-local windows,

\[
\widehat W(0)
=
\int W(t)\,dt
=
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^6
\int w_b(x)\,dx
\]

depending on the normalization. Equivalently, the branch-specific normalization can absorb this so that

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
\asymp
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
\]

The clean theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal E_W
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

A further reduction to

\[
\mathcal E_W
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)
\]

requires the separate reference lower bound

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

So there are two distinct inputs:

\[
\text{coercivity: }\mathcal E_W\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W),
\]

\[
\text{normalization: }\mathcal N_{\can}(W)\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
\]

---

# 3. Diagonal dominance is sufficient but not necessary

A sufficient condition is

\[
\sum_{\alpha\ne\beta}
|c_\alpha||c_\beta|
|\widehat W(\omega_\alpha-\omega_\beta)|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})\widehat W(0)\sum_\alpha |c_\alpha|^2.
\]

This is a weighted Bessel/off-diagonal condition. It is too strong in general, because near frequencies and branch symmetries may produce large off-diagonals. But it is useful as a diagnostic.

For the local branch windows,

\[
\widehat W(\xi)
=
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^6
e^{-im\xi}
\widehat w_b(\lambda_b\xi).
\]

The factor

\[
\widehat w_b(\lambda_b\xi)
\]

cuts off

\[
|\xi|\gg \lambda_b^{-1}.
\]

On ordinary scales,

\[
\lambda_b^{-1}\asymp Q.
\]

Thus local \(L^2\) windows do not diagonalize frequencies inside the main ordinary band; they merely suppress far-separated frequencies. Coercivity must still use actual coefficient structure and zero/routed-center distribution.

So diagonal dominance is not the likely proof, but it identifies the off-diagonal object to control:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\alpha,\beta}
c_\alpha\overline{c_\beta}
\sum_{b,m}
\rho_b(m)e^{-im(\omega_\alpha-\omega_\beta)}
\widehat w_b(\lambda_b(\omega_\alpha-\omega_\beta)).
}
\]

---

# 4. Completion cancellation condition

Now include the smooth/completion part. Write

\[
q''_{\can}=U+R,
\]

where \(U\) is the main oscillatory AFE packet, and

\[
R=B_2+E_{\rm tail/completion}
\]

or any residual group not handled in \(U\).

Then

\[
\mathcal E_W
=
\|U\|_W^2
+
2\Re\langle U,R\rangle_W
+
\|R\|_W^2.
\]

A robust sufficient condition is the half-leading estimate

\[
\boxed{
\|R\|_W
\le
\frac12\|U\|_W.
}
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal E_W
=
\|U+R\|_W^2
\ge
(\|U\|_W-\|R\|_W)^2
\ge
\frac14\|U\|_W^2.
\]

But this may be too strong if \(B_2\) is not small. A safer theorem is to include \(B_2\) and all completion terms in the main vector \(c_{\can}\), enlarging the canonical Hilbert space so the lower bound is for the actual full source.

Thus the cleanest route is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{do not split }q''_{\can}\text{ unless the residual is half-leading.}
}
\]

State the aggregate \(L^2\) theorem directly for

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

---

# 5. Ordinary/transition/singular scale compatibility

The same \(L^2\) energy definition unifies the branches, but the scaling matters.

For branch \(b\),

\[
F_{b,m}(x)=\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx).
\]

Then

\[
E_b(m)
=
\lambda_b^6\int |q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)|^2w_b(x)\,dx
=
\lambda_b^5\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right)dt.
\]

Thus smaller \(\lambda_b\) weights the physical \(t\)-integral differently.

The reference energy \(N_b(m)\) must use the same scaling:

\[
N_b(m)
=
\lambda_b^6
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal P_b}
|c_\alpha(m)|^2
\int w_b(x)\,dx,
\]

or whatever the local packet diagonal gives after the \(x\)-normalization.

Otherwise branch energies cannot be compared.

So the branch-complete theorem should be stated as

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bE_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bN_b(m),
}
\]

not with a branch-independent right side until all \(N_b\)’s are normalized.

---

# 6. Coverage theorem in operator form

Let

\[
\mathcal H_{\can}
\]

be the finite-dimensional canonical packet space in the active window. The branch weight \(W\) defines a positive operator

\[
\mathsf G_W:\mathcal H_{\can}\to\mathcal H_{\can}
\]

by

\[
\langle f,\mathsf G_Wf\rangle
=
\int |f(t)|^2W(t)\,dt.
\]

Let \(\mathsf D_W\) be the diagonal/reference operator defining

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W)
=
\langle q''_{\can},\mathsf D_W q''_{\can}\rangle.
\]

Then the normalized \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\langle q''_{\can},\mathsf G_W q''_{\can}\rangle
\ge
Q^{-A}
\langle q''_{\can},\mathsf D_W q''_{\can}\rangle.
}
\]

This is the precise actual-coefficient lower Gram theorem.

A universal theorem would require

\[
\mathsf G_W\ge Q^{-A}\mathsf D_W
\]

as operators on all of \(\mathcal H_{\can}\). That is too strong if \(W\) has holes.

The actual-coefficient version only requires the inequality on the single vector \(q''_{\can}\), or on a small canonical family generated by admissible branches/gauges.

So the exact missing arithmetic input is:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\notin\text{ the low-eigenvalue subspace of }\mathsf G_W
\text{ relative to }\mathsf D_W.
}
\]

This is much weaker than universal lower sampling.

---

# 7. Potential proof using continuous coverage

If the combined branch weight satisfies

\[
W(t)\ge Q^{-A}W_{\rm ref}(t)
\]

pointwise on the active source support, then

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\rm ref}.
\]

Then coercivity is immediate.

So one strong sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch windows physically cover the active source support pointwise.}
}
\]

This is stronger than necessary but may be plausible because every routed center contributes a local interval of size comparable to the zero spacing.

If physical coverage fails, one needs actual-coefficient nonconcentration in the holes.

Thus the branch coverage audit should ask:

\[
\boxed{
W(t)\ge Q^{-A}W_{\rm ref}(t)\ \text{on the active region?}
}
\]

If yes, the aggregate \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem is essentially analytic, not arithmetic.

If no, the missing theorem is nonconcentration of \(q''_{\can}\) in uncovered regions.

---

# 8. Relationship with zeros

A potential concern: if centers \(m\) are zeros of \(Z_\alpha\), the weight \(W\) is concentrated around zeros. Could \(q''_{\can}\) avoid zeros?

Since \(q''_{\can}\) is not \(Z_\alpha\), there is no formal reason it should vanish at zeros. But it could, in principle, be small in neighborhoods of zeros if the zeta dynamics force it.

The \(L^2\) theorem is therefore a curvature/source nonflatness assertion:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\text{ has polynomial local }L^2\text{ energy around routed zeros.}
}
\]

This is natural. It is much more plausible than pointwise projection at each zero because even if \(q''_{\can}(m)=0\), its derivative may give local energy.

---

# 9. Sharp theorem stack for \(L^2\)-Gate–1

The refined theorem stack is:

## Theorem A — Branch coverage

\[
W(t)\ge Q^{-A}W_{\rm ref}(t)
\]

on the active support, or an actual-coefficient substitute.

## Theorem B — Canonical reference nonflatness

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\rm ref}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_bN_b(m).
\]

## Theorem C — Completion coherence

The full source

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

is used directly, or any decomposition residual is half-leading.

Together they imply

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bE_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bN_b(m).
\]

If

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bN_b(m)\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b,
\]

then this gives the desired branch-complete aggregate Gate–1.

---

# 10. Current status

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route remains strong, but the theorem should be normalized as:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bE_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_bN_b(m),
}
\]

not directly against \(\sum\rho_b\).

The exact remaining inputs are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch coverage or actual-source nonconcentration in coverage holes;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{canonical reference energy lower bound }N_b\ge Q^{-A};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{full-source completion coherence for }B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
}
\]

This is the precise aggregate local \(L^2\) Gate–1 theorem to use if Gate–2 accepts local \(L^2\) source energy.

## Agent 3 — branch-complete weighted \(L^2\) source theorem

The updated instruction changes the Gate–1 object. Since Gate–2 now consumes scalar \(L^2\) energy of

\[
u=q''_{\can},
\]

the branch Gate–1 layer should be formulated as local \(L^2\) source energy, not as finite-row ordinary observables, transition jets, or singular sine/projection diagnostics. The active target is therefore the aggregate theorem for

\[
E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
=
\int_{I_b}
\left|
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x)
\right|^2dx,
\]

using the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

This matches the coordinator’s updated stack: \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})+\mathrm{WeightedExteriorPivot}_{23}^{\Lambda}+\mathrm{ForbiddenD4}^{\Lambda}\). 

---

# 1. Correct normalized aggregate theorem

For each branch \(b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}\), define

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
:=
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx),
\]

and use a smooth nonnegative local window \(w_b\) on \(I_b\). Set

\[
E_b(m)
:=
\int |F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

The theorem should **not** first be stated as

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m),
\]

because the branch scales \(\lambda_b\) change the normalization. The correct normalized form is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
}
\]

Here \(N_b(m)\) is the branch-scale canonical reference energy for the same source. Only after proving

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m)N_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m)
}
\]

can we compare directly with the total branch weight.

So the Gate–1 theorem splits into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{coercivity: }\sum\rho_b E_b\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho_bN_b,
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{reference nontriviality: }\sum\rho_bN_b\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho_b.
}
\]

These are different inputs.

---

# 2. Fubini form

Changing variables

\[
t=m+\lambda_bx,
\qquad
dx=\lambda_b^{-1}dt,
\]

gives

\[
E_b(m)
=
\lambda_b^5
\int
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right)dt.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
=
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2 W_{\branch}(t)\,dt,
\]

where

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
}
\]

The aggregate theorem becomes

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

Here

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})
:=
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m)N_b(m)
\]

is the canonical reference energy induced by the same branch windows.

This is the clean final interface.

---

# 3. What \(N_b(m)\) should be

Locally, write the branch packet expansion schematically as

\[
q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)
=
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal P_b(m)}
c_{\alpha}^{\can}(m)
e^{-i\lambda_bx\omega_\alpha}
+
\text{smooth/canonical correction terms}.
\]

Then the diagonal branch reference should be

\[
N_b(m)
\asymp
\lambda_b^6
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal P_b(m)}
|c_\alpha^{\can}(m)|^2
\int w_b(x)\,dx
\]

plus the corresponding diagonal contribution of deterministic canonical pieces if they are not represented as oscillatory atoms.

But this definition is only safe if the expansion represents the **full**

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
N_b(m)\text{ must be the full canonical diagonal/reference energy, not the }P_2\text{-only diagonal.}
}
\]

Proxy definitions risk missing cancellation from \(D_2\), \(B_2\), or \(E_2\).

---

# 4. Gram form

For an oscillatory component

\[
U(t)=\sum_\alpha c_\alpha e^{-it\omega_\alpha},
\]

one has

\[
\int |U(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
=
\sum_{\alpha,\beta}
c_\alpha\overline{c_\beta}
\widehat W_{\branch}(\omega_\alpha-\omega_\beta).
\]

For one branch,

\[
\widehat W_b(\xi)
=
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)
\lambda_b^6
e^{-im\xi}
\widehat w_b(\lambda_b\xi).
\]

Thus the branch windows damp frequency differences with

\[
|\xi|\gg \lambda_b^{-1}.
\]

The core coercivity theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
c_{\can}^*G_{W_{\branch}}c_{\can}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\langle c_{\can},D_{\can}c_{\can}\rangle,
}
\]

where \(c_{\can}\) is the actual canonical coefficient vector and \(D_{\can}\) is the full branch diagonal/reference form.

This is an **actual-coefficient lower Gram theorem**. A universal lower frame for all coefficient vectors is still too strong if \(W_{\branch}\) has holes.

---

# 5. Completion coherence

If we split

\[
q''_{\can}=U+R,
\]

where \(U\) is a main oscillatory piece and \(R\) is background/tail/completion, then

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W
=
\|U\|_W^2
+
2\Re\langle U,R\rangle_W
+
\|R\|_W^2.
\]

A sufficient but strong condition is

\[
\boxed{
\|R\|_W\le \frac12\|U\|_W.
}
\]

Then

\[
\|U+R\|_W^2\ge \frac14\|U\|_W^2.
\]

But this may be inappropriate if \(B_2\) or \(D_2\) is a main term, not a perturbation.

So the better theorem is full-source:

\[
\boxed{
\int |B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2|^2W_{\branch}
\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

Any proof using a partial source must include a half-leading or orthogonality/coherence estimate for the omitted pieces.

---

# 6. Branch coverage

The aggregate theorem is easiest if

\[
W_{\branch}(t)
\]

physically covers the active support of \(q''_{\can}\). A strong sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}(t)\ge Q^{-A}W_{\ref}(t)
}
\]

on the active height region, where \(W_{\ref}\) is a smooth reference measure for the canonical source.

Then

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\branch}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\ref}.
\]

If such physical coverage fails, the missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\text{ does not concentrate in the holes of }W_{\branch}.
}
\]

That is an actual-source nonconcentration input.

So the branch-complete theorem has two possible proof routes:

## Route A — physical coverage

Prove \(W_{\branch}\) has polynomial lower density on the active source support.

## Route B — actual-source nonconcentration

Allow holes, but prove \(q''_{\can}\) cannot concentrate in them.

Route A is preferable and should be checked first.

---

# 7. Does local \(L^2\) avoid the scalar zero obstruction?

Yes, largely.

Pointwise sampling at zeros is affected by

\[
Z_\alpha(m)=0.
\]

But local source energy samples

\[
q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\]

on a whole branch-scale interval. A scalar value cancellation at \(x=0\) does not force

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2dx
\]

to be small.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
L^2\text{-Gate--1 avoids the old pointwise scalar projection obstruction.}
}
\]

The new failure mode is genuine source flatness:

\[
q''_{\can}\approx0
\]

throughout branch windows.

That is the right arithmetic obstruction.

---

# 8. Branch-specific notes

## Ordinary branch

For ordinary packets,

\[
\lambda Q\asymp1,
\]

so the local window length is comparable to zero spacing. This is exactly the scale where local \(L^2\) energy is meaningful.

The previous smoothed block-rank-one zero-Gram theorem becomes a proof tool for ordinary \(L^2\)-coverage, not the definition of Gate–1.

## Transition branch

The old transition jet

\[
\mathcal J_R^{\tr}
\]

is no longer the definition. It can be used to prove local \(L^2\) energy if convenient, but finite moment nullspaces are no longer a theorem-level obstruction.

## Singular branch

Similarly, raw sine moments or projected low modes are no longer the definition. A projected energy

\[
\|P_RF_m\|_{L^2}^2
\]

is only a proof tool. The source energy is

\[
\|F_m\|_{L^2}^2.
\]

This eliminates the raw coefficient/tail ambiguity.

---

# 9. Candidate theorem stack

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 stack should be:

## Theorem 1 — branch \(L^2\) coverage

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\ref}(t)\,dt.
\]

## Theorem 2 — canonical source nonflatness

\[
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\ref}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
\]

## Theorem 3 — reference nontriviality

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m).
\]

Together:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_{1,b}^{L^2}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

---

# 10. First nontrivial missing theorem

The first serious missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual-coefficient weighted lower Gram bound for the full canonical source }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

In operator form:

\[
\boxed{
\langle q''_{\can},G_{W_{\branch}}q''_{\can}\rangle
\ge
Q^{-A}
\langle q''_{\can},D_{\can}q''_{\can}\rangle.
}
\]

This is weaker than a universal frame inequality because it is only required for the actual source vector.

The second missing theorem is reference nontriviality:

\[
\boxed{
\langle q''_{\can},D_{\can}q''_{\can}\rangle
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho_b.
}
\]

These two replace the old finite-row projection theorem.

---

# Final verdict

The local \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route is coherent and should replace finite-row Gate–1 **if Gate–2 indeed accepts local \(L^2\) source energy**.

The exact theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\branch}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}),
\qquad
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This uses the full

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The remaining issue is not branch transfer; it is actual-source weighted \(L^2\) nonflatness.
