## Agent 3 continuation — \(L^2\)-Gate–1 via thickness / spectral inequality

The local scalar \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route can be pushed further. The right proof mechanism is not finite-row projection, and not point sampling at zeros. It is a **thick-set lower bound** for the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The key observation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the branch windows form a thick set at the active source bandwidth, then }L^2\text{-Gate--1 follows from a spectral inequality.}
}
\]

This is the cleanest route I see for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\). It aligns with the latest update: Gate–1 is local scalar \(L^2\) energy, and branch observables are proof tools only. 

---

# 1. Correct normalized target

For each branch \(b\),

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
=
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x),
\]

and

\[
E_b(m)
=
\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

The aggregate theorem should be

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m),
}
\]

where \(N_b(m)\) is the full canonical branch reference energy for the same scale and source. Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

The remaining task is to prove this lower bound.

---

# 2. Thickness replaces pointwise projection

Let

\[
\Omega_{\branch}
=
\bigcup_{b,m}
\left\{t=m+\lambda_bx:\ x\in \operatorname{supp}w_b\right\}
\]

be the physical region covered by branch windows.

If \(q''_{\can}\) is effectively bandlimited at physical bandwidth \(B\), then a Logvinenko–Sereda / spectral inequality says:

If \(\Omega_{\branch}\) is \((\ell,\gamma)\)-thick, meaning every interval \(J\) of length \(\ell\) satisfies

\[
|\Omega_{\branch}\cap J|\ge \gamma |J|,
\]

then

\[
\int_{\Omega_{\branch}}|q''_{\can}(t)|^2\,dt
\ge
\exp[-C B\ell\log(C/\gamma)]
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2\,dt.
\]

For ordinary packets,

\[
B\asymp Q.
\]

So if the branch coverage is thick at scale

\[
\ell\asymp Q^{-1}
\]

with

\[
\gamma\ge Q^{-C},
\]

then

\[
B\ell\log(C/\gamma)\asymp \log Q,
\]

and the loss is only polynomial:

\[
\exp[-C\log Q]=Q^{-C}.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-window thickness at inverse active bandwidth gives polynomial }L^2\text{ coercivity.}
}
\]

This is a substantial simplification. The proof no longer needs pointwise zero-conditioned projection avoidance.

---

# 3. Branch-scale version

The physical-bandwidth statement is too crude for deep transition/singular packets. In branch coordinates,

\[
F_{b,m}(x)=\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx).
\]

The active \(x\)-frequency scale is

\[
B_b\asymp \lambda_b Q.
\]

For ordinary packets,

\[
B_b\asymp1.
\]

For transition,

\[
(\log Q)^{-C}\lesssim B_b\lesssim1.
\]

For deep singular,

\[
B_b\ll(\log Q)^{-C}.
\]

So in the local \(x\)-coordinate, the source is low-bandwidth, not high-bandwidth. This is precisely why local \(L^2\) energy is the right object.

A branchwise thickness theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_{b,m}\|_{\mathrm{ref},b}^2
}
\]

whenever the branch window \(w_b\) covers an \(x\)-set thick at scale \(B_b^{-1}\), or simply when \(w_b\) is a full local bump on \(I_b\). Since \(w_b\) is chosen by the proof, this local part is deterministic.

The only aggregate coverage issue is whether the centers \(m\) cover the physical \(t\)-region where the canonical source energy lives.

---

# 4. Clean sufficient theorem: branch thickness + bandlimited canonical source

A proof-quality sufficient theorem is:

### Hypothesis A — effective canonical bandwidth

On each branch window,

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
=
F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}(x)+R_{b,m}(x),
\]

where

\[
\operatorname{spec}(F_{b,m}^{\le B_b})\subset[-B_b,B_b],
\]

and

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)\|R_{b,m}\|_{L^2(I_b)}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm big}}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
\]

### Hypothesis B — branch-local observation thickness

For each branch window,

\[
\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}\|_{L^2(I_b)}^2.
\]

This is deterministic if \(w_b\) is a nondegenerate bump on \(I_b\).

### Hypothesis C — branch reference equivalence

\[
\|F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}\|_{L^2(I_b)}^2
\asymp_{Q^A}
N_b(m).
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
}
\]

This is a precise route to \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\).

---

# 5. What remains arithmetic?

The above reduces the problem to three concrete inputs.

## Input 1 — effective bandwidth / completion coherence

We need the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

to be represented on branch windows by the relevant active packet plus a half-leading remainder.

This is where partial explicit-formula residual estimates may be useful upstream.

A suitable statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b
\left\|
\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)
-
F_{b,m}^{\mathrm{packet}}(x)
\right\|_{L^2(I_b)}^2
\le
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bN_b(m).
}
\]

This is completion coherence.

## Input 2 — reference nontriviality

The diagonal/reference energy must be polynomially nonzero:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bN_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b.
}
\]

This is an actual canonical source nonflatness statement.

It is not a sampling artifact. It says \(q''_{\can}\) has polynomial branch-scale energy in the routed region.

## Input 3 — branch coverage

The branch atlas must not omit the regions where \(q''_{\can}\) carries its reference energy:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary/transition/singular windows cover the active source support with polynomial thickness.}
}
\]

If branch windows are attached only to sparse routed centers with large holes, then the theorem needs an additional actual-source nonconcentration-in-holes input.

---

# 6. Relation to the old zero-Gram law

The earlier block-rank-one zero-Gram law is still useful, but its role changes.

Old role:

\[
\text{prove finite-row Gate--1 at zero centers}.
\]

New role:

\[
\text{prove branch coverage / weighted }L^2\text{ source sampling}.
\]

The scalar zero relation

\[
Z_\alpha(m)=0
\]

is no longer a leading null direction because local \(L^2\) source energy samples a whole neighborhood of \(m\). A value cancellation at one point cannot kill the \(L^2\)-energy unless the source is locally flat.

So the old projection obstruction is replaced by:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local source flatness on branch windows}.
}
\]

This is a more natural and probably easier obstruction.

---

# 7. Strong no-hole version

If one can prove a physical coverage lower bound

\[
W_{\branch}(t)\ge Q^{-A}W_{\ref}(t)
\]

on the active source support, then

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\branch}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\ref}.
\]

Then the aggregate theorem reduces to continuous source nonflatness:

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\ref}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
\]

This is the best case. It avoids delicate zero/routed-center sampling.

So the most valuable next audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do branch windows form a thick physical cover at the active bandwidth?}
}
\]

If yes, \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2\) is much closer to analytic.

If no, the missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\text{ cannot concentrate in uncovered branch holes}.
}
\]

---

# 8. Potential failure mode

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem fails only if one of the following holds:

1. **Coverage failure:**

   Branch windows miss most of the support of \(q''_{\can}\).

2. **Completion cancellation:**

   \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) cancels heavily in \(L^2\) on the branch windows.

3. **Reference collapse:**

   The canonical diagonal/reference energy \(N_b\) is itself tiny.

4. **Source flatness:**

   \(q''_{\can}\) is locally flat on most routed branch windows.

These are now the exact failure modes.

---

# 9. Final theorem statement

The theorem Agent 3 should hand to the stack is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete local }L^2\textbf{ source coercivity.}
}
\]

Let

\[
F_{b,m}(x)=\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx),
\]

and

\[
E_b(m)=\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

Let \(N_b(m)\) be the full canonical branch reference energy for \(F_{b,m}\). Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
}
\]

Moreover,

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

A proof follows from:

\[
\boxed{
\text{effective branch bandwidth / completion coherence}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-window thickness at inverse active bandwidth}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{canonical reference nonflatness}.
}
\]

This is the cleanest upstream Gate–1 formulation currently available.

## Agent 3 — AggL2\((q''_{\can})\) via thickness / spectral inequality

The instruction asks for a branch-complete \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem, now that Gate–2 accepts scalar local \(L^2\) source energy of \(u=q''_{\can}\). So the target is no longer a finite-row Gate–1 theorem; it is a weighted source-energy lower bound for the full canonical object

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

This matches the update’s new architecture:
\[
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
+
\mathrm{WeightedExteriorPivot}_{23}^{\Lambda}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenD4}^{\Lambda}.
\]

---

# 1. Correct branch \(L^2\) setup

For each branch \(b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}\), define

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
:=
\lambda_b^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_b x),
\]

and

\[
E_b(m)
:=
\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx,
\]

where \(w_b\ge0\) is a smooth branch-local observation window.

The normalized aggregate theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
}
\]

Here \(N_b(m)\) is the **full canonical branch reference energy** at the same scale. One should not compare directly to \(\sum\rho_b(m)\) until proving

\[
\boxed{
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m)N_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m).
}
\]

So the proof separates into:

\[
\text{coercivity against }N_b
\]

and

\[
\text{reference nontriviality of }N_b.
\]

---

# 2. Fubini reduction

Changing variables

\[
t=m+\lambda_bx,
\qquad
dx=\lambda_b^{-1}dt,
\]

gives

\[
E_b(m)
=
\lambda_b^5
\int
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right)dt.
\]

Thus

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
=
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt,
\]

where

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
}
\]

The aggregate theorem becomes

\[
\boxed{
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\branch}(t)\,dt
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}),
}
\]

with

\[
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})
=
\sum_b\sum_m\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
\]

---

# 3. Definition of \(N_b(m)\)

The branch reference energy should be defined from the **same full source**.

Locally write

\[
q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)
=
F^{\mathrm{pkt}}_{b,m}(x)+R_{b,m}(x),
\]

where \(F^{\mathrm{pkt}}_{b,m}\) is the active canonical packet representation of

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

on the branch scale, and \(R_{b,m}\) is a residual after truncation/regularization.

A natural reference is

\[
\boxed{
N_b(m):=
\|F^{\mathrm{pkt}}_{b,m}\|_{L^2(I_b,w_b)}^2
}
\]

or its diagonal coefficient equivalent, provided that the packet representation is \(R^C\)-conditioned and branch-normalized.

If one uses only a prime-side diagonal, such as a \(P_2\)-only \(N_b\), then one must also prove a completion/coherence theorem showing that \(D_2,B_2,E_2\) do not cancel or dominate the reference. The safer theorem defines \(N_b\) from the full canonical packet.

---

# 4. Thickness / spectral inequality route

The clean proof route is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-window thickness}
+
\text{effective bandwidth}
\Longrightarrow
L^2\text{ coercivity}.
}
\]

For a function \(F\) with Fourier support in \([-B,B]\), a Logvinenko–Sereda type inequality says that if the observation set \(\Omega\) is \((\ell,\gamma)\)-thick,

\[
|\Omega\cap J|\ge \gamma |J|
\]

for every interval \(J\) of length \(\ell\), then

\[
\int_{\Omega}|F|^2
\ge
\exp[-C B\ell\log(C/\gamma)]
\int |F|^2.
\]

In branch coordinates,

\[
F_{b,m}(x)=\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx),
\]

and the active \(x\)-bandwidth is

\[
B_b\asymp \lambda_b Q.
\]

Thus:

- ordinary: \(B_b\asymp1\);
- transition: \((\log Q)^{-C}\lesssim B_b\lesssim1\);
- deep singular: \(B_b\ll(\log Q)^{-C}\).

So branch-local \(L^2\) observation is well-conditioned if the branch windows are thick at scale \(B_b^{-1}\). Since \(w_b\) is chosen as a local bump on \(I_b\), the local observation itself is deterministic. The real coverage issue is whether the **centers** \(\mathcal C_b\) cover the physical \(t\)-region where the canonical source has energy.

---

# 5. Proof stack

A precise theorem stack is:

## Theorem A — Effective branch bandwidth / completion coherence

For each branch,

\[
F_{b,m}
=
F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}+R_{b,m},
\]

with

\[
\operatorname{spec}(F_{b,m}^{\le B_b})\subset[-B_b,B_b],
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)\|R_{b,m}\|_{L^2(I_b)}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\mathrm{big}}}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
}
\]

This is where partial explicit-formula residual estimates are useful.

## Theorem B — Branch observation coercivity

For the bandlimited part,

\[
\boxed{
\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}\|_{L^2(I_b)}^2.
}
\]

This is deterministic if \(w_b\) is a nondegenerate smooth bump on the local branch interval.

## Theorem C — Reference equivalence

\[
\boxed{
\|F_{b,m}^{\le B_b}\|_{L^2(I_b)}^2
\asymp_{Q^A}
N_b(m).
}
\]

Together, A–C imply

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m).
}
\]

## Theorem D — Reference nontriviality

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This is the actual-source nonflatness statement.

---

# 6. Branch coverage formulation

Equivalently, define the physical branch weight

\[
W_{\branch}(t)
=
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
\]

A strong sufficient coverage theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}(t)
\ge
Q^{-A}W_{\ref}(t)
}
\]

on the active support of \(q''_{\can}\). Then

\[
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\branch}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |q''_{\can}|^2W_{\ref}.
\]

If this physical coverage fails, the missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\text{ does not concentrate in the holes of }W_{\branch}.
}
\]

So the first coverage audit should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do ordinary/transition/singular windows form a thick physical cover at the active bandwidth?}
}
\]

If yes, AggL2 is substantially easier. If no, a genuine actual-source nonconcentration theorem is needed.

---

# 7. Full-source completion

The theorem must use

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

A proof using a truncated model

\[
F_X=B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}
\]

requires a localized residual estimate

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}-F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\le
Q^{-A}
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

Then one may reduce to a finite prime/dual mean-square theorem.

But without such a residual estimate, a \(P_2\)-only or truncated proof is only proxy evidence.

---

# 8. Failure modes

AggL2 can fail only through one of the following:

1. **Branch holes:** \(W_{\branch}\) misses where \(q''_{\can}\) has energy.

2. **Completion cancellation:** \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) cancels in \(L^2\) on the branch coverage.

3. **Reference collapse:** \(\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})\) is itself tiny.

4. **Genuine local flatness:** \(q''_{\can}\) is small throughout most local branch windows.

These are now the exact obstructions. The old finite-row projection obstruction is gone for Gate–1.

---

# 9. Final theorem

The cleaned-up theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-complete aggregate local }L^2\textbf{ source coercivity.}
}
\]

Let

\[
F_{b,m}(x)=\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx),
\]

and

\[
E_b(m)=\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

Let \(N_b(m)\) be the full canonical branch reference energy. Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m),
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This follows from:

\[
\boxed{
\text{effective branch bandwidth / completion coherence}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-window thickness at inverse active bandwidth}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{canonical reference nonflatness}.
}
\]

That is the exact AggL2\((q''_{\can})\) theorem stack I would use going forward.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 03:58:00)

I’ll push the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 theorem into the remaining hard part: proving reference nontriviality and ruling out completion cancellation for the full canonical source.

_Thought for 6s_

## Agent 3 continuation — reference nontriviality for \( \mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can}) \)

The current Gate–1 object is now clean:

\[
E_b(m)=\int_{I_b}\left|\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx)\right|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

The aggregate theorem is:

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m),
\]

plus reference nontriviality

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)N_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
\]

The prior step reduced coercivity to branch thickness, effective bandwidth, and completion coherence. The remaining issue is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can }N_b(m)\text{ itself collapse for the full canonical source?}
}
\]

This is the exact “source nonflatness” problem for the new \(L^2\)-Gate–1 architecture. The current architecture explicitly wants this local scalar \(L^2\) source energy instead of the old finite-row Gate–1 definition. 

---

# 1. Reference energy should be defined by a local diagonal, not by \(q''_{\can}\) itself

A subtle point: if we define

\[
N_b(m)=E_b(m),
\]

then the theorem is tautological and useless. Instead \(N_b(m)\) must be the **branch-scale reference diagonal** attached to the local canonical packet expansion.

Write locally

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
=
\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx).
\]

Assume a branch packet representation

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
=
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal P_b(m)}
c_\alpha^{(b)}(m)e^{-ix\xi_\alpha^{(b)}}
+
R_{b,m}(x),
\]

where

\[
\xi_\alpha^{(b)}=\lambda_b\omega_\alpha.
\]

Then the natural reference is

\[
\boxed{
N_b(m)
:=
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal P_b(m)}
|c_\alpha^{(b)}(m)|^2
}
\]

up to the normalization of \(w_b\), plus explicit diagonal contributions for any nonoscillatory canonical terms that are not included in the packet basis.

Then the coercivity theorem says

\[
\|F_{b,m}\|_{L^2(w_b)}^2
\gtrsim Q^{-A}N_b(m)
\]

on average.

The reference nontriviality theorem says

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal P_b(m)}
|c_\alpha^{(b)}(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
\]

This is now a diagonal-size theorem for the actual canonical source.

---

# 2. Reference collapse can only happen if the canonical packet has no active mass

For a branch packet, the diagonal is a sum of squares. It cannot cancel internally.

So

\[
N_b(m)\ll Q^{-A}
\]

means that the canonical source coefficients themselves are small on that branch scale.

This is very different from the old pointwise obstruction. It is not caused by

\[
Z_\alpha(m)=0.
\]

It is caused by actual absence of canonical curvature/source mass.

Thus the reference nontriviality input is:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}\text{ has polynomial diagonal mass on routed branch scales.}
}
\]

This is likely much easier than pointwise Gate–1. It is a diagonal explicit-formula estimate, not a zero-conditioned anti-cancellation theorem.

---

# 3. Prime/dual diagonal mass should usually be enough

For the prime-side piece, after AFE normalization, a local packet has coefficients roughly of the form

\[
c_n^{P}
\sim
\lambda_b^3
a_n^{(2)}
\]

where \(a_n^{(2)}\) includes the two-derivative/canonical multiplier and smooth branch cutoff.

The diagonal mass is

\[
N_{P,b}(m)
\sim
\lambda_b^6
\sum_{n\in\mathcal P_b}
|a_n^{(2)}|^2.
\]

For a typical AFE coefficient

\[
a_n\sim n^{-1/2}
\]

with derivative multiplier roughly \((\log n)^2\), this has size

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal P_b}
\frac{(\log n)^4}{n}
\times \lambda_b^6
\times \text{cutoff factors}.
\]

In the ordinary band,

\[
\log n\asymp Q,
\qquad
\lambda_b Q\asymp1.
\]

Then

\[
\lambda_b^6(\log n)^4
\asymp
Q^{-6}Q^4=Q^{-2}.
\]

The sum

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal P_b}\frac1n
\]

over a fixed log-width band is polynomially sized, typically \(\asymp 1\) at the log-scale normalization. So the ordinary reference diagonal is at worst polynomial:

\[
N_{P,\ord}(m)\gtrsim Q^{-C}.
\]

The exact exponent is not important for the architecture; what matters is:

\[
\boxed{
N_b(m)\text{ should not be exponentially or super-polynomially small.}
}
\]

The same reasoning should apply to dual pieces, with the same AFE normalization.

---

# 4. \(B_2\) should not be used as the only reference

The gamma/background term \(B_2\) is deterministic and may be nonzero. But using only \(B_2\) to prove \(N_b\neq0\) is dangerous.

Reason: \(B_2\) is smooth and low-dimensional. It may provide local \(L^2\) energy, but it does not necessarily carry the oscillatory packet structure needed by O2/O3 or downstream source thickening.

So \(B_2\) can contribute to the full diagonal reference, but the safer reference theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
N_{P+D,b}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for the prime plus dual source, with \(B_2,E_2\) included in the full source energy but not used as the sole lower-bound mechanism.

If the prime/dual diagonal fails and only \(B_2\) remains, then the branch may be canonically nonflat but arithmetically too low-rank for the rest of the architecture.

Thus I would classify:

\[
P_2+D_2\text{ diagonal nontriviality}
\]

as the desirable reference theorem, and

\[
B_2\text{-only nontriviality}
\]

as diagnostic or fallback.

---

# 5. Completion coherence is now the key non-cancellation issue

Even if

\[
N_b(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\]

we still need

\[
\|F_{b,m}\|_{L^2(w_b)}^2
\ge Q^{-A}N_b(m).
\]

This can fail only if the actual full source

\[
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

cancels the active packet in \(L^2\).

The clean completion-coherence theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\right\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left\|
P_2+D_2
\right\|_{\diag(W)}.
}
\]

A sufficient version is half-leading:

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+E_2\|_{L^2(W)}
\le
\frac12
\|P_2+D_2\|_{L^2(W)}.
}
\]

But this may be false if \(B_2\) is not small.

A more robust version is an angle/coherence bound:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(W)}
\left(
P_2+D_2,
-\operatorname{span}\{B_2+E_2\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}\|P_2+D_2\|_{\diag(W)}.
}
\]

Equivalently, the smooth/completion piece cannot be the negative of the oscillatory source over most branch windows.

This is a canonical source non-cancellation theorem, but it is far lower-dimensional than the old pointwise Gate–1 problem.

---

# 6. Partial explicit-formula residuals are the right proof tool here

Let

\[
F_X(t)
=
B_2(t)+P_2^{\le X}(t)+D_2^{\le X}(t)
\]

be a truncated canonical model, and define

\[
R_X(t)=q''_{\can}(t)-F_X(t).
\]

A useful residual theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\le
Q^{-A}
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

If this holds, then

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W)}
\ge
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}
-
Q^{-A/2}\mathcal N_{\can}(W)^{1/2}.
\]

So the Gate–1 theorem reduces to proving

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

This is now a finite Dirichlet-polynomial / smooth background mean-square theorem.

This is exactly the correct use of partial explicit-formula residuals: upstream completion coherence, not downstream \(\Lambda\).

---

# 7. Weighted mean square for \(F_X\)

For

\[
F_X=B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X},
\]

the \(L^2(W)\) norm is

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
=
\|P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+
2\Re\langle P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X},B_2\rangle_W
+
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W)}^2.
\]

Since \(\|B_2\|^2\ge0\), only the cross term can hurt. A sufficient condition is

\[
\boxed{
|\langle P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X},B_2\rangle_W|
\le
\frac12
\|P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
+
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W).
}
\]

This follows if \(B_2\) is smooth/low-frequency and the oscillatory packet has small projection onto it under \(W\), unless there is a resonance.

So another concrete theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{smooth background is not resonant with the active prime/dual packet under }W_{\branch}.
}
\]

This is much more approachable than the old finite-row anti-cancellation.

---

# 8. Thick coverage can make the finite mean-square nearly diagonal

If \(W_{\branch}\) is physically thick and smooth, then for the truncated packet

\[
P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}
\]

one expects

\[
\|P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum |c_\alpha|^2.
\]

If \(W\) has no large holes, this can be proved by a spectral inequality or by a weighted Gram bound.

The local \(L^2\) window is important: it damps long-frequency off-diagonals. The remaining near-diagonal terms are controlled by the actual AFE coefficient structure.

Thus the proof route becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{thick }W
+
\text{finite packet mean-square}
+
\text{background nonresonance}
+
\text{tail residual}
\Rightarrow
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can}).
}
\]

---

# 9. Strongest refined theorem

The refined theorem stack is:

## Theorem A — finite canonical packet lower mean square

For

\[
F_X=B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X},
\]

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\mathcal N_X(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

## Theorem B — residual coherence

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}-F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm big}}\mathcal N_X(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

## Theorem C — reference comparison

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_X(W_{\branch})
\asymp_{Q^A}
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

This is now fully concrete.

---

# 10. Remaining hard points

The first genuinely nontrivial theorem is Theorem A:

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W).
\]

It can fail if:

1. \(W\) has holes and \(F_X\) concentrates in them;
2. \(P_2^{\le X}\) and \(D_2^{\le X}\) cancel coherently;
3. \(B_2\) cancels the oscillatory packet;
4. \(F_X\) is genuinely small because the canonical curvature source is flat.

The most plausible way to prove it is:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\branch}\text{ is thick at the active bandwidth}
}
\]

plus a spectral inequality.

If \(W\) is not thick, the theorem becomes actual-source nonconcentration in the uncovered set.

---

# 11. Final current endpoint

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route has been reduced to a concrete upstream theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{finite canonical packet lower mean square plus residual coherence.}
}
\]

More explicitly:

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W_{\branch}),
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X})\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm big}}\mathcal N_X(W_{\branch}).
}
\]

This is the cleanest proof target for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\).

## Agent 3 continuation — finite packet lower mean square under thick weights

The current \(L^2\)-Gate–1 target is:

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch}),
\]

with

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The previous reduction was:

1. truncate to a finite canonical packet

\[
F_X=B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X},
\]

2. prove finite packet lower mean square,

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mathcal N_X(W_{\branch}),
\]

3. prove residual coherence,

\[
\|q''_{\can}-F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm big}}\mathcal N_X(W_{\branch}).
\]

Now I pushed the finite packet mean-square theorem.

---

# 1. Spectral inequality gives a clean sufficient condition

Let

\[
F_X(t)=B_2(t)+\sum_{\alpha\in\mathcal I_X}c_\alpha e^{-it\omega_\alpha}
\]

with all active frequencies contained in an interval of length

\[
B_X.
\]

For the ordinary scale,

\[
B_X\asymp Q,
\]

after normalizing to the local packet. For transition and singular, the branch-coordinate bandwidth is \(\lambda Q\le1\), but in physical \(t\)-coordinates the active frequency width is still \(\asymp Q\) for the underlying prime-log packet.

Suppose the branch weight satisfies a thick-set lower bound relative to a reference weight:

\[
W_{\branch}(t)\ge w_0\,\mathbf 1_{\Omega}(t),
\]

and \(\Omega\) is \((\ell,\gamma)\)-thick on the active height window:

\[
|\Omega\cap J|\ge \gamma |J|
\]

for every interval \(J\) of length \(\ell\). Then a Logvinenko–Sereda/Nazarov type inequality gives

\[
\int_{\Omega}|F_X(t)|^2dt
\ge
\exp[-C B_X\ell\log(C/\gamma)]
\int |F_X(t)|^2dt
\]

for exponential polynomials with frequency width \(B_X\).

Thus if

\[
B_X\ell\log(C/\gamma)\le C\log Q,
\]

then

\[
\int |F_X|^2W_{\branch}
\ge
Q^{-C}
\int |F_X|^2W_{\ref}.
\]

So the finite packet theorem follows from:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch coverage thick at scale }\ell\lesssim Q^{-1}\operatorname{polylog}(Q)
}
\]

with

\[
\gamma\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This is a positive route.

---

# 2. Branch windows naturally have the right scale

For ordinary packets,

\[
\lambda\asymp Q^{-1}.
\]

If routed centers have mean spacing

\[
\asymp Q^{-1},
\]

and the local windows have width

\[
\asymp \lambda\asymp Q^{-1},
\]

then the union of branch windows is thick at scale

\[
\ell\asymp Q^{-1}
\]

provided there are no large gaps in routed centers.

This is exactly the scale required by the spectral inequality.

So the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 route reduces the sampling problem to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{routed centers plus transition/singular charts give a thick cover at zero-spacing scale.}
}
\]

This is much cleaner than pointwise lower sampling.

---

# 3. Plus/dual cancellation under \(L^2\)

A remaining concern is cancellation between

\[
P_2^{\le X}
\quad\text{and}\quad
D_2^{\le X}.
\]

Write

\[
U=P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}.
\]

The spectral inequality applies to \(U\) as a whole:

\[
\|U\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
\]

But the reference diagonal

\[
\mathcal N_X
\]

might count

\[
\|P_2\|_{\diag}^2+\|D_2\|_{\diag}^2
\]

rather than the actual \(L^2\) norm of \(U\). If \(P_2\) and \(D_2\) nearly cancel globally, then

\[
\|U\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ll
\|P_2\|_{\diag}^2+\|D_2\|_{\diag}^2.
\]

So the reference must be chosen carefully.

The safest choice is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_X(W)
:=
\|B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

Then the spectral inequality gives

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\mathcal N_X(W).
\]

This avoids needing to prove plus/dual noncancellation at the diagonal level.

Reference nontriviality becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

That is a genuine source nonflatness theorem, but it avoids artificial diagonal/cancellation bookkeeping.

---

# 4. Better definition of \(N_{\can}\)

This suggests that the right canonical reference energy is not the coefficient diagonal. It should be a continuous reference norm:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\can}(W_{\branch})
:=
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
}
\]

for a smooth reference weight \(W_{\ref}\) on the same active region.

Then the aggregate Gate–1 theorem is simply a **spectral observability theorem**:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\branch})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

Reference nontriviality is:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

This definition avoids:

- \(P_2\)-only proxy errors;
- \(P_2/D_2\) cancellation issues;
- \(B_2\) classification ambiguity;
- coefficient diagonal normalization across branches.

It also makes the theorem branch-invariant.

This is a significant simplification.

---

# 5. New clean Gate–1 theorem

Define:

\[
W_{\obs}(t):=W_{\branch}(t)
\]

from branch windows, and choose a smooth reference weight \(W_{\ref}(t)\) on the same active height region.

Then the aggregate Gate–1 theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This is much cleaner than coefficient-diagonal \(N_b\).

The proof inputs:

1. \(W_{\obs}\) is thick relative to \(W_{\ref}\) at bandwidth \(Q\):

\[
W_{\obs}\text{ observes a thick subset of }\operatorname{supp}W_{\ref}.
\]

2. \(q''_{\can}\) is effectively bandlimited/moderate-growth at bandwidth \(Q\), or its high-frequency residual is controlled.

3. \(q''_{\can}\) has nontrivial \(L^2(W_{\ref})\) energy.

---

# 6. Effective bandwidth issue

Strictly, \(q''_{\can}\) is not compactly bandlimited. It is an AFE/explicit-formula object with tails.

So we need a decomposition:

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\]

where \(F_X\) has frequency width

\[
B_X\asymp Q
\]

or whatever branch-relevant bandwidth is used, and

\[
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2+\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm big}}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
\]

Then spectral observability for \(F_X\) yields observability for \(q''_{\can}\).

This is precisely where partial EF residual estimates enter.

So the theorem stack becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{partial EF bandwidth truncation}
+
\text{thick-set spectral inequality}
+
\text{reference nonflatness}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Reference nonflatness under \(W_{\ref}\)

The remaining source theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

This should be the easiest part if \(W_{\ref}\) is macroscopic and smooth. One can use standard mean-square estimates for \(q''_{\can}\), or at least for the finite AFE approximation.

For the truncated model

\[
F_X=B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X},
\]

one expects

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\asymp
\sum |c_\alpha|^2
\]

up to manageable off-diagonals, because \(W_{\ref}\) is not zero-supported and can be chosen macroscopic.

Thus reference nonflatness is now a continuous mean-square theorem, not a zero-restricted theorem.

This is a major simplification.

---

# 8. Branch-complete coverage is now an observability condition

The branch centers no longer need to provide direct coefficient diagonal control. They only need to define an observation weight \(W_{\obs}\) that is thick relative to \(W_{\ref}\).

So the coverage theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\obs}=W_{\ord}+W_{\tr}+W_{\sing}
\text{ is }(Q^{-1}\operatorname{polylog},Q^{-C})\text{-thick on }\operatorname{supp}W_{\ref}.
}
\]

If this fails, then there are physical holes of size larger than inverse bandwidth. A bandlimited source could concentrate there. Then one needs actual-source nonconcentration in holes.

Thus coverage is the true branch-routing condition.

---

# 9. Consequence for singular branch

This fully confirms that singular projections should not be Gate–1 definitions.

If singular windows are needed for coverage, they just contribute to \(W_{\obs}\). Whether a finite singular projection captures energy is irrelevant to Gate–1.

So the singular branch proof obligation becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{singular windows help make }W_{\obs}\text{ thick.}
}
\]

No raw \(S_R\), no \(P_R\), no low-mode capture, unless used as a technical device to prove local bandwidth/residual estimates.

---

# 10. Final simplified theorem stack

The best current formulation of \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\) is:

## Theorem A — Observability

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

Sufficient conditions:

- \(W_{\obs}\) is thick relative to \(W_{\ref}\) at inverse active bandwidth;
- \(q''_{\can}\) is effectively bandlimited after partial EF truncation;
- high-frequency residual is \(Q^{-A}\)-small.

## Theorem B — Reference mean square

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

Sufficient conditions:

- finite AFE mean square lower bound;
- partial EF residual small;
- no full-source completion cancellation.

Together:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{b,m}\rho_bE_b(m)
=
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

---

# 11. Why this is better than \(N_b\)

The previous \(N_b\)-diagonal formulation was technically precise but cumbersome. It required deciding what counts as diagonal energy for \(B_2,D_2,E_2\).

The reference \(L^2(W_{\ref})\) formulation avoids that. It treats the full canonical source as a function and asks for observability from \(W_{\obs}\).

This is better aligned with Gate–2, which consumes local \(L^2\) source energy.

---

# 12. Remaining hard input

The first nontrivial input is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{thick-set observability for the partial-EF-truncated }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

The second is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{macroscopic reference mean-square lower bound for }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

Both are standard-looking analytic number theory / spectral inequality statements, much cleaner than the old finite-row projection theorem.

## Final status

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 attack now has a clean route:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
=
\text{observability from thick branch windows}
+
\text{reference mean-square nonflatness}.
}
\]

## Agent 3 — AggL2\((q''_{\can})\): observability + finite canonical anti-lock

I’m taking the updated architecture as fixed for this attack: Gate–1 is now local scalar \(L^2\) energy of \(u=q''_{\can}\), and the old finite-row / transition-jet / singular-projection objects are proof tools only. The relevant prompt asks me to prove or reduce

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2,
\]

plus reference nonflatness, using

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,\qquad
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The shared update also emphasizes that this must be for the full canonical source, not a proxy. 

---

# 1. Define the observation and reference weights

For each branch \(b\in\{\ord,\tr,\sing\}\), define

\[
F_{b,m}(x)
=
\lambda_b^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda_bx),
\]

and

\[
E_b(m)
=
\int_{I_b}|F_{b,m}(x)|^2w_b(x)\,dx.
\]

Changing variables \(t=m+\lambda_bx\),

\[
E_b(m)
=
\lambda_b^5
\int
|q''_{\can}(t)|^2
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right)dt.
\]

Thus

\[
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)E_b(m)
=
\int |q''_{\can}(t)|^2W_{\obs}(t)\,dt,
\]

where

\[
\boxed{
W_{\obs}(t)
=
\sum_b\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}
\rho_b(m)\lambda_b^5
w_b\!\left(\frac{t-m}{\lambda_b}\right).
}
\]

Choose a smooth macroscopic reference weight \(W_{\ref}\) supported on the same active height region, with comparable global mass:

\[
\int W_{\ref}(t)\,dt
\asymp
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m)
\]

after branch normalization.

Then the new Gate–1 theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2,
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This avoids artificial diagonal bookkeeping for \(B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\).

---

# 2. Thick-set observability theorem

Let

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

Assume \(F_X\) is effectively an exponential polynomial with active physical bandwidth

\[
B_X\asymp Q
\]

on the relevant height window. More precisely, after branch rescaling \(x=(t-m)/\lambda_b\), the local branch bandwidth is

\[
B_b\asymp \lambda_b Q,
\]

so:

\[
B_{\ord}\asymp1,
\qquad
(\log Q)^{-C}\lesssim B_{\tr}\lesssim1,
\qquad
B_{\sing}\ll(\log Q)^{-C}.
\]

A Logvinenko–Sereda / Nazarov spectral inequality gives the following usable form:

If \(W_{\obs}\) dominates a set \(\Omega\) that is \((\ell,\gamma)\)-thick relative to \(W_{\ref}\), with

\[
B_X\ell\log(C/\gamma)\le C\log Q,
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

For ordinary scale, this means thickness at roughly

\[
\ell\lesssim Q^{-1}\operatorname{polylog}(Q),
\qquad
\gamma\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Branchwise, the corresponding condition is thickness at inverse local bandwidth \(B_b^{-1}\) in the \(x\)-coordinate, or equivalently at the physical scale \(\lambda_b B_b^{-1}\).

So the branch-routing condition becomes:

\[
\boxed{
W_{\obs}\text{ is thick relative to }W_{\ref}\text{ at inverse active bandwidth.}
}
\]

This is now a geometric coverage hypothesis, not a pointwise zero-projection hypothesis.

---

# 3. Finite canonical anti-lock

The finite packet theorem needs

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
\]

Here

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The possible obstruction is \(L^2\)-level anti-lock:

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\approx0
\]

on the reference support.

A clean sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
+
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
+
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\right).
}
\]

This may be stronger than necessary. A weaker and probably better theorem is simply:

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

That form avoids classifying \(B_2\) as perturbative or dominant. It treats \(F_X\) as the finite canonical approximation to the actual source.

The proof route is a standard weighted mean-square lower bound for the finite AFE model:

\[
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\]

plus a background nonresonance estimate:

\[
\left|
\langle P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},B_2\rangle_{W_{\ref}}
\right|
\le
\frac12
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
+
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
\]

If \(B_2\) is not perturbative, keep it inside \(F_X\) and prove the direct finite-source lower mean square.

---

# 4. Residual coherence

Let

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-F_X.
\]

We need

\[
\boxed{
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
+
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm big}}
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

Then

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}
\ge
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}
-
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})},
\]

and, combining spectral observability for \(F_X\),

\[
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
\]

With residual control also on \(W_{\ref}\),

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\asymp_{Q^A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
\]

So:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

This is where partial explicit-formula residuals should be used.

---

# 5. Full theorem stack

The proof-quality theorem stack is:

## Theorem A — Thick observability for \(F_X\)

Assume \(W_{\obs}\) is thick relative to \(W_{\ref}\) at inverse active bandwidth and \(F_X\) has effective bandwidth \(B_X\). Then

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

## Theorem B — Finite canonical nonflatness

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

Equivalently, \(B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\) is not \(L^2\)-flat on the reference region.

## Theorem C — EF residual coherence

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}-F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
+
\|q''_{\can}-F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm big}}
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
}
\]

A–C imply

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

This is exactly \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\).

---

# 6. First real obstruction

The first genuinely nontrivial theorem is Theorem B:

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

The possible failure is an \(L^2\)-level AFE anti-lock:

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\approx0
\]

on the reference support.

This is much better than the old pointwise projection obstruction because it is a continuous mean-square lower bound, not a pointwise zero-conditioned statement.

If \(W_{\ref}\) is macroscopic and smooth, this should be approachable through AFE mean-square estimates and background nonresonance.

---

# 7. Failure modes

The AggL2 theorem fails only if one of these occurs:

1. **Thickness failure:**
   \(W_{\obs}\) has holes larger than inverse active bandwidth.

2. **Finite canonical anti-lock:**
   \(B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\) is small in \(L^2(W_{\ref})\).

3. **Residual failure:**
   \(R_X\) is not small in \(L^2(W_{\obs}\vee W_{\ref})\).

4. **Reference normalization failure:**
   \(\sum\rho\) is too large relative to the actual reference source energy.

These are now the exact upstream Gate–1 obstructions.

---

# Final Agent 3 deliverable

The clean target theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
=
\mathrm{ThickObs}(W_{\obs},W_{\ref})
+
\mathrm{FiniteCanonicalNonflatness}(F_X)
+
\mathrm{EFResidualCoherence}(R_X).
}
\]

In equations:

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

The first missing theorem to attack is the finite canonical \(L^2\) anti-lock bound for

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

## Agent 3 continuation — finite canonical \(L^2\) anti-lock

The remaining Gate–1 input is now the finite canonical mean-square lower bound

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m),
\qquad
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The old finite-row projection issue is gone. The new obstruction is an \(L^2\)-level AFE anti-lock:

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\approx0
\]

on the macroscopic reference support. The current architecture explicitly wants this as part of \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\), after Gate–1 was redefined as local scalar \(L^2\) source energy. 

---

# 1. Reduce anti-lock to a Gram lower bound

Let

\[
U:=P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The desired finite-source lower bound is

\[
\|B_2+U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal M_X,
\]

where \(\mathcal M_X\) is the finite canonical reference size, for example

\[
\mathcal M_X
\asymp
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
+
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
+
\|B_2\|_{W_{\ref}}^2,
\]

if that norm is already known to be polynomially comparable to the coefficient diagonal.

Expanding,

\[
\|B_2+U\|^2
=
\|U\|^2+\|B_2\|^2+2\Re\langle U,B_2\rangle.
\]

A sufficient condition is

\[
|\langle U,B_2\rangle|
\le
\left(\frac12-Q^{-A}\right)\|U\|^2
+
Q^C\|B_2\|^2
\]

together with

\[
\|U\|^2+\|B_2\|^2\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal M_X.
\]

More cleanly, prove the block-Gram lower bound

\[
\boxed{
\left\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\right\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
+\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
+\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

This is exactly the finite canonical anti-lock theorem.

---

# 2. Prime–dual cancellation is the main term to test

Ignore \(B_2\) first. Let

\[
P(t)=\sum_{n\le X} a_n n^{-it},
\]

and write the dual piece schematically as

\[
D(t)=e^{i\Theta(t)}\sum_{n\le X^\vee} b_n n^{it}
\]

or, depending on normalization, as another folded packet with a branch phase. The \(P\)-\(D\) cross term is

\[
\langle P,D\rangle_W
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{b_{n'}}
\int W(t)
e^{-it\log n}
e^{-i\Theta(t)}
e^{-it\log n'}
\,dt
\]

up to convention-dependent signs.

The phase derivative is of the form

\[
-\log n-\log n'-\Theta'(t).
\]

In completed-zeta normalization,

\[
\Theta'(t)
\]

is of size \(Q\) on height \(t\sim e^Q\). Hence stationary interaction occurs only near a multiplicative saddle condition such as

\[
\log n+\log n'\approx \Theta'(t).
\]

This is the usual AFE coupling. Away from that saddle, integration by parts over a macroscopic \(W_{\ref}\)-window gives strong decay.

So the finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem splits into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonstationary prime--dual cross terms are small;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{stationary prime--dual terms do not produce coherent negative cancellation.}
}
\]

The second statement is the real arithmetic content.

---

# 3. Stationary phase gives a structured kernel, not arbitrary cancellation

In the stationary region, the cross term should reduce to a bilinear form

\[
\sum_{n,n'}
a_n\overline{b_{n'}}
K(n,n')
\]

where \(K(n,n')\) is concentrated near the saddle relation and has a stationary-phase phase factor.

If this kernel is approximately unitary between the prime and dual packets, then maximal cancellation would require

\[
D\approx -P
\]

as functions on the reference window.

But the AFE dual is not freely chosen; \(b_n\), the dual cutoff, and \(\Theta(t)\) are fixed by the functional equation. Thus the anti-lock problem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
P_{2,\le X}
\text{ is not an approximate negative eigenfunction of the AFE folding operator.}
}
\]

Define the folding operator \(\mathcal F_W\) by

\[
\langle P,D\rangle_W
=
\langle P,\mathcal F_W D_0\rangle
\]

in coefficient space, where \(D_0\) is the unphased dual coefficient vector. Then

\[
\|P+D\|_W^2
=
\|P\|_W^2+\|D\|_W^2+2\Re\langle P,D\rangle_W.
\]

The dangerous case is

\[
\Re\langle P,D\rangle_W
\approx
-\|P\|_W\|D\|_W
\]

with comparable norms. Equivalently,

\[
P\approx -D
\]

in \(L^2(W_{\ref})\).

So a precise theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{AFE}\text{-}\mathrm{AntiLock}_{L^2}:
\quad
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

---

# 4. A useful sufficient condition: phase variation of the fold

If the folded dual phase varies across \(W_{\ref}\) by at least a polynomial amount, then exact anti-lock cannot persist over the whole window.

Suppose locally

\[
D(t)=e^{i\Theta(t)}\widetilde D(t),
\]

where \(\widetilde D(t)\) has comparable bandwidth to \(P(t)\). If

\[
P(t)\approx -e^{i\Theta(t)}\widetilde D(t)
\]

throughout the reference window, then differentiating gives

\[
P'(t)
\approx
-e^{i\Theta(t)}
\left(
i\Theta'(t)\widetilde D(t)+\widetilde D'(t)
\right).
\]

If \(P,\widetilde D\) have bandwidth \(\asymp Q\), and \(\Theta'(t)\asymp Q\), this is not contradictory by itself. But if

\[
\Theta''(t)
\]

creates phase curvature across the window, then maintaining anti-lock forces higher derivative alignment conditions. Over a macroscopic reference window, these conditions become overdetermined unless the packets are tuned to the functional-equation saddle.

Thus the anti-lock theorem can be attacked through a **curved fold non-eigenfunction estimate**:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the AFE folding operator has no eigenvalue near }-1
\text{ on the finite canonical packet subspace.}
}
\]

This is a finite operator theorem, not a pointwise zero theorem.

---

# 5. \(B_2\) cannot cancel an oscillatory packet except through a low-frequency projection

Now include

\[
B_2.
\]

Since \(B_2\) is smooth/gamma-background, it lives in a very low-dimensional / low-frequency subspace compared with \(P+D\).

A robust sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|\Pi_{\mathrm{low}}(P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X})\right\|_{W_{\ref}}
\le
Q^{-A}
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}},
}
\]

where \(\Pi_{\mathrm{low}}\) projects onto the span needed to approximate \(B_2\). Then \(B_2\) cannot cancel the oscillatory packet except for a negligible low-frequency component.

Equivalently, prove

\[
|\langle P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},B_2\rangle_{W_{\ref}}|
\le
Q^{-A}
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}\|B_2\|_{W_{\ref}}.
\]

This is a standard smooth-background nonresonance estimate.

If \(B_2\) is not negligible and is included in \(F_X\), the direct finite canonical mean-square theorem avoids classifying it. But for proof, the nonresonance view is useful.

---

# 6. Finite operator formulation

Let

\[
\mathcal H_P,\quad \mathcal H_D,\quad \mathcal H_B
\]

be the finite-dimensional packet spaces for \(P_{2,\le X}\), \(D_{2,\le X}\), and \(B_2\) under \(W_{\ref}\). Define the synthesis map

\[
\mathcal S:
\mathcal H_P\oplus\mathcal H_D\oplus\mathcal H_B
\to
L^2(W_{\ref})
\]

by

\[
\mathcal S(p,d,b)=P_p+D_d+B_b.
\]

Then finite canonical anti-lock is simply

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal S)\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on the actual canonical coefficient subspace.

A universal lower singular bound for all coefficients may be too strong. But the actual source only uses a structured vector

\[
(p_{\can},d_{\can},b_{\can}).
\]

Thus the minimal theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathcal S(p_{\can},d_{\can},b_{\can})\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|p_{\can}\|^2+\|d_{\can}\|^2+\|b_{\can}\|^2
\right).
}
\]

The stronger theorem

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal S)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

would be a clean sufficient condition.

---

# 7. What would make the theorem fail?

The finite anti-lock theorem fails if there exists a polynomially accurate relation

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\approx0
\]

on the macroscopic reference window.

Equivalently,

\[
P_{2,\le X}
\approx
-D_{2,\le X}-B_2.
\]

That would mean the finite AFE pieces solve a homogeneous relation with one smooth background component. This is far more rigid than the old pointwise cancellation. It would imply a global approximate functional relation on the reference window, not just bad values at sampled zeros.

So the anti-lock obstruction is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{macroscopic finite AFE self-cancellation of the curvature source.}
}
\]

This should be testable by continuous mean-square technology.

---

# 8. Possible theorem using known-style mean squares

A plausible proof strategy:

1. Prove

\[
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
+
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
\]

2. Prove cross correlation bound

\[
|\langle P_{2,\le X},D_{2,\le X}\rangle_{W_{\ref}}|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}.
\]

3. Prove background nonresonance

\[
|\langle P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},B_2\rangle|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|\|B_2\|
\]

unless \(B_2\) itself contributes positive energy.

Then

\[
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
\]

The difficult part is the cross-correlation bound between \(P\) and \(D\), especially near the AFE saddle.

---

# 9. Best refined target

The finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Macroscopic finite AFE curvature anti-lock.}
}
\]

For the finite canonical source

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},
\]

on the smooth macroscopic reference weight \(W_{\ref}\),

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2+
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2+
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\right),
}
\]

or, minimally,

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

This is the first real upstream theorem after thick observability.

---

# 10. Final current endpoint

The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 proof stack is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Thick observability}
+
\text{Macroscopic finite AFE curvature anti-lock}
+
\text{EF residual coherence}.
}
\]

The first term is geometric/spectral. The third is tail/completion control. The second is the arithmetic heart of AggL2.

This is a much cleaner problem than finite-row Gate–1:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove the finite completed curvature source does not self-cancel in macroscopic }L^2.
}
\]

## Agent 3 — finite \(L^2\) AFE anti-lock as the degree-one branch-charge model

The update changes my target slightly. The \(L^2\)-Gate–1 layer is now clean enough that the first hard upstream theorem is no longer “branch transfer” or “finite-row visibility.” It is the finite canonical anti-lock statement

\[
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho,
\]

or, more structurally, that the completed finite curvature source is not \(L^2\)-small on the macroscopic reference window. The update also identifies this as the degree-one prototype of the branch-charge survival problem that later reappears in exterior determinant O2/O3. 

I will attack that degree-one model.

---

# 1. Degree-one branch-charge decomposition

Write the finite canonical source as

\[
F_X(t)=B_2(t)+P_X(t)+D_X(t),
\]

where

\[
P_X=P_{2,\le X},\qquad D_X=D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The finite anti-lock theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right)
}
\]

or at least

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

The dangerous relation is

\[
B_2+P_X+D_X\approx0
\quad\text{in }L^2(W_{\ref}).
\]

This is a macroscopic functional relation, not a pointwise zero phenomenon.

---

# 2. Remove \(B_2\) first: the prime/dual core

First ignore \(B_2\). The core anti-lock is

\[
\boxed{
\|P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

Expanding,

\[
\|P_X+D_X\|^2
=
\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2
+
2\Re\langle P_X,D_X\rangle.
\]

So failure requires

\[
\Re\langle P_X,D_X\rangle
\le
-(1-Q^{-A})
\|P_X\|\|D_X\|
\]

and

\[
\|P_X\|\asymp \|D_X\|.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
P_X\approx -D_X
\quad\text{in }L^2(W_{\ref}).
\]

Thus the degree-one branch-charge theorem is exactly an anti-eigenvalue statement for the AFE fold:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the finite AFE fold has no approximate eigenvalue }-1
\text{ on the curvature source packet.}
}
\]

---

# 3. The fold operator formulation

Represent the finite prime packet as

\[
P_X(t)=\sum_{n\le X} a_n n^{-it}.
\]

Write the folded dual schematically as

\[
D_X(t)
=
e^{i\Theta(t)}
\sum_{n\le X^\vee} b_n n^{it},
\]

with the exact signs depending on the chosen completed normalization.

Define the fold operator \(\mathcal F_W\) by

\[
D_X=\mathcal F_W P_X
\]

at the coefficient or synthesized-function level. Then the core theorem becomes

\[
\boxed{
\|(I+\mathcal F_W)P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2.
}
\]

More generally, because \(P_X\) and \(D_X\) are not literally the same coefficient vector, define the synthesis map

\[
\mathcal S(p,d)=P_p+D_d.
\]

Then anti-lock is

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal S)
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on the actual AFE-compatible coefficient subspace.

The actual-coefficient version is weaker:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{\can}+D_{\can}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_{\can}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|D_{\can}\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

---

# 4. Why exact anti-lock should be hard but implausible

Suppose

\[
P_X(t)\approx -D_X(t)
\]

on a macroscopic window. Then

\[
P_X(t)\approx
-e^{i\Theta(t)}\widetilde D_X(t),
\]

where \(\widetilde D_X\) is an opposite-branch Dirichlet packet.

Differentiate:

\[
P_X'(t)
\approx
-e^{i\Theta(t)}
\left(
i\Theta'(t)\widetilde D_X(t)
+
\widetilde D_X'(t)
\right).
\]

A second derivative gives

\[
P_X''(t)
\approx
-e^{i\Theta(t)}
\left(
(i\Theta')^2\widetilde D_X
+
i\Theta''\widetilde D_X
+
2i\Theta'\widetilde D_X'
+
\widetilde D_X''
\right).
\]

Because \(\Theta'(t)\asymp Q\), this is not immediately contradictory: both packets have natural bandwidth \(Q\). The decisive term is curvature of the fold:

\[
\Theta''(t)
\]

and higher derivatives. Exact anti-lock over a macroscopic window would require \(P_X\) to be an approximate solution of a fold-induced differential system. That is far more rigid than arbitrary cancellation.

So the right theorem is not “phase varies, therefore no cancellation.” It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite AFE packets are not approximate solutions of the negative folded eigenmode equation.}
}
\]

---

# 5. A sufficient spectral gap theorem

Define the folded branch operator on the finite packet Hilbert space:

\[
\mathcal U_X:
\mathcal H_P\to \mathcal H_D,
\]

including the AFE phase, cutoff, and dual normalization. Pull it back to \(\mathcal H_P\) using the natural coefficient identification, giving an operator

\[
\mathcal K_X:\mathcal H_P\to\mathcal H_P.
\]

The desired anti-lock follows if

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(-1,\operatorname{Spec}_{\mathrm{num}}(\mathcal K_X))
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\|(I+\mathcal K_X)f\|
\ge Q^{-A}\|f\|
}
\]

for the actual finite packet sector.

This is a clean finite operator theorem.

It has two possible proof routes:

## Route A — non-involution of the truncated fold

The exact functional equation is an involution at the completed-function level, but finite truncation plus curvature differentiation should break exact \(-1\)-eigenmodes on the truncated curvature packet.

Need to prove:

\[
\mathcal K_X^2=I+\mathcal E
\]

does not have a \(-1\)-eigenspace compatible with the curvature multiplier/cutoff.

## Route B — stationary phase kernel is not negative identity

Compute the kernel of the prime-dual cross form:

\[
\langle P_X,D_X\rangle_{W_{\ref}}
=
\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{b_{n'}}K(n,n').
\]

Show the operator norm of the negative part satisfies

\[
\boxed{
-\Re K
\le
(1-Q^{-A})I
}
\]

on the actual coefficient vector or on the relevant finite subspace.

This is a weighted stationary-phase / AFE cross-correlation theorem.

---

# 6. Reintroducing \(B_2\)

Now add \(B_2\). The finite anti-lock theorem becomes

\[
B_2+P_X+D_X\not\approx0.
\]

If the prime-dual core already satisfies

\[
\|P_X+D_X\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2
\right),
\]

then \(B_2\) can hurt only if

\[
B_2\approx -(P_X+D_X).
\]

But \(B_2\) is smooth/low-complexity. So it suffices to prove

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{W_{\ref}}
\left(
P_X+D_X,
-\mathcal B
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}\|P_X+D_X\|,
}
\]

where \(\mathcal B\) is the finite-dimensional smooth background space containing \(B_2\).

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\|\Pi_{\mathcal B}(P_X+D_X)\|_{W_{\ref}}
\le
(1-Q^{-A})\|P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}.
}
\]

So \(B_2\) introduces a low-rank projection obstruction, not a full branch-charge obstruction.

This is much easier than the prime-dual anti-lock. It is a smooth-background nonresonance theorem.

---

# 7. Degree-one branch-charge survival theorem

The theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Degree-one branch-charge survival.}
}
\]

Let

\[
F_X=B_2+P_X+D_X
\]

be the finite completed curvature source on a macroscopic smooth reference window. Then

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

A sufficient package is:

1. Prime-dual anti-lock:

\[
\|P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2).
\]

2. Background nonresonance:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{W_{\ref}}
(P_X+D_X,-\mathcal B)
\ge
Q^{-A}\|P_X+D_X\|.
\]

3. Background self-energy included positively:

\[
\|B_2\|^2\ge0.
\]

---

# 8. Relation to O2/O3 branch survival

This is the degree-one analogue of determinant branch survival. At exterior degree \(N=R+2\), one has

\[
D_{\full}
=
\sum_{r=0}^{N}D_r,
\]

where \(r\) counts branch charge.

The degree-one theorem is the case

\[
N=1:
\qquad
F_X=B_2+P_X+D_X.
\]

So any method that proves degree-one branch-charge survival may lift to determinant branch survival.

In particular:

- AFE fold spectral gap at degree one corresponds to branch-charge Gram lower bounds at exterior degree.
- \(\alpha\)-twisting at degree one should give a test case for determinant \(\alpha\)-twisting.
- Background nonresonance at degree one corresponds to background/tail sector control in the full determinant.

Thus this theorem is not just for Gate–1; it is a toy model for O2/O3 scalar compatibility.

---

# 9. What I would try next

The most concrete next attack is to compute the prime-dual cross operator

\[
K(n,n')
=
\int W_{\ref}(t)
e^{-it\log n}
\overline{
e^{i\Theta(t)}e^{it\log n'}
}
\,dt
\]

by stationary phase.

The stationary condition is

\[
\Theta'(t)
\approx
\log(nn')
\]

or the appropriate signed variant.

The goal is to show that the resulting bilinear form cannot be \(-I\)-like:

\[
\boxed{
\Re\sum_{n,n'}a_n\overline{b_{n'}}K(n,n')
>
-(1-Q^{-A})
\left(
\sum|a_n|^2+\sum|b_n|^2
\right)/2.
}
\]

If the stationary phase kernel has a nontrivial oscillatory phase or amplitude mismatch, this should hold.

This is now a concrete analytic number theory / stationary-phase problem.

---

# Final status

The finite \(L^2\) anti-lock problem has been reduced to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prime-dual AFE fold has no approximate }-1\text{ eigenmode on the finite curvature packet}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{smooth background }B_2\text{ is not resonant with the surviving oscillatory source.}
}
\]

This is the first hard upstream theorem inside \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\), and it is the degree-one prototype of the later determinant branch-survival problem.

## Agent 3 continuation — stationary-phase model for finite \(L^2\) AFE anti-lock

The active upstream theorem is now the finite canonical \(L^2\) anti-lock bound

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho,
\qquad
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The hard part is the prime–dual interaction inside

\[
U=P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The previous reduction said this is equivalent to showing the AFE fold has no approximate \(-1\)-eigenmode on the finite curvature packet. I now push that into the stationary-phase kernel.

---

# 1. The prime–dual cross term

Use the schematic normal form

\[
P(t)=\sum_n a_n e^{-it\log n},
\]

and

\[
D(t)=e^{i\Theta(t)}\sum_m b_m e^{it\log m},
\]

with \(W=W_{\ref}\) smooth and macroscopic. Then

\[
\langle P,D\rangle_W
=
\sum_{n,m} a_n\overline{b_m}
\int W(t)e^{-it\log n}e^{-i\Theta(t)}e^{-it\log m}\,dt
\]

up to harmless convention signs. Thus the kernel is

\[
K(n,m)
=
\int W(t)\exp\left(-i\Phi_{n,m}(t)\right)\,dt,
\]

where

\[
\Phi_{n,m}(t)
=
t\log(nm)+\Theta(t).
\]

Depending on the normalization of \(\Theta\), signs may flip, but the stationary condition always has the form

\[
\boxed{
\log(nm)\approx \Theta'(t).
}
\]

At height \(T\sim e^Q\),

\[
\Theta'(t)\sim Q,
\]

so the stationary region is the hyperbola

\[
\boxed{
nm\approx e^Q\asymp T/(2\pi).
}
\]

Therefore the cross term is not a diagonal inner product. It is a **hyperbolic Hankel-type operator** coupling \(n\) to \(m\approx e^Q/n\).

---

# 2. Nonstationary terms are harmless

If

\[
|\log(nm)-\Theta'(t)|
\ge \Delta
\]

throughout the support of \(W\), then repeated integration by parts gives

\[
K(n,m)
\ll_A
\|W\|_{C^A}\Delta^{-A}.
\]

So only pairs satisfying

\[
\log(nm)
=
Q+O(H^{-1})
\]

matter, where \(H\) is the macroscopic reference-window length.

Thus

\[
\langle P,D\rangle_W
\]

is controlled by a structured near-hyperbola bilinear form.

That already rules out arbitrary global anti-lock. Cancellation must occur through the AFE saddle geometry.

---

# 3. Stationary phase structure

For saddle pairs, let \(t_{n,m}\) solve

\[
\Theta'(t_{n,m})=\log(nm).
\]

Then stationary phase gives

\[
K(n,m)
=
W(t_{n,m})
\frac{e^{-i\Phi_{n,m}(t_{n,m})+i\pi\sigma/4}}
{\sqrt{|\Theta''(t_{n,m})|}}
+
\mathrm{Err}_{n,m}.
\]

The important point is that \(K(n,m)\) carries a nontrivial oscillatory phase depending on \(n,m\) through \(t_{n,m}\). It is not a negative scalar multiple of the identity.

So the cross operator has the form

\[
\boxed{
(\mathcal K b)_n
=
\sum_{m:\ nm\approx e^Q}
\mathfrak k(n,m)b_m,
}
\]

where \(\mathfrak k(n,m)\) is an oscillatory hyperbola kernel.

The anti-lock failure would require

\[
P\approx -D,
\]

meaning this hyperbola operator must send the actual dual coefficient vector to almost the negative of the prime coefficient vector after synthesis. That is a strong arithmetic alignment condition.

---

# 4. A clean sufficient anti-lock condition

Let

\[
A:=\|P\|_W^2,\qquad B:=\|D\|_W^2,\qquad C:=\Re\langle P,D\rangle_W.
\]

Then

\[
\|P+D\|_W^2=A+B+2C.
\]

The desired bound

\[
\|P+D\|_W^2
\ge Q^{-A_0}(A+B)
\]

is equivalent to

\[
C
\ge
-\left(\frac12-Q^{-A_0}\right)(A+B).
\]

A sufficient condition is the operator anti-lock bound

\[
\boxed{
|\langle P,D\rangle_W|
\le
(1-Q^{-A_0})\|P\|_W\|D\|_W.
}
\]

This is exactly the statement that the two synthesized packets are not almost collinear with opposite phase in \(L^2(W)\).

Equivalently, the normalized correlation

\[
\mathfrak c(P,D)
=
\frac{\langle P,D\rangle_W}{\|P\|_W\|D\|_W}
\]

satisfies

\[
\boxed{
\Re \mathfrak c(P,D)>-1+Q^{-A_0}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Why the hyperbola kernel should not be \(-I\)

The cross kernel is approximately supported on

\[
nm\approx e^Q.
\]

Thus \(\mathcal K\) resembles a weighted reciprocal transform

\[
m\mapsto e^Q/n.
\]

The identity operator would require \(m=n\), i.e.

\[
n^2\approx e^Q,
\qquad
n\approx e^{Q/2}.
\]

Only the central region near the square-root length can behave identity-like. Away from that central locus, the fold maps one side of the packet to the other.

Therefore, if the finite packet has nontrivial mass across a genuine log interval, the fold cannot be a scalar \(-I\) on the whole packet.

This gives a possible theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Hyperbola fold anti-identity.}
}
\]

If the prime and dual packets occupy a log-frequency interval of width \(\gg Q^{-C}\), then the AFE fold operator cannot act as \(-I\) on the actual curvature coefficient vector, with only polynomial loss.

---

# 6. Reduction to log-frequency variable

Set

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q},
\qquad
y=\frac{\log m}{Q}.
\]

The saddle condition is

\[
x+y\approx 1.
\]

The fold map is therefore

\[
x\mapsto 1-x.
\]

The anti-lock condition becomes roughly

\[
p(x)\approx -e^{i\varphi(x)}d(1-x),
\]

where \(\varphi(x)\) is the stationary-phase phase.

If the actual AFE coefficients satisfy

\[
d(1-x)\approx \overline{p(x)}
\]

up to smooth weights, then

\[
P+D
\]

is more like a cosine-type completed packet, not identically zero. It can have zeros pointwise, but its \(L^2\)-mass over a macroscopic window is positive.

Thus the degree-one anti-lock theorem is closely related to the elementary fact that

\[
f+e^{i\varphi}\bar f
\]

cannot vanish in \(L^2\) unless the phase and \(f\) are tuned pointwise. With nonconstant \(\varphi\), it cannot vanish over a macroscopic interval for a finite-bandwidth \(f\) except at high cost.

---

# 7. Stronger route: split into symmetric and antisymmetric parts

Under the fold \(x\mapsto 1-x\), decompose the coefficient packet into symmetric and antisymmetric parts:

\[
p=p_++p_-,
\]

where

\[
p_+(x)=p_+(1-x),
\qquad
p_-(x)=-p_-(1-x),
\]

after including the smooth AFE weights.

The completed packet \(P+D\) sees one of these parity components depending on the phase convention. Exact cancellation would require all mass to lie in the wrong parity component with the wrong phase.

So one can try to prove:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the actual curvature coefficient vector has polynomial mass in the visible fold-parity sector.}
}
\]

This is a coefficient-side version of the anti-lock theorem.

It may be easier because the curvature multiplier \((\log n)^2\) is even under the fold only after centering; deviations from exact symmetry can force visible mass.

---

# 8. The \(B_2\) interaction

Add \(B_2\). Since \(B_2\) is smooth, it lives in a low-dimensional phase sector. It can cancel only the low-frequency projection of \(U=P+D\).

Let \(\mathcal B\) be the span of the background profiles under \(W_{\ref}\). Then a sufficient condition is

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})U\|_W^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|_W^2.
}
\]

If this holds, then

\[
B_2
\]

cannot cancel the oscillatory component except on a polynomially small subspace.

Thus the finite anti-lock splits into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prime--dual fold anti-lock}}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{background nonresonance}}.
\]

Both are continuous \(L^2\) statements.

---

# 9. Potential proof package

A proof-quality finite anti-lock package could be:

## Lemma 1 — hyperbola fold decomposition

The cross term satisfies

\[
\langle P,D\rangle_W
=
\langle p,\mathcal K_{\rm hyp}d\rangle
+
O(Q^{-A})\|p\|\|d\|,
\]

where \(\mathcal K_{\rm hyp}\) is a stationary-phase hyperbola operator supported near \(x+y=1\).

## Lemma 2 — no \(-1\) eigenmode

For the actual finite curvature coefficient vector,

\[
\Re\langle p,\mathcal K_{\rm hyp}d\rangle
\ge
-(1-Q^{-A})
\|p\|\|d\|.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\|P+D\|_W^2
\ge
Q^{-A}(\|P\|_W^2+\|D\|_W^2).
\]

## Lemma 3 — background nonresonance

\[
|\langle P+D,B_2\rangle_W|
\le
(1-Q^{-A})\|P+D\|_W\|B_2\|_W
\]

unless \(\|B_2\|_W^2\) itself supplies the desired lower bound.

Then

\[
\|B_2+P+D\|_W^2\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
\]

---

# 10. Relation to determinant branch survival

This degree-one fold anti-lock is the model for the exterior determinant branch-survival problem.

At degree one, branch charges are:

\[
B,\quad P,\quad D.
\]

At degree \(N\),

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_r D_r,
\]

where \(r\) counts branch type. The same fold \(x\mapsto1-x\) and phase \(\Theta\) govern the branch-charge cross terms.

Therefore proving degree-one anti-lock by hyperbola/stationary phase gives a template for proving determinant branch survival:

\[
\boxed{
\text{stationary-phase branch-charge Gram is not singular in the bad direction.}
}
\]

This is a useful bridge between AggL2 and O2/O3.

---

# 11. Current endpoint

The first upstream hard theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Macroscopic finite AFE fold anti-lock.}
}
\]

In concrete form:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2+
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\right).
}
\]

Then add:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Background nonresonance}}
\]

to control \(B_2\).

The most direct proof route is stationary phase for the prime–dual cross kernel, showing the hyperbola fold cannot act as \(-I\) on the actual finite curvature packet over a macroscopic reference window.
