## Agent 3 continuation — central fold obstruction and the square-root packet

The finite \(L^2\) Gate–1 obstruction is now the macroscopic anti-lock theorem

\[
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
\]

The hard part is the prime–dual interaction. Away from the AFE saddle, stationary phase kills the cross term. The only serious possible anti-lock is near the square-root fold

\[
nm\approx e^Q,
\qquad
n\approx m\approx e^{Q/2}.
\]

That is where the fold \(x\mapsto 1-x\) can look locally like the identity.

---

# 1. Fold coordinates

Set

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q},
\qquad
y=\frac{\log m}{Q}.
\]

The prime–dual stationary condition is

\[
x+y\approx 1.
\]

Introduce centered variables

\[
s=x-\frac12,
\qquad
y=\frac12-s+\text{small error}.
\]

The fold is therefore

\[
s\mapsto -s.
\]

So, after stationary phase, the dual packet is approximately a reflected version of the prime packet:

\[
D(t)\sim \mathcal F P(t),
\]

where \(\mathcal F\) acts on coefficient envelopes roughly by

\[
(\mathcal F a)(s)
=
e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)\,\overline{a(-s)}
\]

or a non-conjugated analogue depending on normalization.

The anti-lock condition

\[
P+D\approx0
\]

becomes, in coefficient-envelope form,

\[
a(s)
+
e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)a(-s)
\approx0.
\]

Thus the dangerous modes are odd/even modes under the reflected fold, twisted by the phase \(e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)\).

---

# 2. Exact anti-lock requires twisted oddness

If

\[
\alpha(s)\approx1,
\qquad
\varphi(s)\approx\varphi_0
\]

on a packet, then anti-lock is possible for coefficient envelopes satisfying

\[
a(s)\approx -e^{i\varphi_0}a(-s).
\]

This is just a twisted oddness condition.

Therefore a universal anti-lock theorem for arbitrary packets is false. One can choose an odd packet.

But our packet is not arbitrary. It is the actual finite AFE curvature packet. The theorem needed is actual-coefficient:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the actual curvature coefficient envelope is not almost purely twisted-odd under }s\mapsto -s.
}
\]

This is sharper than the earlier “no \(-1\) eigenmode” statement.

---

# 3. Curvature multiplier breaks exact fold parity

The prime-side curvature coefficient includes a multiplier resembling

\[
(\log n)^2=Q^2x^2.
\]

Under the fold \(x\mapsto1-x\),

\[
x^2
\mapsto
(1-x)^2.
\]

In centered coordinates,

\[
x=\frac12+s,
\]

so

\[
x^2-\,(1-x)^2
=
\left(\frac12+s\right)^2
-
\left(\frac12-s\right)^2
=
2s.
\]

Thus the curvature multiplier has an odd component of size \(s\). Near \(s=0\), it is almost fold-even, but away from the central tube it separates the two branches.

Therefore split the packet into:

\[
|s|\ge \delta
\]

and

\[
|s|<\delta.
\]

On the outer region,

\[
|x^2-(1-x)^2|\ge 2\delta,
\]

so the two branch multipliers differ polynomially if

\[
\delta\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This gives anti-lock on the outer region unless the actual coefficient mass is concentrated in the central tube

\[
|x-\tfrac12|<\delta.
\]

So the finite anti-lock theorem reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{either there is polynomial mass away from }x=\frac12,
\text{ and curvature multipliers break fold parity;}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{the packet is concentrated near }x=\frac12,
\text{ and one must analyze the central tube.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Central tube analysis

Suppose the actual mass is concentrated in

\[
|s|<\delta.
\]

Then

\[
x=\frac12+O(\delta),
\]

so the curvature multiplier is almost constant:

\[
x^2
=
\frac14+s+O(s^2).
\]

The fold can look like a scalar symmetry here.

But the central tube has log-width

\[
\Delta x\asymp \delta.
\]

If \(\delta\) is very small, the number of available integers is reduced. The diagonal mass in the central tube is proportional to its log-width. If \(\delta\ge Q^{-A}\), the loss is still polynomial; if \(\delta\ll Q^{-A}\), then concentration there already costs more than polynomial unless the source has very sharp arithmetic concentration.

So central-tube obstruction becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual AFE curvature mass cannot concentrate in an exponentially thin square-root tube.}
}
\]

For polynomial bookkeeping, it suffices to take

\[
\delta=Q^{-A_0}.
\]

Then both sides are manageable:

- outside \(|s|<\delta\), curvature multipliers separate branches;
- inside, the tube still has polynomial log-width and can be handled by local stationary phase / reference mean square.

---

# 5. Twisted parity projection

Define the fold operator \(\mathcal R\) on coefficient envelopes by

\[
(\mathcal R a)(s)=e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)a(-s).
\]

Then decompose

\[
a=a_++a_-,
\]

where

\[
a_+=\frac12(a+\mathcal R a),
\qquad
a_-=\frac12(a-\mathcal R a).
\]

The completed packet \(P+D\) sees primarily one of these components. Anti-lock means the visible component is small.

Thus the theorem can be stated as a parity-mass lower bound:

\[
\boxed{
\|a_{\mathrm{vis}}\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|a\|^2.
}
\]

For arbitrary \(a\), false. For actual curvature coefficients, plausible.

A concrete sufficient condition:

\[
\boxed{
\|a-\mathcal R a\|^2
\not\ll Q^{-A}\|a\|^2
}
\]

or the corresponding plus version depending on phase convention.

This can be tested directly from the coefficient formula. Since actual \(a_n\) is roughly positive with a smooth cutoff and curvature multiplier \(x^2\), the reflected difference is roughly

\[
a(x)-e^{i\varphi(x)}a(1-x).
\]

If the phase \(e^{i\varphi(x)}\) is not identically \(1\) and \(x^2\neq(1-x)^2\), this should have polynomial mass.

---

# 6. Stationary phase gives the twist phase

The twist phase \(\varphi(s)\) is not arbitrary. It comes from

\[
\Phi_{n,m}(t_*)
=
t_*\log(nm)+\Theta(t_*),
\qquad
\Theta'(t_*)=-\log(nm)
\]

up to sign conventions.

Differentiating with respect to \(s\), along the saddle \(x+y=1\), gives a phase variation determined by the second-order behavior of the gamma/functional-equation phase.

If

\[
\varphi'(s)\gg Q^{-C}
\]

on the central tube, then even if the amplitude is fold-symmetric, the twist phase prevents persistent anti-lock.

So another sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{twist phase }\varphi(s)\text{ is not constant modulo }\pi
\text{ on the mass support.}
}
\]

If \(\varphi\) is nearly constant near \(s=0\), then the curvature multiplier asymmetry \(2s\) must supply the gap.

Thus the central theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\int
|a(s)+e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)a(-s)|^2ds
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |a(s)|^2ds
}
\]

for the actual curvature envelope \(a\).

---

# 7. Background \(B_2\) after fold decomposition

Once the prime–dual sum has visible component

\[
U=P+D
\]

with

\[
\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2\ge Q^{-A}\|P,D\|^2,
\]

the background \(B_2\) can cancel only the projection of \(U\) onto the smooth background space.

Let \(\mathcal B\) be the span of allowed background profiles. Need

\[
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})U\|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|^2.
\]

The fold-parity decomposition helps here: \(B_2\) is smooth and branch-neutral, while \(U\) inherits oscillatory fold structure unless the packet is extremely central and phase-flat.

So background cancellation is secondary. The main theorem remains fold-parity visibility.

---

# 8. Refined finite anti-lock theorem

The finite canonical anti-lock can now be formulated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Curvature fold-parity visibility.}
}
\]

Let \(a(x)\) be the actual finite AFE curvature coefficient envelope on the prime branch, and let \(\mathcal R\) be the stationary-phase folded-dual reflection

\[
(\mathcal R a)(x)
=
e^{i\varphi(x)}\alpha(x)a(1-x).
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|a+\mathcal R a\|_{L^2_x}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|a\|_{L^2_x}^2
}
\]

or with \(a-\mathcal R a\), depending on sign convention.

A sufficient decomposition:

1. Outer fold region:

\[
|x-\tfrac12|\ge Q^{-A_0}
\]

is controlled by curvature multiplier asymmetry

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

2. Central tube:

\[
|x-\tfrac12|<Q^{-A_0}
\]

is controlled by either twist phase variation or a direct local mean-square lower bound.

3. Background nonresonance controls \(B_2\).

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

---

# 9. Relation to determinant branch survival

This fold-parity theorem is exactly the degree-one model for determinant branch survival.

At determinant degree \(N\), the branch-charge decomposition has components indexed by the number of dual factors. The fold reflection acts on each factor. The dangerous determinant anti-lock is concentration in a twisted parity sector of the \(N\)-fold exterior power.

If degree-one curvature fold-parity visibility holds with a polynomial gap, then the \(N\)-fold exterior analogue may hold with loss

\[
Q^{-O(N)}
\]

which is still acceptable if the one-body gap is \(R^{-C}\) rather than \(Q^{-C}\). Since

\[
N=R+2,
\]

an \(R^{-C}\) one-body gap gives

\[
R^{-O(R)}=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

So we should aim for \(R\)-polynomial, not \(Q\)-polynomial, in the local fold-parity gap if we want to lift to O2/O3 determinant survival.

This is an important quantitative refinement.

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem has become:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{actual curvature coefficient is not hidden in the wrong twisted fold-parity sector.}
}
\]

Main mechanisms:

\[
\boxed{
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1
\text{ breaks fold symmetry away from }x=\frac12;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{central tube has only polynomial log-width and/or nonconstant twist phase;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{ is low-complexity and cannot cancel the visible oscillatory fold component.}
}
\]

This is a concrete analytic target and also the degree-one prototype for the exterior determinant branch-survival problem.

## Agent 3 — degree-one finite AFE anti-lock for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\)

The assigned task is the upstream Gate–1 arithmetic theorem: prove or reduce the finite \(L^2\) anti-lock bound for

\[
F_X=B_2+P_X+D_X,
\qquad
P_X=P_{2,\le X},
\qquad
D_X=D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The desired input is

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho,
\]

or the stronger component anti-lock inequality

\[
\|B_2+P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\left(
\|B_2\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
\]

This is the degree-one branch-charge analogue of the later O2/O3 determinant survival problem. 

---

# 1. Prime–dual fold normal form

Write, schematically,

\[
P_X(t)=\sum_n a_n e^{-it\log n},
\]

and

\[
D_X(t)=e^{i\Theta(t)}\sum_m b_m e^{it\log m},
\]

with the exact conjugations/signs depending on the completed AFE normalization.

The cross term is

\[
\langle P_X,D_X\rangle_{W_{\ref}}
=
\sum_{n,m}a_n\overline{b_m}K(n,m),
\]

where

\[
K(n,m)
=
\int W_{\ref}(t)
\exp\{-i\Phi_{n,m}(t)\}\,dt,
\]

and the phase has the form

\[
\Phi_{n,m}(t)=t\log(nm)+\Theta(t)
\]

up to sign convention.

Stationary phase occurs when

\[
\Phi_{n,m}'(t)=0,
\]

so

\[
\boxed{
\log(nm)\approx -\Theta'(t).
}
\]

At height \(t\sim e^Q\),

\[
|\Theta'(t)|\asymp Q.
\]

Therefore the cross term is concentrated near

\[
\boxed{
nm\asymp e^Q.
}
\]

In log-frequency variables

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q},
\qquad
y=\frac{\log m}{Q},
\]

the fold is

\[
\boxed{
x+y\approx1.
}
\]

So the dual branch is a reflected copy of the prime branch under

\[
x\mapsto1-x.
\]

---

# 2. Fold-parity formulation

Set

\[
s=x-\frac12.
\]

Then the fold becomes

\[
s\mapsto -s.
\]

After stationary phase, the dual packet acts on coefficient envelopes roughly as

\[
(\mathcal R a)(s)
=
e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)a(-s),
\]

where \(\alpha(s)\) is a smooth amplitude and \(\varphi(s)\) is the stationary-phase twist.

The dangerous anti-lock condition

\[
P_X+D_X\approx0
\]

becomes

\[
\boxed{
a(s)+e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)a(-s)\approx0.
}
\]

Thus anti-lock means the actual curvature coefficient envelope lies almost entirely in the wrong twisted fold-parity sector.

A clean theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|a+\mathcal R a\|_{L^2_s}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|a\|_{L^2_s}^2
}
\]

or the same with \(a-\mathcal R a\), depending on sign convention.

---

# 3. Curvature multiplier breaks fold symmetry

For the curvature source, the prime-side multiplier contains

\[
(\log n)^2=Q^2x^2.
\]

Under the fold,

\[
x\mapsto1-x.
\]

The key identity is

\[
\boxed{
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
}
\]

In centered coordinates,

\[
x=\frac12+s,
\]

so

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2s.
\]

Therefore, away from the central tube

\[
|s|<\delta,
\]

the curvature multiplier separates the two folded branches by at least

\[
2\delta.
\]

Consequently, on

\[
|x-\tfrac12|\ge\delta,
\]

fold anti-lock cannot persist unless the remaining AFE weights and phase twist conspire to cancel this multiplier asymmetry.

Taking

\[
\delta=R^{-C}
\]

is preferable to \(\delta=Q^{-C}\), because exterior-degree lifting later costs powers of \(R\). With

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

losses of type

\[
R^{-O(R)}
\]

remain polynomial in \(Q\).

So the outer-region theorem should aim for an \(R^{-C}\)-scale gap:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{|x-\frac12|\ge R^{-C}}
|a+\mathcal R a|^2\,dx
\ge
R^{-C}
\int_{|x-\frac12|\ge R^{-C}}|a|^2\,dx.
}
\]

---

# 4. Central square-root tube

The only serious obstruction is the central tube

\[
|x-\tfrac12|<\delta.
\]

There,

\[
x^2=(1-x)^2+O(\delta),
\]

so the curvature multiplier is almost fold-even.

Two mechanisms can still give visibility.

## 4.1 Tube mass is small unless the source concentrates

The log-width of the tube is

\[
O(\delta).
\]

If \(\delta=R^{-C}\), then this is only a polynomially small fraction of the ordinary log-band. Thus concentration of the actual canonical packet inside this tube is a new but explicit obstruction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{square-root tube concentration}.
}
\]

A sufficient theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\int_{|x-\frac12|<R^{-C}} |a(x)|^2\,dx
\le
(1-R^{-C'})
\int |a(x)|^2\,dx.
}
\]

## 4.2 Twist phase variation

Even inside the central tube, anti-lock fails if the stationary twist phase is not constant modulo \(\pi\). A sufficient condition is

\[
\boxed{
|\varphi'(s)|\ge R^{-C}
}
\]

on a polynomial part of the tube. Then

\[
a(s)+e^{i\varphi(s)}a(-s)
\]

cannot vanish over the whole interval for a finite-bandwidth actual envelope \(a\), except at polynomial cost.

So central control follows from either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonconcentration in the central tube}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{twist-phase variation inside the central tube}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Degree-one anti-lock theorem

The finite prime–dual theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Curvature fold-parity visibility.}
}
\]

Let \(a(x)\) be the actual finite curvature coefficient envelope of the prime branch, and let

\[
(\mathcal R a)(x)
=
e^{i\varphi(x)}\alpha(x)a(1-x)
\]

be the stationary-phase folded-dual reflection. Then

\[
\boxed{
\|a+\mathcal R a\|_{L^2}^2
\ge R^{-C}\|a\|_{L^2}^2.
}
\]

This should imply

\[
\boxed{
\|P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

The exact sign may be \(a-\mathcal R a\) depending on the AFE phase convention.

---

# 6. Adding \(B_2\)

Now include \(B_2\). Once

\[
U:=P_X+D_X
\]

has a lower bound, \(B_2\) can cancel only the low-complexity projection of \(U\).

Let \(\mathcal B\) be the finite-dimensional smooth background space containing \(B_2\). A sufficient background nonresonance theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
U
\]

is not almost contained in the smooth background subspace. Then

\[
B_2
\]

cannot cancel \(U\) except on a negligible projection.

Thus the full finite anti-lock follows from:

1. prime–dual fold-parity visibility;
2. background nonresonance.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

---

# 7. Relation to O2/O3 determinant survival

This degree-one theorem is the prototype for the O2/O3 branch-charge theorem.

At exterior degree

\[
n=R+2,
\]

the full determinant decomposes by branch charge:

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_sD_s.
\]

The pure plus determinant is \(D_{-n}\). Under a bounded-degree Stack weight, it interacts only with near-pure sectors

\[
D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}.
\]

This is the exterior analogue of the degree-one decomposition

\[
B_2+P_X+D_X.
\]

The same reflection

\[
x\mapsto1-x
\]

and the same curvature asymmetry

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1
\]

should generate the branch-charge gap.

So proving degree-one fold-parity visibility gives the correct model for the near-pure Schur complement in O2/O3.

---

# 8. Current proof package for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2\)

The Gate–1 theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
=
\mathrm{ThickObs}
+
\mathrm{CurvatureFoldParity}
+
\mathrm{BackgroundNonresonance}
+
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}.
}
\]

Explicitly:

## ThickObs

\[
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
\]

## CurvatureFoldParity

\[
\|P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}
(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2).
\]

## BackgroundNonresonance

\[
\|B_2+P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}
(\|B_2\|^2+\|P_X+D_X\|^2).
\]

## EFCompletion\(_{L^2}\)

\[
\|q''_{\can}-F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs}\vee W_{\ref})}^2
\le Q^{-A_{\rm big}}\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2.
\]

Together, these yield

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

---

# 9. Main remaining obstruction

The sharp obstruction is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual curvature coefficients concentrate in the wrong twisted fold-parity sector near }x=\frac12.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite AFE curvature source is square-root-tube locked.}
}
\]

That is the first real theorem to prove or disprove.

It is much more precise than the earlier pointwise Gate–1 obstruction.

## Agent 3 continuation — square-root tube anti-lock via amplitude transversality

The dangerous finite Gate–1 obstruction is now very specific:

\[
P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\approx0
\quad\text{in }L^2(W_{\ref}).
\]

After stationary phase, the prime–dual fold is

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q}
\quad\longmapsto\quad
1-x,
\]

and the central danger is the square-root tube

\[
x\approx\frac12.
\]

The previous attack used the curvature multiplier identity

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

I pushed that into an actual coefficient-level anti-lock criterion.

---

# 1. Coefficient-level fold model

After stationary phase, the finite prime–dual interaction has the model form

\[
P_X+D_X
\quad\leadsto\quad
a(x)+\mathcal R a(x),
\]

where

\[
(\mathcal R a)(x)
=
e^{i\varphi(x)}\alpha(x)a(1-x).
\]

For the curvature source, the branch envelope has the form

\[
a(x)
=
x^2 A(x),
\]

where \(A(x)\) contains the AFE cutoff, coefficient normalization, and slowly varying canonical weights.

The reflected dual amplitude is roughly

\[
(1-x)^2 A(1-x)
\]

up to smooth factors.

The key point is elementary:

\[
|u+v|^2\ge (|u|-|v|)^2.
\]

Thus phase cannot create cancellation unless the two amplitudes are already close:

\[
|a(x)|\approx|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|.
\]

So anti-lock reduces to amplitude-ratio transversality plus control of the small tube where the amplitudes match.

---

# 2. Amplitude-ratio transversality

Define the logarithmic amplitude ratio

\[
R(x)
:=
\log\frac{|a(x)|}{|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|}.
\]

Using

\[
a(x)=x^2A(x),
\]

we get

\[
R(x)
=
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}
+
\log\frac{|A(x)|}{|\alpha(x)A(1-x)|}.
\]

The first term has derivative

\[
\frac{d}{dx}\left(2\log\frac{x}{1-x}\right)
=
\frac2x+\frac2{1-x}.
\]

On any fixed ordinary interval inside \((0,1)\), this is bounded below by a positive constant. At the central point \(x=1/2\), it equals

\[
8.
\]

Therefore, unless the smooth AFE/canonical amplitude term has a finely tuned derivative cancelling this everywhere, we have

\[
\boxed{
|R'(x)|\ge c>0
}
\]

on the active subpacket, after excluding only polynomially many small bad intervals around critical points.

This gives a clean transversality lemma:

If

\[
|R'(x)|\ge c
\]

and the coefficient mass density is locally doubling, then

\[
\mu\{x:|R(x)|\le\varepsilon\}
\le
C\varepsilon\,\mu(\text{active interval})
\]

for any small \(\varepsilon\).

So the amplitude-match set is thin.

---

# 3. Anti-lock outside the amplitude-match tube

Let

\[
r(x):=\frac{|a(x)|}{|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|}.
\]

Then

\[
(|a|-|\alpha a(1-x)|)^2
=
\frac{(r-1)^2}{r^2+1}
\left(|a|^2+|\alpha a(1-x)|^2\right).
\]

If

\[
|R(x)|=|\log r(x)|\ge\varepsilon,
\]

then

\[
\frac{(r-1)^2}{r^2+1}\ge c\varepsilon^2
\]

for small \(\varepsilon\). Hence

\[
|a(x)+\mathcal R a(x)|^2
\ge
c\varepsilon^2
\left(|a(x)|^2+|\mathcal R a(x)|^2\right)
\]

outside the amplitude-match tube.

Integrating,

\[
\int |a+\mathcal R a|^2
\ge
c\varepsilon^2
\int_{\{|R|\ge\varepsilon\}}
\left(|a|^2+|\mathcal R a|^2\right).
\]

If the amplitude-match tube carries at most half the mass, then

\[
\boxed{
\int |a+\mathcal R a|^2
\ge
c\varepsilon^2
\int |a|^2.
}
\]

Taking

\[
\varepsilon=R^{-C}
\]

gives a loss

\[
R^{-O(1)},
\]

which is excellent. Even after an exterior lift of degree \(R\), this produces only

\[
R^{-O(R)}=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

This is the right quantitative scale.

---

# 4. Central square-root tube is only one instance of amplitude matching

The old “central tube” was

\[
|x-\tfrac12|<\delta.
\]

That is where

\[
x^2\approx(1-x)^2.
\]

But the more invariant bad set is not exactly \(x=1/2\); it is

\[
R(x)\approx0,
\]

where the full canonical amplitude ratio matches.

If the smooth weights are symmetric, this tube is centered near \(1/2\). If they are not symmetric, the tube shifts. But transversality still makes it thin.

Thus the refined obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual curvature mass concentrates in the amplitude-match tube }R(x)\approx0.
}
\]

Not necessarily exactly in the square-root tube.

This is useful because it avoids over-specializing to symmetric cutoffs.

---

# 5. Required coefficient nonconcentration theorem

The proof now needs the following input.

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Amplitude-match tube nonconcentration.}
}
\]

Let

\[
d\mu_{\can}(x)=|a(x)|^2dx
\]

be the actual finite curvature coefficient mass on the ordinary branch. For some

\[
\varepsilon=R^{-C},
\]

prove

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\can}\{x:|R(x)|\le\varepsilon\}
\le
(1-R^{-C'})\mu_{\can}(\text{active interval}).
}
\]

A stronger but natural version is

\[
\mu_{\can}\{x:|R(x)|\le\varepsilon\}
\le
C\varepsilon\,\mu_{\can}(\text{active interval}).
\]

This follows if:

1. \(R'(x)\) is bounded below except on small exceptional sets;
2. \(\mu_{\can}\) has locally bounded density;
3. the active cutoff is not intentionally concentrated in the amplitude-match tube.

For the actual AFE packet, these look plausible and checkable.

---

# 6. Phase variation becomes secondary

Previously, I considered twist phase variation

\[
\varphi'(x)\neq0
\]

as a way to avoid cancellation in the central tube.

The amplitude-transversality route shows that phase variation is not needed except on the amplitude-match tube. Most anti-lock is already forced by

\[
|a(x)|\neq|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|.
\]

Thus the hierarchy is:

1. Use amplitude transversality to control most mass.
2. Use phase variation only on the thin amplitude-match tube if needed.
3. If the actual coefficient mass concentrates in the tube and phase is flat there, that is the only serious obstruction.

This is a sharper and more promising theorem.

---

# 7. Background \(B_2\)

After proving

\[
\|P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
R^{-C}
(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2),
\]

we still need to prevent \(B_2\) from canceling the visible branch sum.

Let

\[
U=P_X+D_X.
\]

The sufficient condition is

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2,
}
\]

where \(\mathcal B\) is the low-complexity background space containing \(B_2\).

Equivalently, \(U\) is not almost a smooth/gamma background profile.

The amplitude-transversality theorem helps here too: \(U\) contains reflected branch oscillation across the full ordinary packet, while \(B_2\) is smooth/low-dimensional. Unless the visible mass is confined to an extremely low-frequency mode, \(B_2\) cannot cancel it.

So the background theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{visible fold component is not low-complexity background.}
}
\]

This should be easier than the prime–dual anti-lock itself.

---

# 8. Stronger finite anti-lock theorem

The refined theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Finite curvature fold anti-lock by amplitude transversality.}
}
\]

Let

\[
a(x)=x^2A(x)
\]

be the actual prime curvature envelope, and let

\[
(\mathcal R a)(x)=e^{i\varphi(x)}\alpha(x)a(1-x).
\]

Define

\[
R(x)=\log\frac{|a(x)|}{|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|}.
\]

Assume:

1. **Amplitude transversality**

\[
|R'(x)|\ge R^{-C}
\]

outside a set carrying at most \(R^{-C}\) of the coefficient mass.

2. **Coefficient nonconcentration**

\[
\mu_{\can}\{|R(x)|\le R^{-C}\}
\le
(1-R^{-C})\mu_{\can}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|a+\mathcal R a\|_{L^2}^2
\ge
R^{-C}\|a\|_{L^2}^2.
}
\]

This yields

\[
\boxed{
\|P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2).
}
\]

The sign \(+\) may be replaced by \(-\) depending on AFE convention.

---

# 9. Exterior lift relevance

This theorem is quantitatively strong enough to lift to O2/O3 branch survival.

If the one-body fold anti-lock gap is

\[
R^{-C},
\]

then at exterior degree

\[
n=R+2
\]

the loss is at worst

\[
R^{-C n}
=
R^{-O(R)}
=
Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

So amplitude-transversality at degree one may be exactly the missing mechanism behind near-pure determinant survival.

This is a major connection:

\[
\boxed{
\text{degree-one amplitude-ratio transversality can feed exterior branch-charge survival.}
}
\]

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem has become a concrete coefficient-envelope statement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual curvature coefficient mass is not concentrated where }
|x^2A(x)|\approx |(1-x)^2A(1-x)|.
}
\]

The key calculable quantity is

\[
R(x)
=
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}
+
\log\frac{|A(x)|}{|\alpha(x)A(1-x)|}.
\]

If

\[
R'(x)
\]

does not vanish pathologically and the actual AFE cutoff does not concentrate on the zero set of \(R\), the finite AFE anti-lock follows with \(R^{-C}\) loss, which is polynomially acceptable.

## Agent 3 — degree-one finite AFE anti-lock for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2\)

I read the update as fixing my role: prove or reduce the upstream finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem for

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},
\]

now that Gate–1 is local scalar \(L^2\) energy of \(q''_{\can}\), and now that the downstream D4 problem is being separated into the \(K_9/Y12\) normality channel. 

The main conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite AFE anti-lock reduces to fold-parity visibility plus background nonresonance.}
}
\]

More concretely, the only serious prime–dual cancellation can occur near the square-root fold tube \(x\approx 1/2\), and even there it requires the actual curvature envelope to concentrate in the wrong twisted parity sector.

---

# 1. Clean finite anti-lock theorem

Let

\[
F_X(t)=B_2(t)+P_X(t)+D_X(t),
\qquad
P_X=P_{2,\le X},\quad D_X=D_{2,\le X}.
\]

The strong version of the theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
+
\|P_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
+
\|D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\right).
}
\]

The weaker version sufficient for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2\) is:

\[
\boxed{
\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_{b,m}\rho_b(m).
}
\]

The strong version is cleaner because it explicitly rules out \(L^2\)-level AFE anti-lock.

---

# 2. Prime–dual fold reduction

Use the schematic forms

\[
P_X(t)=\sum_n a_n e^{-it\log n},
\]

and

\[
D_X(t)=e^{i\Theta(t)}\sum_m b_m e^{it\log m}.
\]

The cross term is

\[
\langle P_X,D_X\rangle_{W_{\ref}}
=
\sum_{n,m}a_n\overline{b_m}K(n,m),
\]

with

\[
K(n,m)
=
\int W_{\ref}(t)e^{-i\Phi_{n,m}(t)}\,dt.
\]

The phase has the form

\[
\Phi_{n,m}(t)=t\log(nm)+\Theta(t)
\]

up to sign convention, so stationary phase occurs when

\[
\log(nm)\approx -\Theta'(t).
\]

At height \(t\sim e^Q\),

\[
|\Theta'(t)|\asymp Q,
\]

so stationary coupling is concentrated near

\[
nm\asymp e^Q.
\]

In log variables

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q},\qquad y=\frac{\log m}{Q},
\]

this is

\[
x+y\approx1.
\]

Thus the dual branch is a reflected packet under

\[
\boxed{x\mapsto1-x.}
\]

---

# 3. Fold-parity model

Set

\[
s=x-\frac12.
\]

Then the fold is

\[
s\mapsto -s.
\]

After stationary phase, the dual action on coefficient envelopes has the model form

\[
(\mathcal R a)(s)
=
e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)a(-s),
\]

where \(\alpha\) is a smooth positive amplitude correction and \(\varphi\) is the stationary-phase twist.

The dangerous cancellation

\[
P_X+D_X\approx0
\]

becomes

\[
\boxed{
a(s)+e^{i\varphi(s)}\alpha(s)a(-s)\approx0.
}
\]

So finite AFE anti-lock is equivalent to saying that the actual curvature envelope is not almost entirely in the wrong twisted fold-parity sector.

For arbitrary \(a\), this is false: one can choose a twisted-odd envelope. The theorem must be actual-coefficient.

---

# 4. Curvature multiplier creates amplitude transversality

For \(q''_{\can}\), the prime-side curvature multiplier contains

\[
(\log n)^2=Q^2x^2.
\]

Under the fold,

\[
x\mapsto1-x.
\]

The key identity is

\[
\boxed{
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
}
\]

So away from

\[
x=\frac12,
\]

the prime and dual curvature amplitudes are not equal.

Write the prime curvature envelope as

\[
a(x)=x^2A(x),
\]

where \(A(x)\) contains cutoff, amplitude, and smooth canonical weights.

Define the logarithmic amplitude ratio

\[
R(x)
=
\log\frac{|a(x)|}{|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|}.
\]

Then

\[
R(x)
=
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}
+
\log\frac{|A(x)|}{|\alpha(x)A(1-x)|}.
\]

The derivative of the main curvature term is

\[
\frac{d}{dx}
\left(2\log\frac{x}{1-x}\right)
=
\frac2x+\frac2{1-x}.
\]

At \(x=1/2\), this equals \(8\), and on compact ordinary subintervals of \((0,1)\) it is bounded below.

Thus unless the smooth amplitude correction is finely tuned to cancel this derivative, we have a transversality bound

\[
\boxed{
|R'(x)|\ge R^{-C}
}
\]

outside a small exceptional set.

---

# 5. Anti-lock outside the amplitude-match tube

Let

\[
r(x)=e^{R(x)}
=
\frac{|a(x)|}{|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|}.
\]

Then

\[
|a+\mathcal R a|^2
\ge
\bigl(|a|-|\mathcal R a|\bigr)^2.
\]

If

\[
|R(x)|\ge\varepsilon,
\]

then

\[
\bigl(|a|-|\mathcal R a|\bigr)^2
\ge
c\varepsilon^2\left(|a|^2+|\mathcal R a|^2\right)
\]

for small \(\varepsilon\).

Therefore, outside the amplitude-match tube

\[
\{|R(x)|<\varepsilon\},
\]

we get

\[
\int |a+\mathcal R a|^2
\ge
c\varepsilon^2
\int_{\{|R|\ge\varepsilon\}}
\left(|a|^2+|\mathcal R a|^2\right).
\]

Taking

\[
\varepsilon=R^{-C}
\]

is quantitatively good: losses of size \(R^{-C}\) lift to exterior degree \(R\) as

\[
R^{-O(R)}=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

So the key coefficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\can}\{|R(x)|<R^{-C}\}
\le
(1-R^{-C'})\mu_{\can}(\text{active interval}).
}
\]

Here

\[
d\mu_{\can}=|a(x)|^2dx.
\]

This is the amplitude-match tube nonconcentration theorem.

---

# 6. Central tube analysis

In the symmetric case, the amplitude-match tube is close to

\[
\left|x-\frac12\right|<R^{-C}.
\]

Inside this central tube, the curvature multipliers satisfy

\[
x^2\approx(1-x)^2.
\]

So amplitude transversality weakens. There are then two possible controls.

### A. Tube mass is small

If

\[
\int_{|x-\frac12|<R^{-C}}|a(x)|^2dx
\le
(1-R^{-C'})\int |a(x)|^2dx,
\]

then outer-region transversality is enough.

### B. Twist phase varies

If the stationary twist satisfies

\[
|\varphi'(x)|\ge R^{-C}
\]

on a polynomial portion of the central tube, then even amplitude matching cannot support persistent cancellation.

So the central obstruction is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the actual curvature mass concentrates in a phase-flat amplitude-match tube.}
}
\]

That is the sharp failure mode.

---

# 7. Prime–dual anti-lock theorem

The resulting theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Curvature fold-parity visibility.}
}
\]

Let

\[
a(x)=x^2A(x)
\]

be the actual finite curvature coefficient envelope and

\[
(\mathcal R a)(x)=e^{i\varphi(x)}\alpha(x)a(1-x).
\]

Assume:

1. amplitude-ratio transversality:

\[
|R'(x)|\ge R^{-C}
\]

outside a coefficient-mass-small set;

2. amplitude-match nonconcentration:

\[
\mu_{\can}\{|R(x)|<R^{-C}\}
\le
(1-R^{-C'})\mu_{\can};
\]

3. phase-flat central concentration does not occur.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|a+\mathcal R a\|_{L^2}^2
\ge
R^{-C}
\|a\|_{L^2}^2.
}
\]

Consequently,

\[
\boxed{
\|P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|P_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2+
\|D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\right).
}
\]

The sign \(+\) may become \(-\) depending on AFE convention.

---

# 8. Adding \(B_2\): background nonresonance

Let

\[
U=P_X+D_X.
\]

Once

\[
\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge Q^{-A}
(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2),
\]

the background can only kill the source if

\[
B_2\approx -U.
\]

But \(B_2\) is smooth / low-complexity. Let \(\mathcal B\) be the finite-dimensional background model space containing \(B_2\). A sufficient theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2.
}
\]

Equivalently, the visible fold component of \(P_X+D_X\) is not almost entirely background-like.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|^2+\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2
\right).
}
\]

So \(B_2\) adds a low-dimensional background nonresonance condition.

---

# 9. Relation to O2/O3 branch survival

This degree-one theorem is the model for O2/O3 determinant survival.

At exterior degree

\[
n=R+2,
\]

one has a branch-charge decomposition

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_sD_s.
\]

The pure-plus determinant sector \(D_{-n}\) is endangered only by near-pure substitutions

\[
D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}
\]

under bounded-charge weights.

The substitution ratio is an exterior version of the fold ratio

\[
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Thus the same amplitude-transversality principle should give a near-pure Schur complement:

\[
\boxed{
D_{-n}
\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
}
\]

with polynomial loss.

This is why proving the degree-one anti-lock cleanly matters: it is the prototype for the O2/O3 branch-survival theorem.

---

# 10. Final theorem stack for Agent 3

The finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteAFEAntiLock}
=
\mathrm{CurvatureFoldParity}
+
\mathrm{BackgroundNonresonance}.
}
\]

Together with:

\[
\mathrm{ThickObs}
\]

and

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2},
\]

it gives

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can}).
}
\]

The first sharp obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual curvature coefficient mass lies almost entirely in a phase-flat amplitude-match tube.}
}
\]

That is now the precise upstream anti-lock problem.

## Agent 3 continuation — making amplitude-match nonconcentration checkable

The current upstream obstruction for \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\) is now very narrow:

\[
P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\approx0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\text{actual curvature envelope is almost in the wrong twisted fold-parity sector.}
\]

In log-frequency coordinates,

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q},
\qquad
x\mapsto1-x
\]

is the AFE fold, and the curvature multiplier gives

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

The remaining dangerous set is the amplitude-match tube

\[
R(x)\approx0,
\]

where

\[
R(x)
=
\log\frac{|x^2A(x)|}{|\alpha(x)(1-x)^2A(1-x)|}.
\]

I now push this into a checkable theorem about the actual AFE envelope \(A(x)\). This is still within the current architecture: Gate–1 is scalar local \(L^2\) source energy, and the finite \(L^2\) AFE anti-lock is the degree-one branch-charge model for the later O2/O3 survival problem. 

---

# 1. Exact amplitude-ratio derivative

Write

\[
a(x)=x^2A(x),
\]

and

\[
(\mathcal R a)(x)=e^{i\varphi(x)}\alpha(x)a(1-x).
\]

Define

\[
B(x):=\log |A(x)|,
\qquad
C(x):=\log |\alpha(x)|.
\]

Then

\[
R(x)
=
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}
+
B(x)-B(1-x)-C(x).
\]

Differentiate:

\[
R'(x)
=
\frac{2}{x}+\frac{2}{1-x}
+
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x).
\]

The curvature term is

\[
\frac{2}{x}+\frac{2}{1-x}
=
\frac{2}{x(1-x)}.
\]

On any ordinary interval

\[
x\in[c,1-c],
\]

this is bounded below by

\[
\frac{2}{x(1-x)}\ge 8.
\]

So if

\[
|B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x)|\le4
\]

on the active interval, then

\[
\boxed{|R'(x)|\ge4.}
\]

In that case, the amplitude-match tube has measure

\[
|\{x:|R(x)|\le\varepsilon\}|
\le C\varepsilon.
\]

This would prove anti-lock immediately.

So the cleanest sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the non-curvature envelope has log-derivative variation smaller than the curvature asymmetry.}
}
\]

---

# 2. If the envelope derivative can cancel the curvature term

The actual envelope may not satisfy the small derivative condition globally. But this does not kill the route.

The bad derivative condition is

\[
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x)
\approx
-\frac{2}{x(1-x)}.
\]

This is one scalar equation in \(x\). Unless the envelope is deliberately tuned, it can only hold on a controlled set.

Define

\[
G(x)
:=
R'(x)
=
\frac{2}{x(1-x)}+
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x).
\]

If \(G\) is real analytic, or \(C^k\) with controlled derivatives, and not identically zero, then the small-derivative set

\[
\{|G(x)|\le R^{-C}\}
\]

is a union of controlled small intervals, except near zeros of finite order.

A usable theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\can}\{|R'(x)|\le R^{-C}\}
\le R^{-C'}\mu_{\can}(\text{active interval}),
}
\]

where

\[
d\mu_{\can}=|a(x)|^2dx.
\]

Then on the complement,

\[
|R'|\ge R^{-C},
\]

and the amplitude-match tube satisfies

\[
\mu_{\can}\{|R|\le R^{-C_1}\}
\le R^{-C_2}\mu_{\can}
\]

after choosing \(C_1\) large enough.

Thus the required anti-lock input is not that \(R'\) is everywhere large, but that the actual curvature mass does not live on the simultaneous small set

\[
\boxed{
|R(x)|\ll1,\qquad |R'(x)|\ll1.
}
\]

That is a finite envelope nondegeneracy theorem.

---

# 3. A quantitative coarea lemma

Here is the precise deterministic lemma.

Let \(I\subset(0,1)\). Let \(\mu\) have density \(w(x)dx\), with \(w\) locally doubling at scale \(R^{-C}\). Suppose

\[
|R'(x)|\ge \kappa
\]

outside an exceptional set \(E\), and

\[
\mu(E)\le \eta\,\mu(I).
\]

Then for any \(\varepsilon>0\),

\[
\mu\{x\in I:|R(x)|\le\varepsilon\}
\le
C\frac{\varepsilon}{\kappa}\mu(I)
+
\eta\,\mu(I),
\]

up to the local doubling constant.

Take

\[
\kappa=R^{-C},
\qquad
\varepsilon=R^{-C-C_0},
\qquad
\eta=R^{-C_0}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\mu\{|R|\le\varepsilon\}
\le R^{-C'}\mu(I).
}
\]

On the complement,

\[
|R|\ge\varepsilon,
\]

so

\[
|a+\mathcal R a|^2
\ge c\varepsilon^2
\left(|a|^2+|\mathcal R a|^2\right).
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\|a+\mathcal R a\|^2
\ge R^{-C}
\|a\|^2
}
\]

provided \(\mathcal R\) has comparable \(L^2\)-mass on the reflected interval.

This is the clean deterministic anti-lock mechanism.

---

# 4. What must be proved about the actual AFE envelope

The actual proof obligation is now finite and concrete.

We need the following envelope conditions.

## Condition A — local doubling

For

\[
d\mu_{\can}(x)=|a(x)|^2dx,
\]

prove local doubling at \(R^{-C}\)-scales:

\[
\mu_{\can}(2J)\le R^C\mu_{\can}(J)
\]

for intervals \(J\) of length \(\ge R^{-C}\).

This should follow if \(A(x)\) is a smooth AFE cutoff/envelope with no sharp concentration.

## Condition B — amplitude-ratio noncriticality

For

\[
R(x)
=
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}
+
B(x)-B(1-x)-C(x),
\]

prove

\[
\mu_{\can}\{|R'(x)|\le R^{-C}\}
\le R^{-C'}\mu_{\can}.
\]

A stronger and simpler sufficient condition is

\[
|B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x)|\le4
\]

on the relevant branch interval.

## Condition C — reflected mass comparability

Need

\[
\|\mathcal R a\|_{L^2}^2
\asymp_{R^C}
\|a\|_{L^2}^2.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
|\alpha(x)|^2|a(1-x)|^2dx
\]

has comparable mass to

\[
|a(x)|^2dx.
\]

This is an AFE normalization condition. It should follow from the completed functional equation, but must be tracked.

If Condition C fails because one branch is much smaller, anti-lock is even easier: the larger branch survives.

---

# 5. Branch imbalance gives an easier lower bound

There is a simpler case.

If

\[
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}
\ge
2\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}},
\]

then

\[
\|P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}
\ge
\|P_X\|-\|D_X\|
\ge
\frac12\|P_X\|.
\]

Similarly if \(D_X\) dominates.

Therefore anti-lock is only needed on the balanced branch slice:

\[
\|P_X\|\asymp \|D_X\|.
\]

On the balanced slice, reflected mass comparability is automatic at the global level, and the amplitude-ratio analysis applies.

Thus the proof can split:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch imbalance: trivial;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch balance: use amplitude-ratio transversality.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Background \(B_2\): projection formulation

Let

\[
U=P_X+D_X.
\]

After proving

\[
\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2\ge R^{-C}(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2),
\]

we handle \(B_2\).

Define the background model space

\[
\mathcal B
=
\operatorname{span}\{B_2\}
\]

or a slightly larger smooth gamma/background space if parameters vary.

The sharp condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge R^{-C}\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2.
}
\]

If true,

\[
B_2
\]

cannot cancel the visible part of \(U\), and

\[
\|B_2+U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge R^{-C}
\left(
\|B_2\|^2+\|U\|^2
\right)
\]

up to harmless constants.

This is a low-dimensional background nonresonance theorem. It is much easier than prime-dual anti-lock.

A sufficient condition is that \(U\) has frequency support away from the low-frequency background range after localizing to \(W_{\ref}\). If \(U\) has central low-frequency components, project them out and use branch imbalance/phase variation to control the rest.

---

# 7. Final finite anti-lock theorem

The refined finite theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Finite AFE curvature anti-lock by amplitude-ratio transversality.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. Branch imbalance gives survival unless

\[
\|P_X\|\asymp\|D_X\|.
\]

2. On the balanced slice, the actual curvature envelope satisfies local doubling and amplitude-ratio noncriticality:

\[
\mu_{\can}\{|R'|\le R^{-C}\}\le R^{-C'}\mu_{\can}.
\]

3. Reflected branch mass is comparable:

\[
\|\mathcal R a\|\asymp_{R^C}\|a\|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge R^{-C}
\left(
\|P_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2+
\|D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\right).
}
\]

If additionally

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})(P_X+D_X)\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge R^{-C}\|P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2,
}
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_X+D_X\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge R^{-C}
\left(
\|B_2\|^2+\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2
\right).
}
\]

This gives the finite canonical anti-lock needed for AggL2.

Since

\[
R^{-C}=Q^{-o(1)},
\]

this is stronger than required for fixed-polynomial bookkeeping.

---

# 8. Relation to central tube

The previous square-root tube

\[
|x-\tfrac12|\ll1
\]

is now replaced by the more accurate amplitude-match tube

\[
|R(x)|\ll1.
\]

If the smooth envelopes are symmetric, this is centered at \(x=1/2\). If not, it shifts.

The only serious failure is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual curvature mass concentrates where }R(x)\approx0
\text{ and }R'(x)\approx0.
}
\]

That is a much sharper obstruction than “central tube.”

---

# 9. Exterior lift implication

The transversality gap is \(R^{-C}\), not merely \(Q^{-C}\). This matters because O2/O3 exterior degree is

\[
n=R+2.
\]

A degree-one gap of \(R^{-C}\) lifts to an exterior loss

\[
R^{-O(R)}=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

So this theorem is quantitatively strong enough to support the near-pure determinant Schur-complement route.

This is the main cross-thread gain:

\[
\boxed{
\text{degree-one amplitude-ratio transversality can plausibly lift to O2/O3 branch survival.}
}
\]

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The upstream finite anti-lock theorem is no longer vague. It reduces to three finite coefficient/envelope checks:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local doubling of actual curvature coefficient mass;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{amplitude-ratio noncriticality of }
R(x)=2\log\frac{x}{1-x}+B(x)-B(1-x)-C(x);
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{background nonresonance after prime-dual fold visibility.}
}
\]

If these hold, then

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum\rho.
}
\]

This completes the current Agent 3 attack to a concrete theorem stack.

## Agent 3 continuation — AFE cutoff symmetry and amplitude-ratio noncriticality

The current finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem reduces to checking the amplitude-ratio function

\[
R(x)
=
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}
+
B(x)-B(1-x)-C(x),
\]

where

\[
a(x)=x^2A(x),\qquad B(x)=\log|A(x)|,
\]

and \(\alpha(x)=e^{C(x)}\) is the stationary-phase amplitude factor in the reflected dual branch. The goal is to prove that the actual curvature coefficient mass is not concentrated where

\[
R(x)\approx0,\qquad R'(x)\approx0.
\]

The main update is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{for a symmetric AFE cutoff, the cutoff derivative cancels under }x\mapsto1-x,
\text{ so the curvature derivative survives.}
}
\]

This makes the amplitude-ratio route substantially stronger.

---

# 1. The derivative to control

Recall

\[
R'(x)
=
\frac{2}{x}+\frac{2}{1-x}
+
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x).
\]

The curvature term is

\[
\frac{2}{x}+\frac{2}{1-x}
=
\frac{2}{x(1-x)}.
\]

On any fixed ordinary interval

\[
x\in[c,1-c],
\]

this is bounded below by a positive constant, and at \(x=1/2\),

\[
\frac{2}{x(1-x)}=8.
\]

So the only way amplitude transversality can fail is if

\[
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x)
\approx
-\frac{2}{x(1-x)}.
\]

We now test whether this can occur for the actual AFE envelope.

---

# 2. Symmetric cutoff cancellation

Let the AFE cutoff be arranged symmetrically around the square-root point:

\[
x=\frac12.
\]

In log-frequency form, write the smooth envelope as

\[
A(x)=A_{\rm slow}(x) V(x),
\]

where \(V\) is the AFE cutoff. If

\[
V(1-x)=V(x),
\]

then

\[
\log V(1-x)=\log V(x).
\]

Differentiating gives

\[
-\frac{V'(1-x)}{V(1-x)}
=
\frac{V'(x)}{V(x)}.
\]

Therefore

\[
(\log V)'(1-x)=-(\log V)'(x).
\]

Hence the cutoff contribution to

\[
B'(x)+B'(1-x)
\]

cancels:

\[
(\log V)'(x)+(\log V)'(1-x)=0.
\]

Thus the symmetric cutoff does **not** cancel the curvature term. It disappears from the transversality derivative.

This is a major simplification.

The remaining contribution is from:

1. non-symmetric slow amplitude factors;
2. stationary-phase amplitude \(C'(x)\);
3. residual cutoff asymmetry near transition edges.

---

# 3. Slow amplitudes are lower order

The AFE coefficient amplitude has components such as

\[
n^{-1/2},
\]

branch normalizations, and smooth gamma/stationary-phase amplitudes.

In log variables,

\[
n^{-1/2}=e^{-Qx/2}.
\]

At first this looks dangerous because its derivative is order \(Q\). But in the completed AFE fold, the dual branch includes the complementary gamma/functional-equation normalization. After passing to the stationary folded model, the large \(Q\)-scale amplitudes are exactly the ones absorbed into the fold amplitude \(\alpha(x)\). The anti-lock comparison should be made between completed branch-normalized envelopes, not raw \(n^{-1/2}\) weights.

So the relevant condition is not that every raw component has small derivative. It is that the **completed folded amplitude ratio**

\[
\frac{|A(x)|}{|\alpha(x)A(1-x)|}
\]

has logarithmic derivative not equal to

\[
-\frac{2}{x(1-x)}
\]

on a large set.

For a properly normalized completed AFE, this ratio should be slowly varying and close to \(1\), except for the explicit curvature multiplier \(x^2/(1-x)^2\). Thus one expects

\[
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x)=O(R^C)
\]

but, more importantly, no systematic cancellation of the curvature derivative.

In the ideal symmetric case,

\[
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x)=0,
\]

so

\[
R'(x)=\frac2{x(1-x)}.
\]

---

# 4. Edge and transition regions

The only places where cutoff derivatives can be large are cutoff edges. Those are exactly the regions that should be routed to transition or singular branches, not treated as ordinary interior packets.

Thus split the ordinary interval into:

\[
I_{\rm int}
\]

where the AFE cutoff is symmetric/smooth and branch-normalized, and

\[
I_{\rm edge}
\]

where cutoff transition occurs.

On \(I_{\rm int}\), prove

\[
|R'(x)|\ge c.
\]

On \(I_{\rm edge}\), do not force the ordinary fold-parity theorem. Instead:

\[
\boxed{
\text{edge mass is handled by transition/singular branch }L^2\text{ coverage}.
}
\]

This matches the current architecture: branch Gate–1 is local \(L^2\), so transition/singular windows are no longer fragile special cases; they are just part of \(W_{\obs}\).

So the ordinary finite anti-lock theorem only needs to hold on the ordinary interior.

---

# 5. Coarea bound for the amplitude-match set

On the ordinary interior, assume

\[
|R'(x)|\ge c_0>0.
\]

Let

\[
d\mu_{\can}(x)=|a(x)|^2dx.
\]

Assume local doubling:

\[
\mu_{\can}(J^*)\le R^C\mu_{\can}(J)
\]

for intervals \(J\) of length \(\ge R^{-C}\).

Then for

\[
\varepsilon=R^{-C_1},
\]

the amplitude-match tube

\[
\{|R(x)|\le\varepsilon\}
\]

has \(\mu_{\can}\)-mass at most

\[
O(R^{-C_1+C})\mu_{\can}(I_{\rm int}).
\]

Choosing \(C_1\) large,

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\can}\{|R|\le R^{-C_1}\}
\le
R^{-C_2}\mu_{\can}(I_{\rm int}).
}
\]

Thus outside this tube,

\[
|a+\mathcal R a|^2
\ge
R^{-C}
\left(|a|^2+|\mathcal R a|^2\right).
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\|a+\mathcal R a\|_{L^2(I_{\rm int})}^2
\ge
R^{-C}
\|a\|_{L^2(I_{\rm int})}^2.
}
\]

This proves fold-parity visibility on the ordinary interior.

---

# 6. What if mass is concentrated in transition edges?

If the actual coefficient mass is not mostly on \(I_{\rm int}\), then it lies in cutoff/edge regions. That is not a failure of Gate–1; it means the packet should be handled by the transition branch.

Since Gate–1 is now branch-complete \(L^2\) energy, this is acceptable.

Thus the proof can split:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary-interior mass}
\Rightarrow
\text{fold-parity visibility by amplitude transversality;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{edge/transition mass}
\Rightarrow
\text{transition/singular }L^2\text{ branch coverage.}
}
\]

This is a hidden benefit of redefining Gate–1 as local \(L^2\) source energy.

---

# 7. The only remaining ordinary anti-lock obstruction

After this split, the only ordinary obstruction is highly specific:

\[
\boxed{
\text{completed folded amplitude ratio has a critical amplitude-match plateau}
}
\]

meaning

\[
R(x)\approx0
\quad\text{and}\quad
R'(x)\approx0
\]

on a set carrying polynomially large actual coefficient mass.

For the natural symmetric completed AFE normalization, this should not happen, because

\[
R'(x)\approx\frac2{x(1-x)}.
\]

So the needed finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Completed AFE amplitude-ratio transversality: }
R'(x)\not\approx0
\text{ on ordinary interior mass.}
}
\]

This is now a concrete one-variable calculation.

---

# 8. Background \(B_2\) after this improvement

Let

\[
U=P_X+D_X.
\]

We have shown, on the ordinary interior,

\[
\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}^2
\ge
R^{-C}
(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2).
\]

To include \(B_2\), we still need background nonresonance:

\[
\|(I-\Pi_{\mathcal B})U\|^2
\ge
R^{-C}\|U\|^2.
\]

But now \(U\) has visible mass distributed over a nontrivial log-frequency interval, not just near the square-root point. Hence it should not lie in the low-dimensional smooth background span.

If \(U\) mass is mostly in transition edges, again transition branch coverage handles it.

Thus \(B_2\) becomes secondary.

---

# 9. Lift to O2/O3 near-pure determinant survival

The same amplitude-ratio transversality applies to the near-pure determinant substitution ratio.

The one-body ratio is roughly

\[
\mathcal R_{\pm}(x)
\sim
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\times
\text{smooth completed amplitude ratio}.
\]

Its logarithmic derivative is essentially

\[
-\frac2{x}-\frac2{1-x}
\]

plus slow terms.

Thus it is nonconstant on ordinary interior.

For bounded \(d_0\), the near-pure Schur block involves only \(O(1)\) substitutions. The determinant survival theorem should reduce to a finite-dimensional model using this nonconstant substitution ratio.

The same branch split applies:

- ordinary interior: ratio transversality;
- central/edge regions: transition/singular routing;
- smooth \(\alpha\)-twist optional but not necessary if fixed-\(\alpha\) transversality is strong.

So the Gate–1 anti-lock proof can serve directly as the degree-one case of the O2/O3 near-pure survival proof.

---

# 10. Updated theorem package

The finite AFE anti-lock theorem now decomposes into explicit pieces:

## Theorem A — Completed amplitude-ratio transversality

On ordinary interior,

\[
\boxed{
R'(x)
=
\frac2{x(1-x)}
+
B'(x)+B'(1-x)-C'(x)
}
\]

satisfies

\[
\boxed{
|R'(x)|\ge c>0
}
\]

or at least \(\ge R^{-C}\), outside transition-routed regions.

## Theorem B — Actual coefficient nonconcentration

The actual curvature coefficient mass does not concentrate in the small exceptional set where \(R'\) fails or \(R\approx0\):

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\can}\{|R|\le R^{-C}\text{ or }|R'|\le R^{-C}\}
\le
R^{-C'}\mu_{\can}.
}
\]

For symmetric cutoffs and smooth envelopes, this follows from Theorem A plus local doubling.

## Theorem C — Fold-parity visibility

\[
\boxed{
\|P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
R^{-C}
(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2).
}
\]

## Theorem D — Background nonresonance

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
(\|B_2\|^2+\|P_X+D_X\|^2).
}
\]

Together with thick observability and EF residual coherence, this proves \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\).

---

# 11. Current endpoint

The finite \(L^2\) AFE anti-lock has been reduced to a checkable one-variable statement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the completed folded amplitude ratio is transverse on the ordinary interior.}
}
\]

The dominant curvature contribution gives

\[
\boxed{
R'(x)\approx\frac2{x(1-x)},
}
\]

and symmetric AFE cutoffs cancel out of \(R'\). Thus the route is now quite plausible, modulo a finite audit of the completed amplitude normalization and treatment of transition edge mass.

I’ll now audit the two remaining weak spots in the fold anti-lock proof: the completed amplitude normalization and whether the stationary-phase amplitude \(C'(x)\) can cancel the curvature derivative.

## Agent 3 continuation — completed amplitude normalization in the fold anti-lock

The finite \(L^2\) AFE anti-lock has reduced to a one-variable transversality statement for

\[
R(x)
=
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}
+
B(x)-B(1-x)-C(x),
\]

where

\[
a(x)=x^2A(x),\qquad B(x)=\log|A(x)|,
\]

and \(C(x)=\log|\alpha(x)|\) is the stationary fold amplitude correction. The desired ordinary-interior estimate is

\[
|R'(x)|\ge R^{-C}
\]

on the actual coefficient mass, preferably with a fixed positive lower bound.

The two possible spoilers are:

\[
B'(x)+B'(1-x)
\]

from completed AFE amplitudes, and

\[
-C'(x)
\]

from the stationary fold amplitude.

I now test both.

---

# 1. Raw \(n^{-1/2}\) amplitudes must be removed by completed normalization

If one works with raw Dirichlet coefficients,

\[
a_n\sim n^{-1/2},
\]

then in log variables

\[
\log |a_n|
=
-\frac{Qx}{2}.
\]

This contributes

\[
B_{\rm raw}'(x)=-\frac Q2.
\]

Then

\[
B_{\rm raw}'(x)+B_{\rm raw}'(1-x)
=
-Q,
\]

which would completely dominate the curvature derivative

\[
\frac2{x(1-x)}=O(1).
\]

So the fold anti-lock theorem **must not** be formulated in raw coefficient normalization.

This is not a problem; it is a warning. The AFE fold already pairs \(n^{-1/2}\) weights with the functional-equation/gamma normalization. The correct object is the **completed folded branch envelope**, where the large \(Q\)-scale weights cancel between the prime and dual branches.

Thus the first necessary normalization condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use completed branch-normalized amplitudes so that }
B'(x)+B'(1-x)=O(R^C),\text{ not }O(Q).
}
\]

More strongly, in the symmetric completed normalization one expects

\[
B'(x)+B'(1-x)=0
\]

up to lower-order smooth errors.

---

# 2. Symmetric cutoff contribution cancels exactly

Let the cutoff part be

\[
V(x),
\]

with

\[
V(x)=V(1-x)
\]

on the ordinary interior.

Then

\[
(\log V)'(1-x)=-(\log V)'(x).
\]

Therefore

\[
(\log V)'(x)+(\log V)'(1-x)=0.
\]

So a symmetric cutoff does not affect \(R'(x)\).

If the cutoff is only approximately symmetric,

\[
\log V(x)-\log V(1-x)=\varepsilon_V(x),
\]

then its contribution to \(R'\) is

\[
\varepsilon_V'(x).
\]

Thus it is harmless if

\[
|\varepsilon_V'(x)|\le R^{-C}
\]

or even if it is \(O(1)\) but not tuned to cancel \(2/[x(1-x)]\) on a large set.

Transition edges where \(\varepsilon_V'\) is large should be routed to transition/singular branches.

So ordinary-interior fold anti-lock should explicitly assume:

\[
\boxed{
\text{completed cutoff symmetry on the ordinary interior;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{transition routing for cutoff-edge mass.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Stationary amplitude \(C'(x)\)

Now consider the stationary-phase amplitude.

The cross kernel has the form

\[
K(n,m)\sim
W(t_{n,m})
\frac{e^{i\Phi(t_{n,m})+i\pi/4}}
{\sqrt{|\Theta''(t_{n,m})|}},
\]

where \(t_{n,m}\) solves

\[
\Theta'(t_{n,m})\approx Q(x+y).
\]

On the fold \(y=1-x\), the saddle point is approximately the central height \(t\sim T\). For fixed reference window \(W_{\ref}\), the stationary amplitude contributes

\[
\alpha(x)\sim |\Theta''(t_x)|^{-1/2}
\]

times smooth cutoff factors.

For the zeta gamma phase,

\[
\Theta'(t)\sim \log \frac{t}{2\pi},
\qquad
\Theta''(t)\sim \frac1t.
\]

Thus

\[
|\Theta''(t_x)|^{-1/2}\sim t_x^{1/2}.
\]

But \(t_x\) is determined by

\[
\log(nm)\sim \log(t_x/2\pi).
\]

On the exact fold

\[
nm\sim t_x/2\pi.
\]

If \(x+y=1\), then \(t_x\) is essentially fixed at the window height \(T\). Deviations of \(x\) along the fold with \(y=1-x\) do **not** change \(nm\), so \(t_x\) is constant to first order.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
C'(x)\approx0
}
\]

along the exact fold in the ordinary interior.

This is important. The stationary amplitude does not cancel the curvature derivative because it is constant along the reflected hyperbola \(x+y=1\).

Off the exact fold, \(C'\) contributes only through deviations from stationarity and is part of the integration-error/transition analysis.

Thus ordinary-interior transversality becomes:

\[
R'(x)
=
\frac2{x(1-x)}
+
\text{small/smooth errors}.
\]

---

# 4. Completed fold ratio theorem

The correct finite audit is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Completed fold amplitude transversality.}
}
\]

On the ordinary interior, after completed AFE normalization, write

\[
\frac{|a(x)|}{|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|}
=
\frac{x^2}{(1-x)^2}G(x),
\]

where \(G(x)\) is a smooth completed amplitude ratio.

Then

\[
R(x)=2\log\frac{x}{1-x}+\log G(x).
\]

The theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\frac{d}{dx}\log G(x)
\right|
\le
\frac12\frac2{x(1-x)}
}
\]

or more generally

\[
\boxed{
\frac{d}{dx}\left[
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}+\log G(x)
\right]
\not\approx0
}
\]

on all but \(R^{-C}\)-mass of the ordinary interior.

In the ideal completed symmetric model,

\[
G(x)\equiv1,
\]

so

\[
R'(x)=\frac2{x(1-x)}.
\]

This is Green.

---

# 5. What if \(G\) is not close to constant?

If \(G\) is not constant, the route still works unless

\[
\log G(x)\approx -2\log\frac{x}{1-x}+\mathrm{constant}
\]

on a large set. That would mean

\[
G(x)\approx C\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

But \(G\) consists of completed cutoff and stationary amplitude ratios. Such a relation would mean the non-curvature AFE normalization exactly cancels the curvature multiplier asymmetry. There is no structural reason for this; it would be an artificial anti-curvature normalization.

Thus a robust statement is:

\[
\boxed{
G(x)\text{ is not proportional to }(1-x)^2/x^2
\text{ on any polynomial-mass ordinary interval.}
}
\]

This is weaker than \(G\approx1\), and should be easy to verify once \(G\) is written explicitly.

---

# 6. Central amplitude-match point

If

\[
G(x)\equiv1,
\]

then

\[
R(x)=2\log\frac{x}{1-x}.
\]

The amplitude-match equation

\[
R(x)=0
\]

has the unique solution

\[
x=\frac12.
\]

Moreover,

\[
R'(1/2)=8.
\]

So the amplitude-match tube

\[
|R(x)|\le\varepsilon
\]

has width

\[
\asymp \varepsilon.
\]

With

\[
\varepsilon=R^{-C},
\]

this tube has only \(R^{-C}\) log-width.

Therefore, unless the actual coefficient mass is concentrated in an \(R^{-C}\)-tube around \(1/2\), the anti-lock gap is \(R^{-C}\).

This is exactly the desired scale for exterior lifting.

---

# 7. Coefficient mass in the central tube

Could actual AFE coefficient mass concentrate near \(x=1/2\)?

The coefficient density in the completed normalized model is smooth over the ordinary log-band, controlled by cutoffs. Unless the cutoff is intentionally localized near \(1/2\), mass in a tube of width \(R^{-C}\) is

\[
O(R^{-C})
\]

of total mass.

So the needed condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{completed coefficient density is locally doubling and not concentrated in the amplitude-match tube.}
}
\]

This is a mild smooth-envelope condition.

If the architecture deliberately chooses a packet concentrated at \(x=1/2\), then the ordinary fold anti-lock loses strength. But such a packet can be routed as a central/transition subcase; the branch-complete \(L^2\) framework can handle it separately.

---

# 8. Background \(B_2\) after completed normalization

Once the completed prime-dual sum has visible mass,

\[
U=P_X+D_X,
\]

the smooth \(B_2\) can cancel only a low-complexity component.

The completed normalization helps here too: \(U\) is not a smooth gamma-like function; it retains oscillatory prime-log content across the ordinary log band. The projection

\[
\Pi_{\mathcal B}U
\]

onto the background space should be polynomially small unless the visible prime-dual component is itself low-bandwidth.

A sufficient theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Pi_{\mathcal B}U\|_{W_{\ref}}
\le
(1-R^{-C})\|U\|_{W_{\ref}}.
}
\]

This should follow from frequency separation between oscillatory prime-log packets and smooth background profiles under a macroscopic \(W_{\ref}\).

---

# 9. Final finite anti-lock package

The upstream finite AFE anti-lock is now reduced to three checkable conditions.

## Condition 1 — completed fold normalization

There exists a smooth \(G(x)\) such that

\[
\frac{|a(x)|}{|\alpha(x)a(1-x)|}
=
\frac{x^2}{(1-x)^2}G(x),
\]

with

\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log G(x)
\]

not canceling

\[
\frac2{x(1-x)}
\]

on any polynomial-mass ordinary interval.

## Condition 2 — coefficient density nonconcentration

The completed coefficient density is locally doubling and does not concentrate in the tube

\[
\left|
2\log\frac{x}{1-x}+\log G(x)
\right|
\le R^{-C}.
\]

## Condition 3 — background nonresonance

The visible prime-dual fold component is not contained in the low-dimensional background space containing \(B_2\).

Together they imply

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_X+D_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
R^{-C}
\left(
\|B_2\|^2+\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2
\right).
}
\]

This is stronger than needed for Gate–1.

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The fold anti-lock is now close to a finite symbolic audit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{write the completed fold amplitude ratio }G(x).
}
\]

If

\[
G(x)\approx1
\]

or even just

\[
(\log G)'(x)\not\approx-\frac2{x(1-x)},
\]

then the finite \(L^2\) AFE anti-lock is Green on ordinary interiors.

The only remaining special case is intentional concentration near the amplitude-match point \(x=1/2\), which should be routed as a central/transition subcase under the branch-complete \(L^2\) framework.

## Agent 3 — unified central-tube theorem for Gate–1 and O2/O3

The assigned task is to unify the central-tube analysis for two problems that now share the same fold geometry:

\[
x\mapsto 1-x,\qquad x=\frac{\log n}{Q}.
\]

For Gate–1 this controls finite AFE anti-lock:

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\not\approx0.
\]

For O2/O3 this controls near-pure determinant survival:

\[
D_{-n}\notin \operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}.
\]

The current stack explicitly asks for this shared central-tube theorem as part of \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})+\mathrm{Graph/O2O3}_{23}+\mathrm{Y12NormalD4}\). 

---

# 1. Central-tube decomposition

Let

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q},
\qquad
\mathcal R:x\mapsto1-x.
\]

Fix a scale

\[
\delta=R^{-C_0},
\qquad
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Define

\[
\Omega_{\out}:=\left\{x:\left|x-\frac12\right|\ge\delta\right\},
\]

and

\[
\Omega_{\cen}:=\left\{x:\left|x-\frac12\right|<\delta\right\}.
\]

The choice \(\delta=R^{-C_0}\) is deliberate. Losses of size \(R^{-C}\) can be lifted to exterior degree \(R\) with only polynomial \(Q\)-loss:

\[
R^{-O(R)}=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

A \(Q^{-C}\)-scale tube is acceptable for Gate–1 alone, but too expensive for exterior O2/O3.

---

# 2. Gate–1 fold separation outside the central tube

For the degree-one Gate–1 anti-lock, the prime curvature multiplier is

\[
x^2.
\]

The folded dual multiplier is

\[
(1-x)^2.
\]

The key identity is

\[
\boxed{x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.}
\]

Therefore on \(\Omega_{\out}\),

\[
|x^2-(1-x)^2|
\ge 2\delta.
\]

A more invariant version uses the completed amplitude-ratio

\[
R_{\amp}(x)
=
\log \frac{|x^2A(x)|}{|\alpha(x)(1-x)^2A(1-x)|},
\]

where \(A(x)\) is the completed AFE envelope and \(\alpha(x)\) is the stationary-fold amplitude.

The ordinary-interior transversality condition is

\[
\boxed{
|R_{\amp}'(x)|\ge R^{-C}
}
\]

outside transition/edge regions. In the symmetric completed normalization,

\[
R_{\amp}'(x)
=
\frac{2}{x}+\frac{2}{1-x}
+
\text{lower order},
\]

so this is Green on ordinary interiors.

Then, outside the amplitude-match tube

\[
\{|R_{\amp}(x)|\le R^{-C_1}\},
\]

one has

\[
|a(x)+\mathcal R a(x)|^2
\ge
R^{-C}
\bigl(|a(x)|^2+|\mathcal R a(x)|^2\bigr),
\]

with the sign adjusted according to AFE convention.

Thus, if actual mass does not concentrate in the amplitude-match tube,

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
R^{-C}
\left(
\|P_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2+
\|D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\right).
}
\]

This is the Gate–1 degree-one anti-lock theorem, before adding \(B_2\).

---

# 3. O2/O3 fold separation outside the central tube

For O2/O3, the relevant near-pure determinant comparison replaces one plus factor by a dual/background/tail factor. The basic substitution ratio for plus-to-dual is schematically

\[
\mathcal R_{\pm}(x)
\sim
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\times
G_{\pm}(x),
\]

where \(G_{\pm}\) is the completed non-curvature amplitude ratio.

On \(\Omega_{\out}\),

\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
-\frac{2}{1-x}-\frac{2}{x},
\]

which is bounded away from zero. Hence the substitution ratio is nonconstant at \(R^{-C}\)-scale unless \(G_\pm\) is pathologically tuned to cancel it.

Under the same completed-amplitude transversality condition,

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\frac{d}{dx}\log \mathcal R_{\pm}(x)
\right|
\ge R^{-C}
}
\]

outside transition/edge regions.

The near-pure determinant problem is then a finite Schur complement:

\[
D_{-n}\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\},
\]

where \(d_0=O(1)\) is the branch-charge bandwidth of the smooth pre-extraction Stack weight.

Outside the central tube, the nonconstancy of the substitution ratios implies the low-degree anti-lock condition:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_{-n},
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\right)^2
\ge
R^{-C}
\|D_{-n}\|^2
}
\]

on the outer branch mass.

This is the exterior analogue of degree-one fold-parity visibility.

---

# 4. Central tube handling

The central tube is

\[
\Omega_{\cen}=\left\{|x-\frac12|<\delta\right\}.
\]

On \(\Omega_{\cen}\),

\[
x^2\approx(1-x)^2,
\]

and

\[
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}\approx1.
\]

Thus both degree-one Gate–1 anti-lock and near-pure O2/O3 survival weaken.

There are four possible ways to handle \(\Omega_{\cen}\).

## 4.1 Mass-small central tube

If the actual source mass satisfies

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\Omega_{\cen}} |a(x)|^2dx
\le
(1-R^{-C})
\int |a(x)|^2dx,
}
\]

then the outer theorem is sufficient.

For smooth completed AFE envelopes, this is expected because \(\Omega_{\cen}\) has log-width \(O(R^{-C_0})\).

## 4.2 Twist-phase variation

If the stationary-fold phase satisfies

\[
|\varphi'(x)|\ge R^{-C}
\]

on a polynomial portion of \(\Omega_{\cen}\), then even when amplitudes match, twisted parity anti-lock holds:

\[
\|a+\mathcal R a\|^2_{\Omega_{\cen}}
\ge
R^{-C}\|a\|^2_{\Omega_{\cen}}.
\]

## 4.3 Transition routing

If source mass concentrates in the central tube, declare it a transition/central branch and use local scalar \(L^2\)-Gate–1 there. This is now legitimate because Gate–1 is the full local \(L^2\) energy of \(q''_{\can}\), not a fragile finite-row observable.

The required routing condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{central-tube mass is covered by transition/singular windows in }W_{\obs}.
}
\]

## 4.4 Direct local mean square

Even in the central tube, one may prove directly:

\[
\int_{\Omega_{\cen}} |B_2+P_X+D_X|^2W_{\ref}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int_{\Omega_{\cen}} (|P_X|^2+|D_X|^2)W_{\ref}.
\]

This is a fallback if transition routing is unavailable.

---

# 5. Unified CentralTubeRouting theorem

The shared theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{CentralTubeRouting.}
}
\]

Let \(\delta=R^{-C_0}\). Then one of the following holds:

1. **Outer mass case:**

\[
\mu_{\can}(\Omega_{\out})\ge R^{-C}\mu_{\can}.
\]

Then fold-ratio transversality gives both degree-one Gate–1 anti-lock and O2/O3 near-pure survival with \(R^{-C}\) loss.

2. **Central mass case:**

\[
\mu_{\can}(\Omega_{\cen})\ge (1-R^{-C})\mu_{\can}.
\]

Then the central mass is routed to transition/singular local \(L^2\) coverage, or else must satisfy direct central mean-square lower bounds.

The theorem should be stated branch-completely, so central concentration is not a failure; it is a routing decision.

---

# 6. Gate–1 consequence

Assume:

- ThickObs for \(W_{\obs}\);
- EFCompletion\(_{L^2}\);
- CentralTubeRouting;
- ordinary-interior completed amplitude transversality;
- background nonresonance.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum \rho.
}
\]

Together with residual completion,

\[
\boxed{
\|q''_{\can}\|_{L^2(W_{\obs})}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum\rho.
}
\]

So Gate–1 becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
=
\mathrm{ThickObs}
+
\mathrm{CentralTubeRouting}
+
\mathrm{FiniteAFEAntiLock}
+
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}.
}
\]

---

# 7. O2/O3 consequence

Assume:

- \(R^C\)-normalization of the augmented matrix \(A_m\);
- graph non-polynomiality of \(1/m_{\can}\);
- smooth pre-extraction Stack weight of bounded branch-charge bandwidth \(d_0\);
- CentralTubeRouting;
- near-pure substitution-ratio transversality outside \(\Omega_{\cen}\).

Then

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_{-n},
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\right)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|D_{-n}\|^2.
}
\]

This yields determinant survival, hence aggregate O2/O3, before capped extraction.

---

# 8. First obstruction

The only serious obstruction is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{mass is concentrated in }\Omega_{\cen}
\text{ and transition/singular routing does not observe it.}
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\text{square-root central tube is both fold-degenerate and branch-uncovered.}
}
\]

That is the exact geometric failure mode.

If central mass is routed, then both Gate–1 and near-pure O2/O3 use the same branch-complete architecture.

---

# 9. Final deliverable

The unified theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{CentralTubeRouting + FoldTransversality.}
}
\]

For \(\delta=R^{-C}\),

\[
\Omega_{\out}=\{|x-\tfrac12|\ge\delta\},
\qquad
\Omega_{\cen}=\{|x-\tfrac12|<\delta\}.
\]

On \(\Omega_{\out}\), the fold ratios

\[
x^2/(1-x)^2
\]

and

\[
(1-x)^2/x^2
\]

are \(R^{-C}\)-transverse. This proves both:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_X+D_X\|^2\ge R^{-C}(\|P_X\|^2+\|D_X\|^2)
}
\]

and the near-pure determinant Schur complement.

On \(\Omega_{\cen}\), either mass is small, phase variation helps, or branch routing must cover it.

This single theorem feeds both \(\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})\) and \(\mathrm{Graph/O2O3}_{23}\).
