---

# Agent 3 — rank-two D4 rescue audit

## 1. The key correction: rank depends on which quotient is used

There are two different rank questions.

### Raw live-pair rank

In the live \(Y12\)-fiber

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y),
\]

D4 gives

\[
s_7^{D4}=Hc_7\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^{Y,D4}=H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Thus the two D4 columns are

\[
v_G^{\mathrm{raw}}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\\
\beta_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
v_{G2}^{\mathrm{raw}}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
0\\
\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Their determinant is

\[
\det[v_G^{\mathrm{raw}},v_{G2}^{\mathrm{raw}}]
=
c_7\alpha_9.
\]

Since the D4 clean matrix has

\[
c_7\alpha_9\neq0,
\]

the **raw live-pair rank is two** if both coordinates \(s_7\) and \(s_9^Y\) survive in the realized carrier quotient.

But raw rank two is not yet a forbidden rank two.

### Forbidden rank after actual-source graph quotient

If actual-source coherence contains the graph

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}},
\]

then the graph direction

\[
(1,\rho_Y)
\]

is coherent/allowed. The forbidden local normal is one-dimensional, represented by

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
\]

Applying \(\ell_Y\) to the D4 columns gives the single moving wall

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

Therefore, **after quotienting the coherent graph**, the local forbidden codomain is one-dimensional and the D4 rank is at most one.

So the rank-two rescue is available only if the \(s_7\)-direction is also forbidden, not coherent.

---

## 2. Precise local rank classification

Let

\[
B_Y
\subset \mathbb C^2
\]

be the actual-source/nuisance image in the live fiber. The relevant local forbidden quotient is

\[
\mathbb C^2/\operatorname{ran}B_Y.
\]

D4 has raw column matrix

\[
M_{D4}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7 & 0\\
\beta_9 & \alpha_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The true local forbidden rank is

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Pi_{B_Y^\perp}M_{D4}
\right).
\]

Now classify by \(\operatorname{ran}B_Y\).

---

## 3. Case analysis

### Case 0: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=0\)

No actual-source coherent direction appears in the live fiber.

Then

\[
\Pi_{B_Y^\perp}=I,
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{D4}=2
\]

because

\[
c_7\alpha_9\neq0.
\]

This is the clean rank-two rescue case.

The wall becomes vector-valued:

\[
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Equivalently, after a bounded change of basis,

\[
\|D4_{\mathrm{forb}}\|^2
\asymp
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)
\]

up to polynomial factors.

This would bypass the moving-line wall

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G
\]

and its forced ODE trap.

### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=1\), nonvertical graph

Suppose

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Then the quotient is one-dimensional. The normal covector is

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7.
\]

The rank is exactly one because

\[
\ell_Y(v_{G2}^{\mathrm{raw}})
=
\alpha_9\neq0.
\]

The wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is the current default architecture.

Rank-two rescue is not available in this case, but the \(Y12\) channel survives.

### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=1\), vertical graph

Suppose

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y=\mathbb C(0,1).
\]

Then the \(Y12\) vertical direction is coherent. The \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) column is killed.

However, the \(s_7\)-direction may remain forbidden. The quotient is still one-dimensional, represented by \(\ell_7\), and

\[
\ell_7(D4)=Hc_7\Gamma_G.
\]

So this does **not** give the \(Y12\) rank-one channel. It gives a possible \(C_7\)-only forbidden channel if \(s_7\) is genuinely forbidden.

That is a different downstream route and probably weaker, because it loses the clean independent \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) wall.

### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2\)

Then

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y=\mathbb C^2.
\]

Both \(s_7\) and \(s_9^Y\) are actual-source coherent. The local \(Y12\) D4 channel dies.

No rank-two or rank-one wall survives in this fiber.

---

## 4. Gram criterion for rank two

Let \(P_Y\) denote projection away from nuisance/source-linear terms before quotienting by \(B_Y\). Let

\[
\tilde v_G=P_Y(c_7e_7+\beta_9e_Y),
\]

\[
\tilde v_{G2}=P_Y(\alpha_9e_Y).
\]

The rank-two condition is

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle \tilde v_G,\tilde v_G\rangle
&
\langle \tilde v_G,\tilde v_{G2}\rangle\\
\langle \tilde v_{G2},\tilde v_G\rangle
&
\langle \tilde v_{G2},\tilde v_{G2}\rangle
\end{pmatrix}
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

But if we additionally quotient by the coherent graph \(\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)\), the codomain becomes one-dimensional and this \(2\times2\) Gram necessarily drops rank.

Thus the rank-two Gram test must be applied **before** imposing a nonzero actual-source graph. It is only relevant when \(B_Y\) has rank zero, or when there is another independent normal component outside the \((s_7,s_9^Y)\) graph model.

---

## 5. Does \(y_7\) survive separately?

The rank-two rescue needs the \(s_7\)-direction to be forbidden, not merely live.

That is the same as saying

\[
e_7\notin
\mathcal A_{\mathrm{allowed}}
\]

after nuisance/source-linear quotient.

But in the current one-slope architecture, the expected allowed fiber direction is precisely

\[
(1,\rho_Y).
\]

That includes an \(s_7\)-component. Therefore \(e_7\) is usually part of the coherent graph and not independently forbidden.

So the expected rank is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank one, not rank two, under the current graph-coherence model.}
}
\]

Rank two would require a stronger local theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2})
=0.
}
\]

That is, no graph coherence at all in the \((s_7,s_9^Y)\)-fiber.

This is possible but not the current structural expectation.

---

## 6. If rank two holds, the wall theorem simplifies

If \(\operatorname{rank}=2\), then the wall factor can be taken as

\[
E_{\wall}^{(2)}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)
}{
\mathcal N_{D4}
}
\]

up to a bounded positive definite \(2\times2\) form.

Then the moving-line obstruction

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G
\]

is no longer the primary wall. The wall finite-type theorem is replaced by a joint sublevel theorem:

\[
\nu
\left(
|\Gamma_G|<\varepsilon,\,
|\Gamma_{G,2}|<\varepsilon
\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\theta\nu.
\]

This is materially easier than controlling a moving null line.

The forced wall-ODE trap also disappears or becomes a codimension-two trap.

Thus rank two would be a major shortcut.

---

## 7. If rank one holds, current hierarchy remains necessary

If the local fiber has graph coherence

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}},
\]

then the actual wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

Then the current nested tower is still required:

1. fiber Schur gives the one-dimensional wall;
2. amplitude Schur checks

\[
F_Y\notin \mathcal C_Y^{\arith};
\]

3. wall finite type checks

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G
\]

does not vanish on polynomial mass;

4. if wall finite type fails, trap-Schur handles the forced ODE branch.

So rank one is viable, just harder.

---

## 8. Current verdict

The rank-two rescue is equivalent to:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=0
}
\]

or, more generally, to the existence of two independent D4 components outside the actual-source fiber image.

Under the current expected graph model

\[
\operatorname{ran}B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

the rank-two rescue is not available. The D4 channel is rank one with wall

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

So the next finite audit should ask:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }B_Y=0\text{ or }B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)?
}
\]

If \(B_Y=0\), use rank two.

If \(B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)\), proceed with the rank-one nested Schur tower.

If \(B_Y\) is vertical or rank two, the \(Y12\) route fails or changes carrier.

## Agent 3 continuation — when \(B_Y=0\) is plausible, and when graph-rank one is forced

I continue with the fixed nomenclature:

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is the coherent graph slope in the \(Y12\)-fiber,

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}},
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\]

and

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

The previous audit showed that rank two is available only if the actual-source image \(B_Y\subset\mathbb C^2\) does **not** already contain a nonvertical graph direction. I now refine when \(B_Y=0\) is plausible versus when rank-one graph coherence is structurally forced.

---

# 1. What \(B_Y=0\) means

Recall

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right]
\subset\mathbb C^2.
\]

The live fiber coordinates are

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y).
\]

Thus

\[
B_Y=0
\]

means:

\[
\boxed{
\text{no legitimate actual-source/nuisance/coherent mechanism creates either }s_7\text{ or }s_9^Y.
}
\]

Then both D4 coordinates are forbidden, and the D4 output is genuinely rank two:

\[
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Since

\[
c_7\alpha_9\neq0,
\]

this is equivalent to a bounded invertible transform of

\[
H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

So \(B_Y=0\) gives the best downstream theorem.

But \(B_Y=0\) is strong. It requires lower connection and \(E_2^Y\) not even to create an \(s_7\)-coherent direction.

---

# 2. Why graph-rank one is structurally more likely

The expected local actual-source coherence is not arbitrary. It often appears as a relation between the lower carrier and the order-nine \(Y12\)-coordinate:

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}}.
\]

This happens if the singular coherence mechanism says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{allowed actual rows may carry the }s_7\text{ pattern, but their }s_9^Y\text{ continuation is determined.}
}
\]

Then

\[
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

instead of \(0\).

This is exactly a graph-type coherence relation. It is not fatal. It only removes one of the two D4 directions and leaves the vertical residual:

\[
s_9^Y-\rho_Ys_7.
\]

D4 then contributes

\[
H\left[
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right].
\]

So graph-rank one is the default expected case if the actual singular model has any coherent order-seven-to-order-nine propagation.

---

# 3. Finite test distinguishing \(B_Y=0\) from graph-rank one

We need to audit each source of \(B_Y\).

## 3.1 Nuisance quotient

If

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}\neq0,
\]

then the singular quotient itself kills part of the live pair. That is usually a quotient-design problem, not arithmetic.

Required:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}=0.
}
\]

If this fails and the nuisance contains \((0,1)\), the \(Y12\) route is dead by overquotienting.

If it contains only \((1,\rho)\), it imposes graph coherence. That may be acceptable, but it means rank two is gone.

## 3.2 First-order actual-source tangents

Expected:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}=0.
}
\]

Reason: \(s_7\) and \(s_9^Y\) are primitive D4 carrier coordinates, especially \(s_9^Y\) with \(D^2N_0\)-origin. Ordinary source parameters are source-linear / EF-linear.

If this fails, we must classify whether it gives:

\[
(1,\rho_Y)
\]

or vertical \((0,1)\). Vertical is fatal for the \(Y12\) rank-one channel.

## 3.3 Lower connection \(T_7+T_{<7}\)

This is the most likely source of graph rank one.

The finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
\subseteq \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

If instead

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0,
\]

then rank two may survive.

If it is nonzero graph-like, rank one survives.

If it contains two slopes or vertical, \(Y12\) rank-one dies or becomes ill-conditioned.

## 3.4 Actual EF second curvature

Because \(Y12\) is quadratic, this is important:

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

Expected favorable condition:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

This should hold if actual EF second curvature is source-linear parameter curvature rather than primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin mixed \(12\).

If it gives graph direction, rank two is gone but rank one may survive.

If it gives vertical direction, \(Y12\) is actual-source curvature and the finite D4 channel dies.

## 3.5 \(E_2^Y\)

At fiber level:

- fixed basepoint: not in \(B_Y\);
- graph-only correction: modifies \(\rho_Y\);
- finite-dimensional amplitude family: belongs to amplitude layer \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\), not necessarily fiber \(B_Y\);
- locally free vertical: fatal.

Thus fiber audit should include only actual tangent directions, not fixed basepoints.

---

# 4. Rank-two sufficient theorem

The rank-two rescue theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{RankTwoD4Fiber.}
}
\]

Assume:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left(
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal T_7
+
\mathcal T_{<7}
+
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right)=0.
\]

Assume also the live pair is \(Q^A\)-conditioned:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_{\live})\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then D4 has a rank-two local forbidden channel:

\[
\|D4_{\forb}\|^2
\asymp_{Q^A}
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

This bypasses:

\[
\rho_Y,\quad \rho_\Gamma,\quad \text{moving-line wall},\quad \text{forced ODE trap}.
\]

It also likely lowers the downstream analytic burden substantially.

---

# 5. Rank-one sufficient theorem

The more likely theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{OneSlopeD4Fiber.}
}
\]

Assume:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left(
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\right)=0,
\]

and

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left(
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\mathrm{graph}}
\right)
\subseteq
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

with

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
\]

Assume no vertical local freedom.

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}\left((0,1),\operatorname{ran}B_Y\right)^2
\ge
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The forbidden covector is

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7,
\]

and D4 produces

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G),
\]

where

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}.
\]

This is the rank-one nested Schur route.

---

# 6. Quantitative condition for graph slope conditioning

If

\[
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

then the distance from the vertical direction is

\[
\operatorname{dist}((0,1),\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y))^2
=
\frac{1}{1+|\rho_Y|^2}.
\]

Thus graph rank-one is only useful if

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
\]

If

\[
|\rho_Y|\gg Q^A,
\]

the graph is nearly vertical, and the normal component becomes too small.

So the \(\rho_Y\) audit must prove not only low-order dependence but also magnitude control:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low},\qquad |\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
}
\]

This should be part of \(\mathrm{WallHonesty}_{Y12}\).

---

# 7. New ordering: rank-two audit before wall finite type

The correct finite order is now:

1. Construct live dual pair \((\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)\).
2. Compute \(B_Y\).
3. Classify:

\[
B_Y=0,\quad
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),\quad
B_Y\text{ vertical},\quad
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2.
\]

Only if the result is

\[
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

do we need:

\[
\rho_\Gamma,
\]

moving-line wall finite type, and trap-Schur.

If \(B_Y=0\), rank two bypasses them.

If vertical/rank two, current \(Y12\) route dies or changes carrier.

So the wall finite-type analysis is conditional on the one-slope outcome.

---

# 8. Current endpoint

Rank-two D4 is possible exactly when the actual-source/nuisance fiber image is zero or leaves two independent normal directions.

The expected structural possibilities are:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0
\Rightarrow
\text{rank-two D4 wall; best case;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)
\Rightarrow
\text{rank-one moving wall; default case;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
(0,1)\in B_Y
\text{ or }
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2
\Rightarrow
Y12\text{ vertical channel dies.}
}
\]

Thus the immediate next finite calculation should be

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2})
\quad\text{is }0,\ \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),\ \text{vertical, or rank two?}
}
\]

Everything downstream depends on this classification.

## Agent 3 continuation — reducing \(B_Y\) classification to origin-filtered columns

I continue with the fixed nomenclature:

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is the \(Y12\)-fiber graph slope,

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\]

and

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The raw graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The previous step showed that everything downstream depends first on the local fiber classification

\[
B_Y
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right]
\subset \mathbb C^2.
\]

The four possible outcomes are:

\[
B_Y=0,
\qquad
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\qquad
(0,1)\in B_Y,
\qquad
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2.
\]

Only the first gives rank-two D4; the second gives the current rank-one moving wall; the last two kill the \(Y12\) vertical channel. The current architecture explicitly asks us to organize the downstream proof around this fiber Schur layer before amplitude and trap Schur layers. 

---

# 1. Define the fiber projection by live dual covectors

Let

\[
e_7
=
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_7],
\]

and

\[
e_Y
=
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Assume the live pair survives with polynomial conditioning. That is, after quotienting nuisance/wrong-signature directions,

\[
\lambda_{\min}G_{\live}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

where \(G_{\live}\) is the Gram matrix of \(e_7,e_Y\).

Then we obtain bounded dual covectors

\[
\ell_7,\qquad \ell_9^Y
\]

such that

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_7)=(1,0),
\]

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9^Y)(e_Y)=(0,1).
\]

Now define

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(v)
=
(\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

This makes the fiber audit concrete:

\[
B_Y=\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(a):a\in
\mathcal A_Y
\right\},
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_Y
=
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal T_7
+
\mathcal T_{<7}
+
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

---

# 2. Origin-filtered column classification

Each column of \(B_Y\) should be classified by origin. This avoids overcounting terms that are superficially quadratic but not primitive \(Y12\).

The primitive \(Y12\) signature is

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\deg_{\src}=2,\ o_N=D^2N_0,\ o_W=D\mathcal W_{\same},\ \mathrm{mixed}=12,\ \mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ \rho_Y,\ [\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

The key filter is the origin pair:

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same}).
\]

Source-linear EF terms, chart curvature, moving-whitening applied to a tail, and gamma/background terms should not contribute to \(\Pi_{\fib,Y}\) unless the labelled transition or singular realization forgets this origin pair.

So the default expected vanishings are:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}=0,
\]

provided the quotient is signature-faithful.

Thus the only likely nonzero columns are:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_7,
\qquad
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{<7},
\qquad
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\mathrm{graph/free}}.
\]

---

# 3. Lower connection columns

The lower connection is the most likely source of graph-rank one.

Suppose a lower coherent order-seven direction produces

\[
s_7=1,
\qquad
s_9^Y=\rho_Y.
\]

Then the column is

\[
b_7=(1,\rho_Y).
\]

This gives

\[
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

if no other independent columns appear.

This is safe provided:

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A
\]

and

\[
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}
\]

with no dependence on

\[
\Gamma_{G,2},\quad q^{(4)},\quad s_9^Y,
\]

or actual order-nine source data.

The graph-killing covector is then

\[
\ell_Y=\ell_9^Y-\rho_Y\ell_7,
\]

and D4 gives

\[
\ell_Y(D4)
=
H\left((\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\right).
\]

So lower connection is not fatal if and only if it is **one-slope graph-like**.

---

# 4. \(E_2^Y\) fiber columns

The \(E_2\) contribution must be separated into fiber-level and amplitude-level effects.

## 4.1 Fixed \(E_2^Y\)

If

\[
\Pi_YE_2=e_Y^{\fixed},
\]

then it shifts

\[
\eta_{\fixed}.
\]

It is not a column of \(B_Y\), because it is not a tangent direction.

So fixed \(E_2^Y\) does **not** affect the fiber rank.

## 4.2 Graph-only \(E_2^Y\)

If \(E_2^Y\) only modifies

\[
\rho_Y
\]

or adds another graph-like coherent relation

\[
(1,\rho_E),
\]

then:

- if \(\rho_E=\rho_Y\), still one-slope graph;
- if \(\rho_E\neq\rho_Y\), then

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2
\]

and the \(Y12\) vertical channel dies.

Thus graph-only \(E_2^Y\) is safe only if it uses the same slope.

## 4.3 Finite-dimensional coherent amplitude \(E_2^Y\)

If \(E_2^Y\) gives finite-dimensional coherent amplitudes

\[
c_j(p)y_Y(p),
\]

but not local tangent freedom in the fiber, it belongs to the amplitude model

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith},
\]

not to \(B_Y\). It is handled by amplitude Schur, not fiber Schur.

## 4.4 Locally free vertical \(E_2^Y\)

If \(E_2^Y\) gives an admissible local tangent

\[
(0,1),
\]

then

\[
(0,1)\in B_Y.
\]

This kills the \(Y12\) vertical channel.

---

# 5. Actual EF second curvature columns

Since \(Y12\) is quadratic, we must include

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

The favorable theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

This should hold if actual EF second curvature is second derivative of source-linear EF parameters, not primitive

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with same-point whitening.

If instead

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
=
(0,1)
\]

or any vector not collinear with \((1,\rho_Y)\), the channel is killed or at least the one-slope model fails.

Thus the second curvature audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}u
\in
\{0\}\ \text{or}\ \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
}
\]

with the expectation that it is actually zero.

---

# 6. Nuisance quotient columns

The singular nuisance quotient is a design-sensitive piece.

If

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}\neq0,
\]

then one of two things is happening:

1. The nuisance quotient is intentionally killing part of the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber.
2. The quotient is too coarse and forgets origin labels.

If \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\) contains \((0,1)\), the \(Y12\) channel is killed by quotient overcollapse.

If it contains \((1,\rho_Y)\), then the graph is quotient-induced, not actual-source-induced. That might still lead to a rank-one wall, but it should be treated cautiously: the forbidden covector is then partly an artifact of quotient choice.

The safest theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal N_{\sing}=0.
}
\]

---

# 7. Finite audit table

The finite audit can be organized as a table:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{source} & \Pi_{\fib,Y}\text{ expected image} & \text{effect}\\
\hline
\mathcal N_{\sing} & 0 & \text{safe}\\
\mathcal T_{\par} & 0 & \text{safe}\\
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9} & 0 & \text{safe}\\
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7} & 0 & \text{rank two possible}\\
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7} & \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y) & \text{rank one}\\
E_2^Y\ \text{fixed} & \text{basepoint only} & \text{safe}\\
E_2^Y\ \text{graph} & \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y) & \text{rank one}\\
E_2^Y\ \text{finite amp.} & \mathcal C_Y^{\arith} & \text{amplitude Schur}\\
E_2^Y\ \text{vertical free} & \mathbb C(0,1) & \text{fatal}\\
\end{array}
\]

The decision is now finite.

---

# 8. Consequences of each outcome

## Outcome A: all columns vanish

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Rank-two D4 wall:

\[
|H|^2\left(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\right).
\]

This bypasses \(\rho_Y,\rho_\Gamma\), moving-wall finite type, and trap-Schur.

## Outcome B: exactly one nonvertical graph

\[
B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Rank-one wall:

\[
F_Y
=
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

Need:

1. \(\rho_Y,\rho_\Gamma\in\mathcal A_{\low}\);
2. amplitude-Schur if \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\neq0\);
3. wall finite type or trap-Schur.

## Outcome C: vertical column

\[
(0,1)\in B_Y.
\]

Primitive \(Y12\) vertical direction is actual-source coherent. Rank-one \(Y12\) route dies.

## Outcome D: two independent columns

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2.
\]

Entire live fiber is actual-source coherent. \(Y12\) route dies.

---

# 9. Current theorem statement

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{FiberColumnClassification}_{Y12}.
}
\]

Assume the live dual pair exists with polynomial conditioning. Then compute the finite set of columns

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
\left[
\mathcal N_{\sing},
\mathcal T_{\par},
\mathcal T_7,
\mathcal T_{<7},
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\right].
\]

If all vanish, rank-two D4 follows.

If they span one nonvertical line

\[
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)
\]

with

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,\qquad \rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low},
\]

rank-one D4 follows with wall

\[
F_Y=H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

If they contain a vertical direction or span \(\mathbb C^2\), the \(Y12\) route fails in this quotient.

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The rank-two rescue and rank-one moving-wall route are now separated by one concrete audit:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal T_{E_2})
=
0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)?
}
\]

If zero: rank two.

If one-slope: rank one.

If vertical/rank two: \(Y12\) route dies.

This is the next finite computation that matters most.

## Agent 3 — \(E_2^Y\) classification: fiber-local, finite coherent, residual, or locally free

Nomenclature I will use:

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is the lower \(Y12\)-fiber graph slope,

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}}.
\]

\[
\rho_\Gamma
=
\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9}
\]

is the effective wall ratio after subtracting the \(\rho_Y\)-graph.

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\]

is the AFE/O2O3 fold-substitution ratio. I will not use \(r\) for any of these.

The graph-subtracted raw \(Y12\) wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
(\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The assigned task is to classify \(E_2^Y:=\Pi_YE_2\), deciding whether it contributes to \(B_Y\), to \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\), or only to residual error. This is one of the two decisive classifications in the current downstream decision tree. 

---

# 1. Primitive \(Y12\)-projection

Let

\[
\Pi_Y
\]

denote the primitive \(Y12\)-sector projection. It detects the refined signature

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
\left(
\deg_{\src}=2,\ 
o_N=D^2N_0,\ 
o_W=D\mathcal W_{\same},\ 
\mathrm{mixed}=12,\ 
\mathrm{same\ point},\ 
w=1,\ 
\text{order }9,\ 
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right).
\]

This is stricter than merely detecting “degree two” or “one whitening slot.” The origin pair

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same})
\]

is essential. It separates the true primitive \(Y12\) carrier from terms such as

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi]\,E_2,\qquad
D^2\mathcal W[\xi_1,\xi_2]\,N_0,\qquad
\partial_\alpha^2(\mathrm{EF\ atom}),
\]

which may look second-order but are not primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin \(Y12\).

---

# 2. Decomposition of \(E_2\)

Use

\[
E_2
=
E_{\tail}
+
E_{\reg}
+
E_{\smooth}
+
E_{\chart}
+
E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}
+
E_{\model}.
\]

The classification below assumes labelled transition is origin-faithful. If raw transition recomputes \(N_0\), then several “zero by origin” statements must be replaced by a normal-form commutator audit.

---

# 3. Component-by-component \(Y12\) audit

## 3.1 \(E_{\tail}\)

This is the prime/dual truncation tail:

\[
P_2-P_{2,\le X},\qquad D_2-D_{2,\le X}.
\]

Its origin is EF-linear. It changes amplitudes, phases, cutoffs, and packet length. It does not create primitive

\[
D\mathcal W_{\same}[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]].
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{\Pi_YE_{\tail}=0}
\]

provided the transition map transports formed source expressions.

If leakage occurs because realization is not perfectly labelled, it belongs to the targeted residual estimate

\[
\sum_p
\left|
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(E_{\tail})
\right|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu.
\]

It does not belong in \(B_Y\) and does not create \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\).

---

## 3.2 \(E_{\reg}\)

Regularization terms are proof residuals or EF-linear completions unless the regularization explicitly inserts a primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin same-point-whitening term.

Expected classification:

\[
\boxed{\Pi_YE_{\reg}=0\quad\text{or residual-controlled}.}
\]

If residual-controlled, it enters

\[
R_X^Y
\]

and the targeted \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\) estimate.

It should not contribute to \(B_Y\) unless the regularization has an admissible free \(Y12\) parameter, which would be a fatal model-choice issue.

---

## 3.3 \(E_{\smooth}\)

Smooth analytic remainder is source-linear, background-like, or proof-residual. It has no primitive mixed source-Hessian origin.

Expected:

\[
\boxed{\Pi_YE_{\smooth}=0}
\]

or, at worst, filtered residual.

---

## 3.4 \(E_{\chart}\)

Chart curvature is subtle. It may be second-order in coordinates, but its origin is usually

\[
\text{chart/coordinate curvature},
\]

or terms like

\[
D^2\mathcal W\cdot N_0,\qquad
D\mathcal W\cdot DN_0.
\]

These are not the primitive origin pair

\[
(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same}).
\]

Thus, under origin-faithful labelled transition,

\[
\boxed{\Pi_YE_{\chart}=0.}
\]

Danger arises if the transition/chart operation recomputes \(N_0\) from raw variables. Then source-linear chart modes could generate a fake primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin term. That case must be routed to the normal-form commutator test

\[
\mathfrak C_Y
=
\Pi_Y
\left(
D^2N_{0,\sing}[L\xi_1,L\xi_2]
-
S D^2N_{0,\tr}[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\right).
\]

---

## 3.5 \(E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}\)

Gamma/background remainder has no mixed \(12\) primitive source-Hessian signature.

\[
\boxed{\Pi_YE_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}=0.}
\]

It is not a \(Y12\)-fiber tangent and not a \(Y12\)-amplitude model.

---

## 3.6 \(E_{\model}\)

This is the only genuinely dangerous bucket.

It may contain an actual primitive \(Y12\)-sector term

\[
\Pi_YE_{\model}\neq0.
\]

Then it must be classified carefully as one of:

\[
\text{fixed basepoint},\quad
\text{graph-only},\quad
\text{finite coherent amplitude},\quad
\text{residual-controlled},\quad
\text{locally free}.
\]

This classification determines whether \(E_2^Y\) affects \(B_Y\), \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\), or only \(R_X^Y\).

---

# 4. Classification of \(E_2^Y\)

Let

\[
E_2^Y:=\Pi_YE_2.
\]

## Case A — absent

\[
E_2^Y=0.
\]

Then \(E_2\) contributes neither to \(B_Y\) nor to \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\).

This is the best case.

---

## Case B — fixed basepoint

\[
E_2^Y(p)=c_0(p)y_Y(p),
\]

where \(c_0\) is canonically fixed, not an admissible local parameter.

Then \(E_2^Y\) shifts the basepoint

\[
\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}}.
\]

It is not a tangent column in \(B_Y\), and it does not create amplitude freedom.

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^Y\text{ fixed} \Rightarrow \text{safe basepoint shift}.
}
\]

---

## Case C — graph-only correction

\(E_2^Y\) modifies the coherent graph relation

\[
s_9^Y=\rho_Ys_7+\eta_{\mathrm{fixed}}.
\]

This contributes to \(B_Y\) only through the graph line

\[
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Safe condition:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
}
\]

with the same slope already used by lower connection.

If \(E_2^Y\) gives a different graph slope

\[
(1,\rho_E),\qquad \rho_E\neq\rho_Y,
\]

then \(B_Y\) becomes rank two and the \(Y12\) route dies locally.

If \(E_2^Y\) gives vertical

\[
(0,1),
\]

then the \(Y12\) vertical direction is actual-source coherent and the rank-one \(Y12\) channel dies.

---

## Case D — finite-dimensional coherent amplitude

Here

\[
E_2^Y(p;\theta)
=
\left(c_0(p)+\sum_{j=1}^{M}a_jc_j(p)+O(a^2)\right)y_Y(p),
\]

with a finite-dimensional coherent amplitude space

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}:=\operatorname{span}\{c_1,\ldots,c_M\}.
}
\]

This does not enter \(B_Y\) as a local fiber tangent unless the amplitudes are extraction-local. It enters the amplitude model

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}.
\]

Safe condition:

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

has no extraction-scale local interpolation power.

Then downstream requires amplitude Schur:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu_{\bd})}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_Y^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_Y\|^2.
\]

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^Y\text{ finite coherent}
\Rightarrow
\text{amplitude-Schur problem, not local no-go}.
}
\]

---

## Case E — residual-controlled

The \(Y12\) component is present but belongs in the residual:

\[
R_X^Y.
\]

Then the required estimate is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left|
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(E_2^Y)
\right|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu.
}
\]

This is safe if proven.

---

## Case F — locally free amplitude

There is an admissible local amplitude \(\eta_Y(p)\) such that

\[
E_2^Y(p;\eta_Y)=\eta_Y(p)y_Y(p)
\]

on extraction-scale atoms.

Then \(E_2^Y\) is locally free.

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^Y\text{ locally free} \Rightarrow
\text{rank-one }Y12\text{ amplitude route dies}.
}
\]

This is the fatal case.

---

# 5. Fiber-local versus amplitude-level effects

The key separation:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y\text{ sees local fiber tangent directions;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\text{ sees coherent amplitude functions.}
}
\]

Thus:

- fixed \(E_2^Y\): basepoint only;
- graph-only \(E_2^Y):\) contributes to \(B_Y=\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)\);
- finite coherent \(E_2^Y:\) contributes to \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\);
- residual \(E_2^Y:\) contributes to \(R_X^Y\);
- locally free \(E_2^Y:\) fatal.

This prevents over-quotienting.

---

# 6. Consequences for \(B_Y\)

The \(E_2\)-contribution to the fiber matrix satisfies one of the following:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})=0,
\]

or

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

or fatal alternatives:

\[
(0,1)\in \Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local}),
\]

or

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local}
+
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y))
=2.
\]

So the desired theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq
\{0\}\ \text{or}\ \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

---

# 7. Consequences for \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\)

If \(E_2^Y\) is finite coherent, then

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\subset
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}.
\]

The full amplitude model is

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
=
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\param}
+
\cdots.
\]

It must satisfy no-local-free conditions:

1. block dimension small:

\[
\dim\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{\arith}\le Q^C,
\]

preferably \(O(1)\);

2. bounded leverage:

\[
\mathfrak L_\omega(p)
\le
Q^C\frac{\dim\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{\arith}}{\nu_\omega(\Omega_\omega)};
\]

3. low-order basis / no atom-local spikes;

4. amplitude-Schur:

\[
\sum_\omega
\operatorname{dist}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_\omega\|F_Y\|^2.
\]

---

# 8. Correct \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\)

After fixed/coherent pieces are included, define

\[
F_X^Y
=
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
+
E_{2,\mathrm{fixed/coh}}^Y
+
\text{other coherent }Y12\text{ pieces}.
\]

Then

\[
R_X^Y=q''_{\can}-F_X^Y.
\]

The targeted upper is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\omega
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})|_{\Omega_\omega},
\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\omega
\left\|
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X^Y)
\right\|_{\Omega_\omega}^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu.
}
\]

This is the \(Y12\)-filtered completion theorem.

---

# 9. Final classification theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{E2YClassification.}
}
\]

Assume the refined \(Y12\)-origin projection is faithful.

Then:

1. \(E_{\tail},E_{\reg},E_{\smooth},E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}\) are \(\Pi_Y\)-zero or residual-controlled.

2. \(E_{\chart}\) is \(\Pi_Y\)-zero unless transition/chart recomputes \(N_0\).

3. \(E_{\model}\) is the only possible primitive \(Y12\)-source.

4. \(E_{\model}^Y\) must be classified as absent, fixed, graph-only, finite coherent, residual-controlled, or locally free.

5. The \(Y12\) route survives the \(E_2\)-audit iff:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq
\{0\}\ \text{or}\ \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

and finite coherent amplitude components satisfy no-local-free plus amplitude-Schur.

6. The route dies if \(E_2^Y\) supplies a vertical fiber tangent, a second graph slope, or extraction-local amplitude freedom.

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The \(E_2^Y\) audit now produces one of three actionable outcomes:

## Outcome 1 — safe

\[
E_2^Y=0,\quad \text{fixed},\quad \text{graph-only same slope},\quad \text{or residual-controlled}.
\]

Proceed with \(B_Y\) classification and wall theorem.

## Outcome 2 — finite coherent

\[
E_2^Y\subset\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
\]

with no local freedom. Proceed with amplitude-Schur and block/global contradiction.

## Outcome 3 — fatal

\(E_2^Y\) is locally free, vertical, or gives a second graph slope. The current rank-one \(Y12\) route fails.

## Agent 3 continuation — finite audit procedure for \(E_2^Y\)

I continue with the fixed nomenclature:

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is the \(Y12\)-fiber graph slope,

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\]

and

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The \(E_2^Y\) audit now has a clean outcome table, but it still needs an executable finite procedure. I’ll make that procedure explicit.

---

# 1. First split: origin test before size estimates

For each component

\[
E_{\bullet}\in
\{E_{\tail},E_{\reg},E_{\smooth},E_{\chart},E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}},E_{\model}\},
\]

the first question is not size. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Origin}(E_{\bullet})
=
(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same},12,\mathrm{same},w=1)?
}
\]

If no, then

\[
\Pi_YE_{\bullet}=0
\]

formally, subject only to labelled-realization consistency.

So the finite audit begins with the origin-pair projection:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_{\bullet}.
}
\]

Only after a nonzero primitive \(Y12\) origin is found do we ask whether it is fixed, graph-like, finite coherent, residual, or locally free.

This avoids wasting analytic estimates on source-linear terms.

---

# 2. Second split: formed-source versus recomputed-source

For each nonzero-looking contribution, determine whether it arises by:

## A. Transporting a formed source

Then the origin label is preserved.

A source-linear or chart term remains non-\(Y12\).

## B. Recomputing \(N_0\) after transition/chart/regularization

Then even source-linear inputs can generate

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

This is dangerous and must be measured by the normal-form commutator

\[
\mathfrak C_Y(\xi_1,\xi_2)
=
\Pi_Y
\left(
D^2N_{0,\sing}[L\xi_1,L\xi_2]
-
S D^2N_{0,\tr}[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\right)
\]

plus any \(D^2L\) term if the source transition \(L\) is nonlinear.

Thus the second audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y=0,\quad \mathfrak C_Y\text{ finite coherent},\quad
\text{or }\mathfrak C_Y\text{ locally free?}
}
\]

If \(\mathfrak C_Y\) is locally free, labelled realization fails in the \(Y12\) channel.

---

# 3. Third split: fiber coordinate or amplitude function?

Suppose

\[
E_2^Y=\Pi_YE_2\neq0.
\]

Write locally

\[
E_2^Y(p)=c_Y(p)y_Y(p).
\]

Now test whether \(c_Y(p)\) corresponds to:

## A. Fiber-local tangent

This contributes to

\[
B_Y\subset\mathbb C^2.
\]

It appears as a direction in the local \((s_7,s_9^Y)\)-fiber.

Possibilities:

\[
0,\qquad
(1,\rho_Y),\qquad
(0,1),\qquad
(1,\rho_E),\ \rho_E\neq\rho_Y.
\]

## B. Amplitude-level coherent function

This contributes to

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}.
\]

It is not a local tangent unless it has atom-local freedom.

## C. Residual

This contributes to

\[
R_X^Y
\]

and must satisfy \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\).

This split is essential:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y\text{ classifies local fiber freedom;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\text{ classifies amplitude-function freedom.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Fiber audit for \(E_2^Y\)

If \(E_2^Y\) contributes to the local fiber, compute

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local}).
\]

There are four cases.

## Case 1: zero

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})=0.
\]

No effect on \(B_Y\).

## Case 2: same graph

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Then \(E_2^Y\) is graph-compatible. It may modify the final value of \(\rho_Y\), but it does not kill the rank-one channel.

Need:

\[
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A,
\qquad
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low}.
\]

## Case 3: vertical

\[
(0,1)\in \Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local}).
\]

Fatal for the \(Y12\) vertical channel.

## Case 4: second graph slope

\[
(1,\rho_E)\in \Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local}),
\qquad
\rho_E\neq\rho_Y.
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2
\]

once combined with the original graph. The current \(Y12\) route dies locally.

Thus the fiber-level theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subseteq
\{0\}\ \text{or}\ \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

---

# 5. Amplitude audit for \(E_2^Y\)

If \(E_2^Y\) contributes amplitude functions, define

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
=
\operatorname{span}\{c_1,\ldots,c_M\}.
\]

Now classify local freedom.

Let \(\Omega_\omega\) be pre-extraction smooth blocks.

Define

\[
\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}
=
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}|_{\Omega_\omega}.
\]

The safe condition is:

\[
\dim \mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}\le Q^C,
\]

preferably \(O(1)\), plus no atom-local basis. A leverage condition is:

\[
\mathfrak L_\omega(p)
=
\sum_{j=1}^{M_\omega}|e_j(p)|^2
\le
Q^C\frac{M_\omega}{\nu_\omega(\Omega_\omega)}.
\]

If this holds, \(E_2^Y\) is finite coherent, not locally free.

Then it enters the full coherent amplitude model

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
=
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\param}
+\cdots.
\]

The required lower theorem is amplitude-Schur:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu_{\bd})}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_Y^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_Y\|^2.
}
\]

If the leverage fails or dimension is atom-scale, \(E_2^Y\) is locally free and fatal.

---

# 6. Residual audit for \(E_2^Y\)

If \(E_2^Y\) is not coherent but small, put it in

\[
R_X^Y.
\]

Then prove

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\omega
\left\|
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X^Y)
\right\|_{\Omega_\omega}^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu.
}
\]

This is the targeted \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\).

Important: this estimate is only for the primitive \(Y12\)-sector. It should be weaker than full \(K_9\)-completion.

---

# 7. Procedure summary

For each \(E_2\)-component:

\[
E_\bullet
\]

perform:

## Step 1 — origin test

\[
\Pi_YE_\bullet=0?
\]

If yes: done.

## Step 2 — transition/realization test

If nonzero, is it due to labelled transition mismatch?

Check:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y=0,\quad \text{finite coherent},\quad \text{or locally free}.
\]

## Step 3 — fiber versus amplitude

If true \(Y12\)-origin remains, decide:

\[
T_{E_\bullet}^{\local}\to B_Y
\]

or

\[
E_\bullet^Y\to\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
\]

or

\[
E_\bullet^Y\to R_X^Y.
\]

## Step 4 — classify outcome

\[
0,\quad
\text{fixed},\quad
\text{graph-only},\quad
\text{finite coherent},\quad
\text{residual},\quad
\text{locally free}.
\]

Only locally free is immediately fatal.

---

# 8. What the audit should output

The \(E_2^Y\) audit should output three objects:

## 8.1 Fiber contribution

\[
B_Y^{E_2}
=
\Pi_{\fib,Y}(T_{E_2}^{\local})
\subset\mathbb C^2.
\]

Classified as:

\[
0,\quad
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),\quad
\mathbb C(0,1),\quad
\text{rank two/second slope}.
\]

## 8.2 Amplitude contribution

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\subset L^2(\nu_{\bd}).
\]

Classified as:

\[
0,\quad
\text{fixed},\quad
\text{finite coherent nonlocal},\quad
\text{locally free}.
\]

## 8.3 Residual contribution

\[
R_{X,E_2}^Y
\]

with required estimate

\[
\sum_\omega
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_{X,E_2}^Y)\|_\omega^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu.
\]

These three outputs feed directly into:

\[
B_Y\text{ classification},
\quad
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\text{ classification},
\quad
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}.
\]

---

# 9. Current finite theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{E2YFiniteAudit.}
}
\]

For the decomposition

\[
E_2
=
E_{\tail}
+
E_{\reg}
+
E_{\smooth}
+
E_{\chart}
+
E_{\gamma,\mathrm{rem}}
+
E_{\model},
\]

the audit produces:

\[
B_Y^{E_2},
\qquad
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2},
\qquad
R_{X,E_2}^Y.
\]

The \(Y12\) route survives the \(E_2\)-layer if:

1. \(B_Y^{E_2}=0\) or \(B_Y^{E_2}\subseteq\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)\);

2. \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\) is zero/fixed or finite coherent without local freedom;

3. amplitude-Schur holds for the resulting \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\);

4. \(R_{X,E_2}^Y\) satisfies targeted \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\).

The route dies if:

\[
B_Y^{E_2}
\]

contains a vertical direction or second slope, or if

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

is locally free.

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The \(E_2^Y\) problem is now executable:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YE_2
\longrightarrow
(B_Y^{E_2},\ \mathcal C_Y^{E_2},\ R_{X,E_2}^Y).
}
\]

This is the missing bridge between the symbolic origin classification and the downstream decision table.

The main expected favorable outcome remains:

\[
B_Y^{E_2}=0\ \text{or}\ \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}=0\ \text{or finite coherent nonlocal},
\]

and

\[
R_{X,E_2}^Y\text{ is small}.
\]

Anything stronger than that, especially vertical or locally free \(Y12\), kills this rank-one route.

## Agent 3 continuation — reducing \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) to a coefficient-map rank test

Continuing with fixed nomenclature:

\[
\rho_Y=s_9^Y/s_7\text{ graph slope},\qquad
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\qquad
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The raw graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The previous reduction made the \(E_2^Y\) audit executable:

\[
\Pi_YE_2
\longrightarrow
(B_Y^{E_2},\mathcal C_Y^{E_2},R_{X,E_2}^Y).
\]

The only genuinely dangerous part is now

\[
E_{\model}^{Y}:=\Pi_YE_{\model}.
\]

I now reduce \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) to a finite coefficient-map rank test. This is the precise way to distinguish fixed, graph-like, finite coherent, and locally free \(Y12\) contributions. The current architecture requires this distinction before the downstream \(Y12\) route can be classified. 

---

# 1. Write the primitive \(Y12\) component as a coefficient map

On each transition–singular patch \(p\), write

\[
E_{\model}^{Y}(p)
=
c_E(p;\theta)\,y_Y(p),
\]

where

\[
y_Y(p)=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing,p}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

is the local primitive \(Y12\) basis vector, and \(\theta\) denotes the admissible actual-source parameters.

The key object is the coefficient map

\[
\boxed{
c_E:\Theta_{\arith}\to L^2(\nu_{\bd}).
}
\]

The classification of \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) is exactly the classification of the image and differential image of \(c_E\).

---

# 2. Four coefficient-map cases

## Case A — fixed coefficient

If

\[
c_E(p;\theta)=c_0(p)
\]

with no admissible variation, then \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) is a fixed basepoint.

It changes

\[
\eta_{\fixed}
\]

but does not enter \(B_Y\) or \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\) as freedom.

Safe.

---

## Case B — graph coefficient

Suppose variation of \(c_E\) is tied to the lower \(s_7\) coordinate:

\[
\delta c_E(p)=\rho_E(p)\,\delta s_7(p),
\]

and the fiber contribution is

\[
(1,\rho_E).
\]

If

\[
\rho_E=\rho_Y
\]

in the live fiber, then this is graph-compatible and safe at the fiber level.

If

\[
\rho_E\neq \rho_Y,
\]

then \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) creates a second graph slope. Combined with the existing graph, this yields

\[
\operatorname{rank}B_Y=2,
\]

so the current \(Y12\) route dies locally.

If

\[
\delta s_7=0,\qquad \delta c_E\neq0,
\]

then \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) creates vertical freedom

\[
(0,1),
\]

which is fatal.

Thus graph safety requires:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T_{E_{\model}}^{\local}
\subseteq \mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

---

## Case C — finite-dimensional coherent coefficient family

Suppose

\[
c_E(p;\theta)
=
c_0(p)+\sum_{j=1}^{M}a_jc_j(p)+O(a^2),
\]

where \(a_j\) are global or block-coherent parameters, not atom-local amplitudes.

Then

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
=
\operatorname{span}\{c_1,\ldots,c_M\}.
\]

This is not locally fatal. It feeds the amplitude-Schur layer:

\[
F_Y\notin \mathcal C_Y^{\arith}.
\]

Safe only if:

\[
\dim\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}\ll \#\{\text{atoms in }\Omega_\omega\},
\]

the leverage is bounded, and amplitude-Schur holds.

---

## Case D — locally free coefficient

If the coefficient map allows

\[
c_E(p;\theta)=\eta(p)
\]

with \(\eta\) locally adjustable at extraction scale, then

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

contains atom-local amplitudes. This is locally free.

Fatal.

---

# 3. Differential rank test

Let

\[
Dc_E(\theta_0)
\]

be the first derivative map at the actual source, and

\[
D^2c_E(\theta_0)
\]

the second derivative map.

Define the local coefficient span on a block \(\Omega_\omega\):

\[
\mathcal I_\omega^{(1)}
=
\operatorname{span}\left\{
\partial_{\theta_j}c_E|_{\Omega_\omega}
\right\},
\]

\[
\mathcal I_\omega^{(2)}
=
\operatorname{span}\left\{
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}c_E|_{\Omega_\omega}
\right\}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}
=
\mathcal I_\omega^{(1)}+\mathcal I_\omega^{(2)}+\cdots
\]

up to the order of actual-source manifold approximation being quotiented.

The no-local-free rank condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}\le Q^C
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}
\text{ has bounded leverage.}
}
\]

The fatal condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\operatorname{Ev}_\omega
(\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2})
\asymp
\#\{\text{atoms in }\Omega_\omega\}
}
\]

with polynomially conditioned interpolation.

This is the precise “locally free” test.

---

# 4. How ordinary parameters should classify

For ordinary actual-source parameters:

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{ phase},\text{ cutoff},\text{ normalization},\text{ background/gamma},
\]

the favorable expectation is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\theta}c_E=0
}
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-origin grade, unless \(E_{\model}\) itself contains a coherent primitive \(Y12\) counterterm.

Reason: these parameters change source-linear EF atoms, phases, weights, cutoffs, and charts. They do not generate a free coefficient of

\[
D\mathcal W_{\same}[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]].
\]

For \(\alpha\), the key check remains:

\[
\partial_\alpha N_0=0
\]

and

\[
\partial_\alpha(D\mathcal W_{\same})
\]

does not create primitive mixed \(12\) \(D^2N_0\)-origin terms.

If both hold, then

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha c_E=\partial_\alpha^2c_E=0
}
\]

in the \(Y12\) primitive grade.

---

# 5. Relation to \(B_Y\)

The local fiber contribution of \(E_{\model}^Y\) is determined by whether variations of \(c_E\) are tied to \(s_7\).

Define the fiber map

\[
\mathcal F_E:
\delta\theta
\mapsto
(\delta s_7,\delta s_9^Y).
\]

Since

\[
\delta s_9^Y=\delta c_E
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-coordinate, we classify:

\[
\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E=0
\Rightarrow
\text{no }B_Y\text{ contribution};
\]

\[
\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E\subseteq\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)
\Rightarrow
\text{graph-compatible};
\]

\[
(0,1)\in \operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E
\Rightarrow
\text{vertical fatal};
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E=2
\Rightarrow
\text{fiber absorbed}.
\]

So \(E_{\model}^Y\) contributes to \(B_Y\) only through the local derivative map \(\mathcal F_E\), not through fixed basepoint values.

---

# 6. Relation to \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\)

The amplitude contribution is the coherent image of the coefficient map:

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\partial_{\theta}c_E,\,
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}c_E,\ldots
\right\}
\]

after removing purely fiber-graph components already absorbed into \(\rho_Y\).

Then

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
=
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\param}
+\cdots.
\]

The amplitude-Schur theorem is applied to

\[
F_Y=H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G)
\]

modulo this space.

Thus the \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) coefficient map feeds exactly one of:

\[
B_Y,\qquad
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith},\qquad
R_X^Y.
\]

It should not be counted twice.

---

# 7. Executable finite audit

For \(E_{\model}^{Y}\), compute or classify the following.

## Audit 1 — fixed value

\[
c_E(p;\theta_0)
\]

Is it zero, fixed, or residual?

If fixed, include it in the basepoint.

## Audit 2 — first derivatives

For all admissible \(\theta_j\), compute

\[
\partial_{\theta_j}c_E.
\]

Classify each derivative as:

\[
0,\quad
\rho_Y\partial_{\theta_j}s_7,\quad
\text{finite coherent amplitude},\quad
\text{vertical/local}.
\]

## Audit 3 — second derivatives

For all admissible \(\theta_i,\theta_j\), compute

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}c_E.
\]

Because \(Y12\) is quadratic, this is mandatory.

Again classify as:

\[
0,\quad
\text{graph-compatible},\quad
\text{finite coherent},\quad
\text{vertical/local}.
\]

## Audit 4 — local entropy

For finite coherent nonzero derivatives, form \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\) and test blockwise:

\[
\dim \mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2},
\]

leverage,

\[
\mathfrak L_\omega(p),
\]

and bounded-complexity of basis functions.

## Audit 5 — amplitude-Schur

If \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\neq0\), include it in \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\) and test:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_Y^{\arith})^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|F_Y\|^2.
\]

---

# 8. Theorem: \(E_{\model}^Y\) coefficient-map classification

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{EModelYCoefficientRank.}
}
\]

Let

\[
c_E:\Theta_{\arith}\to L^2(\nu_{\bd})
\]

be the primitive \(Y12\)-coefficient map of \(E_{\model}\).

Then:

1. if \(Dc_E=D^2c_E=0\), \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) is fixed/residual and safe;

2. if \(Dc_E,D^2c_E\) are graph-compatible with slope \(\rho_Y\), \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) modifies \(B_Y\) only through \(\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)\);

3. if the image is finite-dimensional coherent with bounded leverage, \(E_{\model}^{Y}\) enters \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\) and requires amplitude-Schur;

4. if the image contains vertical or atom-local amplitudes, the \(Y12\) route dies.

This is the finite rank test for \(E_{\model}^{Y}\).

---

# 9. Current endpoint

The \(E_2^Y\) audit is now reduced to a coefficient-map rank classification:

\[
\boxed{
c_E(\theta)=\Pi_YE_{\model}(\theta)/y_Y.
}
\]

The decisive outputs are:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y^{E_2}
}
\]

from local fiber derivatives,

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
}
\]

from finite coherent coefficient functions,

and

\[
\boxed{
R_{X,E_2}^Y
}
\]

from residual-controlled leftovers.

This is now a computable finite audit rather than a qualitative concern.

## Agent 3 continuation — \(E_{\model}^Y\) coefficient-map audit, pushed to computable tests

I continue with the fixed nomenclature:

\[
\rho_Y
\]

is the \(Y12\)-fiber graph slope,

\[
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\]

and

\[
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The raw graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The previous step reduced \(E_{\model}^Y\) to the coefficient map

\[
c_E(\theta;p)
:=
\frac{\Pi_YE_{\model}(\theta;p)}{y_Y(p)}.
\]

Now I push this to an actual finite audit: compute the rank and locality of the image of \(c_E\), then route it to \(B_Y\), \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\), or \(R_X^Y\). This fits the current nested Schur architecture: fiber Schur, amplitude Schur, then trap Schur. 

---

# 1. The coefficient map has three different linearizations

For each patch \(p\), expand

\[
c_E(\theta;p)
=
c_E^0(p)
+
Dc_E[\delta\theta](p)
+
\frac12D^2c_E[\delta\theta,\delta\theta](p)
+
\cdots.
\]

These pieces have different roles.

## 1.1 Basepoint

\[
c_E^0(p)
\]

is only a basepoint. It shifts

\[
\eta_{\fixed}
\]

or contributes a fixed coherent row. It is not a tangent direction.

So:

\[
\boxed{
c_E^0\neq0\text{ is harmless unless it is treated as freely variable.}
}
\]

## 1.2 First derivative

\[
Dc_E[\delta\theta]
\]

is a genuine tangent contribution. If it is local vertical, it enters \(B_Y\) fatally. If it is graph-compatible, it modifies the \(\rho_Y\)-graph. If it is finite coherent but not fiber-local, it enters \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\).

## 1.3 Second derivative

\[
D^2c_E[\delta\theta,\delta\theta]
\]

must be included because \(Y12\) is itself quadratic. This is the key correction: first-order tangent exclusion is insufficient.

The safe expectation is that actual EF second curvature is still EF-linear / chart curvature / weight curvature, not primitive

\[
D^2N_0
\]

with same-point whitening. If so,

\[
\Pi_YD^2c_E=0.
\]

If not, we must classify it exactly like \(Dc_E\).

---

# 2. Fiber projection of \(Dc_E\)

Define the local fiber map

\[
\mathcal F_E(\delta\theta)
=
(\delta s_7,\delta s_9^Y)
=
(\delta s_7,Dc_E[\delta\theta]).
\]

There are four possibilities.

## Case F0 — zero

\[
\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E=0.
\]

Then \(E_{\model}^Y\) does not affect \(B_Y\).

## Case F1 — graph-compatible

\[
\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E
\subseteq
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Then \(E_{\model}^Y\) only modifies the existing graph.

This is safe if

\[
\rho_Y\in\mathcal A_{\low},
\qquad
|\rho_Y|\le Q^A.
\]

## Case F2 — vertical

\[
(0,1)\in\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E.
\]

Then the primitive \(Y12\) vertical direction is actual-source coherent. The \(Y12\) rank-one route dies locally.

## Case F3 — second slope

\[
(1,\rho_E)\in\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E,
\qquad
\rho_E\ne\rho_Y.
\]

Then the combined local fiber image becomes rank two. The current \(Y12\) vertical channel dies.

Thus the required fiber theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Ran}\mathcal F_E
\subseteq
0
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y).
}
\]

---

# 3. Finite coherent amplitude image

Some \(c_E\)-variation may not be local fiber freedom. It may instead produce coherent amplitude functions

\[
c_1(p),\ldots,c_M(p).
\]

Define

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
=
\operatorname{span}\{c_1,\ldots,c_M\}.
\]

The safe coherent case requires three finite conditions.

## 3.1 Dimension

On each pre-extraction block \(\Omega_\omega\),

\[
M_\omega
=
\dim \mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}
\le Q^C,
\]

preferably \(O(1)\).

## 3.2 Leverage

For an orthonormal basis \(e_j\) of \(\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}\),

\[
\mathfrak L_\omega(p)
=
\sum_{j=1}^{M_\omega}|e_j(p)|^2
\]

must satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak L_\omega(p)
\le
Q^C\frac{M_\omega}{\nu_\omega(\Omega_\omega)}.
}
\]

This prevents atom-local interpolation.

## 3.3 Low-order basis

After normalizing by \(H\alpha_9\),

\[
\mathcal D_Y^{E_2}
=
(H\alpha_9)^{-1}\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

should lie in a low-order algebraic/Sobolev class:

\[
\mathcal D_Y^{E_2}\subseteq\mathcal A_{\low}^{(M)}.
\]

If its basis depends on

\[
q^{(4)},\quad \Gamma_{G,2},\quad s_9^Y,
\]

or fitted order-nine source data, then it risks becoming tautological.

---

# 4. \(E_{\model}^Y\) and the amplitude-Schur layer

Let

\[
F_Y
=
H\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

If \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\neq0\), include it in

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
=
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}
+
\mathcal C_Y^{\param}
+
\cdots.
\]

Then the downstream lower is not pointwise

\[
|F_Y|^2.
\]

It is the Schur residual

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu_{\bd})}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_Y^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_Y\|^2.
}
\]

Blockwise version:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\omega
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu_\omega)}
(F_Y,\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_\omega
\|F_Y\|_{L^2(\nu_\omega)}^2.
}
\]

If this fails, we enter the forced ODE/trap-Schur branch.

---

# 5. Coefficient-map rank test

The actual finite audit for \(E_{\model}^Y\) can now be written as a matrix test.

Choose block \(\Omega_\omega\). Let

\[
\Theta_\omega
\]

be the admissible parameter space restricted to that block.

Define the derivative image matrix

\[
M_{E,\omega}^{(1)}
=
\left[
\partial_{\theta_j}c_E|_{\Omega_\omega}
\right]_j,
\]

and second derivative image matrix

\[
M_{E,\omega}^{(2)}
=
\left[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}c_E|_{\Omega_\omega}
\right]_{i,j}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal C_{Y,\omega}^{E_2}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left(
M_{E,\omega}^{(1)},M_{E,\omega}^{(2)},\ldots
\right)
\]

after removing:

- fixed basepoint pieces;
- graph-compatible pieces already absorbed into \(\rho_Y\);
- residual-small pieces placed into \(R_X^Y\).

Now compute:

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{E,\omega}^{\mathrm{free}},
\]

and leverage of its column span.

The safe condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}M_{E,\omega}^{\mathrm{free}}\le Q^C
\quad\text{and bounded leverage.}
}
\]

The fatal condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}M_{E,\omega}^{\mathrm{free}}
\asymp
\#\{\text{atoms in }\Omega_\omega\}
}
\]

with polynomially conditioned local interpolation.

---

# 6. Ordinary EF parameters should give zero \(Y12\)-image

For each ordinary actual-source parameter:

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{phase},\text{cutoff},\text{normalization},\text{background/gamma},
\]

the expected result is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\theta c_E=0,\qquad
\partial_\theta^2c_E=0
}
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-origin grade.

Reason: these parameters deform EF atoms, weights, cutoffs, or charts. They do not introduce primitive

\[
D\mathcal W_{\same}[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]].
\]

The only caveats are:

1. \(\alpha\) must not deform \(N_0\) or same-point whitening geometry in the primitive \(Y12\) direction;

2. chart transitions must not recompute \(N_0\);

3. \(E_{\model}\) must not introduce a dedicated \(Y12\)-renormalization parameter.

So the expected favorable theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
M_{E,\omega}^{(1)}=M_{E,\omega}^{(2)}=0
}
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-grade, except for fixed/coherent explicitly modelled pieces.

---

# 7. Residual-controlled leftover

After extracting fixed, graph, and coherent pieces, define

\[
R_{X,E_2}^Y
\]

by

\[
\Pi_YE_2
=
E_{2,\fixed}^Y
+
E_{2,\graph}^Y
+
E_{2,\coh}^Y
+
R_{X,E_2}^Y.
\]

The targeted estimate is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\omega
\left\|
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_{X,E_2}^Y)
\right\|_{\Omega_\omega}^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu.
}
\]

This is the \(E_2\)-component of \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\).

---

# 8. Decision tree for \(E_{\model}^Y\)

## If \(c_E\) is fixed

Safe. Add to basepoint.

## If \(Dc_E,D^2c_E\) are graph-compatible

Safe if same \(\rho_Y\). Update \(\rho_Y\) if needed.

## If \(Dc_E,D^2c_E\) are zero but \(c_E\) has small tail

Safe as residual.

## If image is finite-dimensional coherent

Conditionally safe. Add to \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\) and prove amplitude-Schur.

## If image is locally free

Fatal. Current \(Y12\) route dies.

---

# 9. Theorem statement

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{EModelYCoefficientMapAudit.}
}
\]

Let

\[
c_E(\theta;p)
=
\Pi_YE_{\model}(\theta;p)/y_Y(p).
\]

Then the \(E_{\model}^Y\) layer is safe if the coefficient map decomposes as

\[
c_E
=
c_{\fixed}
+
c_{\graph}
+
c_{\coh}
+
c_{\res},
\]

where:

1. \(c_{\fixed}\) is fixed basepoint;

2. \(c_{\graph}\) contributes only to the existing line \(\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y)\);

3. \(c_{\coh}\) spans a finite-dimensional nonlocal coherent space \(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\);

4. \(c_{\res}\) satisfies targeted \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\);

5. no component supplies vertical or atom-local \(Y12\) freedom.

If these hold, \(E_{\model}^Y\) does not kill the \(Y12\) D4 channel.

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The \(E_2^Y\) audit is reduced to a finite derivative-rank and leverage test for the coefficient map

\[
c_E:\Theta_{\arith}\to L^2(\nu_{\bd}).
\]

The expected favorable result is:

\[
Dc_E=D^2c_E=0
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-origin grade, except for fixed or finite coherent nonlocal pieces.

If instead \(c_E\) has atom-local free image or vertical fiber derivative, the current \(Y12\) route fails.

## Agent 3 continuation — proving \(Dc_E=D^2c_E=0\) by source-origin filtration

I continue with the same nomenclature:

\[
\rho_Y=s_9^Y/s_7\text{ graph slope},\qquad
\rho_\Gamma=\frac{\beta_9-\rho_Yc_7}{\alpha_9},
\qquad
R_{\fold}(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The raw graph-subtracted wall is

\[
\ell_{\raw}(\Gamma)=\alpha_9(\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G).
\]

The current target is the \(E_{\model}^Y\) coefficient map

\[
c_E(\theta;p)
=
\frac{\Pi_YE_{\model}(\theta;p)}{y_Y(p)}.
\]

The previous step reduced the problem to a finite derivative-rank test. I now push the favorable route: show that for ordinary actual-source parameters,

\[
Dc_E=D^2c_E=0
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-origin grade, unless the parameter genuinely changes the nonlinear normal form \(N_0\) or same-point whitening geometry. This is exactly the “not locally free” audit needed by the current downstream Schur tower. 

---

# 1. Define the origin filtration

Introduce a filtration by source-origin type:

\[
\mathfrak F
=
(\deg_{\src},o_N,o_W,\mathrm{mixed},\mathrm{same},w,\rho,\mathrm{order}).
\]

The primitive \(Y12\) grade is

\[
\mathfrak F_Y
=
(2,D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same},12,\mathrm{same},1,\rho_Y,9).
\]

Thus

\[
\Pi_YZ\neq0
\]

only if \(Z\) contains the specific formal origin

\[
D\mathcal W_{\same}\bigl[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\bigr]
\]

with mixed branch \(12\), same-point slots, one whitening slot, and order nine.

This excludes terms that are merely:

\[
\deg_{\src}=2,
\qquad
w=1,
\qquad
\mathrm{order}=9.
\]

The origin label is the key.

---

# 2. Ordinary EF parameters are source-linear

Let

\[
\theta\in
\{m,\lambda,\alpha,\mathrm{phase},\mathrm{cutoff},\mathrm{normalization},\mathrm{background/gamma}\}.
\]

For the ordinary actual-source family, the finite source has the schematic form

\[
F_X^\theta
=
B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta.
\]

Differentiating with respect to \(\theta\) gives variations of:

\[
\text{phase},\quad
\text{scale},\quad
\text{cutoff},\quad
\text{amplitude},\quad
\text{gamma/background normalization}.
\]

These are EF-linear operations. They do not introduce a new primitive nonlinear Hessian

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Therefore, for ordinary parameters,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_\theta F_X^\theta=0.
}
\]

Second derivatives are still EF-parameter curvature:

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta
=
\text{second phase/scale/cutoff/amplitude derivatives}.
\]

They may be second-order in parameters, but their origin is still EF-linear/coordinate, not primitive \(D^2N_0\). Hence

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta=0.
}
\]

This is the main structural vanishing.

---

# 3. Apply this to \(E_{\model}^Y\)

The only way \(E_{\model}\) contributes to \(Y12\) is if it contains a component

\[
E_{\model}^{Y}
=
c_E(\theta;p)y_Y(p)
\]

with primitive origin \(\mathfrak F_Y\).

Now differentiate:

\[
\partial_\theta E_{\model}^{Y}
=
(\partial_\theta c_E)y_Y
+
c_E\,\partial_\theta y_Y.
\]

The second term

\[
c_E\,\partial_\theta y_Y
\]

is a basis-motion term. In labelled coordinates it is not a new free \(Y12\)-amplitude; it is absorbed into the coherent realization/transport of the fixed carrier. The actual coefficient variation is

\[
\partial_\theta c_E.
\]

For ordinary EF parameters, if \(c_E\) is not explicitly a model parameter, then

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\theta c_E=0
}
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\) grade.

Similarly,

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}c_E=0.
}
\]

Thus the expected favorable classification is:

\[
E_{\model}^{Y}
=
\text{fixed basepoint}
+
\text{residual-controlled tail},
\]

not tangent/free amplitude.

---

# 4. The three exceptions

The vanishing theorem has exactly three exceptions.

## Exception 1: \(\theta\) changes \(N_0\)

If

\[
\partial_\theta N_0\neq0,
\]

then differentiating can generate

\[
D^2N_0
\]

or related primitive nonlinear source-Hessian terms.

Then

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\theta E_{\model}
\]

may be nonzero.

This must be classified as:

\[
0,\quad
\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),\quad
(0,1),\quad
\text{finite coherent amplitude},\quad
\text{locally free}.
\]

If it is vertical or locally free, the route dies.

## Exception 2: \(\theta\) changes same-point whitening geometry

If

\[
\partial_\theta(D\mathcal W_{\same})\neq0
\]

in a way coupled to \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\), then the origin pair

\[
(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same})
\]

may be affected.

This can create a graph-compatible correction or a finite coherent amplitude. It is fatal only if it gives vertical or locally free \(Y12\)-amplitude.

## Exception 3: \(E_{\model}\) includes an explicit \(Y12\)-renormalization parameter

If the canonical model contains a parameter

\[
\eta_Y
\]

with

\[
E_{\model}^{Y}(\eta_Y;p)=\eta_Y(p)y_Y(p),
\]

then

\[
\partial_{\eta_Y}c_E=1.
\]

If \(\eta_Y(p)\) is local at extraction scale, this is locally free and fatal.

If \(\eta_Y\) is global or finite-dimensional, it enters

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

and is handled by amplitude-Schur.

---

# 5. Parameter-by-parameter audit

## \(m\)-translation

\[
\partial_m e^{-im\log n}=-i\log n\,e^{-im\log n}.
\]

This is EF-linear.

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_m E_{\model}=0,\qquad
\Pi_Y\partial_m^2E_{\model}=0.
}
\]

## \(\lambda\)-scaling

\(\lambda\) changes local coordinates and branch scale. It is chart/coordinate origin unless it recomputes \(N_0\).

If labelled transition transports formed carriers,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_\lambda E_{\model}=0,\qquad
\Pi_Y\partial_\lambda^2E_{\model}=0.
}
\]

If the \(\lambda\)-transition recomputes \(N_0\), the obstruction is exactly the normal-form commutator

\[
\mathfrak C_Y.
\]

## \(\alpha\)-tilt

If \(\alpha\) is a phase/weight/branch tilt,

\[
\partial_\alpha
\]

is EF-linear.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha E_{\model}=0,\qquad
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha^2E_{\model}=0.
}
\]

If \(\alpha\) changes \(N_0\) or same-point whitening geometry, it enters one of the exception cases.

Thus the precise \(\alpha\)-compatibility condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha N_0=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha(D\mathcal W_{\same})=0.
}
\]

## Cutoff / smoothing

Cutoff perturbations are proof choices, not actual-source parameters, unless explicitly admitted.

If admitted, they are cutoff-shape derivatives, EF-linear:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_{\mathrm{cutoff}}E_{\model}=0
}
\]

unless the cutoff changes the nonlinear source map.

## Background/gamma

Gamma/background parameters are not mixed \(12\) primitive source-Hessian terms.

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_{\gamma}E_{\model}=0.
}
\]

---

# 6. Consequence: expected \(E_{\model}^Y\) classification

Under the structural assumptions:

1. actual parameters do not vary \(N_0\);
2. actual parameters do not vary primitive same-point whitening in a \(D^2N_0\)-coupled way;
3. no explicit local \(Y12\)-renormalization parameter is present;
4. labelled transition transports formed carriers,

we get

\[
\boxed{
Dc_E=0,\qquad D^2c_E=0.
}
\]

Therefore

\[
E_{\model}^{Y}
\]

is at most:

\[
\boxed{
\text{fixed basepoint}+\text{residual-controlled tail}.
}
\]

It contributes neither to

\[
B_Y
\]

nor to

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
\]

as a free/coherent amplitude space.

This is the best expected outcome.

---

# 7. If \(Dc_E\neq0\): finite rank routing

If some derivative is nonzero, route it as follows.

## Fiber graph check

Compute

\[
\mathcal F_E(\delta\theta)
=
(\delta s_7,\delta s_9^Y).
\]

If

\[
\mathcal F_E(\delta\theta)\in\mathbb C(1,\rho_Y),
\]

absorb into graph.

If

\[
\mathcal F_E(\delta\theta)=(0,1),
\]

fatal.

If two slopes appear, fatal.

## Amplitude coherence check

If derivatives give functions \(c_j(p)\) but not local fiber freedom, set

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}=\operatorname{span}\{c_j\}.
\]

Then require:

\[
\dim\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\le Q^C,
\]

bounded leverage, low-order basis, and amplitude-Schur.

## Residual check

If derivative terms are small tails, put them in \(R_X^Y\) and prove targeted \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\).

---

# 8. Theorem: source-linear parameters do not free \(Y12\)

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{SourceLinearNoFreeY12.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. \(N_0\) is fixed under actual EF parameters;
2. \(D\mathcal W_{\same}\) is fixed in the primitive \(Y12\)-origin grade;
3. transition is labelled/formed-source preserving;
4. no explicit local \(Y12\)-renormalization parameter is present.

Then for all actual EF parameters \(\theta\),

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_\theta E_{\model}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_Y\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}E_{\model}=0.
}
\]

Therefore \(E_{\model}^Y\) does not supply local fiber freedom or coherent amplitude freedom. Any \(E_{\model}^Y\) contribution is fixed or residual-controlled.

This is the clean favorable theorem.

---

# 9. Remaining finite checks

The theorem depends on four finite checks:

## Check A — \(N_0\)-fixedness

\[
\partial_\theta N_0=0
\]

for all admitted parameters.

## Check B — primitive whitening fixedness

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\theta(D\mathcal W_{\same})=0.
\]

## Check C — no local \(Y12\)-renormalization

There is no admitted parameter \(\eta_Y(p)\) with

\[
E_{\model}^Y=\eta_Y(p)y_Y(p)
\]

at extraction scale.

## Check D — labelled transition naturality

\[
\mathfrak C_Y=0
\]

or finite coherent/residual, not locally free.

If these pass, \(E_2^Y\) is safe.

---

# 10. Current endpoint

The \(E_{\model}^Y\) coefficient-map rank test strongly favors the safe outcome:

\[
\boxed{
Dc_E=D^2c_E=0
}
\]

for ordinary actual-source parameters, because they are source-linear and do not change the primitive nonlinear source-Hessian \(D^2N_0\).

The only real hazards are:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\theta N_0\neq0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\theta(D\mathcal W_{\same})\text{ couples to }D^2N_0,
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{explicit local }Y12\text{ renormalization freedom}.
}
\]

Absent those, \(E_2^Y\) is fixed/residual and does not obstruct the \(Y12\)-normal D4 channel.
